| Quality Measurement | Objective, ex-ante | Subjective, ex-post |
| Location Decision | Cost-driven | Demand-driven |
| Outsourcing Risk | Low (supply chain) | High (brand) |
| Demand Variability | Moderate | **High** |
| Productivity Measure | Units/hour | Transactions/hour |
| Automation | High (Industry 4.0) | Increasing (RPA, AI) |

#### Table 2.4.2 — Key Operational Formulas (with units)

| Metric | Formula | Units | Sector |
|--------|---------|-------|--------|
| Productivity | $P = \dfrac{O}{I}$ | units/hour | Both |
| Capacity Utilization | $CU = \dfrac{\text{Actual Output}}{\text{Max Capacity}} \times 100$ | **%** | Both |
| Inventory Turnover | $IT = \dfrac{\text{COGS}}{\text{Avg. Inventory}}$ | times/year | Manufacturing |
| Service Level | $SL = 1 - \dfrac{\text{Stockouts}}{\text{Total Demand}}$ | dimensionless | Both |
| Customer Churn Rate | $CR = \dfrac{\text{Lost Customers}}{\text{Total Customers at Start}} \times 100$ | **%** | Service |
| First Pass Yield | $FPY = \dfrac{\text{Good Units}}{\text{Total Units Produced}} \times 100$ | **%** | Manufacturing |
| Occupancy Rate | $OR = \dfrac{\text{Used Capacity}}{\text{Total Capacity}} \times 100$ | **%** | Service |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Notation Rule:** When writing the formulas in your answer sheet, ensure to use **CU**, **IT**, etc. as defined here. KTU valuation key awards marks only when the **denominator and numerator** are clearly stated.

### 2.5 Why Manufacturing and Service Operations Need Different Strategies

- **Manufacturing** competes on **cost, quality, and delivery speed** of the *product*.
- **Service** competes on **experience, customization, and trust** of the *interaction*.
- A manufacturing firm can mask a defect (refund, recall) — a service firm cannot (a bad haircut cannot be recalled).

> **Real-world utility:** This dichotomy drives career choices too — a Mechanical Engineering student working in an automotive plant applies *production* concepts; the same student working in a hospital equipment maintenance firm applies *service operations* concepts. KTU's OEIET723 syllabus is designed to give every B.Tech student a working vocabulary for both.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Frameworks & Derivations

### 3.1 The Hayes-Wheelwright Product-Process Matrix (Analytical Foundation)

Although KTU Module 1 is largely conceptual, examiners occasionally ask students to *derive* the operational position of a firm on the **Process Matrix**. Below is the rigorous step-by-step logic.

**Given:** A firm with the following operational data.

- Product Volume ($V$) = 10,000 units/month
- Product Variety ($N$) = 5 SKUs
- Process Standardization Index ($\sigma$) = 0.65 (dimensionless, $0 \le \sigma \le 1$)

**Step 1 — Compute the Volume-Variety Ratio (VVR):**

$$VVR = \dfrac{V}{N} = \dfrac{10{,}000}{5} = 2{,}000 \text{ units/SKU/month}$$

*Explanation:* This ratio indicates how repetitive the production is. A high VVR means low variety, high volume — typical of **batch/assembly line** processes.

**Step 2 — Classify the Process Type using VVR thresholds (KTU reference):**

| VVR Range | Process Type | Sector Tendency |
|-----------|--------------|-----------------|
| $\vert VVR \vert > 5{,}000$ | **Continuous Flow / Assembly Line** | Pure Manufacturing |
| $1{,}000 \le \vert VVR \vert \le 5{,}000$ | **Batch Production** | Quasi-Manufacturing |
| $100 \le \vert VVR \vert < 1{,}000$ | **Job Shop** | Hybrid |
| $\vert VVR \vert < 100$ | **Project / Custom Service** | Pure Service |

*Explanation:* With VVR = 2,000, the firm falls into **Batch Production** — a hallmark of firms like bakeries, pharmaceutical batch plants, or garment factories.

**Step 3 — Compute the Process Capability Index (Cp):**

$$C_p = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma_{\text{process}}}$$

*Explanation:* $C_p$ measures how well a process fits within specification limits. KTU Module 1 mentions this in passing; Module 2 (Quality Management) deepens it. We include it here to demonstrate how a service firm with $\sigma = 0.65$ (low standardization) would have a low $C_p$, justifying the use of **poka-yoke** (mistake-proofing) or **SERVQUAL** audits.

### 3.2 Service Capacity Calculation (Derivation)

KTU frequently tests a service capacity problem. Given:
- Number of service providers (barbers) = $b = 4$
- Average service time per customer = $t = 30$ minutes
- Operating hours per day = $H = 8$ hours
- Effective utilization factor = $\eta = 0.85$

**Step 1 — Compute the daily raw service capacity (in customer-slot units):**

$$C_{\text{raw}} = \dfrac{b \times H \times 60}{t} = \dfrac{4 \times 8 \times 60}{30} = 64 \text{ customer-slots/day}$$

*Explanation:* This is the maximum *theoretical* throughput assuming zero breaks, zero variability, and zero idle time — an unrealistic assumption in real services.

**Step 2 — Apply the effective utilization factor:**

$$C_{\text{effective}} = C_{\text{raw}} \times \eta = 64 \times 0.85 = 54.4 \approx 54 \text{ customers/day}$$

*Explanation:* The factor $\eta$ accounts for setup, breaks, and variability. The fractional value 0.4 customer represents the **service perishability** problem — we cannot "store" the unfilled slot.

**Step 3 — Compute the Revenue Impact of Unused Capacity:**

$$R_{\text{lost}} = (C_{\text{raw}} - C_{\text{effective}}) \times \text{Avg. Revenue per Service}$$

If avg. revenue = ₹200/customer, then:

$$R_{\text{lost}} = (64 - 54.4) \times 200 = ₹1{,}920 \text{ lost per day}$$

*Explanation:* This is the **perishability cost of service** — a concept KTU examiners love to test in Part B (14-mark) questions.

### 3.3 Productivity Differential — Why Service Productivity is Harder to Measure

Unlike manufacturing, where:

$$P_{\text{manuf}} = \dfrac{\text{Units Produced}}{\text{Labor Hours}}$$

Service productivity must account for **quality of experience**:

$$P_{\text{service}} = \dfrac{\text{Output Quantity} \times \text{Quality Rating}}{\text{Labor Hours}}$$

If a salon serves 10 customers/day with an average quality rating of 4.2/5 in 8 labor-hours:

$$P_{\text{service}} = \dfrac{10 \times 4.2}{8} = 5.25 \text{ rating-units per hour}$$

*Explanation:* The **intangibility** of services forces managers to embed subjective quality into the productivity metric. This is the operational reason why service firms struggle with benchmarking versus manufacturing rivals.

### 3.4 Summary of Analytical Logic

- **Step 1 →** Quantify the operations (volume, variety, time).
- **Step 2 →** Classify using established frameworks (VVR, IHIP).
- **Step 3 →** Apply real-world correction factors ($\eta$, $\sigma$, perishability).
- **Step 4 →** Compute managerial impact (lost revenue, productivity index).

This four-step logic is the **template KTU expects in any 14-mark Part B answer** on this topic.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Goods-Service Continuum — Block-Level Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram maps the conceptual flow of operations as a firm moves from pure goods to pure services. It is rendered as a **left-to-right functional architecture** to ensure all node IDs are alphanumeric and no reserved keywords are used.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1[Pure Manufacturing] --> A2[Quasi Manufacturing]
    A2 --> A3[Hybrid Operations]
    A3 --> A4[Quasi Service]
    A4 --> A5[Pure Service]

    B1[Tangible Output 10] --- A1
    B2[Tangible Output 7] --- A2
    B3[Tangible Output 5] --- A3
    B4[Tangible Output 3] --- A4
    B5[Tangible Output 0] --- A5

    C1[Steel Plant] --- A1
    C2[Fast Food] --- A2
    C3[Restaurant] --- A3
    C4[Hospital] --- A4
    C5[Consulting] --- A5

    D1[High Inventory] --- A1
    D2[Low Customer Contact] --- A1
    D3[High Customer Contact] --- A5
    D4[Zero Inventory] --- A5
```

*Explanation:* Reading left to right, **tangibility decreases**, **customer contact increases**, and **inventory possibility vanishes**. This single diagram can be sketched in your exam sheet to score **2–3 easy marks** even on an unfamiliar question.

### 4.2 Process Flow — How an Order Fulfills Differently in Each Sector

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Customer Order Received] --> R2{Product Type?}
    R2 -->|Tangible Goods| M1[Schedule Production]
    M1 --> M2[Manufacture in Plant]
    M2 --> M3[Quality Inspection]
    M3 --> M4[Warehouse Storage]
    M4 --> M5[Dispatch via Logistics]
    M5 --> M6[Customer Receives Product]

    R2 -->|Service| S1[Confirm Appointment]
    S1 --> S2[Service Provider Engages]
    S2 --> S3[Real Time Delivery]
    S3 --> S4[Immediate Consumption]
    S4 --> S5[Feedback Collection]
```

*Explanation:* The **left branch** (Manufacturing) has a long, linear pipeline with buffers (warehouses). The **right branch** (Service) is compact, with **no buffer** between production and consumption — this is the visual embodiment of the **IHIP** characteristics.

### 4.3 Strategic Decision Tree — Sector Identification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Is output tangible?] --> Q2{Yes}
    Q1 --> Q3{No}
    Q2 --> Q4[Is it storable in inventory?]
    Q4 -->|Yes| Q5[Pure Manufacturing Sector]
    Q4 -->|No| Q6[Quasi Manufacturing - E.g. Restaurant Meal]
    Q3 --> Q7[Is there direct customer provider contact?]
    Q7 -->|Yes| Q8[Pure Service Sector]
    Q7 -->|No| Q9[Quasi Service - E.g. ATM Banking]
```

*Explanation:* This decision tree is an **exam-time lifesaver**. If you forget the IHIP rule during the exam, run any firm through this tree and arrive at a defensible classification.

### 4.4 The KTU Module 1 Concept Map

```mermaid
mindmap
  root((Mfg vs Service))
    Goods
      Tangible
      Inventoriable
      HighCapital
      LowContact
    Services
      IHIP
        Intangibility
        Heterogeneity
        Inseparability
        Perishability
    Continuum
      QuasiMfg
      QuasiSvc
    Frameworks
      VVR
      HayesWheelwright
      SERVQUAL
```

*Explanation:* This mind map is the **single-page revision chart** for Module 1. A KTU-graded answer that opens with this mind map (or a hand-drawn equivalent) is awarded **impression marks** for structured thinking.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: Define "Operations Management" and distinguish between manufacturing operations and service operations with two examples each. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**

**Operations Management (OM)** is the science and art of designing, operating, and improving the production systems that create goods and/or deliver services. As per KTU 2024 Scheme, OM is the management of the *transformation process* — converting inputs (materials, labor, capital, information) into outputs (goods or services) that satisfy customer needs.

**Distinction Table:**

| Aspect | Manufacturing | Service |
|--------|---------------|---------|
| Output | Tangible (e.g., smartphone from Foxconn) | Intangible (e.g., flight by Air India) |
| Inventory | Possible — warehouses, WIP | Generally not possible |
| Customer Contact | Low (back-office) | High (front-office) |

**Two examples each:**
- *Manufacturing:* Car assembly (Maruti Suzuki); Mobile phone production (Apple contract in India).
- *Service:* Banking (SBI); Healthcare (Apollo Hospitals).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write a *definition* of OM but **omit the distinguishing examples**. KTU valuation key explicitly requires 2 + 2 examples. **Loses 1 mark** if missing.

---

#### Question 2: Explain the term "Perishability" of services with a suitable example. Why does it not apply to manufacturing goods? [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Model Answer:**

**Perishability** is the characteristic of services whereby unused service capacity is lost forever and cannot be inventoried for future use. In other words, services cannot be stored.

**Example:** Consider an airline operating a 180-seat flight from Kochi to Delhi. If only 120 seats are sold, the 60 empty seats represent **60 units of lost revenue** that *cannot* be sold tomorrow — once the plane takes off, the capacity is gone. Similarly, an unsold hotel room for a particular night is lost at dawn the next day.

**Why it does not apply to manufacturing:** A manufacturing firm (e.g., a cement plant) that produces 1,000 bags of cement in a day and sells only 700 can store the remaining 300 bags in a warehouse for weeks or months. The 300 bags retain their value and can be sold later. This **inventory buffering** is impossible for services, which is the structural reason manufacturing firms face less revenue volatility than pure service firms.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Do not confuse *perishability* with *deterioration*. A banana is perishable **in the sense of decay**, but in KTU terminology, perishability specifically means **unstoreable capacity** of a service, not biological spoilage.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### Question A: Differentiate between Manufacturing and Service operations. Discuss the IHIP characteristics of services with suitable examples. How do these characteristics create unique challenges for operations managers in the service sector? [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Sub-Part (a) — Manufacturing vs Service Operations [7 Marks]**

| # | Parameter | Manufacturing | Service |
|---|-----------|---------------|---------|
| 1 | Output | Tangible, storable | Intangible, often perishable |
| 2 | Customer Contact | Low | High |
| 3 | Inventory | Possible (WIP, FGI) | Not possible |
| 4 | Lead Time | Long | Short |
| 5 | Capital Intensity | High | Low to moderate |
| 6 | Quality Control | Ex-ante (pre-delivery) | Ex-post (post-delivery) |
| 7 | Location | Cost-driven | Demand-driven |
| 8 | Variability | Low | High |
| 9 | Automation | High | Growing (AI, RPA) |
| 10 | Skill Requirement | Technical | Interpersonal + Technical |

**Sub-Part (b) — IHIP Characteristics & Managerial Challenges [7 Marks]**

The **IHIP** model describes the four defining traits of services:

1. **Intangibility:** A customer cannot touch or test a service before buying. *Example:* A patient cannot "test-drive" a heart surgery. *Challenge:* Marketing must focus on *tangibilizing* the intangible — through branding, testimonials, physical evidence (clean hospital, branded uniform).

2. **Heterogeneity:** Service delivery varies with provider, time, customer. *Example:* Two surgeries by the same doctor on the same day can differ. *Challenge:* Standardization is hard; managers use **SERVQUAL** audits, training, and standard operating procedures (SOPs).

3. **Inseparability:** Production and consumption occur simultaneously. *Example:* A haircut is consumed as it is produced. *Challenge:* The provider *is* the product; training and recruitment become strategic priorities.

4. **Perishability:** Unsold capacity is lost. *Example:* Empty airline seat at takeoff. *Challenge:* Managers use **yield management**, dynamic pricing, and overbooking to minimize revenue loss.

**Conclusion:** These four traits force service operations managers to focus more on **people, process design, and demand smoothing** than on inventory — the exact opposite of manufacturing managers.

---

#### Question B: What is the Goods-Service Continuum? Explain with a diagram. How does the position of a firm on this continuum influence its operations strategy? Illustrate with three real-world examples. [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Sub-Part (a) — The Goods-Service Continuum [7 Marks]**

The **Goods-Service Continuum** is a conceptual model (popularized by Kotler, used widely in KTU 2024 syllabus) that places all economic offerings on a single horizontal axis ranging from **pure tangible goods** on the left to **pure intangible services** on the right. Most real firms lie somewhere in the middle.

**Diagram (to be drawn in answer sheet):**

```
Pure Goods (T=10)  |  Quasi-Mfg (T=7)  |  Hybrid (T=5)  |  Quasi-Svc (T=3)  |  Pure Service (T=0)
Steel Plant        |  Fast Food        |  Restaurant    |  Hospital         |  Consulting Firm
SAIL               |  McDonald's       |  Paradise      |  Apollo           |  McKinsey
```

Where $T$ = tangibility score on a 0–10 scale.

**Sub-Part (b) — Influence on Operations Strategy [7 Marks]**

The position on the continuum dictates **four operational decisions:**

1. **Capacity Strategy:** Pure goods → large fixed capital, long lead times. Pure services → flexible labor, just-in-time staffing.
2. **Inventory Strategy:** Pure goods → buffer stock, safety stock. Pure services → no inventory; *use demand forecasting and yield management*.
3. **Quality Strategy:** Pure goods → statistical process control (SPC), Six Sigma. Pure services → SERVQUAL, mystery shopping.
4. **Location Strategy:** Pure goods → near raw materials / transport hubs. Pure services → near customers.

**Three Real-World Examples:**

- **Tata Steel (Pure Goods, T=10):** Capital-intensive, exports globally, inventory-heavy, low customer contact.
- **Domino's Pizza (Quasi-Manufacturing, T=7):** Standardized kitchen processes, modest inventory, some customer contact (delivery, online ordering).
- **Apollo Hospital (Pure Service, T=1):** Knowledge-intensive, no inventory of "surgery," high customer contact, perishability of doctor-time.

**Conclusion:** The continuum helps managers recognize that **operations strategy is not one-size-fits-all**; it must be **tailored to the tangibility and customer-contact profile** of the firm.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Internal Choice:** Many students answer **only Question A** and skip **Question B**. In KTU 2024, you may be given a choice between A and B in Part B. **Always read the question paper carefully** — you only need to answer one (14 marks), but attempting both without choice may lead to **negative moderation** if either is incomplete. Pick the one you know best and write it fully.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Block — Memorize Before Exam Day**

- [x] **Manufacturing** = Tangible, storable, inventoriable, low customer contact, long lead time.
- [x] **Service** = Intangible, perishable, inseparable, heterogeneous (use **IHIP**).
- [x] **Goods-Service Continuum** = Most real firms are *hybrids*; place them on a 0–10 tangibility scale.
- [x] **Productivity (Manufacturing):** Units produced ÷ Labor hours.
- [x] **Productivity (Service):** Quantity × Quality rating ÷ Labor hours.
- [x] **Capacity Utilization (CU):** Actual output ÷ Max capacity × 100.
- [x] **Perishability Cost:** Lost revenue from unsold/unused service capacity.
- [x] **VVR (Volume-Variety Ratio):** $V/N$; high VVR → assembly line; low VVR → job shop/service.
- [x] **Key Tools:** Hayes-Wheelwright Matrix, SERVQUAL, Yield Management, Poka-Yoke, SPC.
- [x] **Strategic Implication:** As a firm moves right on the continuum, **inventory disappears, customer contact rises, and operations becomes people-centric**.
- [x] **Always give 2 examples in Part A** — KTU examiner notes penalize missing examples.
- [x] **Always draw a diagram in Part B** — even a rough continuum sketch earns 1–2 impression marks.
- [x] **Distinguish perishability (service) from deterioration (biological).** These are *not* the same.
- [x] **Three real-world examples** in any 14-mark answer = guaranteed 1 extra mark for "application."
- [x] **Use IHIP acronym** when describing services — it is the KTU gold standard.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Operations Decision Making
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEIET723 - PRODUCTION AND OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Operations Decision Making # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Operations Decision Making** is the systematic process of selecting the most appropriate course of action among several alternatives to achieve the operational goals of an organization. It encompasses the structured identification, analysis, and resolution of problems related to the transformation of inputs (raw materials, labor, capital, information) into outputs (goods and services) in a production or service environment. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 Aligned)** syllabus for Production and Operations Management, operations decisions form the backbone of how an organization plans, controls, and optimizes its production systems. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Operations decisions are broadly classified along two dimensions: the **function involved** (process, quality, capacity, inventory, supply chain) and the **time horizon** (strategic, tactical, operational). Every decision taken in an organization falls into one of these categories. ### 1.2 The Three Hierarchical Levels of Operations Decisions **Strategic Decisions (Long-term, 3–10 years):** - Deciding the *what* and *where* of operations. - Examples: facility location, capacity expansion, choice of technology, vertical integration. - Made by top-level management (CEO, Board of Directors). **Tactical Decisions (Medium-term, 1–12 months):** - Deciding the *how much* and *when* of operations. - Examples: production planning, workforce levels, inventory policies, vendor selection. - Made by middle-level management (Production Managers, Plant Heads). **Operational Decisions (Short-term, daily/weekly):** - Deciding the *how* of day-to-day activities. - Examples: job scheduling, machine loading, daily workforce assignment, quality control. - Made by lower-level management (Supervisors, Foremen). ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen 🍳 > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: "The Chef in a Kitchen"** Imagine a head chef running a successful restaurant. The chef's decision-making mirrors operations management beautifully: | Operations Manager | Head Chef | |---|---| | **Strategic Decision:** Should we open a new branch in Kochi or Trivandrum? | **Strategic:** Should the restaurant open a new location or launch a cloud kitchen? | | **Tactical Decision:** How many staff to hire for the festival season? | **Tactical:** How much rice, vegetables, and chicken should we stock for Onam week? | | **Operational Decision:** Which worker handles which table today? | **Operational:** Which cook prepares the Biriyani and which handles the Fish Curry? | Just as a chef balances **taste, cost, speed, and customer satisfaction**, an operations manager balances **quality, cost, delivery, and flexibility** — collectively known as the **competitive priorities of operations**. ## 1.4 Why Is Operations Decision Making Important? > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Definition (must memorize for KTU exams):** > *"Operations Decision Making is the process of applying managerial, analytical, and quantitative techniques to design, plan, operate, and control the production system so that organizational objectives are achieved efficiently and effectively."* - It directly impacts **productivity** (output per input). - It determines **cost competitiveness** in the market. - Poor operational decisions lead to **waste, delays, defects, and customer dissatisfaction**. - Good operational decisions improve **profitability, agility, and sustainability**. ## 1.5 The Decision-Making Environments Operations decisions are made under different levels of information availability: 1. **Decision Making under Certainty:** The manager knows the exact outcome of each alternative (e.g., investing in government bonds). 2. **Decision Making under Risk:** The manager knows the probabilities of different outcomes (e.g., demand forecasting with historical data). 3. **Decision Making under Uncertainty:** The manager has little or no information about outcomes (e.g., launching a completely new product in a new market). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Six-Step Decision-Making Process According to KTU's recommended POM frameworks (Heizer-Render & Krajewski-style methodology), every operations decision follows this **structured sequence**: 1. **Identify the Problem** — Recognize the gap between current and desired state. 2. **Develop Alternatives** — Generate feasible options (minimum 2–3). 3. **Evaluate Alternatives** — Analyze each using criteria (cost, benefit, risk). 4. **Select the Best Alternative** — Choose using quantitative/qualitative tools. 5. **Implement the Decision** — Execute through plans, budgets, and resources. 6. **Monitor and Control** — Track outcomes; take corrective action if needed. > [!NOTE] > **Memory Trick (KTU favorite):** Use the acronym **IDEAS-M** → *Identify → Develop → Evaluate → Alternative → Select → Monitor*. ## 2.2 Strategic, Tactical, and Operational Decisions — Comparison | Dimension | Strategic | Tactical | Operational | |---|---|---|---| | **Time Horizon** | Long (3–10 years) | Medium (1–12 months) | Short (daily/weekly) | | **Decision Maker** | Top Management | Middle Management | Lower Management | | **Level of Detail** | Aggregated, broad | Moderate | Highly specific | | **Capital Involved** | Very high | Moderate | Low | | **Reversibility** | Hard to reverse | Partially reversible | Easily reversible | | **Information Required** | External + Internal | Mostly internal | Internal | | **Example** | Plant location | Production schedule | Machine loading | ## 2.3 The Four Stages of Operations Management (Hill's Framework) Operations decisions span across four interconnected stages: - **Stage 1 — Design:** Determining the configuration of the operation (product/service design, process selection, facility location, capacity). - **Stage 2 — Planning & Control:** Managing the flow of work (forecasting, inventory, MRP, scheduling). - **Stage 3 — Improvement:** Enhancing performance continuously (TQM, Six Sigma, Lean, Kaizen). - **Stage 4 — Delivery:** Executing the operation and ensuring on-time, quality output. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following formulas are **high-yield for KTU University Exams**. Memorize the structure, units, and meaning of each. | # | Concept | Formula | Meaning of Variables | Unit | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Break-Even Point (units)** | $BEP = \dfrac{FC}{P - VC}$ | $FC$ = Fixed Cost, $P$ = Price per unit, $VC$ = Variable Cost per unit | Units | | 2 | **Break-Even Point (₹ sales)** | $BEP_{₹} = \dfrac{FC}{1 - \dfrac{VC}{P}}$ | All costs in ₹; ratio is contribution margin | Rupees | | 3 | **Contribution Margin** | $CM = P - VC$ | Selling price minus variable cost per unit | ₹/unit | | 4 | **Margin of Safety (MOS)** | $MOS = \dfrac{\text{Actual Sales} - BEP_{₹}}{\text{Actual Sales}} \times 100$ | Cushion above break-even | % | | 5 | **Expected Monetary Value (EMV)** | $EMV = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \cdot O_i$ | $P_i$ = Probability of outcome $i$, $O_i$ = Outcome (payoff) value | ₹ | | 6 | **Expected Value of Perfect Information (EVPI)** | $EVPI = EV_{wPI} - EMV_{best}$ | Value of perfect information vs. best decision | ₹ | | 7 | **Payback Period** | $PB = \dfrac{\text{Initial Investment}}{\text{Annual Cash Inflow}}$ | Time to recover the investment | Years | | 8 | **Productivity** | $\text{Productivity} = \dfrac{\text{Output}}{\text{Input}}$ | Output per unit input | Units/hour, etc. | | 9 | **Capacity Utilization** | $CU = \dfrac{\text{Actual Output}}{\text{Design Capacity}} \times 100$ | How much of design capacity is used | % | | 10 | **Cycle Time** | $CT = \dfrac{\text{Available Time}}{\text{Units Required}}$ | Time between successive completions | Time/unit | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Reminder for KTU Exams:** When asked "Should we accept a special order?" — always compute **incremental revenue vs. incremental cost** and ensure the order covers at least variable cost. Do not include fixed costs in the special-order analysis if they remain unchanged. ## 2.5 Real-World Utility - **Break-Even Analysis** is used by startups in Kerala's MSME sector to decide whether to launch a new product line. - **Decision Trees** are used in pharmaceutical companies to choose between R&D projects. - **Payback Period** is the favourite metric of Indian banks (e.g., SBI, KVB) while sanctioning SME loans. - **EMV** is widely used in insurance, oil exploration, and inventory decisions under probabilistic demand. ## 2.6 Categories of Quantitative Decision-Making Tools | Tool | Type of Problem Solved | |---|---| | **Linear Programming (LP)** | Optimal allocation of limited resources | | **Decision Tree Analysis** | Sequential decisions under risk | | **Break-Even Analysis** | Volume-cost-profit trade-off | | **Simulation (Monte Carlo)** | Complex, stochastic systems | | **Inventory Models (EOQ)** | Order quantity & reorder decisions | | **PERT/CPM** | Project scheduling and crashing | | **Queuing Theory** | Waiting line optimization | | **Forecasting Models** | Demand prediction | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Break-Even Point (BEP) from First Principles The **Break-Even Point** is the production/sales volume at which **Total Revenue (TR) equals Total Cost (TC)**. The firm makes neither profit nor loss. ### Step 1: Define Total Cost Total cost is the sum of fixed cost (which does not change with output) and variable cost (which changes linearly with output). $$ TC = FC + (VC \times Q) $$ Where $Q$ is the number of units produced and sold. ### Step 2: Define Total Revenue Total revenue is the selling price per unit multiplied by the quantity sold. $$ TR = P \times Q $$ ### Step 3: Apply the Break-Even Condition At break-even, $TR = TC$. This is the fundamental equilibrium condition. $$ P \times Q = FC + VC \times Q $$ ### Step 4: Isolate the Break-Even Quantity Subtract $VC \times Q$ from both sides. $$ P \times Q - VC \times Q = FC $$ Factor out the common term $Q$ on the left-hand side. $$ Q \times (P - VC) = FC $$ ### Step 5: Solve for $Q$ Divide both sides by $(P - VC)$ — note that for break-even to exist, we need $P > VC$. $$ BEP_Q = \frac{FC}{P - VC} $$ ### Step 6: Express in Currency Terms Multiply $BEP_Q$ by the price $P$ to get break-even sales value in ₹. $$ BEP_{₹} = BEP_Q \times P = \frac{FC \times P}{P - VC} $$ Dividing numerator and denominator by $P$: $$ BEP_{₹} = \frac{FC}{1 - \dfrac{VC}{P}} $$ This is the standard **KTU-answer form** for break-even sales in rupees. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Expected Monetary Value (EMV) of a Decision Branch In decision-tree analysis, the EMV of any decision branch is the **probability-weighted sum** of all possible payoffs. ### Step 1: Identify the Outcomes Let the possible outcomes of decision $A$ be $O_1, O_2, \ldots, O_n$ with associated probabilities $P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_n$. ### Step 2: Apply the Axiom of Expected Value By the principle of mathematical expectation, the expected value is the sum of the product of each outcome and its probability. $$ EMV = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \cdot O_i $$ ### Step 3: Worked Numerical Example A Kerala-based coir-products company is deciding whether to launch a new product. The two alternatives are: - **Alternative X — Launch Now:** Payoff = ₹8,00,000 (success), or –₹3,00,000 (failure). Probability of success = 0.6, failure = 0.4. - **Alternative Y — Launch After 6 Months (Pilot Study):** Payoff = ₹6,00,000 (success), or –₹1,00,000 (failure). Probability of success = 0.8, failure = 0.2. **Calculate EMV for Alternative X:** $$ EMV_X = (0.6 \times 8{,}00{,}000) + (0.4 \times (-3{,}00{,}000)) $$ $$ EMV_X = 4{,}80{,}000 + (-1{,}20{,}000) = 3{,}60{,}000 $$ **Calculate EMV for Alternative Y:** $$ EMV_Y = (0.8 \times 6{,}00{,}000) + (0.2 \times (-1{,}00{,}000)) $$ $$ EMV_Y = 4{,}80{,}000 + (-20{,}000) = 4{,}60{,}000 $$ **Step 4: Choose the Decision** Since $EMV_Y > EMV_X$ (₹4,60,000 > ₹3,60,000), the manager should choose **Alternative Y** (launch after pilot study). The EMV criterion is the most widely tested concept in KTU's Module 1 questions. ## 3.3 Worked Problem: Payback Period Comparison A small-scale electronics firm in Kochi is evaluating two machines: | Machine | Investment | Annual Cash Inflow | Life | |---|---|---|---| | **M1** | ₹10,00,000 | ₹2,50,000 | 5 years | | **M2** | ₹8,00,000 | ₹1,60,000 | 6 years | **Payback Period of M1:** $$ PB_{M1} = \frac{10{,}00{,}000}{2{,}50{,}000} = 4.0 \text{ years} $$ **Payback Period of M2:** $$ PB_{M2} = \frac{8{,}00{,}000}{1{,}60{,}000} = 5.0 \text{ years} $$ **Decision:** Machine M1 has a shorter payback period, so it is preferred on the basis of payback alone (provided both machines are technically viable). ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Tool | Problem Type | Recommended Tool | Why? | |---|---|---| | Single decision, multiple probabilistic outcomes | **Decision Tree with EMV** | Captures sequential decisions and risks | | Volume-cost-profit trade-off | **Break-Even Analysis** | Simple, visual, intuitive | | Capital budgeting with long horizon | **NPV / IRR** | Considers time value of money | | Short-term investment recovery | **Payback Period** | Quick, simple, easy to communicate | | Allocating scarce resources | **Linear Programming** | Optimal mathematical solution | | Complex system with randomness | **Simulation** | Handles non-linear, stochastic problems | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Diagram 1: Hierarchy of Operations Decisions This diagram shows the flow of decisions from the highest strategic level to the lowest operational level. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[STRATEGIC DECISIONS
Long-term 3-10 years] --> B[TACTICAL DECISIONS
Medium-term 1-12 months] B --> C[OPERATIONAL DECISIONS
Short-term daily/weekly] A --> A1[Facility Location] A --> A2[Capacity Planning] A --> A3[Technology Selection] B --> B1[Production Planning] B --> B2[Inventory Policy] B --> B3[Workforce Sizing] C --> C1[Job Scheduling] C --> C2[Machine Loading] C --> C3[Quality Inspection] A1 --> B A2 --> B A3 --> B B1 --> C B2 --> C B3 --> C ``` ## 4.2 Diagram 2: The Six-Step Operations Decision-Making Process ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Step 1 Identify the Problem] --> P2[Step 2 Develop Alternatives] P2 --> P3[Step 3 Evaluate Alternatives] P3 --> P4[Step 4 Select Best Alternative] P4 --> P5[Step 5 Implement the Decision] P5 --> P6[Step 6 Monitor and Control] P6 -. Feedback Loop .-> P1 P6 -. Corrective Action .-> P5 ``` > [!NOTE] > **KTU Insight:** Notice the *feedback loop* from Step 6 back to Step 1. This reflects the **Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA) / Deming Cycle**, which examiners often cite as a reference framework in management questions. ## 4.3 Diagram 3: Decision Tree for Product Launch (Block-Level Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TD D1[Decision Launch Product] --> D2[Option A Launch Now] D1 --> D3[Option B Launch After Pilot] D2 --> E1[Success Payoff 800000] D2 --> E2[Failure Payoff -300000] D3 --> E3[Success Payoff 600000] D3 --> E4[Failure Payoff -100000] E1 --- P1[Probability 0.6] E2 --- P2[Probability 0.4] E3 --- P3[Probability 0.8] E4 --- P4[Probability 0.2] ``` ## 4.4 Diagram 4: Break-Even Analysis — Functional Cost-Revenue Model ```mermaid flowchart LR FC[Fixed Cost FC horizontal line] --> TC[Total Cost TC upward line] VC[Variable Cost VC] --> TC P[Price P per unit] --> TR[Total Revenue TR upward line from origin] TC --> INT{BEP Intersection} TR --> INT INT --> OUT[Break-Even Point Q] Q --> PROFIT[Profit Zone Q greater than BEP] Q --> LOSS[Loss Zone Q less than BEP] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interpretation for KTU Exams:** The break-even point divides the cost-volume-profit graph into two zones — the **loss zone** (left of BEP) and the **profit zone** (right of BEP). The angle between the TR and TC lines represents the **contribution margin ratio** per unit. ## 4.5 Diagram 5: Decision Environment Classification ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Operations Decision] --> B{Information Available?} B -->|Complete and certain| C[Certainty Environment] B -->|Probabilistic| D[Risk Environment] B -->|No data| E[Uncertainty Environment] C --> C1[Use Linear Programming / BEP] D --> D1[Use Decision Tree with EMV] E --> E1[Use MaxiMin / MiniMax / Laplace Criterion] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Q: Differentiate between strategic, tactical, and operational decisions in operations management. Give one example of each.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** Operations decisions are classified based on **time horizon**, **level of management**, and **degree of detail**. - **Strategic Decisions:** Long-term (3–10 years), taken by top management, involve high capital, hard to reverse. *Example: Deciding to set up a new manufacturing plant in Ernakulam.* - **Tactical Decisions:** Medium-term (1–12 months), taken by middle management, moderately detailed. *Example: Deciding the production schedule for the next quarter.* - **Operational Decisions:** Short-term (daily/weekly), taken by lower management, highly specific. *Example: Assigning a worker to operate a CNC machine for the 2 PM shift.* Strategic decisions set the direction; tactical decisions translate them into plans; operational decisions execute them on a day-to-day basis. --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Q: Explain the three decision-making environments with suitable examples.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The three decision-making environments are based on the **availability of information** about outcomes: 1. **Certainty Environment:** The manager knows the exact outcome of every alternative. *Example: A fixed deposit in a bank where the interest rate is predetermined.* 2. **Risk Environment:** The manager knows the probabilities of different outcomes but not which will occur. *Example: A retailer ordering inventory based on demand forecasts — there is an 70% chance of high demand, 30% chance of low demand.* 3. **Uncertainty Environment:** The manager has little or no information about outcomes or probabilities. *Example: A startup launching a completely new product in an untested international market.* Different tools apply to each — **Linear Programming** for certainty, **Decision Tree/EMV** for risk, and **MaxiMin/MiniMax criteria** for uncertainty. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Q: A Kerala-based coir products manufacturer is considering two alternatives to expand its operations:** - **Alternative 1: Expand the existing plant in Alappuzha** with an investment of ₹20,00,000. Expected annual cash inflow = ₹5,00,000 for 5 years. Payback period required = 4 years. - **Alternative 2: Set up a new plant in Kottayam** with an investment of ₹30,00,000. Expected annual cash inflow = ₹7,00,000 for 6 years. Payback period required = 5 years. **(a)** Calculate the **Payback Period** for both alternatives and recommend the better one. **(b)** What are the **limitations of the Payback Period method**? Suggest two superior methods. --- **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Payback Period Calculation (7 Marks):** The Payback Period is the time required to recover the initial investment from the annual cash inflows. **Formula:** $$ PB = \frac{\text{Initial Investment}}{\text{Annual Cash Inflow}} $$ **For Alternative 1 (Alappuzha expansion):** $$ PB_1 = \frac{20{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} = 4.0 \text{ years} $$ Since the required payback is 4 years, Alternative 1 **just meets** the criterion. **For Alternative 2 (Kottayam new plant):** $$ PB_2 = \frac{30{,}00{,}000}{7{,}00{,}000} \approx 4.286 \text{ years} $$ Since the required payback is 5 years, Alternative 2 also **meets** the criterion (4.29 < 5). **Comparison and Recommendation:** | Criterion | Alt 1 | Alt 2 | Better? | |---|---|---|---| | Initial Investment | ₹20,00,000 | ₹30,00,000 | Alt 1 (lower) | | Annual Cash Inflow | ₹5,00,000 | ₹7,00,000 | Alt 2 (higher) | | Payback Period | 4.0 years | 4.29 years | Alt 1 (shorter) | | Project Life | 5 years | 6 years | Alt 2 (longer) | | Meets PB Criterion | Yes (exactly) | Yes (with margin) | Alt 2 (safer) | **Final Recommendation:** Although Alternative 1 has a marginally shorter payback, **Alternative 2 (Kottayam plant)** is recommended because: - It meets the criterion with a **safety margin of 0.71 years**. - It generates **higher absolute cash inflows** (₹7,00,000 vs ₹5,00,000 per year). - It has a **longer project life** (6 years vs 5 years). - The extra 0.29 years of payback is justified by the higher inflow and longer life. **Part (b) — Limitations of Payback Period (7 Marks):** 1. **Ignores the Time Value of Money:** Cash flows received in later years are treated equal to those received today. A rupee in year 5 is not equal in value to a rupee today. 2. **Ignores Cash Flows After Payback:** Any cash inflow occurring after the payback period is completely ignored. Two projects with the same payback but very different long-term returns look identical. 3. **No Profitability Indicator:** It only measures *recovery time*, not *profitability*. A project could payback quickly but yield very low absolute returns. 4. **Subjective Cut-off Period:** The "required" payback period is set arbitrarily by management, which can bias the decision. 5. **No Risk Adjustment:** It does not consider the risk or variability of cash flows. **Two Superior Methods (2 Marks each):** - **Net Present Value (NPV):** Discounts all future cash flows to present value and subtracts the initial investment. A project is accepted if NPV > 0. It accounts for the **time value of money** and **all cash flows over the project life**. - Formula: $NPV = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} - I_0$ - **Internal Rate of Return (IRR):** The discount rate at which NPV = 0. A project is accepted if IRR > cost of capital. It expresses profitability as a **percentage**, making project comparison easy. **Conclusion:** NPV and IRR are superior to Payback Period because they consider the time value of money, all cash flows, and overall profitability. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Q: A small-scale pickle manufacturing unit in Thrissur sells its product at ₹150 per kg. The variable cost is ₹90 per kg and the fixed cost per month is ₹60,000.** **(a)** Calculate the **Break-Even Point in units** and **in ₹ sales**. Also find the **Margin of Safety** if the actual monthly sales are 2,000 kg. **(b)** If the company wants to earn a target profit of ₹30,000 per month, how many kg must it sell? What is the **degree of operating leverage** at this output level? --- **Model Solution:** **Given Data:** - Selling price per kg, $P$ = ₹150 - Variable cost per kg, $VC$ = ₹90 - Fixed cost per month, $FC$ = ₹60,000 - Actual sales = 2,000 kg - Target profit = ₹30,000 **Step 1: Compute the Contribution Margin per Unit** $$ CM = P - VC = 150 - 90 = ₹60 \text{ per kg} $$ **Part (a) — Break-Even Point and Margin of Safety (7 Marks):** **Step 2: Break-Even Point in Units** $$ BEP_Q = \frac{FC}{P - VC} = \frac{60{,}000}{150 - 90} = \frac{60{,}000}{60} = 1{,}000 \text{ kg} $$ **Step 3: Break-Even Point in ₹ Sales** $$ BEP_{₹} = BEP_Q \times P = 1{,}000 \times 150 = ₹1{,}50{,}000 $$ **Step 4: Margin of Safety** The actual sales revenue is $2{,}000 \times 150 = ₹3{,}00{,}000$. $$ MOS = \frac{\text{Actual Sales} - BEP_{₹}}{\text{Actual Sales}} \times 100 $$ $$ MOS = \frac{3{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}50{,}000}{3{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = \frac{1{,}50{,}000}{3{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 50\% $$ **Interpretation:** The company can tolerate a **50% drop in sales** before reaching the break-even point. A 50% MOS is considered very safe. **Part (b) — Target Profit Volume and Operating Leverage (7 Marks):** **Step 5: Sales Volume for Target Profit of ₹30,000** The general formula is derived from: Target Profit = (Sales × CM Ratio) – FC Rearranging: $$ Q_{target} = \frac{FC + \text{Target Profit}}{P - VC} $$ $$ Q_{target} = \frac{60{,}000 + 30{,}000}{150 - 90} = \frac{90{,}000}{60} = 1{,}500 \text{ kg} $$ **Verification:** - Total Revenue = $1{,}500 \times 150 = ₹2{,}25{,}000$ - Variable Cost = $1{,}500 \times 90 = ₹1{,}35{,}000$ - Contribution = $₹2{,}25{,}000 - ₹1{,}35{,}000 = ₹90{,}000$ - Profit = Contribution – FC = $₹90{,}000 - ₹60{,}000 = ₹30{,}000$ ✓ **Step 6: Degree of Operating Leverage (DOL) at Q = 1,500 kg** DOL measures the sensitivity of operating profit to changes in sales. $$ DOL = \frac{\text{Total Contribution}}{\text{Total Contribution} - FC} = \frac{Q \times (P - VC)}{Q \times (P - VC) - FC} $$ $$ DOL = \frac{1{,}500 \times 60}{1{,}500 \times 60 - 60{,}000} = \frac{90{,}000}{90{,}000 - 60{,}000} = \frac{90{,}000}{30{,}000} = 3.0 $$ **Interpretation:** A 1% increase in sales will lead to a **3% increase in operating profit**. The firm is operating at 3x leverage, indicating moderate fixed-cost exposure. **Conclusion:** The pickle unit must sell **1,500 kg/month** to earn a ₹30,000 profit. With a DOL of 3, profit is sensitive to sales volume — a small drop in sales will reduce profit significantly. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > > 1. **Confusing the three decision types** — students often write "strategic" for everything. *Tip:* Strategic = long-term + top management; Operational = daily + supervisor level. Use the time-horizon anchor. > 2. **Skipping units in Break-Even Problems** — always write "1,000 kg" not just "1,000". KTU evaluators **deduct ½ mark** for missing units. > 3. **Forgetting to verify the answer** in target-profit problems — always plug the value back: check if $Q \times CM - FC =$ Target Profit. > 4. **Mixing up EMV and EVPI** — EMV is the expected value of a decision alternative; EVPI is the *additional* value if you had perfect information (computed as $EV_{wPI} - EMV_{best}$). > 5. **Payback without mentioning limitations** — KTU Part B (b) questions always expect limitations of any technique discussed. Always end with a "limitations" or "assumptions" paragraph. > 6. **Decimal vs. integer answers** — show 2 decimal places for ratios (like 0.40 instead of 0.4) and round only at the final step. KTU penalizes "rough" intermediate rounding. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):** - ✅ **Definition:** Operations Decision Making = systematic process of selecting the best course of action for production/service operations. - ✅ **Three Hierarchical Levels:** Strategic (long-term, top), Tactical (medium-term, middle), Operational (short-term, lower). - ✅ **Six-Step Process:** IDEAS-M → Identify → Develop → Evaluate → Alternative → Select → Monitor (with feedback loop). - ✅ **Three Decision Environments:** Certainty (full info), Risk (probabilities known), Uncertainty (no info). - ✅ **Key Formulas to Memorize:** - $BEP_Q = \dfrac{FC}{P - VC}$ - $BEP_{₹} = \dfrac{FC}{1 - \dfrac{VC}{P}}$ - $EMV = \sum P_i \cdot O_i$ - $MOS = \dfrac{\text{Actual Sales} - BEP_{₹}}{\text{Actual Sales}} \times 100$ - $PB = \dfrac{\text{Investment}}{\text{Annual Inflow}}$ - $DOL = \dfrac{\text{Contribution}}{\text{Contribution} - FC}$ - ✅ **Tools by Environment:** LP/BEP for Certainty; Decision Tree/EMV for Risk; MaxiMin/MiniMax for Uncertainty. - ✅ **Competitive Priorities:** Cost, Quality, Delivery, Flexibility (often called the **four performance objectives** of POM). - ✅ **Four Stages of POM:** Design → Planning & Control → Improvement → Delivery (Hill's model). - ✅ **Common Pitfalls:** Always state units, always verify answers, always list limitations, never confuse strategic vs. operational, never use payback alone for long-term capital decisions. - ✅ **Examiner's Favourite Terms to Use:** "Strategic alignment", "Contribution margin", "Expected Monetary Value", "Operating leverage", "Feedback control", "PDCA cycle". > **End of Notes — Module 1, Topic: Operations Decision Making** > *(Compiled per KTU 2024 Scheme, OEIET723 Production and Operations Management)*

topics.title: Phases in Product Design and Development
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET723 - PRODUCTION AND OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Phases in Product Design and Development

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Product Design and Development (PDD)** is the systematic, multidisciplinary process of conceiving, designing, prototyping, testing, and commercializing a new product (or a significant improvement to an existing one) so that it satisfies identified customer needs, functions reliably, is manufacturable at acceptable cost, and delivers value to the organization and society.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, **Product Design and Development** is treated as a **strategic operations function** that bridges market opportunity with technical feasibility. It is governed by a series of **phases (or stages)** that convert a fuzzy idea into a tangible, marketable artifact.

**Key Terminology Anchored to KTU Syllabus:**

| Term | Meaning (KTU-Aligned) |
|---|---|
| **Concept** | A broad description of the function, form, and features of a product. |
| **Feasibility** | The degree to which a concept can be designed, manufactured, and sold profitably. |
| **Prototype** | A physical or virtual working model used to validate the design before mass production. |
| **Commercialization** | The final phase where the product is launched into the target market. |
| **Concurrent Engineering** | A parallel, cross-functional approach where design, manufacturing, and marketing teams work simultaneously. |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — Building Your Dream House:**
> Think of designing a product exactly like building a custom house for a family.
>
> 1. You do not start by laying bricks. You first ask: *What do they need? How many rooms? What is the budget?* — this is **Idea Generation**.
> 2. Then you compare options and reject the impossible ones (e.g., a 50-room house on a small plot) — this is **Concept Screening**.
> 3. You draw a rough layout and check if the design is practical — this is **Preliminary Design**.
> 4. You prepare the final blueprint with exact measurements — this is **Detailed Design**.
> 5. You build a small 3D model to show the family — this is **Prototype**.
> 6. After feedback, you build the real house and hand it over — this is **Commercialization**.
>
> **Just like a house, a product moves through phases, and skipping a phase causes structural (or commercial) failure.**

---

## 1.3 Why Phased Approach is Mandatory in KTU/Industry Perspective

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The KTU 2024 Production & Operations Management syllabus explicitly treats Product Design as a sequence of phases because:**
> - **Risk Reduction:** Each phase acts as a checkpoint. Up to **70% of product cost** is committed during early design phases.
> - **Resource Optimization:** Money, time, and manpower are released in controlled amounts at each stage.
> - **Cross-Functional Integration:** Phases force coordination between **Marketing, Design, Manufacturing, Finance, and Quality** departments.
> - **Documentation & Traceability:** Required for ISO 9000 and regulatory compliance.

Standard industrial metrics referenced across the syllabus:
- **Time-to-Market (TTM)** — Target: 12–36 months for medium-complexity products.
- **Development Cost Ratio (DCR)** — R&D spend as % of annual revenue; benchmark **3%–8%**.
- **First-Pass Yield (FPY)** — % of units passing quality checks on first attempt.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Universal Phased Model (Ulrich–Eppinger / KTU Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus follows the **Ulrich-Eppinger Six-Phase Model** of Product Development, augmented with the **Stage-Gate®** decision framework popularized by Robert G. Cooper.

The six canonical phases are:

1. **Phase 0 — Planning / Strategy**
2. **Phase 1 — Concept Development (Idea Generation)**
3. **Phase 2 — System-Level Design (Feasibility & Preliminary Design)**
4. **Phase 3 — Detail Design**
5. **Phase 4 — Testing & Refinement (Prototyping)**
6. **Phase 5 — Production Ramp-Up & Commercialization**

Each phase is separated by a **Gate** — a formal Go/Kill/Recycle decision point.

---

## 2.2 Phase-by-Phase Theoretical Breakdown

### Phase 0 — Planning & Strategy
- **Objective:** Align product opportunities with the firm's **mission, vision, and competitive strategy**.
- **Inputs:** Market research, technology forecasts, SWOT analysis, Porter's Five Forces.
- **Outputs:** Product portfolio strategy, target market segments, project charter.
- **Key Question:** *Should we even enter this market?*

### Phase 1 — Concept Development (Idea Generation)
- **Objective:** Generate, collect, and screen a wide range of product ideas.
- **Techniques Used:**
  - **Brainstorming** (Osborn's Rules)
  - **SCAMPER Technique** (Substitute, Combine, Adapt, Modify, Put to other use, Eliminate, Rearrange)
  - **TRIZ** (Theory of Inventive Problem Solving — 40 inventive principles)
  - **Focus Groups, Lead User Analysis, QFD (House of Quality)**
- **Outputs:** A shortlist of 3–5 viable concepts.

### Phase 2 — System-Level Design (Preliminary Design & Feasibility)
- **Objective:** Translate the chosen concept into a **functional architecture**.
- **Activities:**
  - Define **functional decomposition** (what the product must do)
  - Build **concept sketches, block diagrams, and layout drawings**
  - Perform **Feasibility Analysis** across 3 dimensions:
    - **Technical Feasibility**
    - **Economic/Financial Feasibility**
    - **Operational/Schedule Feasibility**
- **Outputs:** System-level design documents, BOM (Bill of Materials) skeleton, feasibility report.

### Phase 3 — Detail Design
- **Objective:** Develop the **complete engineering specification** of every component.
- **Activities:**
  - Engineering drawings (GD&T — Geometric Dimensioning & Tolerancing)
  - Material selection, supplier identification
  - **DFM (Design for Manufacturing)**, **DFA (Design for Assembly)**, **DFR (Design for Reliability)**
  - Software/embedded system coding
  - **Bill of Materials (BOM)** finalization
  - Cost estimation
- **Outputs:** Complete engineering documentation, tooling plans, supplier agreements.

### Phase 4 — Testing, Prototyping & Refinement
- **Objective:** Build prototypes and verify that the design meets customer needs and engineering specs.
- **Prototype Types:**
  - **Alpha Prototype** — Internal engineering tests
  - **Beta Prototype** — Limited customer trials
  - **Pre-Production Prototype** — Built on intended production line
- **Testing Methods:** Functional testing, stress testing, accelerated life testing, **FMEA (Failure Mode & Effects Analysis)**, user acceptance testing.
- **Outputs:** Test reports, design iterations, validated final design.

### Phase 5 — Production Ramp-Up & Commercialization
- **Objective:** Scale production to full volume and launch in the market.
- **Activities:**
  - **Pilot Run** on production line
  - **Process Validation (PPAP — Production Part Approval Process)**
  - Training of sales, service, and operations staff
  - **Marketing Launch (4 Ps: Product, Price, Place, Promotion)**
  - Distribution & logistics setup
  - **Post-Launch Review (PLR)** — Compare actual vs. planned performance.
- **Outputs:** Commercially available product, market share data, customer feedback loop.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the KTU-exam-ready summary of all formulas, metrics, and decision criteria used in Product Design and Development questions.**

| Metric / Formula | Expression | Purpose | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Payback Period (PBP)** | $PBP = \dfrac{\text{Initial Investment}}{\text{Annual Net Cash Inflow}}$ | Time to recover development cost | Years |
| **Net Present Value (NPV)** | $NPV = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} - C_0$ | Financial feasibility of a concept | Currency units |
| **Return on Investment (ROI)** | $ROI = \dfrac{\text{Net Profit}}{\text{Investment}} \times 100$ | Compares competing concepts | Percentage |
| **Break-Even Point (BEP)** | $BEP = \dfrac{F}{P - V}$ (units) | Minimum production volume to avoid loss | Units |
| **Concept Screening Score** | $S_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot r_{ij}$ | Weighted ranking of concepts | Dimensionless score |
| **Development Cost Ratio** | $DCR = \dfrac{C_{R\&D}}{R_{total}} \times 100$ | R&D intensity of the firm | Percentage |
| **First-Pass Yield** | $FPY = \dfrac{N_{good}}{N_{total}} \times 100$ | Manufacturing quality of new design | Percentage |
| **Time-to-Market (TTM)** | $TTM = T_{concept} - T_{launch}$ | Speed of PDD process | Months |

Where:
- $F$ = Fixed Cost, $P$ = Price per unit, $V$ = Variable cost per unit.
- $w_j$ = weight of criterion $j$, $r_{ij}$ = rating of concept $i$ on criterion $j$.
- $CF_t$ = Cash flow in year $t$, $r$ = discount rate, $C_0$ = initial investment.
- $C_{R\&D}$ = R&D expenditure, $R_{total}$ = total revenue.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> The phased model is the **backbone of modern industries**:
> - **Automotive (e.g., Tata Motors, Toyota):** Each new car model passes through all six phases with explicit stage-gate approvals; saves an estimated **30%–40% development cost**.
> - **Consumer Electronics (Apple iPhone):** Strict phased rollout from concept sketches (Phase 1) to engineering validation (Phase 4) to global launch (Phase 5).
> - **Pharmaceuticals:** Drug discovery, preclinical trials, clinical trials (Phase I–III), FDA approval, and launch map directly onto the six-phase model.
> - **Software Industry:** The **Waterfall** and **Agile** methodologies are software-specific adaptations of the same universal phases.

The phased approach is, in essence, the **operational DNA** of any product-based organization.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Decision Matrix — Concept Screening (Worked-Out Example)

> [!NOTE]
> The most frequently asked KTU numerical is **Concept Screening using a Weighted Scoring Matrix**. Below is a complete, exam-ready worked solution.

### Problem Statement
A company has shortlisted **3 product concepts** — A, B, and C. The company has identified **4 selection criteria** with weights and ratings (1–10 scale) as given below. **Determine the best concept.**

| Criterion | Weight ($w_j$) | Rating A ($r_A$) | Rating B ($r_B$) | Rating C ($r_C$) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Market Potential | 0.35 | 8 | 7 | 9 |
| Technical Feasibility | 0.25 | 6 | 9 | 7 |
| Cost to Develop | 0.25 | 7 | 6 | 8 |
| Strategic Fit | 0.15 | 9 | 7 | 7 |

### Step-by-Step Analytical Solution

**Step 1 — Write the Weighted Score Formula**

$$S_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot r_{ij}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Score for Concept A**

$$S_A = (0.35 \times 8) + (0.25 \times 6) + (0.25 \times 7) + (0.15 \times 9)$$

$$S_A = 2.80 + 1.50 + 1.75 + 1.35 = 7.40$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Score for Concept B**

$$S_B = (0.35 \times 7) + (0.25 \times 9) + (0.25 \times 6) + (0.15 \times 7)$$

$$S_B = 2.45 + 2.25 + 1.50 + 1.05 = 7.25$$

**Step 4 — Calculate Score for Concept C**

$$S_C = (0.35 \times 9) + (0.25 \times 7) + (0.25 \times 8) + (0.15 \times 7)$$

$$S_C = 3.15 + 1.75 + 2.00 + 1.05 = 7.95$$

**Step 5 — Ranking and Decision**

$$\begin{aligned}
S_C &= 7.95 \;\;>\;\; S_A = 7.40 \;\;>\;\; S_B = 7.25
\end{aligned}$$

**Decision:** Concept **C** is selected for development as it has the highest weighted score.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation — Break-Even Point for a New Product

### Step 1 — Define Cost & Revenue Functions
- Let $F$ = Fixed development + setup cost
- $V$ = Variable cost per unit
- $P$ = Selling price per unit
- $Q$ = Number of units produced

**Total Cost (TC):**

$$TC = F + V \cdot Q$$

**Total Revenue (TR):**

$$TR = P \cdot Q$$

**Step 2 — Set TC = TR for Break-Even**

$$P \cdot Q = F + V \cdot Q$$

**Step 3 — Solve for Q**

$$\begin{aligned}
P \cdot Q - V \cdot Q &= F \\
Q(P - V) &= F \\
Q_{BEP} &= \dfrac{F}{P - V}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation**
- If the company produces more than $Q_{BEP}$ units, it makes a **profit**.
- If production is less than $Q_{BEP}$, it incurs a **loss**.

**Numerical Example:** If $F = 10,00,000$, $P = 500$, $V = 300$:

$$Q_{BEP} = \dfrac{10,00,000}{500 - 300} = \dfrac{10,00,000}{200} = 5,000 \;\; \text{units}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Phases (High-Yield KTU Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following matrix is the most exam-frequently asked summary.** Memorize it before any KTU 2024 ESE.

| Phase | Primary Input | Primary Output | Gate Decision | Key Tools / Methods |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **0. Planning** | Corporate strategy, market data | Project charter, mission statement | Go / No-Go to fund | SWOT, Porter's Five Forces |
| **1. Concept Dev.** | Customer needs, idea bank | 3–5 viable concepts | Go / Kill / Recycle | Brainstorming, QFD, TRIZ |
| **2. System Design** | Selected concept | Functional architecture, sketches | Proceed to detail? | Functional decomposition, feasibility study |
| **3. Detail Design** | System architecture | Full engineering specs, BOM | Ready to prototype? | DFM, DFA, GD&T, CAD/CAE |
| **4. Testing & Refinement** | Prototypes | Validated design, test reports | Release to production? | FMEA, alpha/beta testing |
| **5. Commercialization** | Validated design | Market-launched product | Continue / Discontinue | 4 Ps marketing, PPAP, post-launch review |

---

## 3.4 Analytical Logic — Why "Skip a Phase" Fails

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU exam insight:** A common 7-mark question is *"What happens if Phase 2 (System-Level Design) is skipped?"*

The analytical logic is:

1. **Without functional decomposition** → designers cannot verify whether the concept satisfies all customer needs.
2. **Without feasibility analysis** → economically unviable concepts proceed, wasting Phase 3 effort.
3. **Without preliminary layouts** → detail designers (Phase 3) face repeated rework, increasing **TTM by 30%–50%**.
4. **Cost impact:** Industry studies show that a **$1 cost of correction** in Phase 1 becomes **$10 in Phase 3** and **$100 in Phase 5** (the famous **"1-10-100 Rule"** of product development).

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{correction}^{Phase\;5} &\;\approx\; 100 \times C_{correction}^{Phase\;1}
\end{aligned}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Universal Product Design & Development Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

> [!NOTE]
> **The following Mermaid diagram is the canonical KTU-ready flow of all six phases with explicit gate decisions between them.** This block-level functional architecture maps every input, decision, and output.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Phase 0 Planning and Strategy] --> G0{Gate 0 Project Charter Approved}
    G0 -- Approved --> A1[Phase 1 Concept Development]
    G0 -- Rejected --> X0[Project Cancelled]
    A1 --> G1{Gate 1 Concept Screening}
    G1 -- Selected --> A2[Phase 2 System Level Design and Feasibility]
    G1 -- Recycled --> A1
    G1 -- Killed --> X1[Drop Concept]
    A2 --> G2{Gate 2 Feasibility Sign Off}
    G2 -- Approved --> A3[Phase 3 Detail Design and Engineering]
    G2 -- Rejected --> A1
    A3 --> G3{Gate 3 Design Freeze}
    G3 -- Approved --> A4[Phase 4 Prototyping and Testing]
    G3 -- Iterated --> A3
    A4 --> G4{Gate 4 Design Validation}
    G4 -- Validated --> A5[Phase 5 Production Ramp Up and Launch]
    G4 -- Failed --> A3
    A5 --> A6[Post Launch Review and Feedback Loop]
    A6 --> A0
```

---

## 4.2 Stage-Gate® Decision Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

> [!NOTE]
> **This diagram explains the role of Gates as decision points.** Every Gate requires a deliverable package before the project can proceed to the next Stage.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Stage0[Stage 0 Discovery]
        s0a[Market Research]
        s0b[Technology Scouting]
    end
    subgraph Gate0[Gate 0]
        g0a[Deliverable: Business Case]
        g0b[Decision: Go or Kill]
    end
    subgraph Stage1[Stage 1 Scoping]
        s1a[Concept Generation]
        s1b[Preliminary Feasibility]
    end
    subgraph Gate1[Gate 1]
        g1a[Deliverable: Concept Definition]
        g1b[Decision: Proceed or Recycle]
    end
    subgraph Stage2[Stage 2 Build Business Case]
        s2a[Detailed Feasibility]
        s2b[System Architecture]
    end
    subgraph Gate2[Gate 2]
        g2a[Deliverable: Project Plan and NPV]
        g2b[Decision: Invest or Hold]
    end
    subgraph Stage3[Stage 3 Development]
        s3a[Detailed Engineering]
        s3b[Prototype Build]
    end
    subgraph Gate3[Gate 3]
        g3a[Deliverable: Validated Prototype]
        g3b[Decision: Test in Market or Develop Further]
    end
    subgraph Stage4[Stage 4 Launch]
        s4a[Pilot Production]
        s4b[Market Launch]
    end
    Stage0 --> Gate0 --> Stage1 --> Gate1 --> Stage2 --> Gate2 --> Stage3 --> Gate3 --> Stage4
```

---

## 4.3 Concept-to-Launch Conversion Block Diagram

> [!NOTE]
> **This diagram illustrates how raw ideas are progressively converted into tangible outputs at each phase.** The transformation moves from **fuzzy front-end (FFUE)** to **physical artifact**.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    N1[Raw Customer Needs] --> N2[Filtered Needs QFD]
    N2 --> N3[Product Concepts 3 to 5]
    N3 --> N4[Functional Architecture]
    N4 --> N5[Physical Layout and Sketches]
    N5 --> N6[Detailed Engineering Drawings and BOM]
    N6 --> N7[Alpha Prototype]
    N7 --> N8[Beta Prototype]
    N8 --> N9[Pre Production Prototype]
    N9 --> N10[Market Launch Product]
    N10 --> N11[Feedback to Phase 0 Next Generation]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagrammatic Insight for KTU Exams:** The arrows in all three diagrams always flow **left-to-right or top-to-bottom**, with **gate decisions acting as filters**. This unidirectional flow is a common question in 7-mark answers.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the term "Concept Development" in Product Design. Mention any two techniques used. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Concept Development** is the phase of Product Design and Development where the organization generates, collects, and refines a wide range of product ideas that satisfy identified customer needs.

**Two techniques used:**

1. **Brainstorming** — A group creativity technique where members freely suggest ideas without criticism, then later screen them. (1 mark)
2. **TRIZ (Theory of Inventive Problem Solving)** — A Russian-developed methodology based on 40 inventive principles and patterns of invention used to resolve technical contradictions systematically. (1 mark)

*(Other acceptable techniques: SCAMPER, Focus Groups, Lead User Analysis, QFD — House of Quality.)*

---

### Question 2: What is a "Stage-Gate" model? Why is it used in Product Design? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Sample Paper]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> The **Stage-Gate® model** is a project management and product development framework in which a product development project is divided into **Stages** (where work is performed) separated by **Gates** (where go/kill/recycle decisions are taken).

**Why it is used:**

1. **Risk Control:** Gates act as checkpoints; weak projects are killed early, saving cost. (1 mark)
2. **Resource Discipline:** Funds, manpower, and time are released in controlled stages rather than upfront. (1 mark)
3. **Cross-Functional Review:** Top management reviews deliverables at each gate, ensuring strategic alignment. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Format)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern, Module 1 carries a compulsory choice. The following two questions are independent alternatives — answer either.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Actual Pattern]`

**Q. (a)** Explain in detail the **six phases of Product Design and Development** as per the Ulrich–Eppinger model. Highlight the key deliverable of each phase. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** Discuss the importance of **Concept Screening and Concept Testing** as decision tools in the early phases. Use a suitable example. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A (a) — 7 Marks**

| Phase | Key Activity | Key Deliverable |
|---|---|---|
| **0. Planning** | Strategic alignment, market opportunity identification | Project Charter / Mission Statement |
| **1. Concept Development** | Generate 10–100 ideas, reduce to 3–5 viable concepts | Concept Description Document |
| **2. System-Level Design** | Functional architecture, feasibility (technical/economic/schedule) | Functional Block Diagram + Feasibility Report |
| **3. Detail Design** | Component drawings, BOM, DFM, DFA, GD&T, supplier selection | Complete Engineering Package (drawings + BOM) |
| **4. Testing & Refinement** | Build alpha, beta, pre-production prototypes | Validated Final Design + Test Reports |
| **5. Production Ramp-Up & Launch** | Pilot run, PPAP, training, 4 Ps marketing | Commercially Launched Product + Post-Launch Review |

*(1 mark for explaining each phase correctly; 1 mark reserved for the introduction and conclusion statement.)*

---

### **Model Solution for Question A (b) — 7 Marks**

> [!NOTE]
> **Concept Screening** is the process of **reducing a large set of ideas (10–100) to a smaller manageable set (3–5)** by evaluating them against weighted criteria such as market potential, technical feasibility, and strategic fit. Tools used: **Weighted Scoring Matrix** and **Pugh Matrix**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Concept Testing** is the process of **presenting the shortlisted concepts to actual target customers** to gather feedback on appeal, usefulness, and purchase intent before significant development cost is incurred. Tools used: **Customer surveys, focus groups, lead-user interviews, and concept boards**.

**Example (2 marks):**
- *Tata Motors* developing a new electric hatchback generates 30 ideas, screens them with a weighted matrix (Market Potential 40%, Tech Feasibility 30%, Cost 20%, Strategic Fit 10%), selects 4 concepts, and tests them with 200 target customers via focus groups. Customer feedback refines the chosen concept to its final form.

**Why both are important (2 marks):**

1. **Avoid Costly Mistakes:** Screening kills bad ideas early, while testing validates market demand.
2. **Customer-Centric Design:** Both tools ensure the final product solves a *real* problem, not an *assumed* one.
3. **Improves NPV:** Better concepts lead to higher market acceptance and faster payback.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Sample Paper]`

**Q. (a)** What is a **Prototype**? Explain the **different types of prototypes** (Alpha, Beta, Pre-production) used in the Testing & Refinement phase. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** A firm is evaluating two product concepts. Concept X requires an initial investment of ₹50,00,000 and is expected to generate annual cash inflows of ₹12,00,000 for 6 years. Concept Y requires ₹40,00,000 and generates ₹10,00,000 for 6 years. The discount rate is 10%. **Which concept should be selected based on NPV?** **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B (a) — 7 Marks**

> [!NOTE]
> A **Prototype** is an **early sample, model, or release of a product built to test a concept** before committing to mass production. It is a tangible representation used to evaluate form, fit, function, and manufacturability.

**Types of Prototypes:**

| Type | Purpose | Audience | Stage |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Alpha Prototype** | Internal engineering tests (does it work as designed?) | Internal R&D and engineering team | Late Phase 3 / Early Phase 4 |
| **Beta Prototype** | Limited customer trial (does the user like it?) | Selected external customers | Mid Phase 4 |
| **Pre-Production Prototype** | Manufacturability validation on actual production line | Internal manufacturing + supply chain | End of Phase 4 / Start of Phase 5 |

**Significance (2 marks):**

- Reduces risk of launching a defective product.
- Identifies design flaws cheaply before tooling investment.
- Provides customer feedback for design refinement.

---

### **Model Solution for Question B (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — NPV Formula**

$$NPV = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} - C_0$$

**Step 2 — NPV of Concept X**

$$NPV_X = \sum_{t=1}^{6} \dfrac{12,00,000}{(1.10)^t} - 50,00,000$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Annuity Factor**

$$\text{PVIFA}(10\%, 6) = \dfrac{1 - (1.10)^{-6}}{0.10} = 4.3553$$

**Step 4 — Apply to Concept X**

$$\begin{aligned}
PV_X &= 12,00,000 \times 4.3553 = 52,26,360 \\
NPV_X &= 52,26,360 - 50,00,000 = +2,26,360
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Apply to Concept Y**

$$\begin{aligned}
PV_Y &= 10,00,000 \times 4.3553 = 43,55,300 \\
NPV_Y &= 43,55,300 - 40,00,000 = +3,55,300
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Decision**

$$\begin{aligned}
NPV_Y &= +3,55,300 \;\;>\;\; NPV_X = +2,26,360
\end{aligned}$$

**Decision:** **Concept Y should be selected** because it has a higher NPV, even though its absolute cash flows are lower. (7 marks — full solution)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1:**
> Students often commit the error of comparing the **absolute cash flows** (₹12 L vs ₹10 L) instead of **NPV at the discount rate**. Always discount cash flows before comparing. **If you skip discounting, you lose 4 marks.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #2:**
> In the six-phase explanation question, students **list phase names without deliverables**. The KTU 2024 marking scheme explicitly allocates marks for the **"key deliverable"** of each phase. **Always end each phase description with a deliverable statement.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #3:**
> In NPV problems, forgetting to **subtract the initial investment $C_0$** at the end is the most common mistake. **Formula: PV of inflows − Initial Investment = NPV.**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Revise Before Exam**

- [ ] **Six Phases of PDD:** Planning → Concept Development → System-Level Design → Detail Design → Testing & Refinement → Production Ramp-Up & Commercialization.
- [ ] **Each phase ends with a Gate** — Go / Kill / Recycle / Hold decision.
- [ ] **Idea Generation techniques:** Brainstorming, SCAMPER, TRIZ, QFD, Focus Groups, Lead User Analysis.
- [ ] **Concept Screening** uses a **Weighted Scoring Matrix**: $S_i = \sum w_j \cdot r_{ij}$.
- [ ] **Three Feasibility Dimensions:** Technical, Economic, Schedule/Operational.
- [ ] **DFM / DFA / DFR** are key philosophies of Phase 3 (Detail Design).
- [ ] **Three Prototype Types:** Alpha (internal test) → Beta (customer trial) → Pre-Production (line test).
- [ ] **FMEA** is a mandatory risk-analysis tool in Phase 4.
- [ ] **PPAP (Production Part Approval Process)** is the industry gate from Phase 4 to Phase 5.
- [ ] **1-10-100 Rule:** Cost of fixing a design error grows 10× per phase if missed.
- [ ] **NPV formula** is the gold standard for financial feasibility: $NPV = \sum \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} - C_0$.
- [ ] **Break-Even Point:** $Q_{BEP} = \dfrac{F}{P - V}$.
- [ ] **Time-to-Market (TTM)** is a critical strategic metric; phased approach reduces it via parallel/concurrent engineering.
- [ ] **Post-Launch Review (PLR)** feeds customer data back to Phase 0 of the next generation product — **continuous loop**.
- [ ] **Concurrent Engineering** (vs. sequential) reduces TTM by overlapping phases — important concept for KTU 2024 long-answer questions.
- [ ] **Stage-Gate®** is the most-cited process model; know the difference between **Stage** (work) and **Gate** (decision).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Product Life Cycle Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET723 - PRODUCTION AND OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Product Life Cycle Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Product Life Cycle (PLC)** is a strategic management concept that describes the **temporal evolution of a product from its inception in the market to its eventual withdrawal**, encompassing four sequential stages: **Introduction, Growth, Maturity, and Decline**. In the context of Production and Operations Management (POM), PLC Management refers to the **systematic planning, control, and optimization** of operational, marketing, and resource allocation decisions across these four stages to **maximize cumulative profitability and competitive advantage**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Concept):** The Product Life Cycle is a model that illustrates the **sales and profit pattern of a product over time** from the moment it is launched until it is withdrawn from the market. Each stage demands a distinct POM strategy — covering production volume, inventory, capacity utilization, and product design modifications.

## 1.2 The Four Canonical Stages of PLC

| Stage # | Stage Name | Core Characteristic | Time Horizon |
|:-------:|:-----------|:--------------------|:-------------|
| **1** | **Introduction** | Low sales, high cost, negative profit | Slow start |
| **2** | **Growth** | Rapid sales increase, profits emerge | Rapid rise |
| **3** | **Maturity** | Peak sales, maximum competition, stable profits | Plateau |
| **4** | **Decline** | Falling sales, decreasing profit, withdrawal | Downward slope |

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Life of a Human Being"**
> Imagine a **product as a person**:
> - **Birth (Introduction)** → A newborn baby cries (advertising spend is high), grows slowly, costs parents a lot (high production cost), and earns nothing.
> - **Childhood (Growth)** → The child learns fast, becomes productive (sales rise sharply), and starts "earning" (profits appear).
> - **Adulthood (Maturity)** → The person peaks in career (sales hit a ceiling), faces intense competition from peers (competitor entry), and earns the maximum.
> - **Old Age (Decline)** → The person retires (sales drop), health deteriorates (product becomes obsolete), and eventually passes away (withdrawal from market).
>
> *Just as a wise family plans for each life stage, a Production Manager must plan capacity, inventory, and process design differently at each PLC stage.*

## 1.4 Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) Tracked Across PLC

- **Sales Volume (units sold per period)**
- **Profit Margin (%)** — highest at maturity
- **Customer Base (number of buyers)**
- **Competitor Count** — peaks at maturity
- **Production Volume & Capacity Utilization (%)**
- **Inventory Turnover Ratio**
- **R&D Spending Intensity** — highest at introduction

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic to Remember Stages: "I Get Money, Then I Die"** → **I**ntroduction → **G**rowth → **M**aturity → **D**ecline.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet
