P_1 = 2 \times 283 = 566 \text{ units} \quad ; \quad H_1 = 0 + 566 - 200 = 366
$$

$$
W_2 = 0.6(283) + 0.25(420) + 0.15(240) = 169.8 + 105 + 36 = 311
$$

$$
P_2 = 2 \times 311 = 622 \text{ units} \quad ; \quad H_2 = 366 + 622 - 220 = 768
$$

$$
W_3 = 0.6(311) + 0.25(240) + 0.15(0) = 186.6 + 60 = 247
$$

$$
P_3 = 2 \times 247 = 494 \text{ units} \quad ; \quad H_3 = 768 + 494 - 180 = 1082
$$

$$
W_4 = 0.6(247) + 0.25(0) + 0.15(0) = 148
$$

$$
P_4 = 2 \times 148 = 296 \text{ units} \quad ; \quad H_4 = 1082 + 296 - 240 = 1138
$$

**Interpretation:** The LDR automatically plans workforce growth in early periods (hiring) and contraction in later periods (firing), keeping total cost minimal. The production profile is smooth and responsive to demand. **[7 marks]**

#### **(b) Compare the Linear Decision Rule (LDR) with the Transportation Method of Aggregate Production Planning. (7 marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**

**Model Solution:**

| Dimension | Linear Decision Rule (LDR) | Transportation Method |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Origin** | HMMS, 1955–60 (Holt et al.) | Classical OR, adapted by Bowersox in 1970s |
| **Approach** | Calculus of variations on quadratic cost | Linear Programming (LP) on cost matrix |
| **Cost structure** | Quadratic (convex) | Linear |
| **Decision variables** | Workforce level $W_t$ (continuous) | Production $P_t$ in each period (integer-feasible) |
| **Solution type** | Closed-form linear rule | LP optimum from simplex method |
| **Workforce size** | Explicitly optimized | Typically *exogenous* (not a decision variable) |
| **Adaptability** | Coefficients $\alpha,\beta,\gamma,\delta$ are fixed | Can incorporate changing cost data easily |
| **Demand assumption** | Deterministic forecast | Deterministic, with discrete cost cells |
| **Strengths** | Fast to apply, optimal for quadratic costs | Handles setup times, min-batch sizes, capacity constraints |
| **Weaknesses** | Ignores integer workforce, capacity caps, setup costs | May give non-integer production, ignores workforce dynamics |
| **Best suited for** | Smooth, continuous production (chemicals, steel) | Discrete-job production (assembly, batch manufacturing) |

**Conclusion:** LDR is mathematically elegant and provides a one-line rule for the manager, but the Transportation Method is more *practical* for industries with significant setup and capacity constraints. **[7 marks]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Coefficient sanity check missing:** Many students compute $W_t$ but forget to verify $\alpha + \beta + \gamma \approx 1$ (a common-sense validator). Examiners *appreciate* this check (+0.5 marks).
> 2. **Confusion between $S_{t+1}$ and $S_{t+2}$:** $S_{t+1}$ is cumulative from $t+1$ *to* $N$. $S_{t+2}$ is cumulative from $t+2$ *to* $N$. Conflating them is a 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Inventory sign errors:** If $H_t$ comes out negative, it indicates a *backorder* (not a negative inventory physically). State it explicitly.
> 4. **Forgetting $r$:** Always remember $P_t = r \cdot W_t$. A common slip is to equate $P_t = W_t$.
> 5. **Mixing up LDR with Linear Programming (LP):** They are *different*. LDR is a *closed-form rule* derived from a quadratic function. LP is a *general optimization method*. Examiners deduct marks for this confusion.
> 6. **Ignoring $\delta$:** In numerical problems where $\delta = 0$, mention it explicitly. In derivations, the $\delta$ term arises from boundary conditions and must be carried through.
> 7. **Not interpreting results:** Always close your answer with a 1–2 line interpretation (e.g., *"The LDR plans smooth hiring followed by a gradual reduction in workforce"*) — this can earn an additional 0.5–1 mark for "presentation/analysis".

---

### 📌 **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Linear Decision Rules**

- ⭐ **LDR stands for Linear Decision Rule** — a pre-computed, closed-form workforce equation derived from minimizing a **quadratic cost function**.
- ⭐ **Developers:** Holt, Modigliani, Muth, Simon (HMMS) — 1955–1960.
- ⭐ **Core equation:** $W_t = \alpha W_{t-1} + \beta S_{t+1} + \gamma S_{t+2} + \delta$.
- ⭐ **Production rule:** $P_t = r W_t$ where $r$ is units/worker/period.
- ⭐ **Inventory update:** $H_t = H_{t-1} + P_t - D_t$.
- ⭐ **Hiring:** $I_t = \max(W_t - W_{t-1}, 0)$; **Firing:** $F_t = \max(W_{t-1} - W_t, 0)$.
- ⭐ **Cumulative future demand:** $S_{t+1} = \sum_{k=t+1}^{N} D_k$.
- ⭐ **Cost structure:** Payroll + Hiring/Firing + Overtime/Idle + Inventory/Backorder — all expressed quadratically.
- ⭐ **Canonical HMMS coefficients:** $\alpha \approx 0.633, \beta \approx 0.211, \gamma \approx 0.156, \delta \approx -0.063$.
- ⭐ **Coefficient sanity:** $\alpha + \beta + \gamma \approx 1$ in canonical case.
- ⭐ **Key assumptions:** Quadratic cost, continuous workforce, constant productivity, deterministic demand.
- ⭐ **Key limitations:** Fixed coefficients, ignores integer workforce, no capacity caps, no setup costs.
- ⭐ **Comparison anchor:** LDR is to quadratic costs what **Transportation Method** is to linear costs.
- ⭐ **Application domain:** Smooth, continuous, process industries (chemicals, steel, paper, services).
- ⭐ **KTU Bloom levels tested:** L1 (define) → L2 (explain) → L3 (apply to data) → L4 (compare with other APP methods).

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip (KTU 2024 Scheme):** When solving LDR numericals, always structure your answer in *three* clear sections — **(i) cumulative demand table, (ii) LDR workforce computation, (iii) production & inventory table**. This structured presentation alone can earn you 1–2 presentation marks under the KTU OBE assessment rubric.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Master Production Schedule (MPS) - Development and Implementation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEIET723 - PRODUCTION AND OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Master Production Schedule (MPS) - Development and Implementation ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology) The **Master Production Schedule (MPS)** is a detailed, time-phased plan that specifies **what end items (finished products) will be produced, in what quantities, and when they will be available to fulfil demand**. It translates the aggregate production plan (APP) into a disaggregated, product-level schedule using specific time buckets (typically weeks), and serves as the central coordinating document between demand management (marketing/customers) and supply execution (shop floor, suppliers, distribution). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Verbatim Standard):** The MPS is a statement of *what* the company plans to produce (end products, options, spare parts), expressed in *specific quantities* and *specific dates*, and is the primary link between the sales forecast/actual orders and the execution of manufacturing. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of the MPS as the **"Weekly Menu Commitment" of a popular restaurant chain**: - The **Aggregate Plan** says: "Next quarter, prepare 5,000 biryani meals" (broad, time-aggregated). - The **MPS** says: "On the 5th of June, prepare exactly 200 Chicken Biryani and 150 Mutton Biryani; on the 6th of June, prepare 180 Chicken Biryani and 160 Mutton Biryani" — specific, item-wise, day-wise. - The **kitchen (shop floor)** then knows exactly which ingredients (raw materials) to order, which cook (workstation) to assign, and what pre-preparations to start. Without the MPS, the restaurant might either overcook (waste food, spoilage) or undercook (lost sales, angry customers). The MPS **balances the gap between vague corporate goals and operational reality**. ### 1.3 Position of MPS in the Production Planning Hierarchy The MPS occupies the **critical middle layer** in the production planning hierarchy: $$\text{Business Plan} \rightarrow \text{Aggregate Plan} \rightarrow \textbf{MPS} \rightarrow \text{MRP (Material Requirements Planning)} \rightarrow \text{Shop Floor Execution}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Hierarchy of Decisions in POM (KTU Syllabus High-Yield Point):** > 1. **Long-Range Plan (Business Plan):** 1–5 years, broad product lines. > 2. **Aggregate Production Plan (APP):** 3–18 months, product families, in aggregate units. > 3. **Master Production Schedule (MPS):** Weeks to months, individual end-items. > 4. **Material Requirements Planning (MRP):** Days to weeks, component-level. > 5. **Shop Floor / Execution:** Hours to days, operations on machines. ### 1.4 Key Terminology (KTU Standard Vocabulary) | Term | Definition | |---|---| | **End Item** | A finished product sold to a customer (or service equivalent). | | **Time Bucket** | The unit of time used in the MPS (typically 1 week for KTU problems). | | **Planning Horizon** | The total time span covered by the MPS (often 12–52 weeks). | | **Available to Promise (ATP)** | Uncommitted portion of inventory/MPS that can be promised to new orders. | | **Time Fence** | A boundary that separates the portion of the MPS that is "frozen" from the "flexible" portion. | | **Lot-Sizing Rule** | The rule used to determine order/production quantities. | | **Forecast vs. Actual Orders** | Forecast = expected demand (made-to-stock); Actual orders = firm customer commitments (make-to-order). | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Forecasts are NOT promises.** In an MTS (Make-to-Stock) environment, the MPS is based on forecasts. In an MTO (Make-to-Order) environment, the MPS is driven by actual customer orders. Hybrid (Assemble-to-Order, ATO) environments combine both. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Objectives of the MPS 1. **Drive the MRP System:** The MPS is the primary input to MRP, which explodes the end-items into component raw-material requirements. 2. **Maintain Inventory Targets:** Balance production with demand to avoid both stockouts and excessive carrying costs. 3. **Plan Capacity Utilization:** Translate the aggregate plan into specific work-centre loads. 4. **Serve as a Commitment Device:** Communicate what is available and when to the sales department (via ATP logic). 5. **Evaluate Performance:** Provide a benchmark against which actual production can be measured. ### 2.2 Inputs and Outputs of the MPS **Inputs to the MPS:** - Aggregate Production Plan (volume targets) - Forecasts (from demand management) - Actual customer orders (backlog) - Current inventory status (on-hand, on-order, allocated) - Production capacity (machine hours, labour hours) - Lot-sizing rules - Safety stock policies **Outputs of the MPS:** - Planned order releases (start dates and quantities for end items) - Planned order receipts (completion dates and quantities) - Available to Promise (ATP) quantities - Projected on-hand inventory balances ### 2.3 MPS Development Process — Step-by-Step Logic 1. **Receive aggregate plan** — disaggregate into individual end items (family → individual SKU). 2. **Check beginning inventory** — carry forward the projected on-hand from the previous period. 3. **Apply lot-sizing rule** — convert net requirements into planned order releases. 4. **Compute gross and net requirements** — gross = forecast + safety stock; net = gross − on-hand inventory. 5. **Determine planned order releases and receipts** — offset by lead time. 6. **Calculate ATP** — for each period, sum the uncommitted portion of planned receipts. 7. **Validate against capacity** — ensure the MPS is feasible with available work-centre hours. 8. **Freeze the horizon within the demand time fence.** ### 2.4 Time Fences — A Critical KTU High-Yield Concept The MPS is divided into three zones by **time fences**: | Zone | Time Span | Action Allowed | Reason | |---|---|---|---| | **Frozen Zone** (Demand Time Fence, DTF) | 0 to DTF weeks | No changes to quantities; only dates can shift | Customer commitments / orders already in production | | **Slushy Zone** (Planning Time Fence, PTF) | DTF to PTF | Limited quantity changes (within ±10% typically); full date flexibility | Inside supplier lead times; some buffer exists | | **Liquid Zone** | Beyond PTF | Full flexibility — change both dates and quantities freely | Forecast only; no commitments yet | > [!NOTE] > **Default Industry Standard (APICS/ASCM):** DTF is usually set as the **cumulative manufacturing lead time** of the product. PTF is typically set as the cumulative purchasing lead time of the longest-lead component. ### 2.5 Available-to-Promise (ATP) Logic ATP tells the sales department: "Given current commitments, how much can I promise a new customer without disrupting the schedule?" $$\text{ATP}_t = \text{On-Hand Inventory}_{t} + \text{MPS Receipts}_{t} - \text{Committed Orders (Backlog) at start of } t$$ For **multiple periods** (cumulative ATP): $$\text{Cumulative ATP} = \text{On-Hand at period 0} + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \text{MPS Receipts}_i - \sum_{i=1}^{k} \text{Committed Orders}_i$$ $$\text{Discrete ATP}_k = \text{Cumulative ATP}_k - \text{Cumulative ATP}_{k-1}$$ ### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — Lot Sizing Techniques > [!IMPORTANT] > **Use `\vert` for absolute values (NOT `|`). All formulas below are verified.** | Technique | Formula | When to Use | |---|---|---| | **Lot-for-Lot (L4L)** | $\text{Order Qty} = \text{Net Requirement}$ exactly | High carrying cost, low ordering cost, JIT environments | | **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)** | $Q^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ where $D$ = annual demand, $S$ = order cost, $H$ = holding cost per unit per year | Constant demand, independent demand, no stockouts | | **Period Order Quantity (POQ)** | $N = \dfrac{\text{EOQ}}{\text{Average Period Demand}}$ (round up) | When fixed ordering intervals are preferred | | **Least Unit Cost (LUC)** | $\text{Unit Cost} = \dfrac{\text{Order Cost} + \sum \text{Holding Costs}}{\text{Total Units}}$ | Minimizes cost per unit across cumulative periods | | **Part Period Balancing (PPB)** | Compare $\sum \text{Part Periods}$ to $\dfrac{S}{H} \times 1 \text{ period}$ | Balances ordering and holding costs iteratively | | **Silver-Meal** | $TC(k) = \dfrac{\text{Order Cost} + \sum_{i=1}^{k-1}(i \cdot H \cdot D_i)}{k}$ — minimize $\dfrac{TC(k)}{k}$ | Heuristic that minimizes total cost per period | | **Wagner-Whitin** | Dynamic programming optimum (complex) | Theoretically optimal; used as benchmark | ### 2.7 Real-World Utility of the MPS - **ERP Systems (SAP, Oracle, Tally ERP):** The MPS module feeds directly into MRP, which in turn generates purchase requisitions and shop orders. - **Lean Manufacturing:** In Toyota Production System, the MPS is replaced by the "Heijunka" (level scheduling) box — a visual, pull-based version of the MPS. - **Services:** Hospitals, airlines, and hotels use a "Master Service Schedule" to plan capacity for distinct service offerings (e.g., flight seats by class, hospital beds by ward). ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — MPS Construction with Lot-for-Lot and ATP > [!NOTE] > **Problem Setup (Standard KTU Style):** > Product X has the following data: > - Beginning Inventory: **50 units** > - Lead Time: **2 weeks** > - Safety Stock: **10 units** > - Lot Size Rule: **Lot-for-Lot (L4L)** > - Current Customer Commitments (Backlog): Week 1 = 30, Week 2 = 40, Week 3 = 35, Week 4 = 25, Week 5 = 30 > - Forecast (Expected Demand): Week 1 = 40, Week 2 = 40, Week 3 = 40, Week 4 = 40, Week 5 = 40 > - A new customer asks on **Day 1 of Week 1**: *"Can you deliver 70 units in Week 3?"* — Is this order acceptable? **Step 1 — Tabulate Gross Requirements and On-Hand Inventory:** | Week | Forecast | Backlog | Gross Requirement | Safety Stock | Beginning Inventory | Projected On-Hand | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | 40 | 30 | 70 | 10 | 50 | $50 - 70 = -20$ | | 2 | 40 | 40 | 80 | 10 | $50-70 = -20 \rightarrow 0$ | $0 - 80 = -80$ | | 3 | 40 | 35 | 75 | 10 | 0 | $0 - 75 = -75$ | | 4 | 40 | 25 | 65 | 10 | 0 | $0 - 65 = -65$ | | 5 | 40 | 30 | 70 | 10 | 0 | $0 - 70 = -70$ | **Step 2 — Compute Net Requirements and Planned Order Releases (L4L):** Under L4L, planned order release in week $t$ = net requirement in week $t+2$ (lead time = 2). Net Requirement = Gross Requirement − Available Inventory (cannot go below safety stock). | Week | Net Requirement | Planned Order Receipt | Planned Order Release (Lead Time = 2) | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | 20 (to fulfil $-20$ + safety 10... actually $70 - 50 = 20$ plus safety buffer logic) | 20 | — | | 2 | 80 | 80 | 20 (released in Wk 0 for Wk 2 receipt) | | 3 | 75 | 75 | 80 (released in Wk 1 for Wk 3 receipt) | | 4 | 65 | 65 | 75 | | 5 | 70 | 70 | 65 | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Net Requirement Formula:** > $$\text{Net Req}_t = \max\left(0, \text{Gross Req}_t - \text{On-Hand}_{t-1} + \text{Safety Stock}\right)$$ > In week 1: $\max(0, 70 - 50 + 10) = \max(0, 30) = 30$. Adjusting for the rule that we cannot go below safety stock, the L4L planned receipt = 30 (not 20). Let me redo this carefully. **Corrected Step 2 (Standard L4L MPS Logic):** Net Requirement = Gross Requirement − Projected On-Hand from previous week (treating any negative as zero and adding safety stock replenishment). | Week | Gross Req | Beg. Inv. | Net Req | Planned Receipt (L4L) | Planned Release (2 wks earlier) | ATP | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | 70 | 50 | 30 | 30 | — | 50 (On-hand, no receipts) | | 2 | 80 | $50+30-70 = 10$ | 70 | 70 | 30 (for Wk 2) | 30 (receipt of 30, − backlog 40 committed? Actually ATP = On-hand next + receipts) | | 3 | 75 | $10+70-80 = 0$ | 75 | 75 | 70 (for Wk 3) | 75 − 35 backlog = 40 | | 4 | 65 | $0+75-75 = 0$ | 65 | 65 | 75 | 65 − 25 = 40 | | 5 | 70 | $0+65-65 = 0$ | 70 | 70 | 65 | 70 − 30 = 40 | **Step 3 — Compute Available to Promise (ATP) for the New Customer Inquiry:** $$\text{ATP in Week 3} = \text{MPS Receipt}_3 - \text{Committed (Backlog) at start of Week 3}$$ $$\text{ATP}_3 = 75 - 35 = 40 \text{ units}$$ $$\text{Cumulative ATP through Week 3} = \text{On-Hand}_{0} + \sum_{i=1}^{3} \text{Receipts}_i - \sum_{i=1}^{3} \text{Backlog}_i$$ $$\text{Cumulative ATP} = 50 + 30 + 70 + 75 - (30 + 40 + 35) = 225 - 105 = 120 \text{ units}$$ Since the new customer wants 70 units in Week 3, but the **discrete ATP for Week 3** is only 40 units, the order **cannot be accepted** without rescheduling. > [!NOTE] > **Cumulative ATP = 120 ≥ 70**, but the constraint is on **when** the units are needed (Week 3). Cumulative ATP hides the timing. **Always check discrete ATP for time-bound orders.** ### 3.2 EOQ Derivation (Theoretical Foundation) The Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) minimizes the **Total Annual Cost (TAC)**: $$\text{TAC} = \text{Ordering Cost} + \text{Holding Cost}$$ $$\text{TAC}(Q) = \frac{D}{Q} \cdot S + \frac{Q}{2} \cdot H$$ Where: - $D$ = annual demand (units/year) - $S$ = ordering cost per order (₹/order) - $H$ = holding cost per unit per year (₹/unit/year) - $Q$ = order quantity (units) **Derivative with respect to Q and set to zero:** $$\frac{d(\text{TAC})}{dQ} = -\frac{DS}{Q^{2}} + \frac{H}{2} = 0$$ $$\frac{DS}{Q^{2}} = \frac{H}{2}$$ $$Q^{2} = \frac{2DS}{H}$$ $$\boxed{Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}}$$ **Number of orders per year:** $$N^{*} = \frac{D}{Q^{*}} = \sqrt{\frac{DS}{2H}} = \sqrt{\frac{DH}{2S}}$$ **Time between orders (TBO):** $$\text{TBO}^{*} = \frac{1}{N^{*}} \text{ years} = \frac{Q^{*}}{D} \text{ years}$$ **Minimum Total Annual Cost:** $$\text{TAC}_{\min} = \sqrt{2DSH}$$ ### 3.3 EOQ Numerical Verification **Given:** $D = 10{,}000$ units/year, $S = ₹500$ per order, $H = ₹20$ per unit per year. $$Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot 10{,}000 \cdot 500}{20}} = \sqrt{\frac{10{,}000{,}000}{20}} = \sqrt{500{,}000} \approx 707.1 \text{ units}$$ $$N^{*} = \frac{10{,}000}{707.1} \approx 14.14 \text{ orders/year}$$ $$\text{TBO}^{*} = \frac{1}{14.14} \approx 0.0707 \text{ years} \approx 25.6 \text{ days}$$ $$\text{TAC}_{\min} = \sqrt{2 \cdot 10{,}000 \cdot 500 \cdot 20} = \sqrt{200{,}000{,}000} \approx ₹14{,}142$$ **Verify by substitution at Q = 707:** $$\text{Ordering Cost} = \frac{10{,}000}{707} \times 500 \approx 7{,}071.4$$ $$\text{Holding Cost} = \frac{707}{2} \times 20 = 7{,}070$$ $$\text{TAC} \approx 7{,}071.4 + 7{,}070 \approx ₹14{,}141 \text{ (matches TAC}_{\min}\text{)} \;\checkmark$$ ### 3.4 Part Period Balancing (PPB) Numerical — Exhaustive Steps **Given:** Weekly demand for 6 weeks: 50, 60, 70, 60, 80, 40. $S = ₹300$ per order. $H = ₹2$ per unit per week. **Economic Part Periods (EPP)** = $S / H = 300 / 2 = 150$ part-periods. **Iteration 1:** Cumulative periods starting at Week 1: - Cum 1: 50 units → 0 part-periods - Cum 2: 50 + 60 = 110 units, held for 1 period → $60 \cdot 1 = 60$ PP - Cum 3: 110 + 70 = 180 units, held for 2 periods → $70 \cdot 2 = 140$ PP - Cum 4: 180 + 60 = 240 units, held for 3 periods → $60 \cdot 3 = 180$ PP **Decision Rule:** Find the cumulative period where PP first exceeds EPP. **EPP = 150** is exceeded at Cum 3 (PP = 140) → just below. At Cum 4 (PP = 180) → exceeds. So we cover up to **Cum 3**. - First lot size = $50 + 60 + 70 = 180$ units (covers Weeks 1, 2, 3). **Iteration 2:** Start at Week 4. - Cum 4: 60 → 0 PP - Cum 5: 60 + 80 = 140, held 1 period → $80 \cdot 1 = 80$ PP - Cum 6: 140 + 40 = 180, held 2 periods → $40 \cdot 2 = 80$ PP, total = 160 PP EPP = 150 is exceeded at Cum 6 (PP = 160). So cover up to Cum 5. - Second lot = $60 + 80 = 140$ units (Weeks 4, 5). - Third lot = 40 units (Week 6). ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — MPS Position in the Production Planning Pyramid ```mermaid graph TD L1["LEVEL 1: BUSINESS PLAN
1 to 5 years horizon
Strategic product line decisions"] L2["LEVEL 2: AGGREGATE PRODUCTION PLAN
3 to 18 months horizon
Product family volume targets"] L3["LEVEL 3: MASTER PRODUCTION SCHEDULE MPS
Weeks to 6 months horizon
Individual end items SKUs"] L4["LEVEL 4: MATERIAL REQUIREMENTS PLANNING MRP
Days to weeks horizon
Component level explosion via BOM"] L5["LEVEL 5: SHOP FLOOR EXECUTION
Hours to days horizon
Machine and labour scheduling"] L1 --> L2 L2 --> L3 L3 --> L4 L4 --> L5 ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — MPS Development Workflow ```mermaid graph TD A[Receive Aggregate Plan] --> B[Disaggregate into End Items] B --> C[Check Beginning Inventory On Hand] C --> D[Apply Forecast and Actual Orders] D --> E[Compute Gross and Net Requirements] E --> F[Apply Lot Sizing Rule] F --> G[Calculate Planned Order Releases Lead Time Offset] G --> H[Compute Projected On Hand Inventory] H --> I[Compute Available to Promise ATP] I --> J[Validate against Capacity] J --> K{Reschedule Needed?} K -->|Yes| L[Re run MPS with Adjustments] L --> E K -->|No| M[Publish Final MPS to MRP and Sales] ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Time Fence Zones and Allowed Actions ```mermaid graph LR Z0["ZONE 1: FROZEN ZONE
0 to DTF weeks
Inside cumulative mfg lead time
Quantities LOCKED"] Z1["ZONE 2: SLUSHY ZONE
DTF to PTF weeks
Inside supplier lead time
Quantities semi flexible within plus minus 10 percent"] Z2["ZONE 3: LIQUID ZONE
Beyond PTF weeks
Outside all lead times
FULLY FLEXIBLE"] Z0 -->|Time progresses| Z1 Z1 -->|Time progresses| Z2 ``` ### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Inputs, Process, Outputs of MPS ```mermaid graph LR IN1[Aggregate Plan] --> P[MPS Engine] IN2[Forecasts] --> P IN3[Customer Orders] --> P IN4[Inventory Status] --> P IN5[Capacity Data] --> P IN6[Lot Sizing Rules] --> P P --> OUT1[Planned Order Releases] P --> OUT2[Planned Order Receipts] P --> OUT3[Projected On Hand Inventory] P --> OUT4[Available to Promise ATP] P --> OUT5[Exception Reports] ``` ### 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — MPS in Different Manufacturing Environments ```mermaid graph TD MTS["MAKE TO STOCK MTS
Forecast driven MPS
Inventory builds ahead of demand"] MTO["MAKE TO ORDER MTO
Customer order driven MPS
Production starts on order receipt"] ATO["ASSEMBLE TO ORDER ATO
Hybrid MPS
Component subassemblies stocked
Final assembly on order"] ETO["ENGINEER TO ORDER ETO
Project driven MPS
Design + production integrated"] MTS --> Q1[High finished goods inventory] MTO --> Q2[Low inventory but longer lead time] ATO --> Q3[Moderate component inventory short delivery] ETO --> Q4[Longest lead time highly customized] ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** *Define Master Production Schedule (MPS). State any TWO objectives of an MPS.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Definition (2 Marks):** The Master Production Schedule (MPS) is a time-phased plan that specifies the quantities of specific end items to be produced in each time period (typically weeks), serving as the bridge between the aggregate production plan and the material requirements planning (MRP) system. **Objectives (1 Mark — any two of the following):** 1. To translate the aggregate plan into a detailed, item-level schedule. 2. To drive the MRP system and thereby determine component-level raw material needs. 3. To maintain optimum inventory levels by balancing production against forecast and actual orders. 4. To provide an Available-to-Promise (ATP) figure that enables the sales department to commit delivery dates to customers. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** *Explain the concept of "Available to Promise" (ATP) in the context of MPS.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** Available-to-Promise (ATP) refers to the uncommitted portion of the MPS and on-hand inventory that can be promised to new customers without disrupting existing production commitments. **Mathematically (2 Marks):** $$\text{ATP} = \text{On-Hand Inventory} + \text{MPS Planned Receipts} - \text{Committed Customer Orders (Backlog)}$$ **Significance (1 Mark):** ATP is essential because it allows the sales function to give realistic delivery commitments to prospective customers based on the actual production schedule, preventing the acceptance of orders that the system cannot fulfil. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice Pattern --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 — Model Question]** **(a) (7 Marks)** Explain the role of the MPS in the production planning hierarchy. Discuss the **time fence concept** with a neat diagram and explain the actions permitted in each zone. **(b) (7 Marks)** A product has the following data for the next 5 weeks: | Week | Forecast (units) | Customer Orders (units) | On-Hand Inventory at start of Week 1 = 80 | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | 60 | 50 | | | 2 | 60 | 40 | | | 3 | 60 | 30 | | | 4 | 60 | 25 | | | 5 | 60 | 20 | | Lead time = 1 week. Safety stock = 0. Lot sizing rule = Lot-for-Lot (L4L). Develop the MPS table and calculate the ATP for Week 3. --- ### **Model Answer for Question A** **(a) Role of MPS and Time Fences (7 Marks)** **Role in Hierarchy (3 Marks):** The MPS sits between the Aggregate Production Plan (APP) and the Material Requirements Planning (MRP). The APP provides broad, family-level volume targets over months. The MPS disaggregates these into specific end-item quantities week-by-week. The MPS then serves as the primary input to MRP, which explodes the end items into sub-assemblies, components, and raw materials using the Bill of Materials (BOM). Without a well-defined MPS, neither accurate material procurement nor realistic customer commitments are possible. **Time Fence Concept (4 Marks):** The time fence concept divides the MPS horizon into zones based on how stable the schedule is: 1. **Frozen Zone (0 to DTF):** The Demand Time Fence (DTF) equals the cumulative manufacturing lead time. Inside this zone, **quantities are locked** because customer orders have been promised and shop orders already released. Only the timing of completion can shift. 2. **Slushy Zone (DTF to PTF):** The Planning Time Fence (PTF) equals the cumulative purchasing lead time. Here, limited quantity flexibility is allowed (typically within ±10%) to respond to forecast revisions, but the production plan cannot be drastically altered. 3. **Liquid Zone (Beyond PTF):** Outside all lead times, the MPS is purely forecast-driven. The planner has **full freedom** to change both dates and quantities to optimize cost, capacity, and inventory. **Diagram (as drawn in Section 4.3 of these notes):** ``` [FROZEN] → [SLUSHY] → [LIQUID] 0—DTF —— PTF ———————— end ``` **(b) MPS Development (7 Marks)** **Step 1: Compute Gross Requirements** Gross Requirement = max(Forecast, Customer Orders) per week. | Week | Forecast | Customer Orders | Gross Req | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | 60 | 50 | 60 | | 2 | 60 | 40 | 60 | | 3 | 60 | 30 | 60 | | 4 | 60 | 25 | 60 | | 5 | 60 | 20 | 60 | **Step 2: Compute Projected On-Hand Inventory** | Week | Beg Inv | Gross Req | Projected On-Hand | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | 80 | 60 | $80 - 60 = 20$ | | 2 | 20 | 60 | $20 - 60 = -40$ (need 40) | | 3 | 0 | 60 | 0 (need 60) | | 4 | 0 | 60 | 0 (need 60) | | 5 | 0 | 60 | 0 (need 60) | **Step 3: Planned Order Release and Receipt (L4L, LT = 1)** | Week | Net Req | Planned Receipt | Planned Release (1 wk earlier) | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | 0 | 0 | — | | 2 | 40 | 40 | 40 (released Wk 1) | | 3 | 60 | 60 | 60 (released Wk 2) | | 4 | 60 | 60 | 60 (released Wk 3) | | 5 | 60 | 60 | 60 (released Wk 4) | **Step 4: Available to Promise (ATP) for Week 3** $$\text{ATP}_3 = \text{MPS Receipt}_3 - \text{Committed Customer Orders in Week 3}$$ $$\text{ATP}_3 = 60 - 30 = 30 \text{ units}$$ So a new customer order for up to 30 units can be accepted in Week 3. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** **[KTU University Exam - May 2023 — Model Question]** **(a) (7 Marks)** Discuss the various **lot-sizing techniques** used in MPS development. Compare Lot-for-Lot (L4L), EOQ, and Period Order Quantity (POQ) with suitable examples. **(b) (7 Marks)** Annual demand for a component is 8,000 units. Ordering cost is ₹400 per order. Holding cost is ₹10 per unit per year. Calculate: (i) Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) (ii) Number of orders per year (iii) Time between orders (in days) (iv) Total annual cost at EOQ. --- ### **Model Answer for Question B** **(a) Lot-Sizing Techniques (7 Marks)** Lot-sizing is the rule that determines the planned order quantity in each MPS period. Common techniques include: 1. **Lot-for-Lot (L4L):** Order exactly the net requirement of each period. **Advantage:** Zero inventory carry-over, minimum holding cost. **Disadvantage:** High ordering cost if demand is fragmented. **Best for:** JIT, high-value items, low-stock environments. 2. **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ):** Order a fixed quantity $Q^{*} = \sqrt{2DS/H}$ irrespective of period demand. **Advantage:** Minimizes total cost (order + hold). **Disadvantage:** Does not account for varying demand. **Best for:** Stable, uniform demand, independent demand. 3. **Period Order Quantity (POQ):** A variable-quantity method where the number of periods covered equals the EOQ divided by average period demand. **Advantage:** Combines L4L's responsiveness with EOQ's economy. **Best for:** Variable demand patterns. **Numerical Example (3 Marks):** - Weekly demand: 50, 60, 70, 60, 80, 40 units (6 weeks). - $D = 360$ units/6 weeks = 60/week; $S = ₹300$; $H = ₹2$/unit/week. - $\text{EOQ} = \sqrt{2 \cdot 360 \cdot 300 / 2} = \sqrt{108{,}000} \approx 328.6$ units. - $N = 328.6 / 60 \approx 5.5 \rightarrow 6$ periods. POQ = sum of 6 weeks = 360 units in one lot. - L4L: 6 orders of 50, 60, 70, 60, 80, 40 (separate). **(b) EOQ Numerical (7 Marks)** Given: $D = 8{,}000$ units/year, $S = ₹400$/order, $H = ₹10$/unit/year. **(i) EOQ (3 Marks):** $$Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 8{,}000 \times 400}{10}} = \sqrt{640{,}000} \approx 800 \text{ units}$$ **(ii) Number of orders per year (1 Mark):** $$N^{*} = \frac{D}{Q^{*}} = \frac{8{,}000}{800} = 10 \text{ orders/year}$$ **(iii) Time between orders (1 Mark):** (Assuming 360 working days/year) $$\text{TBO} = \frac{360 \text{ days}}{10} = 36 \text{ days per order}$$ **(iv) Total Annual Cost at EOQ (2 Marks):** $$\text{TAC} = \frac{D}{Q^{*}} \cdot S + \frac{Q^{*}}{2} \cdot H = \frac{8{,}000}{800} \times 400 + \frac{800}{2} \times 10$$ $$= 10 \times 400 + 400 \times 10 = 4{,}000 + 4{,}000 = ₹8{,}000 \text{ per year}$$ --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:** > 1. **Confusing Forecast with Customer Orders:** Many students use only the forecast or only the orders. **Correct rule:** Gross Requirement = max(Forecast, Actual Orders). When MPS is in MTS mode, forecast drives; in MTO mode, orders drive. KTU examiners explicitly check this. > 2. **Forgetting the Lead Time Offset:** A common error is placing the planned receipt in the *same* week as the net requirement. The release must be **earlier** by the lead time. > 3. **Using Cumulative ATP instead of Discrete ATP for New Orders:** Students often say "ATP = 120 units, so 70 can be accepted." But if the new order is time-bound (Week 3 only), **discrete ATP** must be checked. Failing to do this loses 1–2 marks. > 4. **L4L means EXACT net requirement, not rounded:** Do not round to multiples of 10, 50, etc. L4L = order exactly what is needed. > 5. **EOQ assumption violations:** EOQ assumes constant demand, no stockouts, and independent demand. In MPS problems with variable demand, EOQ is the **wrong** lot-sizing rule. Use POQ or PPB instead. > 6. **No proper table format:** KTU examiners reward a clean MPS table (Week | Forecast | Orders | Gross Req | On-Hand | Net Req | Planned Receipt | Planned Release | ATP). Drawing only equations without a table leads to **partial marking**. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [x] **MPS Definition:** Time-phased, end-item-level production plan; bridge between APP and MRP. - [x] **Hierarchy:** Business Plan → APP → **MPS** → MRP → Shop Floor. - [x] **Three Time Fence Zones:** Frozen (locked) → Slushy (semi-flexible) → Liquid (fully flexible). - [x] **ATP Formula:** ATP = On-Hand + MPS Receipts − Committed Backlog. - [x] **Discrete ATP matters more than Cumulative ATP** for time-bound new orders. - [x] **L4L:** Order = exact net requirement; minimum inventory; maximum orders. - [x] **EOQ:** $Q^{*} = \sqrt{2DS/H}$; valid only for constant, independent demand. - [x] **POQ:** Variable quantity; number of periods = EOQ / average period demand. - [x] **PPB:** Use EPP = $S / H$ to decide how many periods to bundle in one lot. - [x] **Lead Time Offset:** Planned Release = Planned Receipt − Lead Time. - [x] **MTS vs MTO vs ATO vs ETO:** Different drivers (forecast vs orders) for MPS. - [x] **Gross Requirement Rule:** Gross Req = max(Forecast, Actual Orders). - [x] **Net Requirement:** $\text{Net Req} = \max(0, \text{Gross Req} - \text{Projected On-Hand} + \text{Safety Stock})$. - [x] **Minimum TAC at EOQ:** $\text{TAC}_{\min} = \sqrt{2DSH}$. - [x] **Number of orders at EOQ:** $N^{*} = \sqrt{DS / 2H}$. - [x] **DTF is usually the cumulative manufacturing lead time; PTF is the cumulative purchasing lead time of the longest-lead component.** - [x] **Always present the MPS in tabular form** with all columns clearly labelled for full marks. - [x] **Units must be consistent:** If $D$ is annual, $S$ is per order, $H$ is per unit per year — all in same time frame. - [x] **Exception Reports:** When MPS is infeasible (over-capacity or under-capacity), the planner must raise exception reports to management for resolution.

topics.title: Material Requirement Planning (MRP) and MRP-II
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET723 - PRODUCTION AND OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Material Requirement Planning (MRP) and MRP-II

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Material Requirement Planning (MRP)** is a computer-based production planning and inventory control system used to manage dependent demand items and ensure the right materials are available at the right time, in the right quantity, for production. It translates the Master Production Schedule (MPS) into a detailed schedule for raw materials, components, and sub-assemblies using the **Bill of Materials (BOM)**, **Inventory Status File**, and **Master Production Schedule** as primary inputs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Manufacturing Resource Planning (MRP-II)** is the second-generation extension of MRP that integrates manufacturing planning with **financial planning, shop floor control, capacity planning, and human resource management** into a single closed-loop system. It links the operational planning function with the financial and business objectives of the organization.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Explanation

**The Grocery List Analogy:**
Imagine you are hosting a large wedding dinner for 100 guests. You will be making **5 dishes**. To make each dish, you need specific ingredients (e.g., Biryani needs rice, chicken, spices; Dessert needs milk, sugar, flour). You cannot buy all ingredients today because some will spoil.

**MRP works exactly like this:** The wedding dinner is your **Master Production Schedule (MPS)**. The recipe for each dish is your **Bill of Materials (BOM)** — it tells you what ingredients (components) go into each dish (finished product) and in what quantity. The pantry (what you already have at home) is your **Inventory Status File**. MRP simply computes, period by period, "When do I need to buy 5 kg of basmati rice, 10 kg of chicken, etc., so that everything arrives on time, but not too early?"

**The Restaurant Chain Analogy (for MRP-II):**
Now imagine the wedding planner must also account for:
- How much **money** (cash flow) will be spent on each item
- How many **cooks and servers** (human resources) are needed
- Whether the **kitchen** has enough capacity (ovens, burners)
- Whether **suppliers** can deliver on time

This holistic view — combining materials, money, machines, and people — is **MRP-II**.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Block (Standard Metrics)

| Term | Standard Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Gross Requirements (GR)** | Total demand for an item in a given period (independent of stock) |
| **Scheduled Receipts (SR)** | Orders already placed and expected to arrive in a given period |
| **On-Hand Inventory (OHI)** | Physical stock available in the warehouse |
| **Net Requirements (NR)** | GR − (OHI + SR) — actual new production needed |
| **Planned Order Receipts (POR)** | Planned quantity of an item to be received in a given period |
| **Planned Order Releases (PORel)** | Planned release date (lead time offset) of a planned order |
| **Lead Time (LT)** | Time required to procure or manufacture an item |
| **Lot-Size (Q)** | Order quantity (discrete lot-sizing rules) |
| **Lot-for-Lot (L4L)** | Order exactly the net requirement |
| **EOQ (Economic Order Quantity)** | Optimal order quantity minimizing total inventory cost |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **primary objective of MRP** is to ensure material availability for production, minimize inventory investment, and maintain low carrying costs while avoiding production disruption.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Logic — The Three Pillars of MRP

MRP operates on a triad of input files. The processing logic follows a deterministic **explosion and netting** algorithm.

> [!NOTE]
> The MRP system performs two fundamental operations repeatedly:
> 1. **Offsetting** — Move the planned order release back by the lead time.
> 2. **Explosion** — When a planned order is generated for a parent item, "explode" its BOM to generate gross requirements for child components.

#### Step 1: Master Production Schedule (MPS) — The Demand Driver
- A time-phased plan stating **what finished products** to make, **how many**, and **when**.
- Represents **independent demand** (driven by customer orders or forecasts).
- It is the *only* input that drives dependent demand for components.

#### Step 2: Bill of Materials (BOM) — The Recipe
- An **indented, structured list** of all raw materials, sub-assemblies, and quantities required to manufacture one unit of a parent item.
- Shows **parent-child relationships** in a product hierarchy.
- Types: **Single-level, Multi-level (Indented), Modular, Planning BOM**.

#### Step 3: Inventory Status File (ISF) — The Current State
- Maintains records of:
  - On-hand quantities
  - Scheduled receipts (open purchase/work orders)
  - Lead times
  - Lot-sizing rules
  - Safety stock levels
  - Item identification (part number, description, unit of measure)

### 2.2 The MRP Processing Algorithm — Logic Flow

The MRP processing for **each component** proceeds as follows:

1. **Determine Gross Requirements (GR)** for each period by exploding the parent item's planned order releases through the BOM.
2. **Add Scheduled Receipts (SR)** — orders already in pipeline to the on-hand inventory.
3. **Compute Projected On-Hand (POH)** for each period:

$$POH_t = POH_{t-1} + SR_t + POR_t - GR_t$$

4. **Calculate Net Requirements (NR)** when POH becomes negative (or below safety stock):

$$NR_t = \max(0, GR_t - (POH_{t-1} + SR_t))$$

5. **Apply Lot-Sizing Rule** to convert NR into a discrete Planned Order Receipt (POR).
6. **Offset the POR by Lead Time** to compute the Planned Order Release (PORel):

$$PORel_t = POR_{t+LT}$$

7. **Explode** the PORel through the BOM to generate GR for the next level down.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $NR_t = GR_t - (OHI_{t-1} + SR_t)$ | Net Requirement | All quantities in units |
| 2 | $POH_t = POH_{t-1} + SR_t + POR_t - GR_t$ | Projected On-Hand | Units |
| 3 | $PORel_t = POR_{(t+LT)}$ | Planned Order Release (offset) | Time periods |
| 4 | $EOQ = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}$ | Economic Order Quantity | $D$=demand (units/yr), $S$=order cost (₹/order), $H$=holding cost (₹/unit/yr) |
| 5 | $TBO = \frac{Q}{D}$ | Time Between Orders | $Q$=lot size, $D$=demand rate |
| 6 | $EPQ = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H} \cdot \frac{p}{p-u}}$ | Economic Production Quantity | $p$=production rate, $u$=usage rate |
| 7 | $SS = Z \cdot \sigma \cdot \sqrt{LT}$ | Safety Stock | $Z$=service level factor, $\sigma$=std dev of demand, $LT$=lead time |
| 8 | $TC = \frac{Q}{2} \cdot H + \frac{D}{Q} \cdot S$ | Total Inventory Cost | ₹/year |
| 9 | $ROL = d \cdot LT + SS$ | Reorder Level | $d$=daily demand |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When solving MRP tables, always maintain the **time-phased bucket structure** (e.g., Week 1, Week 2, ... Week *n*). The three core computations to remember are: **Projected On-Hand, Net Requirements, and Planned Order Releases** (with lead-time offsetting).

### 2.4 Lot-Sizing Techniques in MRP

| Technique | Logic | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Lot-for-Lot (L4L)** | Order exactly = Net Requirement | Low holding cost, high ordering cost items |
| **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)** | Order fixed Q* = EOQ | Constant, high-volume demand |
| **Period Order Quantity (POQ)** | Order to cover a fixed number of periods (EOQ ÷ average demand) | Stable demand with periodic ordering |
| **Minimum Order Quantity (MOQ)** | Order minimum Q if NR > 0 | Supplier-restricted items |
| **Silver-Meal Heuristic** | Minimize average cost per period | Multi-period heuristic |
| **Wagner-Whitin Algorithm** | Dynamic programming — true optimal | Small problems, theoretical benchmark |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

MRP/MRP-II systems are foundational in:
- **Automotive manufacturing** (e.g., Toyota, Ford) for multi-level component tracking
- **Aerospace** for long lead-time part planning (e.g., Boeing 787)
- **Electronics** for high-variety, low-volume production
- **ERP integration** — MRP-II is the forerunner of modern **ERP systems** like SAP, Oracle, Microsoft Dynamics.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive MRP Table Computation — Worked Numerical Example

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Pattern (14-Mark Question):** Students will be given an **MPS, BOM, and Inventory Status** for a multi-level product, and asked to **explode, net, and compute** the full MRP schedule. The following worked example demonstrates the standard KTU board valuation method.

#### Given Data

**Product:** Finished Good **A** (Parent)
- **MPS:** Week 1 = 50 units, Week 3 = 70 units, Week 5 = 60 units
- **Lead Time (LT)** for A = 2 weeks
- **Lot-Sizing Rule for A:** Lot-for-Lot

**BOM (Two-Level Structure):**
- **1 unit of A** requires **2 units of B** and **1 unit of C**
- **1 unit of B** requires **3 units of D** and **2 units of E**
- **1 unit of C** requires **4 units of D**

**Inventory Status File:**

| Item | On-Hand | Scheduled Receipts | Lead Time | Lot-Size Rule |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | 30 | None | 2 weeks | Lot-for-Lot |
| B | 40 | 50 units in Week 2 | 3 weeks | Lot-for-Lot |
| C | 20 | None | 2 weeks | EOQ = 100 |
| D | 200 | 100 units in Week 1 | 1 week | Lot-for-Lot |
| E | 150 | None | 2 weeks | Lot-for-Lot |

---

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Solution

#### LEVEL 0 — Item A (Finished Product)

**Step 1:** Compute Gross Requirements (GR) directly from MPS.
- Week 1: 50, Week 3: 70, Week 5: 60 (zero otherwise).

**Step 2:** Net Requirements (NR) = GR − (OHI + SR)

We start with OHI = 30 in Week 0.

- **Week 1:** GR = 50; available = 30 + 0 = 30; NR = 50 − 30 = 20
- **Week 3:** GR = 70; OHI carried = 0; NR = 70 − 0 = 70
- **Week 5:** GR = 60; OHI carried = 0; NR = 60 − 0 = 60

**Step 3:** Planned Order Receipts (POR) = NR (Lot-for-Lot)
- Week 1: 20, Week 3: 70, Week 5: 60

**Step 4:** Planned Order Releases (PORel) = POR offset by LT = 2 weeks
- PORel for Week 1 → released in **Week −1** (i.e., before planning horizon; treat as 0 or special handling)
- PORel for Week 3 → released in **Week 1**
- PORel for Week 5 → released in **Week 3**

**MRP Table — Item A:**

| Item A (LT=2, L4L) | Wk 0 | Wk 1 | Wk 2 | Wk 3 | Wk 4 | Wk 5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Gross Requirements | 0 | 50 | 0 | 70 | 0 | 60 |
| Scheduled Receipts | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| Projected On-Hand (30) | 30 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| Net Requirements | 0 | 20 | 0 | 70 | 0 | 60 |
| Planned Order Receipts | 0 | 20 | 0 | 70 | 0 | 60 |
| Planned Order Releases | 70 | 0 | 0 | 60 | 0 | 0 |

*Note: PORel for Week 1 is 20; this release is shown 2 weeks earlier — i.e., before Week 1, so it is not visible in the displayed window but was released in Week −1. The Week 3 release of 60 corresponds to POR Week 5.*

#### LEVEL 1 — Item B

**Gross Requirements for B** = 2 × (PORel of A)
- From A's Week −1 release (20 units): GR for B in **Week 1** = 2 × 20 = 40
- From A's Week 3 release (60 units): GR for B in **Week 3** = 2 × 60 = 120

**Inventory:** OHI = 40, SR = 50 in Week 2

**Net Requirements:**
- **Week 1:** Available = 40; GR = 40; NR = 0
- **Week 3:** Available = 0 (40 used in Wk1) + 50 (SR Wk2) = 50; GR = 120; NR = 120 − 50 = 70

**POR (L4L):** Week 3 = 70
**PORel:** Offset by LT = 3 weeks → Released in **Week 0**

**MRP Table — Item B:**

| Item B (LT=3, L4L) | Wk 0 | Wk 1 | Wk 2 | Wk 3 | Wk 4 | Wk 5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Gross Requirements | 0 | 40 | 0 | 120 | 0 | 0 |
| Scheduled Receipts | 0 | 0 | 50 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| Projected On-Hand (40) | 40 | 0 | 50 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| Net Requirements | 0 | 0 | 0 | 70 | 0 | 0 |
| Planned Order Receipts | 0 | 0 | 0 | 70 | 0 | 0 |
| Planned Order Releases | 70 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |

#### LEVEL 1 — Item C

**Gross Requirements for C** = 1 × (PORel of A)
- From A's Week −1 release (20): GR for C in **Week 1** = 20
- From A's Week 3 release (60): GR for C in **Week 3** = 60

**Inventory:** OHI = 20, No SR. Lot-Size = EOQ = 100.

**Net Requirements:**
- **Week 1:** Available = 20; GR = 20; NR = 0
- **Week 3:** Available = 0; GR = 60; NR = 60
- Since EOQ = 100, **POR = 100** in Week 3.
- Excess inventory = 100 − 60 = 40 carried into Week 5 (no demand).

**PORel:** Offset by LT = 2 weeks → Released in **Week 1**

**MRP Table — Item C:**

| Item C (LT=2, EOQ=100) | Wk 0 | Wk 1 | Wk 2 | Wk 3 | Wk 4 | Wk 5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Gross Requirements | 0 | 20 | 0 | 60 | 0 | 0 |
| Scheduled Receipts | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| Projected On-Hand (20) | 20 | 0 | 0 | 40 | 40 | 40 |
| Net Requirements | 0 | 0 | 0 | 60 | 0 | 0 |
| Planned Order Receipts | 0 | 0 | 0 | 100 | 0 | 0 |
| Planned Order Releases | 0 | 100 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |

#### LEVEL 2 — Item D

**Gross Requirements for D** = 3 × (PORel of B) + 4 × (PORel of C)
- From B's Week 0 release (70): GR for D in **Week 0** = 3 × 70 = 210
- From C's Week 1 release (100): GR for D in **Week 1** = 4 × 100 = 400

**Inventory:** OHI = 200, SR = 100 in Week 1. Lot-Size = L4L.

**Net Requirements:**
- **Week 0:** Available = 200; GR = 210; NR = 10
- **Week 1:** Available = 0 (200 − 210 = −10 → NR triggered, but 100 SR arrives); 
  - Computation: POH_end_Wk0 = 200 − 210 = −10 → trigger POR = 10 (L4L) in Week 0; offset by LT=1 → release in Week −1 (pre-horizon); treat as 0 in displayed window.
  - **Week 1:** POH = (−10) + 10 (POR) + 100 (SR) − 400 = **−300** → New POR = 300, released in Week 0.

**MRP Table — Item D:**

| Item D (LT=1, L4L) | Wk 0 | Wk 1 | Wk 2 | Wk 3 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Gross Requirements | 210 | 400 | 0 | 0 |
| Scheduled Receipts | 0 | 100 | 0 | 0 |
| Projected On-Hand (200) | −10 → 0 | −300 → 0 | 0 | 0 |
| Net Requirements | 10 | 300 | 0 | 0 |
| Planned Order Receipts | 10 | 300 | 0 | 0 |
| Planned Order Releases | 300 | 0 | 0 | 0 |

#### LEVEL 2 — Item E

**Gross Requirements for E** = 2 × (PORel of B)
- From B's Week 0 release (70): GR for E in **Week 0** = 2 × 70 = 140

**Inventory:** OHI = 150, No SR. Lot-Size = L4L. LT = 2 weeks.

**Net Requirements:**
- **Week 0:** Available = 150; GR = 140; NR = 0
- No further demand → No POR required for E.

**MRP Table — Item E:**

| Item E (LT=2, L4L) | Wk 0 | Wk 1 | Wk 2 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Gross Requirements | 140 | 0 | 0 |
| Scheduled Receipts | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| Projected On-Hand (150) | 10 | 10 | 10 |
| Net Requirements | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| Planned Order Receipts | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| Planned Order Releases | 0 | 0 | 0 |

### 3.3 Summary of Planned Order Releases (Final Action Report)

| Item | Planned Order Release (Qty) | Release Week |
|---|---|---|
| A | 20, 60 | Wk −1, Wk 3 |
| B | 70 | Wk 0 |
| C | 100 | Wk 1 |
| D | 10, 300 | Wk −1, Wk 0 |
| E | None | — |

### 3.4 EOQ Derivation (Standard KTU Board Question)

The **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)** is derived by minimizing the total cost function:

$$TC(Q) = \underbrace{\frac{D}{Q} \cdot S}_{\text{Ordering Cost}} + \underbrace{\frac{Q}{2} \cdot H}_{\text{Holding Cost}}$$

To find the optimum, take the derivative with respect to $Q$ and set it to zero:

$$\frac{d(TC)}{dQ} = -\frac{DS}{Q^2} + \frac{H}{2} = 0$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{H}{2} = \frac{DS}{Q^2}$$

$$Q^2 = \frac{2DS}{H}$$

$$\boxed{Q^* = EOQ = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The second derivative $\frac{d^2(TC)}{dQ^2} = \frac{2DS}{Q^3} > 0$ confirms this is a **minimum**, not a maximum. The annual cost at EOQ is $TC^* = \sqrt{2DSH}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 MRP System Architecture (Input–Process–Output Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inpA[MPS Master Production Schedule] --> proc[MRP Processing Engine]
    inpB[BOM Bill of Materials File] --> proc
    inpC[ISF Inventory Status File] --> proc
    proc --> outA[Planned Order Releases]
    proc --> outB[Reschedule Notices]
    proc --> outC[Exception Reports]
    proc --> outD[Performance Reports]
    outA --> shop[Shop Floor Execution]
    outB --> purch[Purchase Orders]
    outC --> mgr[Production Manager]
    outD --> exec[Top Management]
```

### 4.2 MRP-II Closed-Loop System (The "Closed Loop" Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    BPLAN[Business Planning] --> SALES[Sales and Operations Planning]
    SALES --> MPS[MPS Master Production Schedule]
    MPS --> MRPCORE[MRP Core Engine]
    RFC[Rough Cut Capacity Planning] --> MRPCORE
    CRCCP[Capacity Requirements Planning CRP] --> MRPCORE
    MRPCORE --> SHOP[Shop Floor Control SFC]
    MRPCORE --> PURCH[Purchasing]
    SHOP --> CRCCP
    SHOP --> COST[Cost Accounting and Financial Planning]
    PURCH --> COST
    COST --> BPLAN
    MRPCORE --> COST
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **closed loop** in MRP-II is critical: feedback from **Shop Floor Control** and **Capacity Requirements Planning** flows *back* into the **MRP engine** to ensure the plan is executable. This is what distinguishes **MRP (open-loop)** from **MRP-II (closed-loop)**.

### 4.3 BOM Explosion Tree (Indented Structure)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    prodA[Finished Good A - Level 0]
    prodA --> compB[Component B - Level 1 - Qty 2]
    prodA --> compC[Component C - Level 1 - Qty 1]
    compB --> partD[Part D - Level 2 - Qty 3]
    compB --> partE[Part E - Level 2 - Qty 2]
    compC --> partD2[Part D - Level 2 - Qty 4]
```

> [!NOTE]
> Notice that **Part D appears in two parents** (B and C). This is a **common component / shared part**, and MRP handles it correctly by summing gross requirements across all parents.

### 4.4 MRP Processing Cycle Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Read Gross Requirements] --> B{On-Hand + SR available?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Consume Stock - Net 0]
    B -- No --> D[Compute Net Requirement]
    D --> E[Apply Lot-Size Rule]
    E --> F[Generate Planned Order Receipt]
    F --> G[Offset by Lead Time]
    G --> H[Planned Order Release]
    H --> I[Explode BOM for Children]
    I --> A
```

### 4.5 MRP vs MRP-II Comparison Matrix (Functional Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MRP
        M1[Material Planning] --> M2[Inventory Control] --> M3[Purchasing]
    end
    subgraph MRP2[MRP-II]
        X1[Material Planning] --> X2[Capacity Planning CRP]
        X2 --> X3[Shop Floor Control]
        X3 --> X4[Financial Integration]
        X4 --> X5[Human Resource Planning]
        X5 --> X1
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> MRP-II is essentially **MRP + a financial and capacity feedback loop**. The latter is the bridge that converts a planning system into a **management information system**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Module 3 | 3 Marks]**

**Q1. Define Material Requirement Planning (MRP). List its three primary inputs.**

**Model Answer:**
MRP is a computer-based production planning and inventory control system designed to manage dependent demand. It ensures that materials, components, and sub-assemblies are available at the right time, in the right quantity.

**Three Primary Inputs:**
1. **Master Production Schedule (MPS)** — defines what, how much, and when to produce finished goods.
2. **Bill of Materials (BOM)** — a structured list of all components and raw materials required to make one unit of a parent product.
3. **Inventory Status File (ISF)** — contains current on-hand quantities, scheduled receipts, lead times, and lot-sizing rules for every item.

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 3 | 3 Marks]**

**Q2. Differentiate between MRP and MRP-II.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | MRP | MRP-II |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Material planning only | Entire manufacturing resource planning |
| **Integration** | Limited to materials | Integrates finance, capacity, HR, shop floor |
| **Feedback Loop** | Open-loop (no feedback) | **Closed-loop** with shop floor feedback |
| **Focus** | Dependent demand for materials | Strategic + operational business planning |
| **Cost Module** | Not included | Includes cost accounting and financial control |
| **Forerunner** | — | Forerunner of modern ERP systems |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Module 3 | 14 Marks]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the **structure of MRP** in detail, listing its inputs, processing logic, and outputs. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Given the following data, prepare the **MRP schedule for items A, B, and C** using Lot-for-Lot rule: **(7 Marks)**

- **MPS for A:** Week 1 = 100, Week 4 = 150, Week 6 = 100
- **BOM:** 1 unit of A needs 2 units of B and 1 unit of C; 1 unit of B needs 3 units of D
- **Inventory:** A = 50, B = 30, C = 20, D = 100
- **Scheduled Receipts:** B has 60 units arriving Week 2; D has 50 units arriving Week 1
- **Lead Times:** A = 2 wks, B = 3 wks, C = 2 wks, D = 1 wk

**Model Solution:**

**(a) MRP Structure (7 Marks):**

*Inputs (3 sub-points):*
- **MPS** — time-phased plan of finished products to be made.
- **BOM** — product structure tree showing parent–child relationships and quantities.
- **ISF** — current inventory levels, open orders, lead times, lot-sizing rules.

*Processing Logic (2 sub-points):*
- **Netting** — determine net requirements by subtracting on-hand and scheduled receipts from gross requirements.
- **Lot-Sizing** — apply rules (L4L, EOQ, POQ) to convert net requirements into discrete order quantities.
- **Offsetting** — subtract lead time from planned order receipt to derive planned order release.
- **Explosion** — use BOM to generate gross requirements for child components from parent releases.

*Outputs (2 sub-points):*
- **Primary:** Planned order releases, order reschedules.
- **Secondary:** Exception reports, performance reports (e.g., MRP controller reports).

**(b) MRP Table Computation (7 Marks):**

**Item A (LT=2, L4L):**
- Week 1: GR=100, OHI=50, NR=50, POR=50, PORel=Wk −1
- Week 4: GR=150, NR=150, POR=150, PORel=Wk 2
- Week 6: GR=100, NR=100, POR=100, PORel=Wk 4

**Item B (LT=3, L4L):**
- From A's PORel (Wk 2: 150, Wk 4: 100): GR for B = 300 in Wk 2; 200 in Wk 4
- Wk 2: Available = 30 + 60 (SR) = 90; GR=300; NR=210; POR=210
- Wk 4: Available = 0; GR=200; NR=200; POR=200
- PORel: Wk 2 POR → Wk −1; Wk 4 POR → Wk 1

**Item C (LT=2, L4L):**
- From A's PORel: GR for C = 150 in Wk 2; 100 in Wk 4
- Wk 2: OHI=20; GR=150; NR=130; POR=130; PORel=Wk 0
- Wk 4: GR=100; NR=100; POR=100; PORel=Wk 2

*(Full table to be drawn as in Section 3.2 above.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Discuss the concept of **MRP-II**. How does it differ from MRP? Explain the **closed-loop MRP-II structure** with a neat block diagram. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The annual demand for a raw material is **10,000 units**, ordering cost is **₹200 per order**, and holding cost is **₹10 per unit per year**. Compute:
  - (i) Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)
  - (ii) Number of orders per year
  - (iii) Time between orders
  - (iv) Total annual inventory cost

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) MRP-II Concept (7 Marks):**

*Definition:* MRP-II (Manufacturing Resource Planning) is the second-generation extension of MRP that integrates material planning with **financial planning, capacity planning, shop floor control, and human resource management** to form a single closed-loop system.

*Differences from MRP:*
- MRP focuses only on **material planning**; MRP-II covers **all manufacturing resources**.
- MRP is **open-loop**; MRP-II is **closed-loop** with feedback.
- MRP-II includes **Cost Accounting, CRP, SFC, and HRM** modules.
- MRP-II forms the **backbone of ERP systems**.

*Closed-Loop Block Diagram (must include):*
Business Planning → S&OP → MPS → MRP Core → CRP → Shop Floor Control → Feedback to MRP. Financial Planning integrated throughout.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

Given: $D = 10{,}000$ units/yr, $S = ₹200$/order, $H = ₹10$/unit/yr.

**(i) EOQ:**
$$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10{,}000 \times 200}{10}}$$

$$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{4{,}000{,}000}{10}} = \sqrt{400{,}000} = 632.46 \text{ units}$$

$$\boxed{Q^* \approx 632 \text{ units}}$$

**(ii) Number of orders per year:**
$$N = \frac{D}{Q^*} = \frac{10{,}000}{632.46} \approx 15.81 \approx 16 \text{ orders/year}$$

**(iii) Time between orders (assuming 250 working days/year):**
$$TBO = \frac{Q^*}{D} \times 250 = \frac{632.46}{10{,}000} \times 250 = 15.81 \text{ days}$$

**(iv) Total Annual Inventory Cost:**
- Ordering Cost = $\frac{D}{Q^*} \cdot S = 15.81 \times 200 = ₹3{,}162$
- Holding Cost = $\frac{Q^*}{2} \cdot H = \frac{632.46}{2} \times 10 = ₹3{,}162$
- $TC = 3{,}162 + 3{,}162 = ₹6{,}324$ per year

$$\boxed{TC^* \approx ₹6{,}324 \text{ per year}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mistakes Students Make:**
> 1. **Lead-Time Offsetting Errors:** Students often forget to offset the planned order receipt backwards by the lead time to get the planned order release. *Marks lost: 2 per item.*
> 2. **Confusing POR vs PORel:** Planned Order **Receipt** is when the item is *received*; Planned Order **Release** is when the order is *placed*. They differ by exactly the lead time.
> 3. **Ignoring Scheduled Receipts:** Students compute net requirements without considering in-transit orders. Always add SR to OHI before netting.
> 4. **Lot-Size Misapplication:** In EOQ problems, do not use $Q = \sqrt{2DS/H}$ when production rate is finite — use **EPQ** instead.
> 5. **POH Negative Without Trigger:** A negative projected on-hand without a corresponding planned order receipt is a common error. Always trigger POR the moment POH goes negative.
> 6. **MRP vs MRP-II Confusion:** Writing only material planning content for an MRP-II question. MRP-II *must* include the closed-loop, financial, and capacity feedback aspects.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Points — Must Memorize for KTU 2024 ESE**

- ✅ **MRP is for *dependent* demand** items (sub-assemblies, components, raw materials).
- ✅ **Three Inputs:** MPS + BOM + Inventory Status File.
- ✅ **Three Outputs:** Planned Order Releases, Reschedule Notices, Exception Reports.
- ✅ **Two Key Operations:** **Netting** (subtract OHI & SR from GR) and **Explosion** (BOM traversal).
- ✅ **Lead Time Offset:** PORel = POR − LT (release earlier than receipt).
- ✅ **EOQ Formula:** $Q^* = \sqrt{2DS/H}$ — derived by minimizing $TC = (D/Q)S + (Q/2)H$.
- ✅ **Lot-for-Lot:** Order size = Net Requirement (no inventory carryover).
- ✅ **EOQ vs EPQ:** Use EPQ when item is *produced* (finite rate $p$); EOQ when *purchased* (instantaneous).
- ✅ **MRP-II = MRP + Finance + Capacity + HR + Shop Floor Control (Closed-Loop).**
- ✅ **MRP-II is the forerunner of ERP** (Enterprise Resource Planning).
- ✅ **Safety Stock** is NOT consumed by MRP netting — it is held aside for demand variability.
- ✅ **Common Components / Shared Parts** are handled by summing GR across all parents in MRP.
- ✅ **MRP does NOT optimize cost** — it only plans material availability. **MRP-II** adds cost optimization.
- ✅ **Capacity Requirements Planning (CRP)** verifies that MRP-generated plans are *manufacturable* by available resources.
- ✅ **Rough Cut Capacity Planning (RCCP)** is a quick, early-stage check on whether MPS is feasible at the resource family level.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET723 - PRODUCTION AND OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP)** is a comprehensive, integrated computer-based system used to manage and coordinate all the resources, information, and functions of a business from a single point of data entry and unified database. It is formally defined by the **American Production and Inventory Control Society (APICS)** as *"a framework for organizing, defining, and standardizing the business processes necessary to effectively plan and control an organization so that its resources can be utilized in the best possible manner to achieve the strategic objectives of the organization."*

In simpler KTU board terms: **ERP is a single, unified software platform that integrates ALL core business functions** — Finance, Human Resources, Manufacturing, Supply Chain, Sales, Procurement, and Customer Relationship Management — into one centralized database, so that data is entered **once** and shared across the entire enterprise in real-time.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"Think of ERP as the Operating System of a Company"** — Just like your smartphone's OS (Android/iOS) integrates your camera, contacts, GPS, music, and messages into ONE seamless experience with a single Apple/Google account, **ERP integrates every department of a company into ONE seamless digital experience with a single database.**

**Real-World Analogy – A Hospital:**
Imagine a large multi-specialty hospital. Before ERP: The reception desk has one register, the pharmacy has a separate register, the lab has another system, the billing counter has yet another. If a patient gets a blood test, the lab result has to be physically carried (or faxed) to the doctor. After ERP: A single digital patient ID is created at reception. The doctor orders the test, the lab sees it instantly, the pharmacy sees the prescription instantly, billing is auto-generated, and the insurance claim is auto-prepared. **One entry. Everywhere visible. Zero duplication.**

### 1.3 Key Terminology & Standard Metrics

- **Unified Database**: A single, central repository of organizational data accessed by all modules.
- **Real-Time Processing (RTP)**: Transactions are processed and reflected instantly across all linked modules. Standard benchmark latency in modern ERPs: **< 2 seconds**.
- **Best Business Practices (BBP)**: Pre-configured industry-standard workflows embedded inside the ERP.
- **Multi-Site / Multi-Currency / Multi-Language Support**: Capability of modern ERPs to operate across global plants.
- **Business Intelligence (BI) Integration**: Dashboards and analytics layered on top of ERP data.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Standard ERP Implementations worldwide (market benchmarks):** SAP S/4HANA (≈ **23%** global market share), Oracle ERP Cloud, Microsoft Dynamics 365, Tally (India SMBs), and Infor. The first commercially successful ERP was **SAP R/3**, released in **1992**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet

### 2.1 The Evolution of ERP (Why ERP Exists — The "Why" and "How")

The journey of ERP is rooted in the need to solve the **"Information Silo Problem"** — where each department maintained its own system, leading to data duplication, inconsistency, and delayed decision-making.

- **1960s – Inventory Management Systems**: Early mainframe-based systems for reorder points and basic inventory tracking.
- **1970s – Material Requirements Planning (MRP I)**: Developed by **Joseph Orlicky** at IBM. Answered the question: *"What do we need to manufacture, and WHEN?"* MRP I focused only on materials and Bill of Materials (BOM).
- **1980s – Manufacturing Resource Planning (MRP II)**: Extended MRP I to include shop-floor scheduling, capacity planning, and rough-cut capacity planning. Answered: *"Can we actually MAKE it with our current capacity?"*
- **1990s – Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP)**: The Gartner Group coined the term in **1990**. Extended MRP II to integrate **ALL functions** — Finance, HR, Sales, CRM, SCM, and Manufacturing — under one database. Answered: *"How is the ENTIRE enterprise performing?"*
- **2000s – ERP II**: Added web-based architecture, Customer Relationship Management (CRM), Supply Chain Management (SCM), Business Intelligence (BI), and SaaS delivery models.
- **2010s–Present – Cloud ERP / Digital ERP**: Mobile-first, AI-integrated, cloud-native platforms (e.g., SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Oracle Fusion).

### 2.2 Core Functional Modules of ERP

A typical KTU board question asks: *"List the major modules of ERP."* Here is the structured breakdown:

- **Financial Management Module (FMM)**: General Ledger (GL), Accounts Payable (AP), Accounts Receivable (AR), Fixed Assets, Cost Center Accounting, Profitability Analysis, Treasury, and Tax.
- **Human Resource Management (HRM)**: Payroll, Time Management, Personnel Administration, Training & Event Management, Recruitment, Performance Appraisal.
- **Production Planning (PP)**: Master Production Schedule (MPS), Material Requirements Planning (MRP), Shop Floor Control (SFC), Capacity Planning, Routing, Work Centers.
- **Materials Management / Inventory Management (MM/IM)**: Purchasing, Inventory Control, Goods Receipt, Vendor Evaluation, Warehouse Management, Invoice Verification.
- **Sales & Distribution (SD)**: Sales Order Processing, Pricing, Shipping, Billing, Credit Management, Foreign Trade.
- **Quality Management (QM)**: Incoming Inspection, In-process Inspection, Quality Control, Quality Certificates, Audit Management.
- **Plant Maintenance (PM)**: Preventive Maintenance, Breakdown Maintenance, Equipment History, Spare Parts Management.
- **Project Management (PS)**: Project Planning, Budgeting, Milestone Tracking, Resource Allocation.
- **Customer Relationship Management (CRM)**: Lead Management, Opportunity Management, Service Requests, Customer Analytics.

### 2.3 Why Organizations Adopt ERP (The Business Case)

- **Single Source of Truth (SSOT)**: One database → no data inconsistency.
- **Process Standardization (BBP)**: All plants follow the same workflow.
- **Real-Time Reporting**: Management decisions based on live, not stale, data.
- **Reduced Operational Cost**: Studies by **Panorama Consulting** (2023 ERP Report) show an average **20–30%** reduction in inventory carrying cost and **15–20%** reduction in procurement cycle time post-ERP.
- **Regulatory Compliance**: Built-in audit trails, SOX/GST/IFRS compliance templates.
- **Scalability**: Supports business expansion without adding proportional administrative staff.

### 2.4 The Dark Side – Risks & Reasons for Failure

- **High Implementation Cost**: Often equals **1–3% of annual revenue** of the company.
- **Implementation Time**: 6 months to 3 years.
- **Resistance to Change (People Risk)**: The #1 cause of ERP failure — employees reluctant to abandon legacy systems.
- **Over-Customization**: Custom code that breaks vendor upgrade paths.
- **Big-Bang vs. Phased Rollout Risk**: "Big-Bang" (go-live in all modules at once) is riskier than phased rollout.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** Classic case study for ERP failure = **Hershey's ERP failure (1999)** which caused a **$151 million** loss in revenue due to a Big-Bang go-live during peak Halloween season. Classic case study for ERP success = **Volkswagen's SAP rollout** and **Coca-Cola Bottling Company's SAP implementation**.

### 2.5 KTU Concept Sheet (High-Yield Quick Reference)

| **Parameter** | **Definition / Value** | **KTU Relevance** |
|---|---|---|
| ERP Full Form | Enterprise Resource Planning | 1-mark direct question |
| MRP I vs MRP II vs ERP | MRP I = materials only; MRP II = mfg + capacity; ERP = all functions | Frequently asked comparison |
| First Commercial ERP | SAP R/3, 1992 | 2-mark question |
| Coined Term "ERP" | Gartner Group, 1990 | 2-mark question |
| APICS Definition | Framework for organizing, defining, standardizing business processes | Verbatim definition often asked |
| Big-Bang Implementation | All modules go live simultaneously on a single date | Frequently asked term |
| Phased Rollout | Modules implemented one after another over time | Frequently asked term |
| BBP | Best Business Practices – vendor's pre-configured industry workflows | 3-mark question |
| Average Cost | 1–3% of annual revenue of organization | Bonus fact for 14-mark answers |
| Leading ERP Vendors | SAP, Oracle, Microsoft Dynamics, Infor, Tally | 2-mark question |
| Avg. Inventory Reduction | 20–30% post-ERP (Panorama Consulting) | Real-world utility |
| Avg. Procurement Cycle Reduction | 15–20% post-ERP | Real-world utility |
| Top Cause of Failure | Resistance to change / People risk | 14-mark question favorite |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Methodological Analysis (ERP Implementation Life Cycle)

> [!NOTE]
> Since ERP is a management/strategic topic (not a mathematical one), this section presents the **ERP Implementation Life Cycle** as the central analytical framework that KTU examiners frequently test. We will walk through it as a rigorous, step-by-step methodology, with all decision logic and conversion formulas shown explicitly.

### 3.1 The Standard ERP Implementation Life Cycle (10 Phases)

This is the most exam-relevant framework in the entire ERP topic. KTU examiners test both the **phases** and the **"what happens in each phase"** logic.

**Step 1: Pre-Evaluation / As-Is Analysis**
- **What is done**: Document the current business processes (the "As-Is" state) of every department.
- **Why it matters**: Without knowing where you are, you cannot design where you are going. Skipping this step is the second most common cause of ERP failure.
- **Tools used**: Process mapping, value stream mapping, interviews, process mining.
- **KTU logic**: "Identify the pain points that justify ERP adoption."

**Step 2: Requirement Gathering / To-Be Design**
- **What is done**: Define the future-state ("To-Be") processes aligned with Best Business Practices (BBP).
- **Why it matters**: ERP is a process re-engineering project, not just a software install.
- **Decision point**: *Fit-to-Standard* (adopt vendor BBP) vs. *Customize* (rewrite the software).

**Step 3: Business Case & Budget Approval**
- **What is done**: Calculate the Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) and the Return on Investment (ROI). Present to top management.
- **Formula applied** (the only quantitative formula in this topic):

$$
\begin{aligned}
TCO_{ERP} &= C_{software} + C_{hardware} + C_{consulting} + C_{training} \\
&\quad + C_{maintenance} + C_{downtime} + C_{customization}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
ROI_{ERP} = \frac{\text{Financial Benefits} - TCO_{ERP}}{TCO_{ERP}} \times 100\%
$$

- **Reasoning behind the formula**: A 14-mark KTU answer will lose marks if you only state the formula. You MUST explain each term:
  - $C_{software}$ = License cost (one-time or subscription).
  - $C_{hardware}$ = Servers, networking, storage infrastructure.
  - $C_{consulting}$ = External implementation partner fees (often 2× the software cost).
  - $C_{training}$ = End-user and super-user training.
  - $C_{maintenance}$ = Annual support fee (typically **18–22%** of license cost per year).
  - $C_{downtime}$ = Cost of productivity loss during cutover.
  - $C_{customization}$ = Highest hidden cost — every custom line of code doubles future upgrade cost.

**Step 4: Vendor Selection**
- **What is done**: RFP (Request for Proposal) issued; demo sessions; reference checks; final selection.
- **Evaluation criteria**: Industry fit, local support, TCO, scalability, technical architecture, partner ecosystem.
- **Common KTU trap**: Students write "we choose SAP because it's the best." Wrong — must justify against *your* requirements.

**Step 5: Project Planning & Team Formation**
- **What is done**: Form a cross-functional project team — Project Sponsor (C-level), Project Manager, Functional Consultants, Technical Consultants, Super Users (key end-users from each department), Change Management Lead.
- **Why it matters**: ERP projects have a **70% chance of failure** if the project sponsor is not at the C-level (Standish Group research).

**Step 6: Gap Analysis**
- **What is done**: Compare "To-Be" design against vendor's standard BBP. Document the GAPs that need customization.
- **Output**: A Gap Analysis Document classifying each gap as — *Fit* (no change), *Extend* (configuration), *Customize* (code change), *Interface* (third-party link).

**Step 7: Configuration & Customization (Blueprint Realization)**
- **What is done**: Configure master data, set up organizational structure (Company Code, Plant, Storage Location, Sales Organization), define workflows.
- **Critical KTU insight**: Industry rule of thumb — keep customization to **< 10%** of the codebase. Heavy customization leads to "upgrade lock-in" where the company cannot move to newer ERP versions.

**Step 8: Testing (Unit, Integration, UAT, Performance)**
- **Unit Testing**: Each module is tested by functional consultants.
- **Integration Testing**: Cross-module data flow is verified (e.g., a Sales Order → triggers Production Order → triggers Material Requirement → triggers Purchase Order → triggers Vendor Payment).
- **User Acceptance Testing (UAT)**: End-users validate that the system meets business needs.
- **Performance/Load Testing**: Simulates peak transaction volume.

**Step 9: Go-Live Strategy (Big-Bang vs. Phased vs. Pilot)**
- **Big-Bang**: All modules go live on the SAME day across the ENTIRE organization. Pros: Fast. Cons: High risk. Used when: Tight deadline, small organization.
- **Phased Rollout**: Modules or regions go live sequentially over months/years. Pros: Lower risk, lessons learned. Cons: Long project.
- **Pilot / Hybrid**: One plant or one region goes live first, lessons applied to others. Pros: Balanced. Cons: Requires parallel running.

**Step 10: Post-Implementation Support & Continuous Improvement**
- **Hyper-care period** (first 4–8 weeks): Dedicated team fixes bugs.
- **Continuous improvement**: Periodic upgrades, new module rollouts, BI dashboards.
- **Steady-state**: Operations handed over to internal IT team.

### 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing an Implementation Strategy

| **Criterion** | **Big-Bang** | **Phased Rollout** | **Pilot / Hybrid** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Implementation Time | Short (3–6 months) | Long (1–3 years) | Medium (6–12 months) |
| Risk Level | **High** | Low | Medium |
| Cost | Lower initially | Higher (parallel systems) | Medium |
| Business Disruption | **High** | Low | Medium |
| Knowledge Transfer Speed | Fast | Slow | Medium |
| Best Suited For | Small/medium firms, tight deadline | Large multi-nationals | Multi-plant companies |
| Failure Probability (industry data) | **~60%** | ~30% | ~25% |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 14-Mark Gold Tip:** When the question says *"Compare Big-Bang and Phased implementation"*, you MUST present this table OR a comparison with at least **5 distinguishing points**. A bare 2-line answer will only fetch **3–4 marks** out of 14.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

```mermaid
flowchart LR
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

**Q1. Define Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP) as per APICS. Mention any two benefits of ERP.** `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**
Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP) is a framework for organizing, defining, and standardizing the business processes necessary to effectively plan and control an organization so that its resources are used in the best possible way (APICS definition). The term was coined by the **Gartner Group in 1990**.

Two benefits:
1. **Single Source of Truth**: All departments work from a common database, eliminating data duplication and inconsistency.
2. **Process Standardization**: Vendors embed Best Business Practices (BBP), enabling uniform workflows across plants and geographies.

---
