> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2. Differentiate between MRP I and MRP II. Why did industry move from MRP II to ERP?** `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

| **Parameter** | **MRP I** | **MRP II** |
|---|---|---|
| Scope | Materials only | Manufacturing + Capacity |
| Functions Covered | BOM explosion, reorder planning | Shop floor scheduling, CRP, capacity planning |
| Decision Support | What & when to manufacture | Can we manufacture with current capacity? |
| Year | 1970s | 1980s |

**Why move from MRP II to ERP?**
MRP II was limited to the **manufacturing shop floor**. ERP extended the scope to **ALL business functions** — Finance, HR, Sales, CRM, SCM — under a single integrated database. ERP is essentially MRP II + Finance + HR + CRM + SCM.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**

---

#### ✅ **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Choose A or B**

**Q. (a)** Explain in detail the major functional modules of an Enterprise Resource Planning system. Describe how these modules share data through a unified database. `[7 Marks]`

**(b)** Discuss the key benefits and major challenges of implementing ERP in a manufacturing organization. `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution for (a):**

An ERP system is built as a suite of integrated functional modules that draw from a single unified database. The major modules are:

1. **Financial Accounting (FI)** – Manages general ledger, accounts payable, accounts receivable, bank accounting, asset accounting, and consolidation. Posts entries to the central database, which instantly reflects in cost center reports.

2. **Controlling (CO)** – Cost center accounting, internal orders, profitability analysis, product costing. Receives data from FI, MM, and PP modules.

3. **Materials Management (MM)** – Handles procurement, inventory management, vendor evaluation, goods receipt, and invoice verification. Master data (material master, vendor master) is shared with FI, SD, and PP.

4. **Production Planning (PP)** – Includes Master Production Schedule (MPS), Material Requirements Planning (MRP), Bill of Materials (BOM), Routing, Work Centers, and Shop Floor Control. Reads demand from SD module and explodes BOM to generate planned orders.

5. **Sales and Distribution (SD)** – Handles sales order, delivery, shipping, billing, and credit management. Sales order triggers a delivery in MM and a billing document in FI.

6. **Human Resource Management (HRM)** – Manages personnel administration, payroll, time management, training, and performance appraisal. Shares employee master with FI for cost allocation.

7. **Quality Management (QM)** – Quality planning, inspection, and audit. Triggered automatically during goods receipt in MM.

8. **Plant Maintenance (PM)** – Preventive and breakdown maintenance of equipment, linked to spare parts inventory in MM.

**Data Sharing Mechanism (Unified Database):**
All modules read from and write to a **central logical database** (with physical tables linked by primary keys). Example: A single sales order in SD automatically creates a **delivery document** in MM, an **accounting document** in FI, a **production order** in PP (if make-to-order), and updates **inventory levels** in real time. This eliminates redundant data entry.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Key Benefits:**
1. **Single Source of Truth** – One database eliminates inconsistency across departments.
2. **Process Standardization** – Best Business Practices enforce uniform workflows.
3. **Real-Time Reporting** – Management dashboards reflect current data.
4. **Cost Reduction** – Studies show 20–30% reduction in inventory carrying cost and 15–20% reduction in procurement cycle time.
5. **Regulatory Compliance** – Built-in audit trails, GST, IFRS, SOX templates.
6. **Scalability** – Supports multi-plant, multi-currency operations.

**Major Challenges:**
1. **High Cost** – TCO can be 1–3% of annual revenue.
2. **Long Implementation Time** – 6 months to 3 years.
3. **Resistance to Change** – Employees reluctant to abandon legacy systems; #1 cause of failure.
4. **Over-Customization** – Custom code blocks future upgrades.
5. **Skill Gap** – Lack of in-house ERP talent; dependence on consultants.
6. **Big-Bang vs Phased Risk** – Choosing the wrong go-live strategy leads to operational disruption.
7. **Data Migration Complexity** – Legacy data cleansing is time-consuming.
8. **Vendor Lock-in** – Switching ERP later is extremely expensive.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students commonly lose **3–4 marks** on this question by:
> 1. Listing modules **without explaining how they share data** (the database integration is the heart of ERP — must be explained).
> 2. Mentioning only "advantages" and forgetting "challenges" or treating them briefly.
> 3. Writing generic answers like "ERP is good software" instead of using KTU terminology (BOM, MPS, CRP, BBP, SSOT).
> Always **bold the technical terms** and **use the unified database as the central explanation anchor**.

---

#### ✅ **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q. (a)** Explain the evolution of ERP from MRP I to ERP II with a neat flow diagram. Highlight the key contributions of each stage. `[7 Marks]`

**(b)** Describe the ERP Implementation Life Cycle in detail. Compare the Big-Bang and Phased Rollout strategies. `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution for (a):**

The evolution of ERP is a journey of increasing functional integration, driven by the need to eliminate information silos.

**Stage 1 — Inventory Management Systems (1960s):**
Mainframe-based systems that tracked reorder points and basic inventory levels. Limited to inventory control; no integration.

**Stage 2 — MRP I (1970s) – Material Requirements Planning:**
Developed by **Joseph Orlicky** at IBM. Introduced the **Bill of Materials (BOM)** explosion logic to calculate dependent demand. Answered: *"What materials do we need, and WHEN?"* Scope was limited to materials planning.

**Stage 3 — MRP II (1980s) – Manufacturing Resource Planning:**
Extended MRP I to include **shop floor scheduling, capacity planning (CRP), and rough-cut capacity planning (RCCP)**. Answered: *"Can we actually MAKE it with our current resources?"* Integrated manufacturing data but still isolated from Finance and HR.

**Stage 4 — ERP (1990s):**
The **Gartner Group coined the term in 1990**. ERP extended MRP II to integrate **ALL core business functions** — Finance, HR, Sales, Procurement, Manufacturing, Inventory — under **one unified database**. Answered: *"How is the ENTIRE enterprise performing?"* First commercial ERP: **SAP R/3 (1992)**.

**Stage 5 — ERP II (2000s):**
Added **web-based architecture, Customer Relationship Management (CRM), Supply Chain Management (SCM), Business Intelligence (BI)**, and SaaS delivery models. Extended ERP beyond the enterprise to customers, suppliers, and partners.

**Flow Diagram:**
*(Students should draw a horizontal flow: MRP I → MRP II → ERP → ERP II → Cloud ERP, with year markers and key features at each node.)*

**Model Solution for (b):**

The ERP Implementation Life Cycle consists of 10 phases (see Section 3.1 for detailed explanations):

1. **Pre-Evaluation / As-Is Analysis** – Document current processes and pain points.
2. **Requirement Gathering / To-Be Design** – Define future-state processes.
3. **Business Case and Budget Approval** – Calculate TCO and ROI.
4. **Vendor Selection** – RFP, demos, reference checks.
5. **Project Planning and Team Formation** – Cross-functional team, executive sponsor.
6. **Gap Analysis** – Compare To-Be with vendor BBP.
7. **Configuration and Customization** – Set up master data and workflows.
8. **Testing** – Unit, Integration, UAT, Performance.
9. **Go-Live** – Big-Bang / Phased / Pilot.
10. **Post-Implementation Support** – Hyper-care, continuous improvement.

**Comparison: Big-Bang vs. Phased Rollout**

| **Parameter** | **Big-Bang** | **Phased Rollout** |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | All modules go live on the SAME day | Modules go live sequentially over months/years |
| Time to Full Implementation | Short (3–6 months) | Long (1–3 years) |
| Risk Level | **High** | Low |
| Cost | Lower initial | Higher (parallel systems) |
| Business Disruption | High | Low |
| Failure Probability | ~60% (industry data) | ~30% |
| Knowledge Transfer | Fast | Slow but steady |
| Best Suited For | Small/medium firms, tight deadline | Large multi-nationals |
| Example | Hershey's 1999 (failure) | Most large SAP rollouts |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** For (a), students often skip the **flow diagram** and lose **2 marks** (KTU mandates a diagram for full credit). For (b), students often write the 10 phases as a flat list without explaining the *logic* behind each phase — each phase should have a *"what is done + why it matters"* explanation. Also, the Big-Bang vs Phased comparison MUST be in **tabular or point-by-point format** with **at least 5 distinct parameters**.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Last-Minute KTU Exam Prep**

- ✅ **Full form of ERP** = Enterprise Resource Planning.
- ✅ **Coined by** = Gartner Group, in **1990**.
- ✅ **APICS definition** — Learn the exact verbatim line.
- ✅ **First commercial ERP** = SAP R/3 (1992); **Founder of MRP** = Joseph Orlicky (1970s).
- ✅ **Evolution Path (memorize sequence)**: IMS → MRP I → MRP II → ERP → ERP II → Cloud ERP.
- ✅ **MRP I** = Materials only; **MRP II** = Manufacturing + Capacity; **ERP** = All functions under one database.
- ✅ **Core Idea of ERP** = One unified database, entered once, visible everywhere, in real time.
- ✅ **Major Modules (memorize at least 6)**: FI, CO, MM, PP, SD, HRM, QM, PM.
- ✅ **BBP** = Best Business Practices (vendor's pre-configured workflows).
- ✅ **SSOT** = Single Source of Truth (the unified database).
- ✅ **Top Benefits**: Single Source of Truth, Process Standardization, Real-Time Reporting, Cost Reduction (20–30% inventory cost reduction), Compliance.
- ✅ **Top Challenges**: High cost (1–3% of revenue), long timeline, resistance to change (biggest reason for failure), over-customization, data migration, vendor lock-in.
- ✅ **TCO Components** = Software + Hardware + Consulting + Training + Maintenance + Downtime + Customization.
- ✅ **ROI Formula** = (Benefits − TCO) / TCO × 100%.
- ✅ **10-Phase Life Cycle** = Pre-Eval → Requirements → Business Case → Vendor Selection → Project Planning → Gap Analysis → Configuration → Testing → Go-Live → Post-Implementation.
- ✅ **Three Go-Live Strategies** = Big-Bang (all at once, high risk), Phased (one by one, low risk), Pilot (one site first, then scale).
- ✅ **Big-Bang Failure Example** = Hershey's 1999 (₹/$151 M loss).
- ✅ **Top ERP Vendors** = SAP, Oracle, Microsoft Dynamics, Infor, Tally.
- ✅ **Key Rule of Thumb** = Keep customization < 10% of code; project sponsor MUST be C-level.
- ✅ **Difference between BPR and ERP** = BPR (re-engineering of processes) is often a *predecessor* of ERP (re-engineering enabled by software).

> **End of Notes — Module 3: Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP) | KTU OEIET723**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Capacity Management: Capacity Measures and Influencing Factors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET723 - PRODUCTION AND OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Capacity Management: Capacity Measures and Influencing Factors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 3 — CAPACITY MANAGEMENT: CAPACITY MEASURES AND INFLUENCING FACTORS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of Production and Operations Management (POM), **Capacity** is formally defined as the maximum rate of output that a productive system, facility, machine, process, or system of resources is theoretically capable of producing under specified operating conditions over a given period of time. It is the upper limit of throughput that an operation can sustain.

For KTU 2024 Scheme examinations, the term capacity must always be qualified into one of three precise sub-definitions:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core KTU Capacity Definitions (Board-Exam Gold Standard)**
> - **Design Capacity (DC):** The *theoretical* maximum output rate an operation is *designed* to produce under *ideal* conditions, without considering product mix, quality, maintenance, or variability. It is the "nameplate" or "rated" capacity stated on the plant drawing.
> - **Effective Capacity (EC):** The *realistic* maximum output rate that the operation can produce given its current product mix, quality standards, scheduling, and operational policies. It is always **less than or equal to** Design Capacity: $EC \le DC$.
> - **Actual Output (AO):** The *realized* output actually produced by the system during a specified period. It is bounded above by the Effective Capacity: $AO \le EC \le DC$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition for First-Time Learners)

Imagine a brand-new **train coach with 72 seats** (Design Capacity). The coach is the maximum the manufacturer built it to hold — its physical limit.

However, on a real day, the railway may:
- Keep **4 seats reserved for ladies**,
- **Block 2 seats** for maintenance crew,
- **Disable 1 seat** as a defective/cordoned seat.

So the railway can now *actually sell* only **65 tickets** — this is the **Effective Capacity**.

But the train runs and only **53 passengers** turn up on that day. That **53 passengers** is the **Actual Output**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy Mapping (Remember This for Viva)**
> | Train Analogy | POM Equivalent |
> |---|---|
> | Manufacturer's rated 72 seats | **Design Capacity** |
> | 65 sellable seats (after policy) | **Effective Capacity** |
> | 53 actual passengers | **Actual Output** |

The **gap** between each level is what capacity management seeks to *measure*, *diagnose*, and *reduce* — without compromising quality or worker safety.

### 1.3 Key Quantitative Performance Metrics (Units & Conventions)

The two key **derived metrics** evaluated in KTU papers are:

- **Utilization (U):** The proportion of *Design Capacity* that is actually being used. Expressed as a percentage.
- **Efficiency (E):** The proportion of *Effective Capacity* that is actually being used. Expressed as a percentage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why two different measures?**
> Utilization tells management *whether the firm is using its installed infrastructure at all* (asset deployment). Efficiency tells management *how well the existing process is being run* (operational excellence). KTU questions frequently test the **distinction between utilization and efficiency** — students who confuse them lose marks.

Both metrics are dimensionless when expressed as a ratio, but are **conventionally quoted in percentage (%)** in board exam answers.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Capacity Framework

Capacity in POM exists at **three nested tiers**. Every larger tier contains the smaller one. The hierarchy is the foundation for almost every KTU 2-mark and 14-mark question on this topic.

**Tier 1 — Design Capacity (Theoretical Ceiling)**
This is fixed at the time the plant is *designed* and is rarely altered. It depends on:
- Number of machines purchased
- Floor area and building dimensions
- Number of shifts the plant is *engineered* to run (often 2 or 3 shifts)
- Theoretical cycle time of bottleneck machines

**Tier 2 — Effective Capacity (Realistic Ceiling)**
The operation cannot actually run at Design Capacity because of unavoidable *real-world losses* such as:
- Preventive maintenance shutdowns
- Product-mix changeover (setup) time
- Quality inspection time
- Employee breaks, holidays, absenteeism
- Equipment aging and reduced cycle speeds
- Supply chain delays for raw materials

The ratio of Effective Capacity to Design Capacity is sometimes called the **Effectiveness Ratio** or **Design-to-Effective ratio**:

$$\text{Design-Effectiveness Ratio} = \frac{EC}{DC} \times 100\%$$

A healthy process operates with a design-effectiveness ratio in the **80%–90%** band.

**Tier 3 — Actual Output (Realized Performance)**
The actual output is what was produced during a given period. It is less than Effective Capacity due to:
- Unplanned machine breakdowns
- Absenteeism beyond planned allowance
- Material shortages
- Quality rejections and rework
- Demand fluctuations (under-running capacity)

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Capacity Decisions

- **Step 1:** Establish the **Design Capacity** from plant specifications.
- **Step 2:** Subtract *planned* losses (maintenance, changeovers, breaks) → arrive at **Effective Capacity**.
- **Step 3:** Subtract *unplanned* losses (breakdowns, absenteeism, rework) → arrive at **Actual Output**.
- **Step 4:** Compute **Utilization** and **Efficiency** to diagnose which gap is larger.
- **Step 5:** If utilization is low but efficiency is high → the firm is *under-demanded*; if efficiency is low but utilization is high → the firm has *operational problems* (bottlenecks, breakdowns).

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Insight:** A common KTU question framing is: "The plant has a design capacity of X units and produces Y units. If efficiency is 75%, calculate effective capacity." Students must carefully identify *which formula* to invert.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **minimum mandatory formula set** for answering any Part A or Part B question on capacity measures. Memorize it.

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Design Capacity | $DC$ | Given (plant specification) | units / period |
| 2 | Effective Capacity | $EC$ | Given or $= U_{\text{exp}} \times DC$ | units / period |
| 3 | Actual Output | $AO$ | Given (achieved) | units / period |
| 4 | Utilization | $U$ | $U = \dfrac{AO}{DC} \times 100\%$ | Percentage (%) |
| 5 | Efficiency | $E$ | $E = \dfrac{AO}{EC} \times 100\%$ | Percentage (%) |
| 6 | Design-Effectiveness Ratio | $DER$ | $DER = \dfrac{EC}{DC} \times 100\%$ | Percentage (%) |
| 7 | Expected Output (from Efficiency) | $E_{out}$ | $E_{out} = E \times EC$ | units / period |
| 8 | Expected Output (from Utilization) | $U_{out}$ | $U_{out} = U \times DC$ | units / period |

**Boundary conditions to remember:**

$$0\% \le U \le 100\% \quad \text{and} \quad 0\% \le E \le 100\%$$
$$AO \le EC \le DC$$
$$U = E \times DER \div 100 \;\; \text{(cross-relation)}$$

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of Capacity Measures

- **Capacity Planning:** Long-term investment decisions (buy a new line? add a shift?) depend on utilization trends.
- **Bottleneck Identification:** If efficiency is low despite adequate utilization, the bottleneck is *operational* (process issue), not *strategic* (demand issue).
- **Forecasting & Scheduling:** Effective capacity is the upper bound used in linear programming models for production planning.
- **Performance Benchmarking:** Industries use Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE) — a direct extension of these three capacity tiers (Availability × Performance × Quality).
- **Financial Justification:** Capital budgeting (payback, NPV) of capacity expansions uses incremental design capacity as the revenue driver.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Pitfall:** Students often compute Utilization correctly but report it as Efficiency, or quote $AO/EC$ as Utilization. The examiner strictly follows the formula sheet in Section 2.3. Mis-tagging = -2 to -4 marks per sub-part.

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Basic Two-Question Format (Warm-Up)

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
A small-scale textile unit has a Design Capacity of **5,000 metres of cloth per day**. The plant operates 2 shifts and has an Effective Capacity of **4,200 metres per day**. During the month of March (26 working days), the actual production recorded was **3,900 metres per day**. Calculate (i) Utilization, (ii) Efficiency, and (iii) the Design-Effectiveness Ratio.

**Given Data (Extract Carefully):**
$$DC = 5000 \text{ m/day}, \quad EC = 4200 \text{ m/day}, \quad AO = 3900 \text{ m/day}$$

### Solution

**Part (i) — Utilization**

$$U = \frac{AO}{DC} \times 100\%$$

Substituting the given numerical values:

$$U = \frac{3900}{5000} \times 100\%$$

$$U = 0.78 \times 100\% = 78\%$$

**Interpretation:** The plant is using 78% of its maximum installed/engineering capacity. 22% of the *built* capacity is sitting idle.

**Part (ii) — Efficiency**

$$E = \frac{AO}{EC} \times 100\%$$

$$E = \frac{3900}{4200} \times 100\%$$

$$E = 0.9286 \times 100\% \approx 92.86\%$$

**Interpretation:** The plant is running at ~92.86% of its *realistic* achievable output. This is a strong operational performance indicator.

**Part (iii) — Design-Effectiveness Ratio**

$$DER = \frac{EC}{DC} \times 100\%$$

$$DER = \frac{4200}{5000} \times 100\%$$

$$DER = 0.84 \times 100\% = 84\%$$

**Diagnostic Insight for KTU 14-Mark Answer:**
- $U = 78\%$ and $E \approx 92.86\%$
- Since $E > U$, the firm is **operationally efficient** but has **insufficient demand** to use the full installed capacity.
- The diagnostic conclusion (a frequent KTU sub-part) is: *Increase sales/marketing efforts or reduce Design Capacity (sell a machine) — do not chase process improvements.*

**Cross-Verification using the cross-relation $U = E \times DER / 100$:**

$$U_{\text{check}} = \frac{92.86 \times 84}{100} = \frac{7800.24}{100} = 78.00\% \;\; \checkmark$$

The cross-relation holds.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Multi-Product Plant (KTU Board-Standard 14-Marker)

**Problem Statement:**
A precision engineering firm operates on **3 shifts, 300 days per year**. The Design Capacity of the bottleneck machine is **120 units/day**. The Effective Capacity is 85% of Design Capacity. The firm produces two products — **Product X** and **Product Y** — in the ratio **3:2** (by units). The actual output for the year was **21,000 units of X** and **13,000 units of Y**. The firm also suffered a quality rejection of **5% of effective capacity**.

Calculate:
- (a) The **Effective Capacity** (in units/year) and **Actual Output** (in units/year). (7 marks)
- (b) The **Utilization** and **Efficiency** of the plant. Comment on what these values indicate about the operations manager's priority area. (7 marks)

### Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Convert Design Capacity to annual units.**

The Design Capacity is 120 units per day, and the firm operates 300 days/year.

$$DC_{\text{annual}} = 120 \;\text{units/day} \times 300 \;\text{days/year} = 36{,}000 \;\text{units/year}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Effective Capacity using the 85% factor.**

$$EC_{\text{annual}} = 0.85 \times DC_{\text{annual}}$$

$$EC_{\text{annual}} = 0.85 \times 36{,}000 = 30{,}600 \;\text{units/year}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Actual Output.**

$$AO_{\text{annual}} = X + Y = 21{,}000 + 13{,}000 = 34{,}000 \;\text{units/year}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 34,000 can exceed 30,600:** This is a classic KTU trap. The actual output (34,000) appears to violate the rule $AO \le EC$. This can happen if the firm *overtime-runs* the bottleneck beyond its nominal effective capacity, or if the rejection was absorbed in *actual good output* (i.e., 5% rejection was a separate loss). In such a case, the student must explicitly state this caveat in the answer to score full marks.

**Step 4 — Verify the 5% rejection figure (a common sub-question).**

$$\text{Rejected units} = 5\% \times EC_{\text{annual}} = 0.05 \times 30{,}600 = 1{,}530 \;\text{units/year}$$

$$\text{Good actual output} = AO_{\text{annual}} - \text{Rejected} = 34{,}000 - 1{,}530 = 32{,}470 \;\text{units/year}$$

This is what was *sold* to customers. The remaining 1,530 were reworked or scrapped.

### Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Compute Utilization.**

$$U = \frac{AO_{\text{annual}}}{DC_{\text{annual}}} \times 100\%$$

$$U = \frac{34{,}000}{36{,}000} \times 100\% = 94.44\%$$

**Step 2 — Compute Efficiency.**

$$E = \frac{AO_{\text{annual}}}{EC_{\text{annual}}} \times 100\%$$

$$E = \frac{34{,}000}{30{,}600} \times 100\% = 111.11\%$$

**Step 3 — Interpret the values.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Diagnostic Rule:** $E > 100\%$ is a **red flag**. It means the firm is *exceeding* its realistic capacity ceiling, which can only be sustained by:
> - Overtime working (labor cost up, fatigue risk up),
> - Deferred maintenance (breakdown risk up),
> - Quality shortcuts (rejection rate up).
>
> The operations manager's priority should therefore be: **EXPAND the Design Capacity (add a machine/shift) — do NOT push the current process harder.**

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common Capacity Scenarios

| Scenario | $U$ | $E$ | Diagnostic | Managerial Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Idle plant, strong process | Low | High | Under-demand | Marketing push, or reduce DC |
| Busy plant, weak process | High | Low | Operational bottlenecks | Process re-engineering, TPM |
| Idle plant, weak process | Low | Low | Strategic crisis | Demand creation + process overhaul |
| Overworked plant | Very High | $>100\%$ | Unsustainable load | Capacity expansion (add shifts/machines) |
| Ideal plant | ~80–90% | ~85–95% | Well-balanced | Maintain, monitor, incremental improvement |

## 3.4 Influencing Factors of Capacity (Exam-Focused Categorization)

KTU frequently asks: *"Discuss the factors influencing capacity decisions."* Use the following **two-tier classification** for a full-mark answer.

### A. Internal (Controllable) Factors

- **Facility Design:** Size, layout, technology level of machines, automation.
- **Product Mix / Design:** Standardized products cycle faster; customized products need more changeover time and reduce capacity.
- **Human Factors:** Skill level, training, motivation, shift patterns, absenteeism.
- **Operational Factors:** Maintenance policy (preventive vs. breakdown), setup procedures, quality control strictness, scheduling efficiency.
- **Supply Chain Factors:** Reliability of raw material suppliers, inventory policies, lead times.
- **Workforce Management:** Number of shifts, overtime policy, ergonomics.

### B. External (Uncontrollable) Factors

- **Government Regulations:** Pollution control norms, safety standards, labor laws (Factories Act).
- **Union Agreements:** Restrictions on overtime, shift rotations, manning levels.
- **Market Demand:** Cyclical/seasonal fluctuations that may force under- or over-utilization.
- **Competitor Actions:** New entrants, capacity wars, price competition.
- **Macroeconomic Conditions:** Recession (low demand) or boom (overload).
- **Technology Disruption:** Adoption of new tech can suddenly raise or render obsolete existing capacity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for Board Exam:** **"F-P-H-O-S"** for Internal (Facility, Product, Human, Operational, Supply chain) and **"G-U-M-C-T"** for External (Government, Union, Market, Competitor, Technology).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The Three-Tier Capacity Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["DESIGN CAPACITY DC - Theoretical Maximum - Plant Nameplate Rating"]
    B["EFFECTIVE CAPACITY EC - Realistic Maximum - Accounts for Planned Losses"]
    C["ACTUAL OUTPUT AO - Realized Production - Accounts for Unplanned Losses"]
    D["UTILIZATION U = AO / DC x 100 percent"]
    E["EFFICIENCY E = AO / EC x 100 percent"]
    F["PLANNED LOSSES - Preventive Maintenance - Setup Changeovers - Breaks and Holidays - Product Mix Adjustments"]
    G["UNPLANNED LOSSES - Machine Breakdowns - Absenteeism - Quality Rejection - Material Shortages - Demand Variability"]
    H["OPERATIONAL DIAGNOSIS - U high and E low equals Process Bottleneck - U low and E high equals Under Demand - E above 100 percent equals Overload Crisis"]
    A --> F
    F --> B
    B --> G
    G --> C
    A --> D
    B --> E
    D --> H
    E --> H
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Influencing Factors Classification

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INTERNAL["INTERNAL FACTORS - Controllable by Management"]
        I1["Facility Design and Layout"]
        I2["Product Mix and Design Complexity"]
        I3["Human Factors - Skills, Shifts, Absenteeism"]
        I4["Operational Policies - Maintenance, QC"]
        I5["Supply Chain Reliability"]
    end
    subgraph EXTERNAL["EXTERNAL FACTORS - Beyond Direct Control"]
        E1["Government Regulations and Safety Laws"]
        E2["Union Agreements and Labor Norms"]
        E3["Market Demand Fluctuations"]
        E4["Competitor Capacity Strategies"]
        E5["Technology Disruption"]
    end
    subgraph OUTCOME["CAPACITY OUTCOME"]
        O1["Effective Capacity Achieved"]
        O2["Utilization and Efficiency Scores"]
        O3["Strategic Decision - Expand, Contract, or Maintain"]
    end
    I1 --> O1
    I2 --> O1
    I3 --> O1
    I4 --> O1
    I5 --> O1
    E1 --> O2
    E2 --> O2
    E3 --> O2
    E4 --> O2
    E5 --> O2
    O1 --> O2
    O2 --> O3
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Decision Tree for Capacity Diagnosis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["Compute U and E for the Plant"]
    Q1{"Is U less than 70 percent?"}
    Q2{"Is E less than 80 percent?"}
    Q3{"Is E greater than 100 percent?"}
    A1["Action: Under Demand - Boost Marketing, Reduce DC, Defer Expansion"]
    A2["Action: Operational Bottleneck - Apply TPM, Lean, Six Sigma"]
    A3["Action: Balanced Plant - Maintain and Monitor"]
    A4["Action: CRITICAL OVERLOAD - Expand DC Immediately, Add Shifts or Machines"]
    START --> Q1
    Q1 -- YES --> Q2
    Q1 -- NO --> Q3
    Q2 -- YES --> A2
    Q2 -- NO --> A1
    Q3 -- YES --> A4
    Q3 -- NO --> A3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Use Tip for 14-Mark Answers:** Always reproduce (or paraphrase) the decision-tree logic in a paragraph after computing the metrics. KTU examiners award 1–2 marks for the **"comment"** or **"interpret"** sub-part.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern, 2024 Scheme]**

### **Q1. Distinguish between Design Capacity, Effective Capacity, and Actual Output. Why are all three necessary for effective capacity management? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer (Board Key):**

- **Design Capacity (DC):** The maximum output rate an operation is *engineered* to produce under *ideal* conditions. It is the *nameplate* capacity stated during plant design and rarely changes.
- **Effective Capacity (EC):** The maximum output the operation can realistically achieve after accounting for *planned* losses such as preventive maintenance, setup/changeover time, employee breaks, and product-mix limitations. $EC \le DC$.
- **Actual Output (AO):** The output actually produced in a given period after *unplanned* losses (breakdowns, absenteeism, quality rejection, material shortages) are also absorbed. $AO \le EC \le DC$.

**Why all three are needed:** Design Capacity anchors long-term investment planning. Effective Capacity provides the *realistic ceiling* for scheduling and budgeting. Actual Output measures *realized* performance and exposes operational gaps. Together they allow managers to compute **Utilization** (asset deployment) and **Efficiency** (process excellence) and to choose the right corrective action — expand capacity, market more, or fix the process.

---

### **Q2. A plant has Design Capacity = 800 units/day, Effective Capacity = 640 units/day, and Actual Output = 512 units/day. Calculate Utilization, Efficiency, and the Design-Effectiveness Ratio. What does it indicate? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Utilization:**

$$U = \frac{AO}{DC} \times 100\% = \frac{512}{800} \times 100\% = 64\%$$

**Step 2 — Efficiency:**

$$E = \frac{AO}{EC} \times 100\% = \frac{512}{640} \times 100\% = 80\%$$

**Step 3 — Design-Effectiveness Ratio:**

$$DER = \frac{EC}{DC} \times 100\% = \frac{640}{800} \times 100\% = 80\%$$

**Indication:** $U$ is moderately low (64%) while $E$ is acceptable (80%). The plant is operationally *okay* but is **under-utilized at the strategic level** — likely a *demand-side* problem. The manager should focus on **sales and marketing**, not process improvement.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** Many students write "Efficiency = 64%" confusing it with utilization. The two are *distinct* — the examiner deducts 1 mark for this swap.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — End Semester Pattern, 2024 Scheme]**

### **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks**

**Q.A.** A medium-scale electronics assembly unit has a Design Capacity of **480 units/day**. The plant operates **300 days per year** in **2 shifts**. Due to planned maintenance, employee breaks, and product-mix changeovers, the Effective Capacity is **75% of Design Capacity**. During the year, the firm actually produced **1,02,000 good units**. A quality audit revealed that **8% of the effective output was rejected** and had to be reworked.

**(a)** Compute the **Effective Capacity (in units/year)**, the **Actual Output (in units/year)**, and the **number of rejected units per year**. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Calculate the **Utilization** and **Efficiency** of the plant and provide a **diagnostic interpretation** recommending the operations manager's priority action. (7 Marks)

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A, Part (a) [7 Marks]**

**Given Data Extraction:**
- Design Capacity: $DC = 480$ units/day
- Operating schedule: 2 shifts, 300 days/year (no extra info needed beyond 300 days for annualization)
- Effective Capacity ratio: $EC = 75\% \text{ of } DC$
- Actual good production: $1{,}02{,}000$ units/year
- Rejection rate: 8% of effective output

**Step 1 — Annualize the Design Capacity:**

$$DC_{\text{annual}} = 480 \;\text{units/day} \times 300 \;\text{days/year}$$

$$DC_{\text{annual}} = 1{,}44{,}000 \;\text{units/year}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Effective Capacity (annual):**

$$EC_{\text{annual}} = 0.75 \times DC_{\text{annual}}$$

$$EC_{\text{annual}} = 0.75 \times 1{,}44{,}000$$

$$EC_{\text{annual}} = 1{,}08{,}000 \;\text{units/year}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the rejected units per year:**

$$\text{Rejected} = 8\% \times EC_{\text{annual}} = 0.08 \times 1{,}08{,}000 = 8{,}640 \;\text{units/year}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Actual Output (total produced before rejection):**

$$AO_{\text{annual}} = \text{Good output} + \text{Rejected} = 1{,}02{,}000 + 8{,}640$$

$$AO_{\text{annual}} = 1{,}10{,}640 \;\text{units/year}$$

**Answer Summary (boxed for exam script):**
- Effective Capacity = **1,08,000 units/year**
- Rejected units = **8,640 units/year**
- Actual Output = **1,10,640 units/year**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A, Part (b) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Utilization:**

$$U = \frac{AO_{\text{annual}}}{DC_{\text{annual}}} \times 100\%$$

$$U = \frac{1{,}10{,}640}{1{,}44{,}000} \times 100\%$$

$$U = 0.7683 \times 100\% \approx 76.83\%$$

**Step 2 — Efficiency:**

$$E = \frac{AO_{\text{annual}}}{EC_{\text{annual}}} \times 100\%$$

$$E = \frac{1{,}10{,}640}{1{,}08{,}000} \times 100\%$$

$$E = 1.0244 \times 100\% \approx 102.44\%$$

**Step 3 — Diagnostic Interpretation:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - $E > 100\%$ indicates the plant is **operating beyond its planned/realistic capacity** (likely through overtime, deferred maintenance, or pushing workers).
> - $U \approx 76.83\%$ indicates the *installed* infrastructure is not being saturated.
> - The **8% rejection** confirms a quality problem is being masked by the overload.
>
> **Priority Action:** The operations manager must **expand Design Capacity** (add a shift, install a parallel line) and **address the quality issue** (root-cause analysis on the 8% rejection). Pushing the current process harder is unsustainable and will cause breakdown/fatigue.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Alternative) — 14 Marks**

**Q.B.** A pharmaceutical packaging line has a **Design Capacity of 200 bottles/minute**. The line operates **8 hours/day for 250 days/year**. The line is currently achieving a **Utilization of 70%** and an **Efficiency of 87.5%**.

**(a)** Determine the **Effective Capacity (in bottles/year)** and the **Actual Output (in bottles/year)**. (7 Marks)

**(b)** The management wants to raise the Effective Capacity to **95% of Design Capacity** by reducing changeover time through SMED (Single-Minute Exchange of Die) techniques. After the SMED intervention, the actual output rises to **25,000,000 bottles/year**. Recalculate the new **Utilization** and **Efficiency**, and state whether the intervention is justified. (7 Marks)

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B, Part (a) [7 Marks]**

**Given Data:**
- $DC = 200$ bottles/min
- Operating time: 8 hr/day × 250 days/year = 2,000 hr/year = 1,20,000 minutes/year
- $U = 70\%$
- $E = 87.5\%$

**Step 1 — Annualize the Design Capacity:**

$$DC_{\text{annual}} = 200 \;\text{bottles/min} \times 1{,}20{,}000 \;\text{min/year}$$

$$DC_{\text{annual}} = 2{,}40{,}00{,}000 \;\text{bottles/year} = 2.4 \;\text{crore bottles/year}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Actual Output from Utilization:**

$$AO_{\text{annual}} = U \times DC_{\text{annual}}$$

$$AO_{\text{annual}} = 0.70 \times 2.4 \;\text{crore}$$

$$AO_{\text{annual}} = 1.68 \;\text{crore} = 1{,}68{,}00{,}000 \;\text{bottles/year}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Effective Capacity from Efficiency:**

$$EC_{\text{annual}} = \frac{AO_{\text{annual}}}{E} = \frac{1.68 \;\text{crore}}{0.875}$$

$$EC_{\text{annual}} = 1.92 \;\text{crore} = 1{,}92{,}00{,}000 \;\text{bottles/year}$$

**Verification using Design-Effectiveness Ratio:**

$$DER = \frac{EC}{DC} = \frac{1.92}{2.40} = 0.80 = 80\%$$

This is the *implied* design-effectiveness ratio, consistent with the given $U$ and $E$ (since $U = E \times DER = 0.875 \times 0.80 = 0.70$). ✓

**Answer Summary:**
- Effective Capacity = **1,92,00,000 bottles/year**
- Actual Output = **1,68,00,000 bottles/year**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B, Part (b) [7 Marks]**

**Given Data (Post-Intervention):**
- New $EC_{\text{new}} = 95\% \text{ of } DC = 0.95 \times 2.4 \;\text{crore} = 2.28 \;\text{crore}$ bottles/year
- New $AO_{\text{new}} = 2.5 \;\text{crore}$ bottles/year
- $DC_{\text{annual}}$ remains 2.4 crore bottles/year

**Step 1 — New Utilization:**

$$U_{\text{new}} = \frac{AO_{\text{new}}}{DC_{\text{annual}}} \times 100\%$$

$$U_{\text{new}} = \frac{2.5 \;\text{crore}}{2.4 \;\text{crore}} \times 100\%$$

$$U_{\text{new}} = 1.0417 \times 100\% \approx 104.17\%$$

**Step 2 — New Efficiency:**

$$E_{\text{new}} = \frac{AO_{\text{new}}}{EC_{\text{new}}} \times 100\%$$

$$E_{\text{new}} = \frac{2.5 \;\text{crore}}{2.28 \;\text{crore}} \times 100\%$$

$$E_{\text{new}} = 1.0965 \times 100\% \approx 109.65\%$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation & Justification:**

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Observation:** Even after the SMED intervention, $E_{\text{new}} > 100\%$ and $U_{\text{new}} > 100\%$. This means the line is now **running beyond its full installed capacity**, which is physically impossible for sustained periods — it implies the line is being run on **overtime, with deferred maintenance, and at the cost of quality**.
>
> **Conclusion:** The SMED intervention is **NOT fully justified** in its current form. While the actual output did rise, the plant is now over-stretched. Management should either:
> 1. **Add a parallel line** to raise Design Capacity, **OR**
> 2. **Cap the actual output** at the sustainable effective capacity (2.28 crore) and redirect SMED gains to **reducing unit cost** rather than chasing volume.
>
> Justification: Volume growth at $E > 100\%$ is **unsustainable** and will cause breakdowns, rejection spikes, and labor fatigue within 6–12 months.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Crucial for Top Marks):**
> 1. **Unit consistency:** When $DC$ is in bottles/min and operating time is in hours, *always* convert minutes to hours (or vice versa) before multiplying. Mixing units is the #1 mark-loss reason.
> 2. **Effective Capacity is NOT Design Capacity × Utilization.** It is **$AO/E$** or given as a percentage of $DC$. Students who write $EC = DC \times U$ lose 2–3 marks per occurrence.
> 3. **Quoting Utilization/Efficiency above 100% without a comment** is treated as a logical error by examiners. Always *interpret* the value.
> 4. **In multi-shift problems**, *never* double-count shifts in the utilization formula. The shift count is already embedded in the annualized $DC$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Print and Pin to Wall**

- **Three Tiers:** $AO \le EC \le DC$. Memorize the strict inequality.
- **Two Measures:** Utilization (vs. $DC$) and Efficiency (vs. $EC$). *Never* swap the denominators.
- **Master Formulas:**
  - $U = AO/DC \times 100\%$
  - $E = AO/EC \times 100\%$
  - $EC = AO/E$ and $DC = AO/U$
  - $U = E \times DER / 100$ (cross-check formula)
- **Diagnostic Rule of Thumb:**
  - $U$ low, $E$ high → **Demand problem** (market it).
  - $U$ high, $E$ low → **Process problem** (fix it).
  - $U$ high, $E > 100\%$ → **Overload** (expand $DC$).
- **Influencing Factors:**
  - **Internal (Controllable):** Facility, Product Mix, Human, Operations, Supply Chain — mnemonic **"F-P-H-O-S"**.
  - **External (Uncontrollable):** Government, Union, Market, Competitor, Technology — mnemonic **"G-U-M-C-T"**.
- **Annualization:** $DC_{\text{annual}} = DC_{\text{daily}} \times \text{working days/year}$. Convert all time units to a common base (minutes or hours) before any computation.
- **Quality Rejection:** If rejection is given as a percentage, compute the rejected units first, then add back to good output to get the *true* Actual Output.
- **Cross-Verification:** Always cross-check your $U$, $E$, and $DER$ using the cross-relation. It catches arithmetic mistakes that cost 1–2 marks each.
- **Interpretation is Mandatory:** Every capacity calculation in a 14-mark question must end with a *diagnostic sentence* — KTU examiners award 1–2 marks specifically for managerial interpretation.
- **Sustainable Operating Range:** Healthy plants operate at $U \approx 75\text{–}90\%$ and $E \approx 85\text{–}95\%$. Anything outside this band demands management attention.
- **Design Capacity is Strategic, Effective Capacity is Tactical, Actual Output is Operational.** Use this hierarchy to structure essay-type answers for full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Long-Term vs Short-Term Capacity Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET723 - PRODUCTION AND OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Long-Term vs Short-Term Capacity Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Capacity Planning** is the systematic process of determining the production capacity an organization needs to meet the evolving demands for its products or services. It involves assessing current capacity, forecasting future capacity requirements, and identifying the optimal strategies to bridge any gap between them.

In the KTU 2024 OBE framework, capacity planning is classified along two fundamental horizons:

- **Long-Term Capacity Planning (Strategic)**: Decisions spanning **2 to 10 years** (and beyond), involving major capital investments in facilities, plants, equipment, technology, and workforce expansion.
- **Short-Term Capacity Planning (Operational/Tactical)**: Decisions spanning **less than 12 months**, focusing on workforce scheduling, overtime, inventory buffers, subcontracting, and minor adjustments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight**: Capacity is generally measured in **units of output per unit of time** (e.g., cars/month, patients/day, MW/hour). The three hierarchical measures of capacity are **Design Capacity**, **Effective Capacity**, and **Actual Output**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Highway Lanes Problem

Imagine you are the administrator of a busy **highway corridor**.

- **Long-Term Planning** is your decision to widen the highway from 4 lanes to 8 lanes. This involves massive capital (₹500 crores), 3 years of construction, environmental clearances, and shifts in traffic flow patterns.
- **Short-Term Planning** is your decision on a Friday evening to deploy **traffic police**, activate the **shoulder lane** during peak hours, set up **dynamic tolling**, or **reroute** heavy vehicles. These tweaks optimize the existing 4 lanes within hours.

> [!NOTE]
> **Student Intuition Box**: The *highway is your factory*, the *lanes are machines*, and the *traffic is demand*. When demand grows but capacity is fixed (long-term constraint), you cannot solve it with short-term tweaks indefinitely — eventually you MUST widen the highway.

### 1.3 Design Capacity vs Effective Capacity vs Actual Output

These three metrics form the foundation of all capacity arithmetic in KTU examinations:

$$\text{Design Capacity (DC)} = \text{Theoretical maximum output under ideal conditions}$$

$$\text{Effective Capacity (EC)} = \text{DC} - \text{Planned/Expected Losses (maintenance, breaks)}$$

$$\text{Actual Output (AO)} = \text{EC} - \text{Unplanned Losses (breakdowns, absenteeism)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb**: Always $\text{AO} \leq \text{EC} \leq \text{DC}$. The gap between EC and DC is called **Capacity Cushion**, and the gap between AO and EC is called **Efficiency Loss**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structured Logic Breakdown

**Step 1 — Demand Forecasting**: The firm estimates future demand using time-series, regression, or judgmental methods.

**Step 2 — Capacity Gap Analysis**:
   - If $\text{Demand} > \text{EC}$ → Under-capacity (lost sales risk)
   - If $\text{Demand} < \text{EC}$ → Over-capacity (idle cost burden)

**Step 3 — Strategic Decision (Long-Term)**:
   - Build new facility, buy new machines, enter new geographies
   - **Why?** To fundamentally raise the *Design Capacity ceiling*
   - **Time horizon**: 2–10 years; **Cost**: Very high (capital expenditure)
   - **Reversibility**: Low (sunk cost nature)

**Step 4 — Tactical Decision (Short-Term)**:
   - Overtime, subcontracting, hiring temporary workers, inventory buildup
   - **Why?** To bring *Actual Output* closer to *Effective Capacity* without changing infrastructure
   - **Time horizon**: Days to 12 months; **Cost**: Moderate
   - **Reversibility**: High (can be undone quickly)

**Step 5 — Monitoring & Control**: Track KPIs like utilization, efficiency, and capacity cushion.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula** | **Symbol** | **Expression** | **Boundary / Range** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Capacity Utilization | $\text{CU}$ | $\text{CU} = \dfrac{\text{Actual Output}}{\text{Design Capacity}} \times 100$ | $0 \leq \text{CU} \leq 100$ | % |
| Efficiency | $\eta$ | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Actual Output}}{\text{Effective Capacity}} \times 100$ | $0 \leq \eta \leq 100$ | % |
| Capacity Cushion | $\text{CC}$ | $\text{CC} = 100\% - \text{Utilization}$ | $0 \leq \text{CC} \leq 100$ | % |
| Effective Capacity | $\text{EC}$ | $\text{EC} = \text{DC} \times (1 - \text{Planned Downtime \%})$ | $\text{EC} \leq \text{DC}$ | Units/time |
| Break-Even Quantity (Capacity Expansion) | $Q^{\ast}$ | $Q^{\ast} = \dfrac{F}{P - V}$ | $F$ = fixed cost, $P$ = price, $V$ = variable cost | Units |
| Optimum Capacity (Economies of Scale) | $Q_{\text{opt}}$ | $Q_{\text{opt}}$ at minimum of $\text{AC}(Q)$ | $\text{Min} \rightarrow \dfrac{d(\text{AC})}{dQ} = 0$ | Units |
| Forecasted Capacity Need | $\text{FCN}$ | $\text{FCN} = \text{Current Output} \times (1 + g)^n$ | $g$ = growth rate, $n$ = years | Units |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reminder**: KTU examiners frequently test the difference between *Utilization* and *Efficiency*. **Utilization** uses Design Capacity in the denominator; **Efficiency** uses Effective Capacity. Confusing these two leads to a full 7-mark penalty in Part B questions.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In manufacturing industries (e.g., Kerala's spice processing units, Cochin Shipyard, or Infosys campuses), long-term capacity planning governs decisions like building a new assembly hall costing ₹200 crores, while short-term planning dictates whether to run a second shift during Onam-season demand spikes. In service operations (hospitals, airlines, IT helpdesks), short-term capacity adjustments like dynamic pricing, appointment reallocation, and queue prioritization are daily realities.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Break-Even for Long-Term Capacity Expansion

**Scenario**: A manufacturing firm is considering a ₹50,00,000 investment in new machinery. The new machines will produce a high-demand product sold at ₹500 per unit, with a variable cost of ₹300 per unit. Determine the break-even quantity that justifies the expansion.

**Step 1 — Identify the cost parameters**:
- Fixed Cost of expansion: $F = 50,00,000$
- Price per unit: $P = 500$
- Variable cost per unit: $V = 300$

**Step 2 — Compute contribution margin per unit**:
$$\text{Contribution Margin} = P - V = 500 - 300 = 200 \text{ per unit}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the break-even formula for capacity investment**:
$$Q^{\ast} = \frac{F}{P - V}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute values**:
$$Q^{\ast} = \frac{50,00,000}{200}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate**:
$$Q^{\ast} = 25,000 \text{ units per year}$$

**Interpretation**: The firm must sell at least **25,000 units annually** to recover the ₹50 lakh capital outlay. If forecasted demand exceeds 25,000 units, the long-term capacity expansion is economically justified.

### 3.2 Derivation: Capacity Gap with Compound Growth

**Scenario**: Current output of a plant is **10,000 units/year**. Demand is expected to grow at **12% per annum** for the next 4 years. Current Effective Capacity is **11,000 units/year**. Should the firm invest in long-term expansion, or can short-term measures suffice?

**Step 1 — Compute forecasted demand at year 4**:
$$\text{FCN} = \text{Current Output} \times (1 + g)^n$$

$$\text{FCN} = 10,000 \times (1 + 0.12)^4$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate $(1.12)^4$**:
$$(1.12)^2 = 1.2544$$
$$(1.12)^4 = (1.2544)^2 = 1.5735$$

**Step 3 — Compute final forecast**:
$$\text{FCN} = 10,000 \times 1.5735 = 15,735 \text{ units/year}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Capacity Gap**:
$$\text{Gap} = \text{FCN} - \text{EC} = 15,735 - 11,000 = 4,735 \text{ units/year}$$

**Step 5 — Decision Logic**:
- A **4,735 unit gap** (≈43% of EC) is too large to plug via overtime, subcontracting, or temporary hires — these typically cover ≤10–15% surges.
- **Decision**: A **long-term capacity expansion** is required (e.g., add a new production line costing ₹1.2 crores).

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Long-Term vs Short-Term Capacity Planning

| **Dimension** | **Long-Term Capacity Planning** | **Short-Term Capacity Planning** |
|---|---|---|
| **Time Horizon** | 2 – 10+ years | < 12 months |
| **Decision Level** | Strategic (Top Management) | Tactical/Operational (Plant Managers) |
| **Cost Type** | Capital Expenditure (CapEx) | Operating Expenditure (OpEx) |
| **Investment Size** | Very High (₹ crores) | Low to Moderate |
| **Reversibility** | Low (sunk costs) | High (can be undone) |
| **Key Levers** | New plants, machinery, technology, M&A | Overtime, hiring/firing, subcontracting, inventory |
| **Risk Profile** | High (forecasting error is amplified) | Low (adjustments are reversible) |
| **Forecast Method** | Strategic forecasting (scenario, regression) | Short-range forecasting (moving averages) |
| **Typical KPIs** | Capacity utilization (3-yr rolling), ROI | Daily throughput, OEE, on-time delivery |
| **KTU Example** | Setting up a new bottling plant in Palakkad | Adding a night shift during festival season |

### 3.4 Step-by-Step: Capacity Cushion Calculation

**Scenario**: A hospital's Design Capacity is **60 patients/day**. Effective Capacity (after planned downtime) is **48 patients/day**. Actual patients served yesterday was **42 patients/day**.

**Step 1 — Calculate Capacity Utilization**:
$$\text{CU} = \frac{42}{60} \times 100 = 70\%$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Efficiency**:
$$\eta = \frac{42}{48} \times 100 = 87.5\%$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Capacity Cushion**:
$$\text{CC} = 100\% - 70\% = 30\%$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation**:
- The hospital operates at **70% of its theoretical maximum** but achieves **87.5% of its effective potential**.
- A **30% cushion** means the hospital can absorb a moderate demand spike (up to 18 additional patients) without infrastructure change.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — The Capacity Planning Decision Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["DEMAND FORECAST"] --> nodeB{"GAP ANALYSIS: Demand vs EC"}
    nodeB -- "Gap 10%" --> nodeC["SHORT-TERM LEVERS"]
    nodeB -- "Gap > 25%" --> nodeD["LONG-TERM EXPANSION"]
    nodeC --> nodeC1["Overtime"]
    nodeC --> nodeC2["Subcontracting"]
    nodeC --> nodeC3["Temp Staffing"]
    nodeC --> nodeC4["Inventory Buildup"]
    nodeD --> nodeD1["New Plant"]
    nodeD --> nodeD2["New Machinery"]
    nodeD --> nodeD3["M and A"]
    nodeD --> nodeD4["New Technology"]
    nodeC1 --> nodeE["MONITOR KPIs"]
    nodeC2 --> nodeE
    nodeC3 --> nodeE
    nodeC4 --> nodeE
    nodeD1 --> nodeE
    nodeD2 --> nodeE
    nodeD3 --> nodeE
    nodeD4 --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF{"WITHIN TOLERANCE?"}
    nodeF -- "YES" --> nodeG["MAINTAIN"]
    nodeF -- "NO" --> nodeA
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Capacity Hierarchy (Design → Effective → Actual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeDC["DESIGN CAPACITY\nTheoretical Maximum"]
    nodeEC["EFFECTIVE CAPACITY\nDesign minus Planned Losses"]
    nodeAO["ACTUAL OUTPUT\nEffective minus Unplanned Losses"]
    nodeDC -- "minus PLANNED DOWNTIME" --> nodeEC
    nodeEC -- "minus UNPLANNED LOSSES" --> nodeAO
    nodeAO -. "EFFICIENCY RATIO" .-> nodeEC
    nodeAO -. "UTILIZATION RATIO" .-> nodeDC
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block-Level Architecture — Long-Term vs Short-Term Drivers

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LT["LONG-TERM CAPACITY DOMAIN"]
        ltA["Facility Expansion"]
        ltB["Equipment Purchase"]
        ltC["Technology Adoption"]
        ltD["Geographic Diversification"]
    end
    subgraph ST["SHORT-TERM CAPACITY DOMAIN"]
        stA["Overtime Policy"]
        stB["Subcontracting"]
        stC["Shift Adjustment"]
        stD["Inventory Buffers"]
        stE["Demand Shaping via Pricing"]
    end
    OUT["MEET CUSTOMER DEMAND AT OPTIMAL COST"] --- LT
    OUT --- ST
    LT -. "HIGH CAPEX, LOW REVERSIBILITY" .- OUT
    ST -. "LOW OPEX, HIGH REVERSIBILITY" .- OUT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation**: The three schematics above together form the cognitive scaffolding for the KTU Module 3 outcomes. Students should be able to draw (from memory) the hierarchy in Diagram 2 and the decision tree in Diagram 1 during the 2-hour ESE.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**  
**Differentiate between Design Capacity and Effective Capacity. Why is the gap between them important for a Production Manager?**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:  
*Design Capacity* is the theoretical maximum output of a system under ideal conditions, while *Effective Capacity* is the output expected considering planned losses such as maintenance, breaks, and set-up time. The gap between them is called the **capacity cushion**, which provides a buffer against demand variability and unexpected disruptions. For a Production Manager, this cushion is critical to maintain service levels without overburdening resources.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**  
**List any THREE short-term techniques used to manage capacity fluctuations in a manufacturing firm.**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:  
(i) **Overtime / Undertime** — adjusting working hours. (ii) **Subcontracting** — outsourcing part of production to third-party vendors. (iii) **Hiring temporary workers** — flexible labor pool to meet seasonal peaks. *(Other acceptable answers: inventory buildup, backordering, pricing/promotion adjustments, shift restructuring.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 — Adapted]**  
*(a)* Explain the concept of **Long-Term Capacity Planning**. Discuss its key characteristics, decision variables, and the strategic tools used by firms to evaluate long-term capacity alternatives. **(7 marks)**  
*(b)* A pharmaceutical firm in Kerala is evaluating a proposal to set up a new drug formulation unit. The estimated fixed cost of the project is ₹2 crores. The expected selling price is ₹120 per unit, and the variable cost is ₹70 per unit.  
    (i) Compute the **break-even quantity**.  
    (ii) If the forecasted annual demand is **60,000 units**, advise the firm on whether the investment is justified. State any TWO assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks**:  
**Definition (2 marks)**: Long-term capacity planning refers to strategic decisions made over a horizon of 2–10 years to ensure the firm has sufficient infrastructure, equipment, and workforce to meet future demand.  
**Key Characteristics (2 marks)**: (i) Capital-intensive; (ii) Low reversibility (sunk costs); (iii) Affects competitive positioning; (iv) Driven by long-range demand forecasts.  
**Decision Variables (1.5 marks)**: Facility size, location, technology choice, workforce strength.  
**Strategic Tools (1.5 marks)**: Break-even analysis, Net Present Value (NPV), Decision Tree Analysis, Waiting-Line Analysis, Simulation.

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks**:  
**(i) Break-Even Calculation (3 marks)**:  
$Q^{\ast} = \dfrac{F}{P - V} = \dfrac{2,00,00,000}{120 - 70} = \dfrac{2,00,00,000}{50} = 4,00,000 \text{ units/year}$

**(ii) Decision (4 marks)**:  
- Forecasted demand = **60,000 units/year**, which is **far below** the break-even quantity of **4,00,000 units/year**.
- **Conclusion**: The investment is **NOT economically justified** under the given assumptions.
- **Assumptions stated (2 marks)**: (1) Selling price and variable cost remain constant over the project's life. (2) All output produced is sold (no inventory buildup). *(Accept also: stable demand, no inflation, linear cost behavior.)*

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — Adapted]**  
*(a)* Compare and contrast **Long-Term and Short-Term Capacity Planning** across any FIVE dimensions. Which one is more critical for an organization during a recession? Justify. **(7 marks)**  
*(b)* A textile unit has a **Design Capacity of 50,000 units/month** and operates with **15% planned downtime** for maintenance. Last month, due to power cuts and absenteeism, the unit produced only **38,000 units**.  
    (i) Calculate the **Effective Capacity**.  
    (ii) Compute the **Efficiency** and **Utilization** of the unit.  
    (iii) If the management targets a **capacity cushion of 25%**, what should be the new design capacity? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks**:  
*Five dimensions (5 marks, 1 mark each)*:

| **Dimension** | **Long-Term** | **Short-Term** |
|---|---|---|
| Time horizon | 2–10+ years | < 12 months |
| Cost | CapEx (very high) | OpEx (low to moderate) |
| Reversibility | Low (sunk) | High (flexible) |
| Decision level | Strategic (top mgmt) | Operational (plant mgmt) |
| Examples | New plant, machinery | Overtime, subcontracting |

**Recession Insight (2 marks)**: During a recession, demand contracts sharply. **Short-term capacity planning** becomes more critical because firms must quickly reduce costs by furloughing workers, cutting overtime, and liquidating inventory — none of which require long-term infrastructure changes. Long-term commitments made during the boom can become sunk-cost burdens.

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks**:  
**(i) Effective Capacity (2 marks)**:  
$\text{EC} = \text{DC} \times (1 - \text{Planned Downtime \%}) = 50,000 \times (1 - 0.15) = 42,500 \text{ units/month}$

**(ii) Efficiency and Utilization (3 marks)**:  
$\eta = \dfrac{38,000}{42,500} \times 100 = 89.41\%$  
$\text{CU} = \dfrac{38,000}{50,000} \times 100 = 76\%$

**(iii) New Design Capacity (2 marks)**:  
Target Cushion = 25% ⇒ Target Utilization = 75% ⇒  
$\text{Required DC} = \dfrac{\text{Actual Output}}{0.75} = \dfrac{38,000}{0.75} = 50,666.67 \approx 50,667 \text{ units/month}$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert**:
> - **Mistake 1**: Using Design Capacity instead of Effective Capacity in the *Efficiency* denominator — costs 2 marks.
> - **Mistake 2**: Confusing the question "capacity cushion of 25%" as an *addition* to current capacity. It means utilization must drop to 75% of DC.
> - **Mistake 3**: In Part (a) of Question A, students often write about *forecasting methods* instead of *strategic tools for evaluating alternatives* — this is a common 2-mark deduction.
> - **Mistake 4**: Skipping the assumption statement in break-even problems — examiners explicitly allocate 1–2 marks for stating assumptions.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Capacity** is the maximum output rate of a system, measured in **units per time**.
- **Design Capacity** = Theoretical maximum; **Effective Capacity** = Design minus *planned* losses; **Actual Output** = Effective minus *unplanned* losses.
- **Utilization** uses Design Capacity in the denominator; **Efficiency** uses Effective Capacity.
- **Capacity Cushion** = 100% − Utilization; it is the safety margin the firm maintains.
- **Long-Term Planning** = Strategic, high CapEx, low reversibility, 2–10+ years, requires NPV / break-even / decision-tree analysis.
- **Short-Term Planning** = Tactical, low OpEx, high reversibility, < 12 months, uses overtime, subcontracting, temporary labor, and inventory levers.
- The **Break-Even Quantity** for capacity expansion is $Q^{\ast} = \dfrac{F}{P - V}$; expansion is justified only if forecasted demand exceeds $Q^{\ast}$.
- **Compound Growth Forecasting**: $\text{FCN} = \text{Current Output} \times (1 + g)^n$.
- During **demand surges ≤15%** → prefer short-term levers; **>25%** → plan long-term expansion.
- Always state **assumptions** in break-even and forecasting problems (constant prices, linear costs, demand certainty).
- KTU frequently tests the **Capacity Cushion concept**, so remember its strategic role as a buffer against variability.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Systematic Capacity Planning Approach
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET723 - PRODUCTION AND OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Systematic Capacity Planning Approach

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Systematic Capacity Planning Approach** is a structured, step-by-step managerial decision-making framework used to determine the long-range production capability (workforce, machines, facilities, and supporting infrastructure) required by an enterprise to meet **forecasted future demand** at an acceptable cost and service level.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, capacity refers to the **maximum output rate** that a system, facility, process, or worker can sustain over a specified time horizon under normal operating conditions. The *systematic* approach ensures decisions are not made ad-hoc but through a logical sequence of analytical, financial, and strategic evaluations.

### 1.2 Types of Capacity (KTU Mandatory Distinction)

| Capacity Type | Definition | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| **Design Capacity** | The absolute theoretical maximum output a system was engineered to produce under ideal conditions. | Highest |
| **Effective Capacity** | The realistic maximum output achievable given normal breakdowns, maintenance, and quality constraints. | Middle |
| **Actual Output** | The real output realized, often less than effective capacity due to absenteeism, delays, and quality rejects. | Lowest |

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Capacity > Effective Capacity > Actual Output** — This is the **golden hierarchy** every KTU examiner expects you to know.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Bucket-and-Tap Model

Imagine your kitchen **bucket** as your factory's capacity, and the **tap** as customer demand:

- **Design Capacity** = The total volume of the bucket (if no leaks, no splashes).
- **Effective Capacity** = The bucket minus inevitable losses (a small crack, splashes while pouring).
- **Actual Output** = What you finally managed to serve in the glass.
- **Capacity Planning** = Deciding whether to buy a bigger bucket, patch the cracks, work extra shifts, or hire a second tap-handler.

If demand exceeds the bucket, customers leave thirsty (lost sales). If the bucket is too big, water sits idle (wasted cost). Systematic capacity planning is the **art of right-sizing the bucket** in advance.

### 1.4 Importance in Production & Operations Management

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Capacity is the **upper ceiling** of any production system. All operational plans (scheduling, inventory, workforce, MRP, JIT) become meaningless if capacity is mismatched with demand.

Poor capacity decisions lead to:
- **Under-capacity** → stockouts, delayed deliveries, lost market share.
- **Over-capacity** → idle resources, sunk costs, low return on investment.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Eight-Step Systematic Capacity Planning Framework

The **systematic approach** (heavily tested in KTU ESE) consists of the following sequential stages:

1. **Estimate Future Capacity Requirements**
   - Use demand forecasting techniques (moving average, exponential smoothing, regression, qualitative Delphi method).
   - Convert forecasted *demand* into *capacity units* (machine-hours, labor-hours, units per shift).

2. **Identify Gaps / Need for Capacity Adjustment**
   - Compute the difference between forecasted demand and current effective capacity.
   - If gap > 0 → need expansion. If gap < 0 → consider contraction.

3. **Develop Capacity Alternatives**
   - **Expansion alternatives:** Add shifts, add machines, build new facility, outsource.
   - **Contraction alternatives:** Sell machines, downsize workforce, divest product lines.
   - **Do-nothing alternative:** Status quo (sometimes optimal if cushion is adequate).

4. **Conduct Financial / Economic Analysis**
   - Break-even analysis.
   - Cash flow estimation & Net Present Value (NPV).
   - Return on Investment (ROI).
   - Payback period.

5. **Assess Qualitative / Strategic Factors**
   - Workforce morale and skill availability.
   - Government regulations, environmental clearances.
   - Technological obsolescence risk.
   - Competitor reactions.

6. **Select the Best Alternative**
   - Use decision trees, scoring models, or weighted factor comparison.

7. **Implement the Chosen Alternative**
   - Project planning, procurement, installation, hiring, training.

8. **Monitor, Review & Control**
   - Track actual vs. planned performance.
   - Apply **Capacity Utilization Ratio** and **Efficiency Ratio** continuously.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

| Step | Core Logic |
|---|---|
| Forecasting | Capacity decisions are irreversible in the short run → must be data-driven. |
| Gap analysis | Quantifies the *magnitude* of mismatch before any commitment. |
| Alternatives | Prevents premature fixation on a single option (avoids *anchoring bias*). |
| Financial analysis | Capacity = Capital; investors and boards demand ROI justification. |
| Qualitative filter | Numbers alone ignore human, legal, and reputational dimensions. |
| Selection | Formal decision tools reduce emotional/political bias. |
| Implementation | A brilliant plan poorly executed yields zero capacity. |
| Monitoring | Feedback loop for adaptive control — the essence of **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act)**. |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Every formula below is **directly applicable** for numerical problems in KTU 2024 ESE.

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Units | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Utilization (U)** | $U = \dfrac{\text{Actual Output}}{\text{Design Capacity}} \times 100$ | % | Measures how much of design capacity is being used. |
| **Efficiency (E)** | $E = \dfrac{\text{Actual Output}}{\text{Effective Capacity}} \times 100$ | % | Measures performance relative to realistic ceiling. |
| **Capacity Cushion** | $\text{Cushion} = 100\% - U$ | % | Safety buffer kept idle to absorb demand surges. |
| **Break-Even Volume** | $Q_{BE} = \dfrac{\text{Fixed Cost}}{\text{Price} - \text{Variable Cost}}$ | units | Sales volume to recover all costs. |
| **Expected Monetary Value (EMV)** | $EMV = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \cdot V_i$ | currency | Average payoff across all scenarios. |
| **Expected Value of Perfect Information (EVPI)** | $EVPI = EVwPI - EMV_{best}$ | currency | Maximum worth of additional forecasting info. |
| **Present Value** | $PV = \dfrac{FV}{(1+r)^n}$ | currency | Future cash flow discounted to today. |
| **Net Present Value** | $NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}$ | currency | Profitability of a capacity investment. |
| **Capacity Requirement (CR)** | $CR = \dfrac{\text{Demand Forecast}}{\text{Effective Capacity per unit resource}}$ | machines/labourers | Resources needed to meet demand. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe-Symbol Rule (KTU Markdown):** Whenever you write absolute value `|x|` in your answer booklet, always use the **inline math mode** ($|x|$) — never inside a table cell where `|` will break markdown rendering.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

Systematic capacity planning is used by:
- **Automobile OEMs** deciding plant size for next-generation EVs.
- **Hospitals** planning ICU beds before a flu season.
- **IT firms** sizing data centres based on cloud demand projections.
- **Airlines** ordering aircraft 5–7 years in advance of demand.
- **Kerala Tourism (KTU context)** — houseboat fleet sizing for Onam/Christmas peaks.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Utilization, Efficiency & Cushion

**Problem:** A packaging machine has a **design capacity** of 200 units/hour. Due to maintenance and changeovers, its **effective capacity** is 160 units/hour. Last week it produced **144,000 units** over **60 working hours** of actual operation.

**Step 1 — Compute Actual Output Rate.**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Actual Output Rate} &= \frac{\text{Total Units Produced}}{\text{Total Hours Operated}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{144{,}000}{60} \\[6pt]
&= 2{,}400 \ \text{units/hour}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Utilization.**
$$\begin{aligned}
U &= \frac{\text{Actual Output Rate}}{\text{Design Capacity}} \times 100 \\[6pt]
&= \frac{2{,}400}{200} \times 100 \\[6pt]
&= 1{,}200\% \ \text{(??)}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This result reveals a **unit error** in the problem statement. The design capacity should be **2,400 units/hour** for the data to be internally consistent. We **correct** the design capacity and proceed with a consistent dataset:

**Corrected Data:** Design Capacity = 2,400 units/hour, Effective Capacity = 1,920 units/hour (say, with 20% allowance).

$$\begin{aligned}
U &= \frac{2{,}400}{2{,}400} \times 100 = 100\% \\[6pt]
E &= \frac{2{,}400}{1{,}920} \times 100 = 125\% \ \text{(Impossible!)}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> Efficiency **cannot exceed 100%** under the formal definition. This tells students to **cross-check** whether the effective capacity has been under-estimated or whether the manager is stretching the system beyond safe limits.

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Capacity Requirement Computation

**Problem:** ABC Manufacturing forecasts demand of **180,000 units** for next year. Each unit requires **2.5 machine-hours** on a CNC machine. The plant operates **250 days/year**, **2 shifts/day** of **8 hours each**. Each CNC machine provides an **effective capacity** of **90%** of available hours. How many CNC machines are required?

**Step 1 — Total machine-hours needed.**
$$\begin{aligned}
MH_{\text{needed}} &= \text{Demand} \times \text{Time per unit} \\[6pt]
&= 180{,}000 \times 2.5 \\[6pt]
&= 450{,}000 \ \text{machine-hours}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Effective machine-hours available per CNC per year.**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Hours per day per machine} &= 2 \times 8 = 16 \\[6pt]
\text{Working days per year} &= 250 \\[6pt]
\text{Raw hours per machine} &= 16 \times 250 = 4{,}000 \\[6pt]
\text{Effective hours} &= 4{,}000 \times 0.90 = 3{,}600 \ \text{hours}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Number of machines required.**
$$\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{MH_{\text{needed}}}{\text{Effective hours per machine}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{450{,}000}{3{,}600} \\[6pt]
&= 125 \ \text{machines}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Capacity Cushion.**
If management wants a **15% cushion**, total machines required:
$$\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{adjusted}} &= \frac{125}{1 - 0.15} = \frac{125}{0.85} \approx 147.06 \rightarrow 148 \ \text{machines}
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Decision Tree for Capacity Expansion

**Problem:** A firm must decide between a **Small Plant (₹50 lakh capex)** and a **Large Plant (₹90 lakh capex)**. Demand has a **60% probability of being High** (₹100 lakh annual profit) and **40% of being Low** (₹40 lakh profit). With Small Plant, profit if High = ₹60 lakh, if Low = ₹45 lakh. With Large Plant, profit if High = ₹110 lakh, if Low = ₹20 lakh. Recommend the best alternative using **EMV**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Decision trees are the **highest-frequency** KTU 14-mark question on capacity decisions.

**Step 1 — EMV of Small Plant.**
$$\begin{aligned}
EMV_{\text{Small}} &= (0.60 \times 60) + (0.40 \times 45) \\[6pt]
&= 36 + 18 = 54 \ \text{lakhs}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — EMV of Large Plant.**
$$\begin{aligned}
EMV_{\text{Large}} &= (0.60 \times 110) + (0.40 \times 20) \\[6pt]
&= 66 + 8 = 74 \ \text{lakhs}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compare.**
$$\begin{aligned}
EMV_{\text{Large}} - EMV_{\text{Small}} &= 74 - 54 = 20 \ \text{lakhs (favouring Large)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Recommendation:** Choose **Large Plant**.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Capacity Strategies

| Strategy | Lead Time | Cost | Reversibility | Risk | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Add a shift** | 1–3 months | Low | High | Low | Seasonal demand spikes |
| **Outsource** | 3–6 months | Variable | High | Medium | Capacity shortage < 12 months |
| **Acquire new machines** | 6–12 months | Medium | Medium | Medium | Sustained demand growth |
| **Build new facility** | 18–36 months | Very High | Very Low | High | Long-term strategic expansion |
| **Subcontract** | Immediate | Variable | High | Low | Specialized, low-volume items |
| **Do Nothing** | 0 | Zero | N/A | High (lost sales) | Demand expected to fall |

### 3.5 Worked Example 4 — Break-Even Analysis for Capacity Choice

**Problem:** Fixed Cost of Machine A = ₹10 lakh, Variable Cost = ₹200/unit. Machine B: Fixed = ₹25 lakh, Variable = ₹150/unit. Selling price = ₹500/unit. Find crossover output and recommend.

**Step 1 — Total cost equations.**
$$\begin{aligned}
TC_A &= 10{,}00{,}000 + 200Q \\[6pt]
TC_B &= 25{,}00{,}000 + 150Q
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Equate for crossover.**
$$\begin{aligned}
10{,}00{,}000 + 200Q &= 25{,}00{,}000 + 150Q \\[6pt]
50Q &= 15{,}00{,}000 \\[6pt]
Q^* &= 30{,}000 \ \text{units}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation.**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{If } Q < 30{,}000 &\rightarrow \text{Choose Machine A (lower fixed cost)} \\
\text{If } Q > 30{,}000 &\rightarrow \text{Choose Machine B (lower variable cost)}
\end{aligned}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Eight-Step Systematic Capacity Planning Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["step1: Estimate Demand Forecast"] --> B["step2: Convert Demand to Capacity Units"]
    B --> C["step3: Compute Capacity Gap"]
    C --> D["step4: Generate Capacity Alternatives"]
    D --> E["step5: Perform Financial Analysis"]
    E --> F["step6: Evaluate Qualitative Factors"]
    F --> G["step7: Select Best Alternative"]
    G --> H["step8: Implement and Monitor"]
    H --> A
```

### 4.2 Capacity Hierarchy Pyramid

```mermaid
graph TD
    L1["level1: Design Capacity 100 percent"]
    L2["level2: Effective Capacity 80 to 90 percent"]
    L3["level3: Actual Output 70 to 85 percent"]
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
```

### 4.3 Decision Tree for Build vs. Buy vs. Wait

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R0["root: Demand Forecast Received"]
    R0 --> N1["opt1: Build New Plant"]
    R0 --> N2["opt2: Outsource or Subcontract"]
    R0 --> N3["opt3: Status Quo or Wait"]
    N1 --> C1["case: High Demand"]
    N1 --> C2["case: Low Demand"]
    N2 --> C3["case: High Demand"]
    N2 --> C4["case: Low Demand"]
    N3 --> C5["case: High Demand Loss of Sales"]
    N3 --> C6["case: Low Demand Idle Capacity"]
```

### 4.4 PDCA Feedback Loop in Capacity Monitoring

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["plan: Set Capacity Targets"]
    D1["do: Operate Plant"]
    C1["check: Compare Actual vs Target"]
    A1["act: Corrective Action"]
    P1 --> D1
    D1 --> C1
    C1 --> A1
    A1 --> P1
```

### 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Capacity Planning System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT
        I1["Demand Forecast Module"]
        I2["Current Capacity Database"]
        I3["Cost and Financial Inputs"]
    end
    subgraph PROCESS
        PR1["Gap Analyzer"]
        PR2["Alternative Generator"]
        PR3["Financial Evaluator NPV IRR"]
        PR4["Decision Engine EMV EVPI"]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT
        O1["Recommended Capacity Plan"]
        O2["Implementation Roadmap"]
        O3["KPI Dashboard Utilization Efficiency Cushion"]
    end
    I1 --> PR1
    I2 --> PR1
    I1 --> PR2
    I3 --> PR3
    PR1 --> PR2
    PR2 --> PR3
    PR3 --> PR4
    PR4 --> O1
    O1 --> O2
    O2 --> O3
    O3 --> I2
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
Distinguish between **Design Capacity**, **Effective Capacity**, and **Actual Output**. Why is design capacity rarely achieved in practice?

**Model Answer:**
Design capacity is the absolute maximum output a system can produce under ideal conditions. Effective capacity is the realistic maximum considering allowances for maintenance, set-ups, and quality. Actual output is what is truly produced, often below effective capacity due to absenteeism, breakdowns, and inefficiencies. Design capacity is rarely achieved because of inherent system losses like equipment downtime, changeover times, labour absenteeism, and quality rejections. KTU expects a diagram or hierarchy table here.

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
What is a **capacity cushion**? Why do service organizations maintain larger cushions than manufacturing firms?

**Model Answer:**
