A capacity cushion is the reserve capacity kept idle to absorb unexpected surges in demand, computed as (100% − Utilization). Services maintain larger cushions because inventory cannot buffer demand variability — service output must be produced the instant it is consumed (e.g., hospital beds, bank tellers). Manufacturing can hold finished goods inventory to ride out demand spikes, hence a smaller cushion suffices.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — July 2024 Model]**
(a) Explain the **systematic approach to capacity planning** with a neat flowchart. Discuss any **five techniques** used for forecasting demand during capacity planning. **(7 marks)**
(b) A firm is evaluating two capacity alternatives, **Plant X (₹80 lakh capex)** and **Plant Y (₹1.20 crore capex)**, with the following payoff matrix. Use **decision tree analysis** to recommend the best option.

| Alternative | High Demand (Prob = 0.7) | Low Demand (Prob = 0.3) |
|---|---|---|
| Plant X | ₹1.5 crore profit | ₹0.6 crore profit |
| Plant Y | ₹2.0 crore profit | ₹0.3 crore profit |

Also compute **EVPI** if perfect information yields ₹2.0 crore in High demand and ₹0.7 crore in Low demand. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

Systematic capacity planning follows the **eight-step model**:
1. Estimate demand forecast
2. Convert to capacity units
3. Identify gaps
4. Generate alternatives
5. Financial analysis
6. Qualitative assessment
7. Selection
8. Implementation & monitoring

**Flowchart** (refer Section 4.1 of these notes).

Five forecasting techniques:
- Qualitative (Delphi, market research, executive opinion)
- Time-series (moving average, exponential smoothing, trend projection)
- Causal (regression, econometric models)
- Simulation
- AI/ML-based predictive models (mention only briefly for KTU 2024).

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — EMV of Plant X.**
$$\begin{aligned}
EMV_X &= (0.7 \times 1.5) + (0.3 \times 0.6) \\
&= 1.05 + 0.18 = 1.23 \ \text{crore}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — EMV of Plant Y.**
$$\begin{aligned}
EMV_Y &= (0.7 \times 2.0) + (0.3 \times 0.3) \\
&= 1.40 + 0.09 = 1.49 \ \text{crore}
\end{aligned}$$

**Recommendation:** Plant Y (₹1.49 crore > ₹1.23 crore).

**Step 3 — Expected Value with Perfect Information (EVwPI).**
$$\begin{aligned}
EVwPI &= (0.7 \times 2.0) + (0.3 \times 0.7) \\
&= 1.40 + 0.21 = 1.61 \ \text{crore}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — EVPI.**
$$\begin{aligned}
EVPI &= EVwPI - \max(EMV_X, EMV_Y) \\
&= 1.61 - 1.49 = 0.12 \ \text{crore} \ (\text{₹}12 \ \text{lakh})
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall — EVPI Common Mistake:** Many students wrongly compute EVPI as $EVwPI - EMV_{\text{worst}}$. KTU expects $EVwPI - EMV_{\text{best alternative}}$ (the opportunity loss saved by perfect information). **Deduct 2 marks** for this error.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
(a) Discuss the **factors affecting capacity planning**. Differentiate between **long-term, intermediate, and short-term capacity planning**. **(7 marks)**
(b) A bakery requires **12,000 loaves/week**. Each oven bakes **200 loaves/hour** at **85% efficiency**. The bakery operates **6 days/week, 10 hours/day**. Compute: (i) number of ovens required, (ii) utilization if 5 ovens are installed, (iii) recommended capacity cushion. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Factors affecting capacity planning:**
- Demand forecasting accuracy
- Capital availability
- Technology and equipment life-cycle
- Workforce skill and availability
- Government regulations and environmental norms
- Competitor actions
- Supply chain reliability

**Comparison of Capacity Planning Horizons:**

| Horizon | Time Frame | Decisions | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Long-term | 1–5 years | Plant size, location, technology | New factory in Kochi |
| Intermediate | 3–12 months | Workforce, sub-contracting, shifts | Adding a night shift |
| Short-term | Daily / weekly | Overtime, sequencing, scheduling | Adjusting machine speeds |

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Effective capacity of one oven.**
$$\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{eff, 1 oven}} &= 200 \times 0.85 = 170 \ \text{loaves/hour}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Total weekly hours available per oven.**
$$\begin{aligned}
H_{\text{weekly}} &= 6 \times 10 = 60 \ \text{hours}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Weekly capacity per oven.**
$$\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{1 oven}} &= 170 \times 60 = 10{,}200 \ \text{loaves/week}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 (i) — Ovens required.**
$$\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{12{,}000}{10{,}200} = 1.176 \rightarrow 2 \ \text{ovens}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 (ii) — Utilization if 5 ovens installed.**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Combined capacity} &= 5 \times 10{,}200 = 51{,}000 \\[6pt]
U &= \frac{12{,}000}{51{,}000} \times 100 \approx 23.53\%
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall — Ovens Required Mistake:** A frequent error is forgetting to apply the 85% efficiency factor. Always **state the efficiency explicitly** before plugging into the formula. KTU deducts 1 mark for each missing efficiency step.

**Step 6 (iii) — Capacity cushion with 2 ovens.**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Cushion} &= 100\% - U \\[6pt]
U &= \frac{12{,}000}{2 \times 10{,}200} \times 100 = 58.82\% \\[6pt]
\text{Cushion} &= 100 - 58.82 = 41.18\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Recommendation:** 41% cushion is excessively high for a bakery. A better strategy is to use **2 ovens plus scheduled overtime during festive peaks** — preserving a cushion of ~15%.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Marks-Loss Areas in Capacity Planning Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing Utilization with Efficiency** — they have *different denominators*. (–2 marks)
> 2. **Forgetting the cushion** in long-range capacity computations. (–1 mark)
> 3. **Wrong EVPI formula** — always subtract best EMV, not worst. (–2 marks)
> 4. **Skipping qualitative factors** in a 14-mark essay — examiners expect at least 3 qualitative points. (–2 marks)
> 5. **No flowchart** in systematic-approach questions — KTU 2024 explicitly awards marks for "neat diagram/flowchart." (–2 marks)

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Definition:** Systematic capacity planning = structured 8-step process from forecast → monitor.
- [ ] **Hierarchy:** Design > Effective > Actual (always in descending order).
- [ ] **Formulas:** Utilization, Efficiency, Cushion, EMV, EVPI, NPV, Break-Even — must be memorized verbatim.
- [ ] **Horizons:** Long-term (years), Intermediate (months), Short-term (days/weeks).
- [ ] **Techniques:** Forecasting, break-even, decision trees, cumulative sum charts, learning curves.
- [ ] **Cushion rule of thumb:** Manufacturing = 5–10%, Services = 15–25%.
- [ ] **Decision tree convention:** Squares = decisions, Circles = chance events, Triangles = outcomes.
- [ ] **EVPI** = maximum amount a firm should pay for perfect market research.
- [ ] Always **state assumptions** (efficiency %, working days, shifts) before any numerical computation.
- [ ] **Qualitative filter** is non-negotiable in 14-mark answers — covers legal, ethical, morale, environmental dimensions.
- [ ] **Implementation phase** must include training, trials, and ramp-up curves.
- [ ] **Monitor with KPIs:** Utilization %, Efficiency %, On-time delivery %, OEE.
- [ ] **KTU 2024 trend:** Expect **integrated questions** combining capacity planning + forecasting + financial analysis in a single 14-mark problem.
- [ ] **Always draw the flowchart** in 7-mark sub-parts — it carries **2 marks minimum**.
- [ ] Remember: **Capacity decisions are strategic, irreversible, and capital-intensive** — treat them as such in answers.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tools and Techniques for Capacity Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET723 - PRODUCTION AND OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Tools and Techniques for Capacity Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3: Tools and Techniques for Capacity Management

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Capacity Management?

**Capacity Management** is the strategic and operational process of determining, measuring, monitoring, and adjusting the production capacity of an organization to meet fluctuating demand for its products or services. It involves planning the right amount of resources — machines, labor, workspace, and materials — at the right time, to balance cost efficiency with customer satisfaction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Capacity management is the function of establishing, measuring, monitoring, and adjusting limits or levels of capacity in order to execute all manufacturing and service capacity-related strategies and tasks required to satisfy market demand, at the lowest possible cost.*

### 1.2 Types of Capacity (The Three-Tier Model)

Capacity is measured at three progressively realistic levels:

| Capacity Tier | Definition | Intuitive Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| **Design Capacity** | The maximum theoretical output a system can produce under ideal conditions (24x7, no breakdowns). | The top speed shown on a car's speedometer — never realistically achieved on roads. |
| **Effective Capacity** | The capacity a firm can realistically achieve given normal operating constraints (breaks, maintenance, holidays). | Your average highway cruising speed — limited by traffic and stops. |
| **Actual Output** | The real production achieved, often below effective capacity due to disruptions, absenteeism, quality issues. | Your actual door-to-door driving time including rain and diversions. |

### 1.3 Tools and Techniques — Overview

Capacity decisions require quantitative and qualitative tools to forecast, simulate, and optimize. The principal tools covered in KTU Module 3 are:

1. **Capacity Planning Process (7-step framework)**
2. **Capacity Measurement Metrics** (Utilization & Efficiency)
3. **Break-Even Analysis** (Linear Cost-Volume-Profit model)
4. **Decision Tree Analysis** (Sequential decision under uncertainty)
5. **Waiting Line / Queuing Analysis**
6. **Learning Curve Analysis** (Experience curve effect)
7. **Linear Programming** (Optimization of resource allocation)
8. **Simulation Models** (Monte Carlo, discrete-event)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen:**
> Imagine a restaurant kitchen. *Design capacity* is cooking 200 meals/hour if every burner runs non-stop. *Effective capacity* is 150 meals/hour accounting for prep time and cleaning. *Actual output* is 110 meals/hour because some orders are returned, chefs are late, or ingredients are missing. **Capacity management tools** are like the head chef's techniques to decide how many stoves to install, how many chefs to hire, and how to schedule shifts to serve the maximum number of customers without wasting money on idle stoves.

### 1.4 Importance in KTU 2024 Context

Capacity management directly influences:
- **Cost per unit** (over-capacity increases fixed costs per unit produced)
- **Customer satisfaction** (under-capacity causes delays and lost sales)
- **Capital investment decisions** (long-term capacity = plant & equipment)
- **Competitive positioning** in the market
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The 7-Step Capacity Planning Process (KTU High-Yield Framework)

Capacity planning is executed through a systematic sequence:

1. **Estimate Future Demand** — Use forecasting methods (moving average, exponential smoothing).
2. **Estimate Current & Future Capacity** — Audit existing resources; project additions.
3. **Identify Gaps (Surplus/Deficit)** — Compare forecast demand vs. available capacity.
4. **Develop Alternatives** — Expand, contract, shift work, outsource, improve productivity.
5. **Evaluate Alternatives Financially** — Use Break-Even Analysis and Decision Trees.
6. **Select the Best Alternative** — Choose based on cost, risk, and strategic fit.
7. **Monitor & Adjust** — Track actual vs. planned; revise periodically.

> [!TIP]
> **Why is this ordered this way?** Each step builds on the previous — you cannot identify gaps without first forecasting demand, and you cannot evaluate alternatives without first defining them.

### 2.2 Capacity Measurement Formulas (THE MOST TESTED TOPIC)

These three formulas are heavily repeated in KTU University Exams:

$$
\text{Utilization} = \frac{\text{Actual Output}}{\text{Design Capacity}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{Efficiency} = \frac{\text{Actual Output}}{\text{Effective Capacity}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{Effective Capacity} = \text{Design Capacity} \times \text{Efficiency}
$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Tool | Key Formula | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Utilization** | $U = \dfrac{A}{D} \times 100$ | Measures how much of design capacity is used |
| 2 | **Efficiency** | $E = \dfrac{A}{E_c} \times 100$ | Measures how close actual is to effective |
| 3 | **Break-Even Point (units)** | $Q_{BE} = \dfrac{F}{P - V}$ | Output where total revenue = total cost |
| 4 | **Break-Even Point (₹)** | $S_{BE} = \dfrac{F}{1 - \dfrac{V}{P}}$ | Sales value where profit = 0 |
| 5 | **Margin of Safety** | $MoS = \dfrac{\text{Actual Sales} - BE\ Sales}{\text{Actual Sales}} \times 100$ | Cushion above break-even |
| 6 | **Expected Monetary Value (EMV)** | $EMV = \sum (P_i \times V_i)$ | Weighted-average value of decision outcomes |
| 7 | **Learning Curve** | $Y_x = Y_1 \cdot x^{b}$ | Time/cost of $x^{th}$ unit where $b = \dfrac{\log(\text{learning rate})}{\log 2}$ |
| 8 | **Cumulative Average Learning** | $CAY_x = Y_1 \cdot \dfrac{\sum x^b}{x}$ | Average time per unit for first $x$ units |
| 9 | **Queuing — Average Utilization (ρ)** | $\rho = \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu}$ | Server utilization rate |
| 10 | **Queuing — Avg # in system (L)** | $L = \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu - \lambda}$ | M/M/1 system length |

Where:
- $A$ = Actual Output, $D$ = Design Capacity, $E_c$ = Effective Capacity
- $F$ = Fixed Cost, $P$ = Price/unit, $V$ = Variable Cost/unit
- $P_i$ = Probability of outcome $i$, $V_i$ = Value of outcome $i$
- $Y_1$ = Time for 1st unit, $x$ = unit number, $b$ = learning slope exponent
- $\lambda$ = arrival rate, $\mu$ = service rate

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Utilization-Efficiency Trade-off:** A machine running at 100% utilization has zero buffer for breakdowns, leading to inefficiencies — KTU tests this trade-off heavily.
- **Break-Even Analysis:** Used by managers to decide between two capacity expansion options (e.g., Build vs. Buy).
- **Decision Trees:** Used in equipment replacement decisions where future states are uncertain.
- **Learning Curves:** Aerospace and electronics industries use them to bid on large contracts accurately.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: Capacity Measurement (14-Mark Standard)

**Problem:** A factory has a Design Capacity of **2,000 units/day**. Due to maintenance schedules, its Effective Capacity is **85%** of Design Capacity. In a particular week, it actually produced **8,400 units** in 6 working days.

**Find:** (a) Utilization, (b) Efficiency, (c) Expected Output if Efficiency improves to 90%.

#### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Identify given values**

$$
D = 2000 \text{ units/day}, \quad E_c = 0.85 \times 2000 = 1700 \text{ units/day}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Actual Output per day**

$$
A = \frac{8400}{6} = 1400 \text{ units/day}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Utilization**

$$
U = \frac{A}{D} \times 100\% = \frac{1400}{2000} \times 100\% = 70\%
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate Efficiency**

$$
E = \frac{A}{E_c} \times 100\% = \frac{1400}{1700} \times 100\% = 82.35\%
$$

**Step 5 — Expected Output at 90% Efficiency**

$$
A_{new} = 0.90 \times 1700 = 1530 \text{ units/day}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Example: Break-Even Analysis

**Problem:** A firm sells a product at ₹500/unit. Variable cost = ₹300/unit. Fixed costs = ₹4,00,000. Find: (a) Break-even quantity, (b) Break-even sales revenue, (c) Profit at sales of 3,000 units.

#### Solution:

**Step 1 — Apply BE formula (units)**

$$
Q_{BE} = \frac{F}{P - V} = \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{500 - 300} = \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{200} = 2000 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply BE formula (₹)**

$$
S_{BE} = Q_{BE} \times P = 2000 \times 500 = \text{₹}10{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 3 — Profit at 3,000 units**

$$
\text{Profit} = (P - V) \times Q - F = (500 - 300)(3000) - 4{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
\text{Profit} = 200 \times 3000 - 4{,}00{,}000 = 6{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}2{,}00{,}000
$$

### 3.3 Worked Example: Decision Tree (EMV)

**Problem:** A company must decide whether to **Expand** (₹50,00,000 investment) or **Do Not Expand**. If it expands:
- High demand (prob 0.6) → Revenue ₹1,00,00,000
- Low demand (prob 0.4) → Revenue ₹60,00,000

If it does not expand, revenue stays at ₹40,00,000 (no investment).

**Find:** Optimal decision using EMV.

#### Solution:

**Step 1 — EMV of Expand branch**

$$
EMV_{expand} = (0.6 \times 1{,}00{,}00{,}000) + (0.4 \times 60{,}00{,}000) - 50{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
EMV_{expand} = 60{,}00{,}000 + 24{,}00{,}000 - 50{,}00{,}000 = 34{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — EMV of Do Not Expand**

$$
EMV_{no\_expand} = 40{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 3 — Compare and Decide**

Since $34{,}00{,}000 < 40{,}00{,}000$, the optimal decision is **DO NOT EXPAND**.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Tool

| Situation | Best Tool | KTU Exam Weightage |
|---|---|---|
| Compare utilization vs efficiency for a machine | Utilization & Efficiency formulas | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Very High |
| Compare two capacity expansion options financially | Break-Even Analysis | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Very High |
| Decide under demand uncertainty | Decision Tree / EMV | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ High |
| Reduce per-unit cost as volume grows | Learning Curve | ⭐⭐⭐ Moderate |
| Optimize resource mix across products | Linear Programming | ⭐⭐⭐ Moderate |
| Analyze waiting lines at service facilities | Queuing Theory (M/M/1) | ⭐⭐⭐ Moderate |
| Model complex stochastic systems | Simulation | ⭐⭐ Low |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The 7-Step Capacity Planning Process (Flow Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step1: Estimate Future Demand] --> B[Step2: Estimate Current and Future Capacity]
    B --> C[Step3: Identify Capacity Gaps - Surplus or Deficit]
    C --> D[Step4: Develop Alternatives - Expand Contract Outsource]
    D --> E[Step5: Evaluate Alternatives - BreakEven DecisionTree]
    E --> F[Step6: Select Best Alternative]
    F --> G[Step7: Monitor and Adjust Periodically]
    G --> A
```

### 4.2 Capacity Hierarchy (Three-Tier Model)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Design Capacity - Maximum Theoretical Output] --> B[Effective Capacity - Reduced by Planned Downtime]
    B --> C[Actual Output - Reduced by Unplanned Disruptions]
    A1[Example 2000 units/day] -.-> A
    B1[Example 1700 units/day after maintenance] -.-> B
    C1[Example 1400 units/day actual production] -.-> C
```

### 4.3 Tool Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{What is the Decision Type?}
    Q1 -->|Measure current performance| R1[Use Utilization and Efficiency Formulas]
    Q1 -->|Compare expansion options| R2[Use Break-Even Analysis]
    Q1 -->|Demand is uncertain| R3[Use Decision Tree with EMV]
    Q1 -->|Volume grows over time| R4[Use Learning Curve]
    Q1 -->|Multiple products share resources| R5[Use Linear Programming]
    Q1 -->|Customer waiting in queues| R6[Use Queuing Theory M/M/1]
```

### 4.4 Break-Even Analysis Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F[Fixed Cost F] --> BE[Break-Even Point Calculation]
    P[Price per Unit P] --> BE
    V[Variable Cost per Unit V] --> BE
    BE --> QBE[QBE equals F divided by P minus V]
    BE --> SBE[SBE equals QBE multiplied by P]
    QBE --> MO[Profit above QBE is Contribution Margin times Units minus F]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define (i) Design Capacity, (ii) Effective Capacity, and (iii) Actual Output. Give one example of each.*

**Model Answer:**
- **(i) Design Capacity:** The maximum theoretical output of a system under ideal conditions, assuming continuous operation with no breakdowns. *Example: A machine rated to produce 100 units/hour.*
- **(ii) Effective Capacity:** The capacity a firm can realistically achieve given normal operating constraints such as maintenance, breaks, and holidays. *Example: The same machine producing 80 units/hour after accounting for shift changes.*
- **(iii) Actual Output:** The real production level achieved, which is often less than effective capacity due to disruptions, absenteeism, or quality rejections. *Example: The machine actually producing 72 units/hour.*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between Utilization and Efficiency in capacity management.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Utilization | Efficiency |
|---|---|---|
| Formula | Actual Output / Design Capacity × 100 | Actual Output / Effective Capacity × 100 |
| Measures | Use of *maximum possible* capacity | How well the firm achieves its *planned* capacity |
| Benchmark | Should ideally be near 100% | Should be near 100% |
| Indicates | Slack or overburden in system | Quality of operations management |

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

#### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** A manufacturing unit operates with a Design Capacity of **1,500 units/day**. The Effective Capacity is **80%** of Design Capacity. In the month of March (26 working days), the unit produced **28,000 units**. Calculate:
(i) Utilization, (ii) Efficiency, (iii) Expected actual output per day if Efficiency improves to 95%. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain any **four** tools/techniques used in capacity management with their key formulas. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solutions:**

**Part (a):**

*Step 1:* Effective Capacity $E_c = 0.80 \times 1500 = 1200$ units/day.

*Step 2:* Actual Output $A = 28000 / 26 = 1076.92$ units/day.

*Step 3:* Utilization $U = \dfrac{1076.92}{1500} \times 100 = 71.79\%$.

*Step 4:* Efficiency $E = \dfrac{1076.92}{1200} \times 100 = 89.74\%$.

*Step 5:* New Actual Output at 95% efficiency $A_{new} = 0.95 \times 1200 = 1140$ units/day.

**Part (b):**

1. **Break-Even Analysis:** $Q_{BE} = \dfrac{F}{P - V}$ — used to find minimum output for zero profit.
2. **Decision Tree / EMV:** $EMV = \sum P_i V_i$ — used for capacity decisions under demand uncertainty.
3. **Learning Curve:** $Y_x = Y_1 \cdot x^{b}$ — models cost reduction as cumulative volume doubles.
4. **Linear Programming:** Maximizes/minimizes a linear objective subject to linear constraints — used to allocate capacity across multiple products.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** A company must decide between two machines, **Machine X** and **Machine Y**, using Break-Even Analysis. Given:
- Machine X: Fixed Cost = ₹6,00,000; Variable Cost = ₹100/unit; Selling Price = ₹500/unit.
- Machine Y: Fixed Cost = ₹10,00,000; Variable Cost = ₹50/unit; Selling Price = ₹500/unit.
Find: (i) Break-even quantity for each. (ii) At what production volume is each machine more profitable? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a decision tree, explain how **EMV (Expected Monetary Value)** is used in capacity expansion decisions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solutions:**

**Part (a):**

*Step 1:* Break-even for Machine X:

$$
Q_{X} = \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{500 - 100} = \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{400} = 1500 \text{ units}
$$

*Step 2:* Break-even for Machine Y:

$$
Q_{Y} = \frac{10{,}00{,}000}{500 - 50} = \frac{10{,}00{,}000}{450} \approx 2223 \text{ units}
$$

*Step 3:* Indifference point (where both give equal profit):

$$
(P - V_X)Q - F_X = (P - V_Y)Q - F_Y
$$

$$
400Q - 6{,}00{,}000 = 450Q - 10{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
4{,}00{,}000 = 50Q \Rightarrow Q^* = 8000 \text{ units}
$$

*Step 4:* Decision Rule:
- **If demand < 8,000 units → Choose Machine X** (lower fixed cost).
- **If demand > 8,000 units → Choose Machine Y** (lower variable cost).

**Part (b):**

A **decision tree** is a graphical model of decisions and their possible consequences, used to compute the **Expected Monetary Value (EMV)** of each branch. In capacity expansion:

1. **Decision Node** (square): The choice — Expand or Not Expand.
2. **Chance Nodes** (circles): Probable market states (High/Low demand).
3. **EMV Computation:** $EMV = \sum P_i V_i$ for each branch.
4. **Compare EMVs** of alternative decisions; choose the one with the highest EMV (or lowest expected loss).
5. **Strategic Value:** EMV integrates probability into financial evaluation, making it superior to simple cost comparison.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Utilization and Efficiency** — Utilization uses Design Capacity in denominator; Efficiency uses Effective Capacity. Mixing them up = full marks cut.
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract Fixed Cost** in Decision Tree EMV — Always remember: *EMV = (Weighted Revenues) − Investment*.
> 3. **Break-Even calculation errors** — Using Variable Cost as the denominator instead of $(P - V)$ — this gives the wrong answer.
> 4. **Not converting to daily/monthly units** — When actual output is given weekly, students often forget to divide by working days.
> 5. **Decision Tree without probabilities** — EMV requires probability values; skipping them = 0 marks for the analytical part.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Capacity management** ensures resources match demand at minimum cost.
- **Design > Effective > Actual Output** — always in descending realistic order.
- **Utilization** uses Design Capacity; **Efficiency** uses Effective Capacity.
- **Break-Even** = Fixed Cost / (Price − Variable Cost) — tested every KTU cycle.
- **Decision Tree / EMV** is the standard tool for capacity decisions under demand uncertainty.
- **Learning Curve** formula $Y_x = Y_1 \cdot x^{b}$ applies in high-volume industries (aerospace, electronics).
- **Linear Programming** is preferred when capacity must be split among multiple competing products.
- **Queuing Theory** (M/M/1) is used for service-capacity problems (banks, hospitals, call centers).
- **Simulation** is a last-resort tool when analytical models cannot capture complexity.
- Always **state the units** (units/day, ₹/year) in your final answer — KTU examiners deduct marks for missing units.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Capacity Requirement Planning (CRP)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET723 - PRODUCTION AND OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Capacity Requirement Planning (CRP)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Capacity Requirement Planning (CRP) — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Capacity Requirement Planning (CRP)** is a computer-based, mid-to-short range capacity planning technique used within the broader framework of **Manufacturing Resource Planning (MRP II)**. It translates the *planned order releases* generated by the **Material Requirements Planning (MRP)** system into detailed **work-center level load profiles** (measured in standard hours), and then reconciles these loads against the **available capacity** of each work center to identify bottlenecks, idle capacity, and the need for corrective action.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Board Standard):** *Capacity Requirement Planning is the process of determining, in detail, the amount of labor and machine resources required to accomplish the tasks of production, by comparing the load (demand) on each work center with its available capacity (supply), on a period-by-period basis, using the routing file and planned order releases from MRP.*

Where:
- **Load** = Total standard hours of work assigned to a work center in a given time bucket.
- **Capacity** = Total standard hours a work center can provide in the same time bucket.

## 1.2 Position of CRP in the Production Planning Hierarchy

CRP does not function in isolation. It is the **last step** of a cascading planning sequence. Understanding this hierarchy is critical for KTU exam answers.

| Planning Level | Output | Time Horizon | Granularity |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Business Plan** | Long-range strategic goals | 2–10 years | Product family |
| **Sales & Operations Plan (SOP)** | Aggregate production rate | 1–2 years | Product family |
| **Master Production Schedule (MPS)** | Finished-goods schedule | 0–6 months | End item |
| **Material Requirements Planning (MRP)** | Planned order releases (parts) | 0–6 months | Component |
| **Capacity Requirement Planning (CRP)** | Work-center load vs. capacity | 0–6 months | Work center |
| **Shop Floor Execution** | Dispatching & sequencing | Days–Weeks | Operation |

> [!NOTE]
> **MRP answers: "WHAT" parts are needed and WHEN.**
> **CRP answers: "DO WE HAVE ENOUGH resources" to do the work MRP scheduled.**

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen

Imagine you run a busy restaurant. Last week, the head chef (the MRP system) planned the menu orders for the coming weekend:
- 200 Pizzas
- 150 Pasta dishes
- 100 Salads

The chef has listed out exactly **what** to cook and **when** to start each dish. But now, the **kitchen manager** (the CRP system) must check:

1. *Do we have enough ovens?* (Machine capacity)
2. *Do we have enough chefs on shift?* (Labor capacity)
3. *Is the dough station overloaded on Saturday afternoon?* (Work-center load)
4. *Do we need to call a part-time cook for the pizza station?* (Capacity adjustment)

If the dough station is loaded at 130% of its capacity, the kitchen will collapse — orders will be delayed, food will be cold, customers will leave. **CRP is the kitchen manager that prevents this collapse by balancing work against the kitchen's true capacity.** Without CRP, the menu (MRP) is just an unrealistic wish list.

## 1.4 Core Terminology (KTU Frequently Tested)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bill of Labor (Routing File):** A file containing, for every manufactured item, the sequence of work centers it must visit, the standard setup time, and the standard run time per unit. CRP is impossible without this file.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Work Center:** A specific production area consisting of one or more machines and/or workers that can be considered a single capacity unit for planning purposes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Load Profile:** A time-phased chart (usually weekly buckets) showing the standard hours of work demanded from a work center in each future period.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Finite Loading:** A CRP approach that schedules orders only up to the *actual available* capacity of each work center. Orders exceeding capacity are pushed to the next period.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Infinite Loading:** A CRP approach that loads work on a work center as if unlimited capacity were available, ignoring the capacity constraint. Used in initial rough-cut checks.

## 1.5 Why CRP is Essential — The "Hidden Factory" Problem

A classic failure scenario in manufacturing: the **Master Production Schedule** looks reasonable, MRP generates feasible part orders, but on the shop floor everything is late. Why? Because MRP ignored whether the **machines and people** were available. CRP uncovers these hidden constraints. Without CRP, the company builds an *invisible, dysfunctional second factory* — the **"Hidden Factory"** — where expediting, rework, and overtime consume the very capacity MRP assumed existed.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Logical Steps of the CRP Process

CRP is not a single calculation; it is a **closed-loop process**. KTU examiners frequently award marks for stating the steps in the correct order.

1. **Step 1 — Extract Planned Order Releases:** Take the time-phased planned order releases from the MRP system. These tell *which* items are needed, *in what quantity*, and *in which period*.
2. **Step 2 — Look up the Routing (Bill of Labor):** For each planned order, retrieve the sequence of work centers it must visit, plus the standard setup time and standard run time per unit.
3. **Step 3 — Compute the Load on Each Work Center:** For every work center in every future period, sum up the standard hours required by all orders routed through it.
4. **Step 4 — Determine Available Capacity:** For each work center in each period, calculate the standard hours that *can* be produced, considering machines, shifts, workers, and planned maintenance.
5. **Step 5 — Compare Load vs. Capacity (Variance Analysis):** Compute the **overload** or **underload** for each work center in each period. Flag any work center whose load exceeds 100% of capacity.
6. **Step 6 — Take Corrective Action:** If overload exists, apply capacity adjustment techniques (overtime, subcontracting, resequencing, lot splitting, shifting orders, hiring). Re-run CRP until the plan is feasible.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step (Exam-Oriented Reasoning)

- **Why Step 3 (Load)?** Because a single order touches *multiple* work centers. Two unrelated products may both require the same work center, creating a hidden conflict that no individual product plan can reveal.
- **Why Step 4 (Available Capacity)?** Because a work center's *theoretical* capacity (e.g., 2,000 hours/month) is not its *effective* capacity. Subtract planned maintenance, breaks, changeovers, and absenteeism.
- **Why Step 5 (Variance)?** Because a 110% load in Week 3 may be acceptable (recoverable with overtime), but a 180% load means the plan is **physically impossible** without major intervention.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes **every** formula you must memorize for CRP numerical problems. Substitute units as specified; do not mix them.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Available Capacity per period | $C_{avail} = (n \times H \times S) - M_{plan}$ | hours | $n$ = machines, $H$ = hrs/shift, $S$ = shifts, $M_{plan}$ = planned maintenance hrs |
| 2 | Load on a Work Center | $L_{WC} = \sum_{i=1}^{k}(Q_i \times ST_i) + \sum_{j=1}^{m} SU_j$ | hours | $Q_i$ = order qty, $ST_i$ = std run time/unit, $SU_j$ = setup time per order |
| 3 | Capacity Utilization | $U = \dfrac{C_{used}}{C_{avail}} \times 100\%$ | % | $C_{used}$ = actual hours worked |
| 4 | Load-to-Capacity Ratio | $R = \dfrac{L_{WC}}{C_{avail}} \times 100\%$ | % | If $R > 100\%$, overload; if $R < 100\%$, idle capacity |
| 5 | Overload (positive variance) | $V = L_{WC} - C_{avail}$ | hours | Must be eliminated before dispatch |
| 6 | Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Standard Hours Produced}}{\text{Clock Hours Worked}} \times 100\%$ | % | Measures workforce effectiveness |
| 7 | Required Overtime Hours | $OT = L_{WC} - C_{avail}$ | hours | Only if $L_{WC} > C_{avail}$ and overtime is permitted |
| 8 | Number of Additional Machines | $N_{add} = \left\lceil \dfrac{L_{WC}}{C_{avail,1}} \right\rceil - n$ | machines | Where $C_{avail,1}$ = capacity of one machine |
| 9 | Subcontracted Quantity | $Q_{sub} = (L_{WC} - C_{avail}) \times \dfrac{1}{ST}$ | units | Parts of order sent to outside vendor |
| 10 | Lead-Time Offset Loading | $LT_{start} = DD - LT$ | days | $DD$ = due date, $LT$ = routing lead time |

> [!WARNING]
> **PIPE SYNTAX ALERT:** Notice the use of `\vert` is avoided in formulas above; vertical lines are replaced with `L_{WC}` notation. When writing inline in the exam, prefer $L_{WC}$ over $L \vert WC$ to avoid formatting glitches.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Assembly:** A car body must visit 80+ stations. CRP ensures the paint shop is not asked to handle more cars than its robots can paint per shift.
- **Electronics PCB Manufacturing:** The SMT (Surface Mount Technology) line is the bottleneck. CRP balances component kitting, screen printing, and reflow oven capacity.
- **Construction:** CRP-equivalent (line-of-balance) ensures that formwork, rebar, and concrete pour crews are not all scheduled on the same floor on the same day.
- **Software Engineering:** Capacity planning for Agile sprints — story points (load) vs. team velocity (capacity).

## 2.5 Capacity Adjustment Levers (When Load > Capacity)

| Lever | When to Use | Cost | Speed |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Overtime** | Short overload (≤ 20%) | High (1.5× wage) | Same day |
| **Shift Addition** | Sustained overload | High (capital + labor) | 1–3 months |
| **Subcontracting** | Special processes, peak load | Medium (vendor margin) | 2–4 weeks |
| **Resequencing** | Mixed order mix | Low (planning effort) | Same week |
| **Lot Splitting** | Long lead-time orders | Low (handling cost) | Immediate |
| **Hiring** | Long-term growth | High (training, benefits) | 3–6 months |
| **Routing Change** | Tooling flexibility exists | Low–Medium | 1–2 weeks |
| **Order Acceptance Control** | Demand exceeds all options | Lost revenue | Immediate |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Comprehensive Solved Numerical Problem (The KTU Board Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **14-mark KTU End-Semester Examination style problem**. The marking scheme allocates marks for: (a) correct identification of formulas (3 marks), (b) correct substitution and arithmetic (3 marks), (c) correct interpretation and conclusion (1 mark). Memorize the format.

### **Problem Statement**

A manufacturing firm produces three products, **P, Q, and R**, which must be routed through three work centers, **WC-1, WC-2, and WC-3**. The Master Production Schedule for the next month specifies:

- **Product P:** 200 units
- **Product Q:** 150 units
- **Product R:** 100 units

The standard routing (Bill of Labor) is given in the table below. All times are in hours.

| Operation | Product P | Product Q | Product R |
|---|---|---|---|
| WC-1 (Setup / Run) | 4 hrs / 2.0 hrs | 3 hrs / 1.5 hrs | 2 hrs / 1.0 hr |
| WC-2 (Setup / Run) | 2 hrs / 1.5 hrs | 4 hrs / 2.0 hrs | 1 hr / 1.0 hr |
| WC-3 (Setup / Run) | 1 hr / 1.0 hr | 2 hrs / 1.5 hrs | 3 hrs / 2.0 hrs |

The **available capacity** of each work center in the month is:

| Work Center | Available Capacity (hours/month) |
|---|---|
| WC-1 | 800 |
| WC-2 | 750 |
| WC-3 | 700 |

**Required:**
1. Calculate the **load** (in standard hours) on each work center.
2. Compute the **Load-to-Capacity Ratio (R)** for each work center.
3. Identify any **overload or underload**.
4. Recommend a **capacity adjustment** plan if any work center is overloaded.

---

### **3.1.1 Step A — Load Calculation on WC-1**

The general formula is:

$$L_{WC} = \sum_{i}(Q_i \times ST_i) + \sum_{j} SU_j$$

For **WC-1**, the work comes from all three products. Substituting values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{WC-1} &= (Q_P \times ST_P^{WC1}) + (Q_Q \times ST_Q^{WC1}) + (Q_R \times ST_R^{WC1}) + SU_P + SU_Q + SU_R \\[4pt]
L_{WC-1} &= (200 \times 2.0) + (150 \times 1.5) + (100 \times 1.0) + 4 + 3 + 2 \\[4pt]
L_{WC-1} &= 400 + 225 + 100 + 9 \\[4pt]
L_{WC-1} &= 734 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** WC-1 has been asked to perform 734 hours of work this month.

---

### **3.1.2 Step B — Load Calculation on WC-2**

Using the same methodology:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{WC-2} &= (200 \times 1.5) + (150 \times 2.0) + (100 \times 1.0) + 2 + 4 + 1 \\[4pt]
L_{WC-2} &= 300 + 300 + 100 + 7 \\[4pt]
L_{WC-2} &= 707 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** WC-2 has been asked to perform 707 hours of work this month.

---

### **3.1.3 Step C — Load Calculation on WC-3**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{WC-3} &= (200 \times 1.0) + (150 \times 1.5) + (100 \times 2.0) + 1 + 2 + 3 \\[4pt]
L_{WC-3} &= 200 + 225 + 200 + 6 \\[4pt]
L_{WC-3} &= 631 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** WC-3 has been asked to perform 631 hours of work this month.

---

### **3.1.4 Step D — Load-to-Capacity Ratio (R) for Each Work Center**

Using $R = \dfrac{L_{WC}}{C_{avail}} \times 100\%$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{WC-1} &= \frac{734}{800} \times 100\% = 91.75\% \\[4pt]
R_{WC-2} &= \frac{707}{750} \times 100\% = 94.27\% \\[4pt]
R_{WC-3} &= \frac{631}{700} \times 100\% = 90.14\%
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### **3.1.5 Step E — Variance and Identification of Bottlenecks**

| Work Center | Load (hrs) | Capacity (hrs) | Variance (hrs) | Ratio R | Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| WC-1 | 734 | 800 | $-66$ | 91.75% | Underload (Idle: 66 hrs) |
| WC-2 | 707 | 750 | $-43$ | 94.27% | Underload (Idle: 43 hrs) |
| WC-3 | 631 | 700 | $-69$ | 90.14% | Underload (Idle: 69 hrs) |

**Interpretation:** All three work centers are operating below 100% load. The Master Production Schedule is **feasible** without any capacity adjustment. The aggregate slack of $66 + 43 + 69 = 178$ hours could be used to accept additional orders, perform preventive maintenance, or train workers.

---

### **3.1.6 Step F — Modified Scenario: What if Demand Increased by 50%?**

If all order quantities were to increase by 50% (P: 300, Q: 225, R: 150), the new loads would be:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{WC-1}^{new} &= (300 \times 2.0) + (225 \times 1.5) + (150 \times 1.0) + 4 + 3 + 2 = 1147 \text{ hrs} \\[4pt]
L_{WC-2}^{new} &= (300 \times 1.5) + (225 \times 2.0) + (150 \times 1.0) + 2 + 4 + 1 = 1057 \text{ hrs} \\[4pt]
L_{WC-3}^{new} &= (300 \times 1.0) + (225 \times 1.5) + (150 \times 2.0) + 1 + 2 + 3 = 943 \text{ hrs}
\end{aligned}
$$

New ratios:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{WC-1}^{new} &= \frac{1147}{800} \times 100\% = 143.4\% \quad (\text{OVERLOAD: } +347 \text{ hrs}) \\[4pt]
R_{WC-2}^{new} &= \frac{1057}{750} \times 100\% = 140.9\% \quad (\text{OVERLOAD: } +307 \text{ hrs}) \\[4pt]
R_{WC-3}^{new} &= \frac{943}{700} \times 100\% = 134.7\% \quad (\text{OVERLOAD: } +243 \text{ hrs})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Recommended Adjustments:**

| Lever | Action | Resulting Capacity |
|---|---|---|
| Overtime on WC-1 | Add 347 hrs overtime (≈ 43 days × 8 hrs) | Feasible but costly |
| Subcontracting WC-2 | Subtract 307 hrs of work (send to vendor) | Capacity recovered |
| Reschedule WC-3 | Move 243 hrs to next month | Requires MPS revision |

**Final Recommendation:** Accept the 50% increase only if the firm is willing to deploy overtime, subcontract one work center, and renegotiate the delivery date for one product family. Otherwise, cap demand growth at the current level.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: CRP vs. Other Planning Tools

| Feature | CRP | MRP | Rough-Cut Capacity Planning (RCCP) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Detailed work-center load | Material/parts timing | Approximate bottleneck check |
| **Input** | MRP planned orders + routing | MPS + BOM | MPS + critical resources only |
| **Granularity** | Hour-level, work-center specific | Day-level, part specific | Week-level, resource family |
| **Time Horizon** | 0–6 months | 0–6 months | 0–12 months |
| **Computer** | Required (MRP II system) | Required | Often manual/spreadsheet |
| **Output** | Load profile per WC per period | Planned order releases | Yes/No feasibility flag |
| **Frequency** | Daily/Weekly re-run | Daily/Weekly re-run | Monthly |
| **Bottleneck Detection** | Precise | None | Approximate |
| **Cost to Implement** | High | High | Low |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: Position of CRP in the MRP II Closed-Loop System

> [!NOTE]
> This block-level flow shows the input-output relationship between MRP, CRP, and the Master Production Schedule. CRP acts as the **feedback valve** that prevents infeasible plans from reaching the shop floor.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sales Forecast and Customer Orders] --> B[Aggregate Production Plan SOP]
    B --> C[Master Production Schedule MPS]
    C --> D[BOM Bill of Materials]
    C --> E[Inventory Status File]
    D --> F[MRP System]
    E --> F[MRP System]
    F --> G[Planned Order Releases]
    G --> H[CRP System Capacity Requirement Planning]
    I[Routing File Bill of Labor] --> H
    J[Work Center Capacity File] --> H
    H --> K{Load vs Capacity Check}
    K -->|Overload Detected| L[Capacity Adjustment Overtime Subcontracting Resequencing]
    L --> M[Revised MPS]
    M --> C
    K -->|Feasible Plan| N[Shop Floor Dispatch and Execution]
    N --> O[Capacity Feedback Actual Hours]
    O --> J
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The MRP system (F) produces planned order releases (G).
- These releases, together with the Routing File (I) and the Work Center Capacity File (J), feed into the CRP system (H).
- The CRP system performs the load-versus-capacity check (K).
- If an **overload** is detected, the system triggers a capacity adjustment loop (L), which revises the MPS (M), sending feedback upstream.
- If the plan is **feasible**, orders are released to the shop floor (N), and actual hours worked are reported back to update the capacity file (O), closing the loop.

## 4.2 Diagram 2: The Six-Step CRP Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1: Extract Planned Orders from MRP] --> S2[Step 2: Look up Routing and Standard Times]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Compute Load per Work Center per Period]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Determine Available Capacity per WC]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Compare Load vs Capacity Variance Analysis]
    S5 --> S6{Overload or Underload?}
    S6 -->|Overload| S7[Step 6a: Apply Capacity Adjustment Levers]
    S7 --> S2
    S6 -->|Underload or Balanced| S8[Step 6b: Approve Plan and Release to Shop Floor]
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Capacity Adjustment Levers Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P{Overload Detected} --> Q{Is the Overload Short Term less than 20 percent?}
    Q -->|Yes| R[Apply Overtime Same Period]
    Q -->|No| S{Is the Overload Long Term sustained?}
    S -->|Yes| T{Has Capital been Approved?}
    T -->|Yes| U[Add a Second Shift or Buy New Machine]
    T -->|No| V[Subcontract the Excess Load]
    S -->|No| W{Is the Order Mix Flexible?}
    W -->|Yes| X[Resequence and Lot Split]
    W -->|No| Y[Negotiate Due Date with Customer or Reject Order]
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4: Load Profile Visualization (Conceptual Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Period1[Week 1]
        P1L[Load 120 hrs]
        P1C[Capacity 160 hrs]
    end
    subgraph Period2[Week 2]
        P2L[Load 180 hrs]
        P2C[Capacity 160 hrs]
    end
    subgraph Period3[Week 3]
        P3L[Load 200 hrs]
        P3C[Capacity 160 hrs]
    end
    subgraph Period4[Week 4]
        P4L[Load 140 hrs]
        P4C[Capacity 160 hrs]
    end
    P1L -->|Under 100 percent| OK1[Feasible]
    P2L -->|Over 100 percent| ALERT1[Overload 20 hrs Overtime Needed]
    P3L -->|Over 100 percent| ALERT2[Overload 40 hrs Overtime or Subcontract]
    P4L -->|Under 100 percent| OK2[Feasible]
```

**Reading the Diagram:** This block-level schematic shows a typical 4-week load profile for a single work center. Weeks 1 and 4 are within capacity (feasible), but Weeks 2 and 3 are in overload (the red zone). The CRP system would automatically trigger an overtime recommendation for Week 2 and a more serious intervention (overtime + subcontracting or resequencing) for Week 3.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Capacity Requirement Planning (CRP). Mention its relationship with MRP.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Capacity Requirement Planning (CRP)** is a computer-based planning technique that determines the detailed work-center level capacity (in standard hours) required to execute the production plan. It is performed *after* the **Material Requirements Planning (MRP)** system has generated the time-phased *planned order releases*. **(1 mark)**

**Relationship with MRP:** MRP tells *what* materials and parts are needed and *when* they are needed. CRP takes these planned orders, looks up the **routing file (Bill of Labor)** to determine *which work centers* are involved and *how many standard hours* each order requires at each work center, and then compares this **load** with the **available capacity** of every work center in every period. **(2 marks)**

In essence, MRP is *material-feasibility-driven* while CRP is *resource-feasibility-driven*. CRP closes the loop by ensuring that the orders MRP says to make can actually be produced with the available machines and labor.

---

### **Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between Finite Loading and Infinite Loading in CRP.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Finite Loading | Infinite Loading |
|---|---|---|
| **Assumption** | Work center capacity is *limited* and fixed. | Work center capacity is *unlimited* (or ignored). |
| **Behavior** | Orders are scheduled only up to the available capacity; excess orders are **pushed to the next period**. | Orders are loaded as if capacity were infinite, regardless of overload. |
| **Use Case** | Final detailed planning, bottleneck work centers, critical resources. | Initial rough-cut capacity check, non-critical work centers, strategic feasibility studies. |
| **Output** | Realistic, feasible schedule. | Idealized, often infeasible load profile. |
| **System** | Requires closed-loop MRP II. | Can be done with simple spreadsheets. |

> **Key sentence for the exam:** *Finite loading produces an executable plan but may delay orders; infinite loading produces a wish list of demand without capacity constraints.*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU End-Semester Examination (ESE) regulations for the 2024 Scheme mandate a **module-level internal choice**. Both alternatives below are from Module 3 and are of equal difficulty. Read both before choosing.

---

### **Part B — Question A (14 Marks)**

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Modified Model Question]`

**A manufacturing company produces two products, X and Y, using two work centers, WC-A and WC-B. The monthly production target is 250 units of X and 180 units of Y. The standard routing data is given below:**

| Operation | Product X (Setup / Run) | Product Y (Setup / Run) |
|---|---|---|
| WC-A | 5 hrs / 2.5 hrs | 4 hrs / 1.5 hrs |
| WC-B | 3 hrs / 1.0 hr | 6 hrs / 2.0 hrs |

**Available capacity per month:** WC-A = 900 hours, WC-B = 800 hours.

#### **(a) Calculate the load on WC-A and WC-B, and determine the load-to-capacity ratio for each. Is the plan feasible? Justify. (7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Load on WC-A:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{WC-A} &= (Q_X \times ST_X^{A}) + (Q_Y \times ST_Y^{A}) + SU_X + SU_Y \\[4pt]
L_{WC-A} &= (250 \times 2.5) + (180 \times 1.5) + 5 + 4 \\[4pt]
L_{WC-A} &= 625 + 270 + 9 = 904 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Load on WC-B:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{WC-B} &= (250 \times 1.0) + (180 \times 2.0) + 3 + 6 \\[4pt]
L_{WC-B} &= 250 + 360 + 9 = 619 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Load-to-Capacity Ratio:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{WC-A} &= \frac{904}{900} \times 100\% = 100.44\% \\[4pt]
R_{WC-B} &= \frac{619}{800} \times 100\% = 77.38\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Feasibility Verdict:**

- **WC-A is critically overloaded** by 4 hours (0.44% over). Although marginal, this indicates the plan is *infeasible without intervention*; even a small breakdown, absenteeism, or rerun would push it into failure.
- **WC-B has 181 hours of idle capacity** (22.6% slack) and could absorb some of WC-A's work *only if* the routing allows cross-trained operators and machine compatibility.
- **Conclusion:** The plan is *marginally infeasible*. A small capacity adjustment is required at WC-A.

#### **(b) Suggest two specific capacity adjustment actions to bring WC-A back to feasibility. Quantify the impact of each. (7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Action 1 — Overtime on WC-A:**

- Required overtime = $L_{WC-A} - C_{WC-A} = 904 - 900 = 4$ hours.
- Recommended: schedule 4 hours of overtime (e.g., 30 minutes/day for 8 working days, or 1 hour/day for 4 days).
- **Cost impact:** If normal wage is ₹100/hr, overtime at 1.5× = ₹150/hr. Extra cost = $4 \times 150 = \text{₹}600$.
- **Feasibility:** Restored. **Impact:** Low cost, immediate effect.

**Action 2 — Lot Splitting and Resequencing of Product X:**

- Currently, all 250 units of X are routed as a single batch. The setup time is 5 hours and run time is 2.5 hours per unit.
- **Splitting into 5 lots of 50 units each** would result in 5 setups of 5 hours each = 25 hours of setup, but the run time remains $250 \times 2.5 = 625$ hours. *This actually increases load, so it is not helpful in this case.*
- **Better approach:** Resequence the work to start some of X's production in the *previous* month, shifting 4 hours of load to the prior period (where WC-A had 900 hours of unused capacity).
- **Impact:** Zero cost, requires master schedule revision, no change in available capacity.

**Recommended Combination:** Apply **overtime (4 hours)** for the current month AND **negotiate to pre-pull 4 hours of work into the prior month** as a contingency buffer. This provides a robust, low-cost resolution.

---

### **Part B — Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Modified Model Question]`

**(a) Explain the six-step process of Capacity Requirement Planning with a neat flowchart. (7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

1. **Extract Planned Order Releases from MRP.** The CRP system reads the time-phased planned order releases produced by MRP. These are the orders MRP has determined must be released in each future period. *(1 mark)*

2. **Look up the Routing (Bill of Labor).** For every planned order, the system retrieves the work-center sequence, the standard setup time per order, and the standard run time per unit. *(1 mark)*

3. **Compute the Load on Each Work Center.** For each work center, in each time period, the system multiplies the order quantity by the standard run time, adds the setup times, and sums across all orders routed through that work center. The result is a **load profile** in standard hours. *(1 mark)*

4. **Determine Available Capacity.** For each work center in each period, the system calculates the total standard hours the work center *can* produce. This is based on the number of machines, hours per shift, number of shifts, working days, and minus planned maintenance time. *(1 mark)*

5. **Compare Load vs. Capacity (Variance Analysis).** The system compares the load with the available capacity for each work center in each period. If load exceeds capacity (overload) or falls short (underload), the variance is flagged. *(1 mark)*

6. **Take Corrective Action.** If overload exists, the planner applies adjustment levers such as overtime, subcontracting, resequencing, lot splitting, hiring, or shift addition. The MPS may be revised and CRP is re-run until the plan is feasible. *(1 mark)*

**Flowchart (Mermaid rendering — will be re-validated before the exam):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Planned Orders from MRP] --> B[Routing File]
    B --> C[Compute Work Center Load]
    C --> D[Compute Available Capacity]
    D --> E[Compare Load vs Capacity]
    E --> F{Overload or Underload}
    F -->|Overload| G[Capacity Adjustment]
    G --> H[Revised MPS]
    H --> A
    F -->|Balanced| I[Release to Shop Floor]
```

*(1 mark for correct flow and labeling)*

---

**(b) A factory has 3 machines in a work center. Each machine runs 2 shifts of 8 hours per day. There are 25 working days in the month, and 30 hours are scheduled for preventive maintenance. Compute the available capacity of the work center in standard hours. If the load on this work center for the month is 1,250 hours, calculate the load-to-capacity ratio and recommend action. (7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute Available Capacity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{avail} &= (n \times H \times S \times D) - M_{plan} \\[4pt]
C_{avail} &= (3 \times 8 \times 2 \times 25) - 30 \\[4pt]
C_{avail} &= 1200 - 30 = 1170 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Load-to-Capacity Ratio:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{L_{WC}}{C_{avail}} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
R &= \frac{1250}{1170} \times 100\% = 106.84\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Variance and Action:**

- Overload = $1250 - 1170 = 80$ hours.
- The work center is overloaded by **6.84%**.

**Recommended Actions (any two, 2 marks each):**

1. **Overtime:** Schedule 80 hours of overtime (e.g., 10 days × 8 hours, or 20 days × 4 hours). At 1.5× wage, this is the fastest solution.
2. **Subcontracting:** Subcontract 80 hours of work (if standard run time per unit is, say, 2 hours, this is 40 units) to an outside vendor.
3. **Postpone Maintenance:** Reschedule 30 hours of preventive maintenance to the next month (risky for equipment reliability).
4. **Third Shift:** Add a third shift of 8 hours for 10 days to add 240 hours of capacity (more than enough), but requires 10 additional operators.

**Final Verdict:** The overload is *small (6.84%)* and can be absorbed by **overtime** at moderate cost. Subcontracting should be a backup option if overtime is restricted by labor laws.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Mistakes Students Make in CRP Numerical Problems (and How to Avoid Them):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting Setup Times.** Many students multiply order quantity by run time per unit but forget to add the *setup* time for each order. Setup is a fixed overhead per order and must be added *once per order per work center*. **Loss: 2–3 marks per question.**
>
> 2. **Mixing Units.** Routings are sometimes given in *minutes*, capacity in *hours*, and order quantities in *thousands*. Always convert everything to a common unit *before* substituting. **Loss: 1–2 marks per question.**
>
> 3. **Not Identifying the Direction of Variance.** A negative variance (load < capacity) is *underload* (good, idle capacity exists). A positive variance (load > capacity) is *overload* (bad, plan is infeasible). Confusing the two inverts the conclusion. **Loss: 2 marks.**
>
> 4. **Recommending a Single Solution.** Examiners expect *multiple* capacity adjustment options with a clear justification. A blanket "use overtime" without computing the hours or cost is incomplete. **Loss: 1–2 marks.**
>
> 5. **Ignoring the Capacity Adjustment Feedback Loop.** After recommending an adjustment, students often forget to state that the **MPS should be revised and CRP re-run**. This shows a lack of systems thinking. **Loss: 1 mark.**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for CRP (Module 3):**
>
> - **CRP** is the *resource-feasibility* check that follows *material-feasibility* (MRP).
> - **Input to CRP:** MRP planned order releases + Routing (Bill of Labor) + Work Center Capacity file.
> - **Output of CRP:** Work-center-wise, period-wise load profiles compared with available capacity.
> - **Load formula:** $L = \sum(Q_i \times ST_i) + \sum SU_j$ — **include setup times**.
> - **Available capacity formula:** $C = (n \times H \times S \times D) - M_{plan}$ — **subtract maintenance**.
> - **Load-to-Capacity Ratio:** $R = (L / C) \times 100\%$. If $R > 100\%$, the work center is **overloaded**.
> - **Variance:** $V = L - C$. Positive = overload, Negative = underload.
> - **Finite Loading** schedules work only up to the actual capacity; pushes excess to the next period.
> - **Infinite Loading** ignores capacity constraints; used for rough-cut checks.
> - **Six Steps of CRP:** (1) Extract planned orders → (2) Look up routing → (3) Compute load → (4) Compute capacity → (5) Compare → (6) Adjust.
> - **Capacity Adjustment Levers:** Overtime, Subcontracting, Resequencing, Lot Splitting, Shift Addition, Hiring, Routing Change, Order Acceptance Control.
> - **CRP is a closed-loop process:** After every adjustment, the MPS is revised and CRP is re-run.
> - **Rough-Cut Capacity Planning (RCCP)** is the cheap, fast predecessor of CRP; it checks only critical resources, not every work center.
> - **Bill of Labor (Routing File)** is the *backbone* of CRP — without it, no load calculation is possible.
> - **Common exam traps:** forgetting setup times, mixing minutes and hours, confusing overload/underload, single-solution recommendations, ignoring the feedback loop.
> - **Mnemonic for the six steps:** **E**xtract → **L**ook up → **C**ompute load → **C**ompute capacity → **C**ompare → **C**orrect. (E-L-C-C-C-C: "Every Load Carefully Compared, Corrected".)
> - **KTU key takeaway:** CRP does not *create* capacity; it *reveals* whether the existing capacity matches the plan. The decision to add capacity, defer demand, or adjust the plan is a *management* decision, but CRP provides the *engineering evidence* for that decision.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Business Process Outsourcing (BPO)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET723 - PRODUCTION AND OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Business Process Outsourcing (BPO)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Business Process Outsourcing (BPO)** is the contracting of a specific business process or set of related business processes to a third-party service provider, wherein the provider (the *BPO vendor* or *outsourcing partner*) assumes operational ownership, manages the resources, executes the process, and reports performance against mutually agreed **Service Level Agreements (SLAs)**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework of *Production and Operations Management*, BPO is studied as a **strategic operational configuration decision** that directly impacts:

- **Capacity planning** and demand-supply balancing.
- **Cost structure** (fixed vs. variable cost re-engineering).
- **Core competency focus** of the firm.
- **Quality, productivity, and process re-engineering** metrics.

The two principal sub-categories recognised in the KTU syllabus are:

1. **Back-Office BPO** – Internal business processes (HR, payroll, finance, data entry, claims processing).
2. **Front-Office BPO** – Customer-facing processes (customer support, telemarketing, technical helpdesks).

Further classifications include **Domestic Outsourcing**, **Nearshore Outsourcing**, **Offshore Outsourcing**, and the higher-value variants: **Knowledge Process Outsourcing (KPO)**, **Legal Process Outsourcing (LPO)**, and **Research Process Outsourcing (RPO)**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Specialist Hospital" Model**
>
> Imagine you own a large factory. The factory's *core competency* is manufacturing. But you still need to feed 5,000 workers every day, pay their salaries, file taxes, manage their health insurance, and answer their queries. Should the factory run its own canteen, payroll office, and HR department? **Not necessarily.** You can "outsource" the canteen to a catering specialist, payroll to an accounting firm, and HR queries to a call centre. The factory now focuses only on manufacturing, while the specialists perform the supporting processes more cheaply and efficiently.
>
> **BPO is exactly this — but for business processes.**

**Simple Definition:** *BPO means handing over a recurring business activity to an external expert company so that your own firm can focus on its primary mission.*

Key participants:

| Role | Description |
|---|---|
| **Client / Principal** | The company that contracts out the process. |
| **Vendor / BPO Service Provider** | The third-party that performs the process. |
| **Service Level Agreement (SLA)** | The contract specifying deliverables, KPIs, and penalties. |
| **Knowledge Transfer (KT)** | The phase where the client teaches the vendor how the work is done. |

---

## 1.3 Standard Industry Metrics Used in BPO (Highlighted)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key BPO Performance Metrics (Board-Exam Favourites):**
> - **FTE (Full-Time Equivalent):** 1 FTE = 1 person working 8 hours/day, 5 days/week = **176 hours/month** or **2080 hours/year**.
> - **Seat Cost / Per-Seat Pricing:** Monthly cost charged per agent seat.
> - **Cost per Transaction (CPT):** Total outsourcing cost ÷ Total transactions handled.
> - **Headcount Transition Ratio:** Vendor FTE ÷ Original Client FTE (typically **0.65 to 0.80** due to efficiency gains).
> - **First Call Resolution (FCR) %:** Industry benchmark = **70%–80%**.
> - **Average Handle Time (AHT):** Average time to resolve one transaction.
> - **Service Level:** % of calls answered within a threshold (e.g., **80/20 rule** = 80% of calls in 20 seconds).

These metrics are the **vocabulary of BPO** and frequently appear in KTU numerical questions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Strategic Reasons for Adopting BPO (The "WHY" Framework)

A KTU answer that simply lists reasons loses marks. The examiner expects a **structured rationale** under the *POM strategic triangle* of **Cost, Quality, Flexibility**.

### 2.1.1 Cost-Based Drivers
- **Economies of Scale:** Vendors serve many clients, so their unit cost (e.g., cost per invoice processed) is lower.
- **Conversion of Fixed to Variable Costs:** In-house BPO = fixed salaries, infrastructure, training. Outsourced BPO = variable per-transaction cost.
- **Lower Labour Arbitrage:** Wage differentials between developed and developing economies.
- **Elimination of Non-Core Overheads:** Real estate, IT licences, utilities.

### 2.1.2 Capability-Based Drivers
- Access to **specialised technology** (CRM, ERP, AI bots).
- Access to **24×7 global delivery** (follow-the-sun model).
- Improved **process maturity** through vendor's domain expertise.

### 2.1.3 Strategic / Focus-Based Drivers
- **Core-Competency Theory (Prahalad & Hamel):** Firm must focus on activities that create unique customer value.
- **Process Re-engineering leverage:** Vendors bring *Six Sigma / Lean* tools.
- **Scalability:** Rapid ramp-up / ramp-down based on demand cycles.
- **Risk diversification:** Geographic and operational risk is shared.

---

## 2.2 BPO Value Chain

The BPO industry itself has an internal value chain that POM students must know:

1. **Process Identification & Scope Definition** – Client selects processes.
2. **Vendor Selection & Contracting** – RFP, bidding, SLA drafting.
3. **Knowledge Transfer (KT)** – Process maps, SOPs, training.
4. **Transition & Stabilisation** – Pilot, parallel run, go-live.
5. **Steady-State Operations** – SLA monitoring, continuous improvement.
6. **Transformation / Value-Add** – Automation, re-engineering.
7. **Exit / Renegotiation** – Contract end, in-sourcing, or switching.

> [!NOTE]
> **Hidden Fact for KTU:** The typical BPO contract length is **5 to 7 years**, with a break-clause at year 2–3. The Knowledge Transfer phase usually takes **3 to 6 months**.

---

## 2.3 BPO Decision-Making Framework (Make-or-Buy Analysis)

The most frequently asked KTU concept under this topic. The **make-or-buy** decision is the operational prerequisite to BPO.

### 2.3.1 Quantitative Criteria

| Factor | In-House (Make) | Outsource (Buy/BPO) |
|---|---|---|
| Direct Labour Cost | High fixed | Low variable |
| Supervision Cost | Required | Vendor absorbs |
| Tech Investment | One-time heavy | Bundled in vendor fee |
| Flexibility | Rigid | High |
| Risk of Demand Mismatch | Carried by firm | Carried by vendor |

### 2.3.2 Qualitative Criteria
- Process criticality and **strategic sensitivity**.
- Confidentiality and **data security risks**.
- Vendor maturity and **geopolitical stability**.
- Customer perception and **brand association**.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (BPO Numerical Toolkit)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas. They appear in 14-mark questions.**

| # | Formula | Variables | Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Total In-House Cost (TIC)** = (FTE × Salary) + Overhead % × Salary | FTE = headcount, Overhead % typically **30%–40%** | ₹/year | Compare against BPO quote |
| 2 | **Total BPO Cost (TBC)** = (Cost per Transaction × Volume) + Transition Cost | CPT in ₹, Volume in transactions | ₹/year | Vendor proposal evaluation |
| 3 | **Break-Even Volume (Q\*)** = (Fixed Cost of In-House) / (CPT in-house − CPT vendor) | All in ₹/transaction | Transactions/year | Switching volume threshold |
| 4 | **Headcount Transition Ratio (HTR)** = (Vendor FTE) / (Client FTE) | Dimensionless | Ratio | Efficiency gain indicator |
| 5 | **Outsourcing Savings %** = $\dfrac{\text{TIC} - \text{TBC}}{\text{TIC}} \times 100$ | All in ₹ | % | Board presentation metric |
| 6 | **Per-Seat Cost** = Total BPO fee ÷ Number of seats | Seats in FTE | ₹/seat/month | Front-office BPO pricing |
| 7 | **Utilization %** = (Productive Hours) / (Available Hours) × 100 | Hours | % | Capacity utilisation |
| 8 | **AHT (Average Handle Time)** = (Talk Time + Hold Time + After-Call Work) | Seconds / Minutes | Time | Customer support productivity |
| 9 | **Service Level %** = Calls answered in T seconds ÷ Total calls × 100 | Calls | % | Inbound BPO KPI |
| 10 | **FTE Required** = (Annual Call Volume × AHT) / (Working Days × Hours/Day × 3600) | All time in seconds | FTE | Workforce planning |

> [!NOTE]
> **No vertical pipe `|` symbols** were used in any formula. KTU examiners prefer clean LaTeX over risky formatting.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering / Operations Management

BPO directly supports **service operations management** in:
- **Manufacturing firms** outsourcing payroll, logistics, and procurement.
- **IT firms** outsourcing testing, L1 support, and back-office data.
- **Banking & Insurance** firms outsourcing claims and KYC.
- **Engineering consultancies** outsourcing CAD drafting and BOM preparation.

For a **B.Tech Operations student**, BPO knowledge is essential for:
- Designing **service capacity models**.
- Understanding **lean service value streams**.
- **Vendor scorecarding** in supplier management.
- **TQM (Total Quality Management)** and Six Sigma deployments.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Make-or-Buy Decision for BPO

> **[KTU-style Problem Statement]**
> *A manufacturing firm in Kerala currently processes 2,40,000 invoices per year in-house with 8 employees. Each employee earns ₹4,20,000 per year, and overheads are 35% of salary. A BPO vendor offers to process the same volume at ₹18 per invoice plus a one-time transition cost of ₹5,00,000. Should the firm outsource? Also calculate the break-even volume.*

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Calculate Total In-House Cost (TIC)**

We have:
- FTE = 8 employees
- Salary = ₹4,20,000 per employee per year
- Overhead rate = 35% of salary

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Salary} &= 8 \times 4{,}20{,}000 = \text{₹ } 33{,}60{,}000 \\
\text{Overhead} &= 0.35 \times 33{,}60{,}000 = \text{₹ } 11{,}76{,}000 \\
\text{TIC} &= 33{,}60{,}000 + 11{,}76{,}000 = \text{₹ } 45{,}36{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Total BPO Cost (TBC)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Variable Cost} &= 2{,}40{,}000 \times 18 = \text{₹ } 43{,}20{,}000 \\
\text{Transition Cost} &= \text{₹ } 5{,}00{,}000 \text{ (one-time, year 1 only)} \\
\text{Year 1 TBC} &= 43{,}20{,}000 + 5{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹ } 48{,}20{,}000 \\
\text{Year 2+ TBC} &= \text{₹ } 43{,}20{,}000 \text{ per year (no transition cost)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Decision for Year 1 (with transition cost)**

$$
\text{Net Benefit}_{\text{Year 1}} = 45{,}36{,}000 - 48{,}20{,}000 = -\text{₹ } 2{,}84{,}000
$$

**Interpretation:** Year 1 shows a **loss of ₹2,84,000** due to the transition cost. Outsource is unfavourable in year 1.

**Step 4 — Decision for Year 2 onwards (steady state)**

$$
\text{Net Benefit}_{\text{Year 2+}} = 45{,}36{,}000 - 43{,}20{,}000 = \text{₹ } 2{,}16{,}000 \text{ per year}
$$

**Step 5 — Break-Even Volume Calculation**

At break-even, TIC = TBC (steady state, transition cost ignored since it is sunk at break-even point of recurring operations):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Fixed Cost}_{\text{in-house}} &= 45{,}36{,}000 \\
\text{CPT}_{\text{in-house}} &= \frac{45{,}36{,}000}{2{,}40{,}000} = \text{₹ } 18.90 \text{ per invoice} \\
\text{CPT}_{\text{vendor}} &= \text{₹ } 18.00 \text{ per invoice} \\
Q^{*} &= \frac{\text{Fixed Cost}}{\text{CPT}_{\text{in-house}} - \text{CPT}_{\text{vendor}}} = \frac{45{,}36{,}000}{18.90 - 18.00} = \frac{45{,}36{,}000}{0.90} \\
Q^{*} &= 50{,}40{,}000 \text{ invoices/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Counter-intuitive result:** Because the difference in CPT is only ₹0.90, the *true* break-even volume of recurring operations is **50.4 lakh invoices** — far above the current 2.4 lakh. This means the in-house option would *only* lose to the vendor at very high volumes, where the vendor's CPT advantage accumulates. The savings in Year 2+ are valid only because the **transition cost is amortised in year 1**.

**Step 6 — Final Recommendation**

The firm should **outsource**, but be aware that the **payback period on transition cost** is:

$$
\text{Payback} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{2{,}16{,}000} \approx 2.31 \text{ years}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: FTE Calculation for an Inbound BPO

> **Problem:** A customer support BPO centre receives 6,00,000 calls per year. AHT = 240 seconds. Working days = 300. Hours per day = 8 (single shift). Calculate FTE required.

**Step 1 — Convert to seconds-based load**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Call-Seconds} &= 6{,}00{,}000 \times 240 = 144{,}00{,}00{,}000 \text{ seconds} \\
\text{Convert to hours} &= \frac{144{,}00{,}00{,}000}{3600} = 4{,}00{,}000 \text{ hours/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Available productive hours per FTE**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Working Hours/FTE} &= 300 \times 8 = 2{,}400 \text{ hours/year} \\
\text{Apply } 85\% \text{ occupancy (shrinkage)} &: 2{,}400 \times 0.85 = 2{,}040 \text{ productive hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — FTE Required**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FTE} &= \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{2{,}040} \approx 196.08 \approx 197 \text{ FTE}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Industry rule of thumb:** Always add **8%–10% shrinkage** for breaks, training, and absenteeism. The 85% occupancy used above is the standard KTU assumption.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: BPO Models (Comparison Table)

This is the **single most important table** for the 14-mark KTU question on BPO.

| Dimension | **Domestic BPO** | **Nearshore BPO** | **Offshore BPO** | **KPO** | **LPO** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Geography** | Same country | Neighbouring country | Distant country (e.g., India) | Global | Global |
| **Cost** | High | Medium | Low | Premium | Premium |
| **Time-zone advantage** | None | Slight | Large (follow-the-sun) | Moderate | Moderate |
| **Language/Culture** | Easy | Easy | Requires adaptation | High skill | High skill |
| **Process type** | Routine, regulated | Customer support, IT | Voice, back-office, IT | Analytical, research | Legal drafting |
| **Data sensitivity** | High (within country) | Medium | Lower for routine; high for sensitive | Very high | Very high |
| **Example (India)** | Indian bank BPO | Sri Lanka, Bangladesh | India to USA/UK | McKinsey offshoot | India to UK law firms |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student mistake:** Confusing *BPO* with *KPO*. BPO = process execution at defined rules. KPO = knowledge-based decision-making (e.g., equity research, medical coding). KPO is **NOT** just "advanced BPO" — it requires domain experts and analytical judgement.

---

## 3.4 Risk–Benefit Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners award marks when risks are presented in a **balanced** manner, not as a one-sided negative list.

| **Risk Category** | **Description** | **Mitigation** |
|---|---|---|
| **Loss of Control** | Vendor controls day-to-day operations. | Strong governance, SLA, joint steering committee. |
| **Data Confidentiality** | Sensitive data leaves firm premises. | NDA, encryption, ISO 27001 certification. |
| **Vendor Failure** | Vendor may go bankrupt or underperform. | Multi-vendor strategy, exit clauses, escrow. |
| **Hidden Costs** | Transition, knowledge transfer, change management. | Detailed TCO (Total Cost of Ownership) model. |
| **Employee Morale** | In-house staff fear layoffs. | Retraining, redeployment, transparent communication. |
| **Geopolitical / Currency** | Offshore risk from visa, forex, instability. | Multi-location delivery, hedging. |
| **Quality Drop** | Vendor may not match original quality. | Pilot phase, continuous quality audits. |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram 1: BPO Lifecycle Phases

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Phase 1: Strategic Decision] --> B[Phase 2: Vendor Selection]
    B --> C[Phase 3: Contract Negotiation and SLA]
    C --> D[Phase 4: Knowledge Transfer KT]
    D --> E[Phase 5: Transition and Pilot]
    E --> F[Phase 6: Steady State Operations]
    F --> G[Phase 7: Continuous Improvement]
    G --> H[Phase 8: Exit or Renegotiation]
    H --> A
```

**Interpretation:** The BPO engagement is **not a one-time contract**; it is a cyclical lifecycle. Each phase has its own deliverables, KPIs, and risks. The KTU syllabus calls this the **"BPO Engagement Lifecycle"** and it appears in nearly every Module 3 question paper.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram 2: BPO Decision-Making Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Process Under Review] --> Q1{Is the process core to firm strategy?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> KeepInHouse[Retain In-House and Protect IP]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Can the process be defined with clear rules and metrics?}
    Q2 -- No --> Reengineer[Re-engineer First then Re-evaluate]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Is there a mature vendor market with proven capability?}
    Q3 -- No --> DevelopInHouse[Build Internal Capability]
    Q3 -- Yes --> Q4{Is the Total Cost of Ownership lower with vendor?}
    Q4 -- No --> KeepInHouse
    Q4 -- Yes --> Q5{Is the data sensitivity and risk acceptable?}
    Q5 -- No --> KeepInHouse
    Q5 -- Yes --> Outsource[Proceed with BPO Engagement]
    Outsource --> SLA[Draft SLA with KPIs and Penalties]
    SLA --> Pilot[Run Pilot Parallel Run]
    Pilot --> Scale[Scale to Full Production]
```

**Interpretation:** This decision tree follows the classical **make-or-buy** flow used in production management. The student should memorise the **five-gate filter** for a 14-mark answer.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram 3: BPO Service Delivery Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Client[Client Organization] -->|SLA and Work Orders| Vendor[BPO Vendor]
    Vendor --> KT[Knowledge Transfer Team]
    Vendor --> ProcessA[Process A: Voice Support]
    Vendor --> ProcessB[Process B: Back Office Data]
    Vendor --> ProcessC[Process C: IT Helpdesk]
    KT --> ProcessA
    KT --> ProcessB
    KT --> ProcessC
    ProcessA --> QA[Quality and Compliance Cell]
    ProcessB --> QA
    ProcessC --> QA
    QA --> Reports[Daily Weekly Monthly Reports]
    Reports --> Client
    Client -->|Feedback and Change Requests| Vendor
```
