**Interpretation:** This block diagram shows the **closed-loop feedback system** between client and vendor. Notice that the *Quality and Compliance Cell* is a separate vertical that audits all processes. This is a hallmark of mature BPO governance.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram 4: BPO Cost Composition Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TCO[Total Cost of Ownership TCO] --> Direct[Direct Outsourcing Cost]
    TCO --> Transition[Transition and KT Cost]
    TCO --> Governance[Governance and Monitoring Cost]
    TCO --> Hidden[Hidden Costs Rework and Quality Issues]
    Direct --> PerSeat[Per Seat or CPT Pricing]
    Direct --> Volume[Volume Based Charges]
    Transition --> Training[Vendor Training]
    Transition --> Tools[IT Integration and Tools]
    Governance --> FTEManager[Client FTE for Vendor Management]
    Governance --> Audit[External Audits]
    Hidden --> Rework[Process Errors and Rework]
    Hidden --> Penalty[SLA Penalties]
```

**Interpretation:** The **TCO model** is critical. Students who quote only the vendor's per-seat cost lose 3 marks. The full TCO includes **transition, governance, and hidden rework costs**, which can be **15%–25%** of the headline price.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define Business Process Outsourcing (BPO). List any four types of BPO.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** BPO is the contracting of a specific, well-defined business process to an external service provider that assumes operational responsibility under a Service Level Agreement.
- **Four Types (2 marks):**
  1. **Back-Office BPO** – Internal processes like payroll, HR, finance.
  2. **Front-Office BPO** – Customer-facing processes like call centres.
  3. **Domestic BPO** – Vendor located in the same country.
  4. **Offshore BPO** – Vendor located in a distant country (e.g., India serving the USA).
  - *(Optional 5th/6th: Nearshore, KPO, LPO.)*

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**What is a Service Level Agreement (SLA) in the context of BPO? Mention two key components.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** An SLA is a formal, contractually binding agreement between the client and the BPO vendor that defines the *scope*, *deliverables*, *KPIs*, *service credits*, and *escalation procedures* for the outsourced process.
- **Two Key Components (2 marks):**
  1. **Performance Metrics** – e.g., Service Level (80/20), FCR %, AHT.
  2. **Penalty / Service Credit Clause** – financial deduction if KPIs are missed.
  - *(Other valid components: reporting frequency, change management process, exit clause.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – KTU Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]*

> **(a)** Explain in detail the **strategic reasons** why organisations adopt BPO. Discuss the *make-or-buy framework* with a suitable example. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A company currently processes 3,00,000 transactions per year in-house with 10 employees earning ₹3,60,000 each. Overheads are 30% of salary. A BPO vendor quotes ₹15 per transaction plus a one-time transition cost of ₹3,00,000.
> Calculate:
> 1. Total In-House Cost.
> 2. Total BPO Cost (Year 1 and Year 2+).
> 3. Savings % in Year 2+.
> 4. Payback period on the transition cost. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A**

#### **Part (a) – 7 marks**

**Strategic Reasons (3.5 marks):**
1. **Cost Reduction:** Conversion of fixed costs to variable costs, economies of scale, labour arbitrage. Example – Indian BPOs serve US banks at 40%–60% lower cost.
2. **Focus on Core Competency:** Following Prahalad & Hamel's framework, firms must invest in activities that create unique customer value.
3. **Access to Specialised Skills & Technology:** Vendors bring CRM, ERP, analytics, and 24×7 capability.
4. **Scalability & Flexibility:** Vendor absorbs demand peaks (e.g., tax season for payroll BPO).
5. **Risk Diversification:** Operational and geographic risks are shared.

**Make-or-Buy Framework (3.5 marks):**
- **Definition:** A make-or-buy analysis is a strategic decision whether to produce a good/service internally (make) or purchase it from an external supplier (buy/BPO).
- **Quantitative factors:** Cost comparison, break-even volume.
- **Qualitative factors:** Strategic sensitivity, confidentiality, vendor maturity.
- **Example:** A hospital may outsource its *medical billing* (non-core, rules-based) but retain *clinical decision-making* (core, high-skill).

> [!NOTE]
> **Board-marking tip:** Always end the 7-mark answer with a *concluding recommendation* sentence — e.g., "Hence BPO is justified when the process is non-core, rules-based, has mature vendors, and shows a positive TCO differential."

---

#### **Part (b) – 7 marks**

**Given:** Volume = 3,00,000 transactions/year; FTE = 10; Salary = ₹3,60,000; Overhead = 30%; CPT (vendor) = ₹15; Transition cost = ₹3,00,000.

**Step 1 — Total In-House Cost (TIC) (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Salary} &= 10 \times 3{,}60{,}000 = \text{₹ } 36{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{Overhead} &= 0.30 \times 36{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹ } 10{,}80{,}000 \\
\text{TIC} &= 36{,}00{,}000 + 10{,}80{,}000 = \text{₹ } 46{,}80{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Total BPO Cost (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Year 1 TBC} &= (3{,}00{,}000 \times 15) + 3{,}00{,}000 = 45{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹ } 48{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{Year 2+ TBC} &= 3{,}00{,}000 \times 15 = \text{₹ } 45{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Savings % in Year 2+ (1.5 marks):**

$$
\text{Savings \%} = \frac{46{,}80{,}000 - 45{,}00{,}000}{46{,}80{,}000} \times 100 = \frac{1{,}80{,}000}{46{,}80{,}000} \times 100 \approx 3.85\%
$$

**Step 4 — Payback Period (1.5 marks):**

$$
\text{Annual Savings} = 46{,}80{,}000 - 45{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹ } 1{,}80{,}000
$$

$$
\text{Payback} = \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{1{,}80{,}000} \approx 1.67 \text{ years}
$$

**Final Recommendation:** The firm should outsource — the savings in Year 2+ are positive, and the transition cost is recovered in under 2 years. However, the margin is thin (3.85%), so the firm should negotiate CPT below ₹15 to widen the buffer.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

> **(a)** Describe the **BPO engagement lifecycle** with a neat block diagram. Explain the importance of the *Knowledge Transfer (KT)* phase. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** An inbound BPO centre receives 5,00,000 calls/year. AHT = 300 seconds. Working days = 280. Hours per shift = 8. Assume 80% occupancy.
> 1. Calculate the FTE required.
> 2. If each FTE costs the vendor ₹25,000/month fully loaded, calculate the annual vendor cost.
> 3. If the same work is done in-house at the same FTE, what is the per-call cost? **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B**

#### **Part (a) – 7 marks**

**BPO Engagement Lifecycle (5 marks):** Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 and explain each phase (1 mark each for 5 phases; 2 marks for the diagram).

1. **Strategic Decision:** Identify processes for outsourcing based on make-or-buy analysis.
2. **Vendor Selection:** Issue RFP, evaluate bids, shortlist vendors.
3. **Contract Negotiation:** Sign Master Service Agreement (MSA) and SLA.
4. **Knowledge Transfer (KT):** Vendor team is trained on client processes, tools, and quality standards — *typically 3 to 6 months*.
5. **Transition & Pilot:** Parallel run, then full go-live.
6. **Steady State & Continuous Improvement:** Process optimisation, automation, and value-add.
7. **Exit / Renewal:** As per contract terms.

**Importance of Knowledge Transfer (2 marks):**
- KT is the *foundation of service quality*. Poor KT leads to errors, escalations, and SLA breaches.
- It includes SOPs, process maps, tool training, security protocols, and shadowing.
- A well-executed KT reduces the *stabilisation period* and improves vendor productivity by 30%–40%.

---

#### **Part (b) – 7 marks**

**Step 1 — FTE Required (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Call-Seconds} &= 5{,}00{,}000 \times 300 = 15{,}00{,}00{,}000 \text{ seconds} \\
\text{Convert to hours} &= \frac{15{,}00{,}00{,}000}{3600} = 41{,}666.67 \text{ hours/year} \\
\text{Productive Hours per FTE} &= 280 \times 8 \times 0.80 = 1{,}792 \text{ hours} \\
\text{FTE} &= \frac{41{,}666.67}{1{,}792} \approx 23.25 \approx 24 \text{ FTE (rounded up)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Annual Vendor Cost (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Annual Cost per FTE} &= 25{,}000 \times 12 = \text{₹ } 3{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{Total Annual Vendor Cost} &= 24 \times 3{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹ } 72{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Per-Call Cost In-House (2 marks):**

$$
\text{Cost per Call} = \frac{72{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} = \text{₹ } 14.40 \text{ per call}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting occupancy/shrinkage:** If you calculate FTE without applying 80% occupancy, your FTE will be artificially low (~21), and the per-call cost will be wrong by ~15%. Always show the occupancy line.
> 2. **Forgetting transition cost in Year 1:** In Question A, students often compare Year 1 TBC without including the ₹3,00,000 transition cost. This earns only 4 of 7 marks.
> 3. **Confusing BPO with KPO/IT Outsourcing:** BPO is *process execution*, not *software development*. Writing IT-outsourcing examples in a BPO answer loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Writing make-or-buy without TCO:** Students quote only headline CPT. Always mention **TCO = Direct + Transition + Governance + Hidden**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-second revision — memorise these bullets before the exam:**

- ✅ **BPO = contracting a business process** to a third-party vendor under an SLA.
- ✅ **Two main types:** Back-Office (internal) and Front-Office (customer-facing).
- ✅ **Three geographic types:** Domestic, Nearshore, Offshore.
- ✅ **BPO ≠ KPO ≠ LPO:** BPO is rule-based; KPO is knowledge-based; LPO is legal-specific.
- ✅ **Five strategic reasons:** Cost, Focus, Capability, Scalability, Risk-sharing.
- ✅ **Make-or-Buy framework** uses **5-gate filter:** Core? Definable? Vendor market? TCO favourable? Risk acceptable?
- ✅ **BPO Lifecycle has 7 phases** — Strategic → Vendor Selection → Contract → KT → Transition → Steady State → Exit.
- ✅ **KT phase = 3 to 6 months** and is the most critical success factor.
- ✅ **Key formulas:** TIC, TBC, Q* (break-even), Savings %, FTE, Per-Call Cost, Payback.
- ✅ **TCO = Direct + Transition + Governance + Hidden costs** — never quote only vendor headline.
- ✅ **SLA KPIs:** Service Level %, FCR %, AHT, CSAT, NPS.
- ✅ **Standard benchmarks:** FCR = 70–80%, Service Level = 80/20, Occupancy = 80–85%.
- ✅ **Contract length:** 5–7 years; typical break-clause at 2–3 years.
- ✅ **Top BPO risks:** Loss of control, data breach, hidden costs, vendor failure, employee morale.
- ✅ **Indian BPO context:** India is the global leader in offshore BPO with cities like Bengaluru, Hyderabad, and Kochi (Infopark, Technopark) as hubs.
- ✅ **1 FTE = 2080 productive hours/year** (or 176 hours/month, 8 hours/day).
- ✅ **For a 14-mark answer,** always end with a **concluding recommendation sentence.**

---

**End of KTU-Premier-Engine V10 Notes — BPO, Module 3.**
*Prepared for APJ Abdul Kalam Technological University, 2024 Scheme — B.Tech OEIET723 Production and Operations Management.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Objectives of PAC
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET723 - PRODUCTION AND OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Objectives of PAC

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Production Activity Control (PAC)** is the functional domain within a Manufacturing Resource Planning (MRP II) or Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP) system that is responsible for the **execution, monitoring, and control of manufacturing operations** in a shop floor environment. It acts as the critical bridge between the planning layer (Master Production Schedule / MRP) and the physical shop floor execution.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, PAC is defined as the **set of functions that plan, release, dispatch, schedule, and monitor shop floor orders** in a manufacturing enterprise, with the overarching aim of translating the static, time-phased plan generated by MRP into actual, value-added, physical production.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** PAC is the *short-range, detailed scheduling and execution* subsystem that takes the planned orders from MRP and converts them into released shop orders, ensuring that the right materials, in the right quantities, are processed on the right machines, at the right time, in the right sequence.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are the **conductor of a large orchestra**:
- The **composer** is the Master Production Schedule (MPS) — he writes the music.
- The **sheet music in front of each musician** is the MRP plan — it tells each player what notes to play.
- **You, the conductor**, are the **PAC system** — you control the tempo, decide when the violinist starts, when the percussionist comes in, monitor if anyone is missing a beat, and ensure the entire concert finishes on time.

Without a conductor, even the best orchestra would collapse into chaos. Similarly, without PAC, even the best production plan would never materialize on the shop floor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:** PAC does NOT create the production plan. It **executes** the plan created by MRP/MPS. Remember the flow: **MPS → MRP → PAC → Shop Floor**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics in PAC (KTU High-Yield Terms)

The following performance metrics are critical to evaluating PAC objectives:

- **Setup Time:** Time required to prepare a machine for a new job.
- **Cycle Time:** Time required to produce one unit from start to finish.
- **Throughput Time:** Total time a job spends in the system (waiting + processing).
- **Work-In-Process (WIP):** Units that have started but not yet completed production.
- **Due Date Performance / Schedule Attainment:** Percentage of orders completed on or before the due date.
- **Machine Utilization:** Percentage of available time a machine is actively productive.

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** When answering KTU questions, always relate PAC objectives to either **cost**, **delivery**, **quality**, or **flexibility** — these are the four pillars of modern manufacturing competitiveness.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Strategic Position of PAC in the Production Hierarchy

To understand the *objectives* of PAC, it is essential to first locate PAC within the **Production Planning & Control (PPC) hierarchy**. PAC operates at the **execution level**, the lowest and most operational tier.

The hierarchy flows as follows:

- **Level 1 — Strategic:** Business Plan & Aggregate Production Plan
- **Level 2 — Tactical:** Master Production Schedule (MPS)
- **Level 3 — Operational Planning:** Material Requirements Planning (MRP)
- **Level 4 — Execution (PAC):** Order Release, Scheduling, Dispatching, Monitoring, Corrective Action

## 2.2 Core Objectives of PAC — Structured Analysis

The objectives of PAC can be organized into **six functional clusters**. Each cluster addresses a specific operational concern on the shop floor.

### Objective 1: Effective Utilization of Resources (Machines, Labor, Materials)

- **The "Why":** Idle machines and idle labor represent a direct loss of invested capital. A machine sitting unused, or a worker waiting for the next assignment, is generating cost without generating revenue.
- **The "How":** PAC achieves this by **load leveling** — distributing the workload evenly across all work centers based on available capacity. It ensures no machine is overloaded (causing bottlenecks) and no machine is underloaded (causing idle time).

### Objective 2: Meeting Delivery Schedules / Due Date Performance

- **The "Why":** In a market economy, **on-time delivery (OTD)** is a primary competitive differentiator. Missing a delivery date often incurs **penalty clauses, lost future orders, and damaged customer trust**.
- **The "How":** PAC uses **priority sequencing rules** (e.g., Earliest Due Date, Critical Ratio, Slack per Operation) to ensure that jobs with the most urgent customer commitments are processed first.

### Objective 3: Minimizing Work-In-Process (WIP) Inventory

- **The "Why":** WIP inventory ties up working capital, occupies valuable floor space, and is at risk of damage, obsolescence, or quality degradation. The **Little's Law** (from Queueing Theory) establishes the mathematical link between WIP and flow time.
- **The "How":** PAC minimizes WIP by **synchronizing operations** — ensuring the output of one work center feeds the next without unnecessary waiting. This is achieved through **finite capacity scheduling** and **kanban-style pull triggers** (in JIT environments).

### Objective 4: Reducing Manufacturing Lead Time / Cycle Time

- **The "Why":** Shorter lead times mean faster response to customer orders, lower safety stock requirements, and improved competitiveness.
- **The "How":** PAC reduces lead time by **eliminating non-value-added activities** (transportation, waiting, inspection queues) and by **optimizing the sequence** of operations to minimize setups and queuing delays.

### Objective 5: Maintaining and Improving Product Quality

- **The "Why":** Defects discovered late in the production process are exponentially more expensive to rework or scrap. Quality issues on the shop floor disrupt the entire PAC schedule.
- **The "How":** PAC integrates **Statistical Process Control (SPC) checkpoints** at critical work centers. By detecting deviations early and triggering **corrective action**, PAC prevents defective output from propagating downstream.

### Objective 6: Cost Reduction and Operational Flexibility

- **The "Why":** Every shop floor decision has a cost implication. Setup time, overtime, expedited shipments, and rework all inflate manufacturing cost.
- **The "How":** PAC provides the **real-time visibility** needed to detect problems early, when corrective action is cheapest. It also enables **quick re-prioritization** when disruptions occur (e.g., a machine breakdown, a rush order, a missing component).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table summarizes the key mathematical relationships that govern PAC performance. These formulas are frequently tested in KTU university examinations.

| Metric | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Work-In-Process (Little's Law) | $WIP = TH \times T$ | $TH$ = Throughput Rate, $T$ = Average Flow Time | Units |
| Throughput Time | $T = T_p + T_w + T_q$ | $T_p$ = Processing, $T_w$ = Wait, $T_q$ = Queue | Hours |
| Machine Utilization | $U = \dfrac{\text{Active Time}}{\text{Available Time}} \times 100$ | Percentage of capacity actually used | % |
| Overall Equipment Effectiveness | $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ | $A$=Availability, $P$=Performance, $Q$=Quality | Decimal (0–1) |
| Setup-to-Cycle Ratio | $SCR = \dfrac{\text{Setup Time}}{\text{Cycle Time}}$ | Indicator for group technology / SMED suitability | Ratio |
| Critical Ratio (Priority Rule) | $CR = \dfrac{D - T}{R}$ | $D$=Due Date, $T$=Today, $R$=Remaining Process Time | Ratio |
| On-Time Delivery (OTD) | $OTD = \dfrac{\text{Orders On Time}}{\text{Total Orders Shipped}} \times 100$ | PAC effectiveness indicator | % |
| Capacity Load | $CL = \dfrac{\text{Required Hours}}{\text{Available Hours}} \times 100$ | Work center load percentage | % |
| Manufacturing Lead Time | $MLT = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(T_{setup_i} + T_{run_i} + T_{queue_i})$ | Total time from order release to completion | Days |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL:** In markdown tables, the vertical bar `|` is reserved as the column separator. Mathematical expressions that contain absolute values or conditional logic must use `\vert` or `\mid` (e.g., $\vert x \vert$ instead of $|x|$).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In modern manufacturing industries, the objectives of PAC are not merely academic — they drive measurable business outcomes:

- **Automotive Industry (e.g., Maruti Suzuki, Toyota):** PAC objectives are embedded in the **Toyota Production System (TPS)**. Minimizing WIP (Objective 3) is operationalized through the *kanban* system, while reducing lead time (Objective 4) is the central tenet of *just-in-time* manufacturing.
- **Electronics Manufacturing (e.g., Flex, Foxconn):** Meeting delivery schedules (Objective 2) is critical due to the rapid obsolescence of consumer electronics. PAC systems are integrated with **MES (Manufacturing Execution Systems)** to provide real-time shop floor visibility.
- **Process Industries (e.g., refineries, pharmaceuticals):** Maintaining quality (Objective 5) is paramount due to regulatory compliance. PAC integrates with **LIMS (Laboratory Information Management Systems)** to track batch quality attributes.
- **Aerospace & Defense (e.g., HAL, Boeing):** Effective resource utilization (Objective 1) is critical due to the high value of aerospace-grade materials and the long production cycles.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Logical Walkthroughs

## 3.1 Derivation: Little's Law and its Application to PAC

**Little's Law** is a foundational theorem in queueing theory that is directly applied to quantify the WIP reduction objective of PAC. Below is the step-by-step derivation and its operational meaning.

### Step 1: Defining the Variables

Let us define the variables used in the derivation:

$$TH = \text{Throughput Rate (units per unit time)}$$

$$T = \text{Average Flow Time (time per unit)}$$

$$WIP = \text{Work-In-Process Inventory (units)}$$

### Step 2: Intuitive Setup

Consider a stable production system that has been operating for a long time. In such a system, the number of units entering per unit time must equal the number of units leaving per unit time (otherwise, WIP would be growing or shrinking indefinitely). This is the **steady-state assumption**.

### Step 3: Dimensionless Analysis

We observe that:

$$\text{Units of } WIP = \left(\frac{\text{Units}}{\text{Time}}\right) \times \text{Time}$$

This is dimensionally consistent. Now we express this mathematically.

### Step 4: Formal Statement

For any stable production system in steady state, the work-in-process inventory is given by:

$$WIP = TH \times T$$

### Step 5: Operational Implication for PAC

This equation is **profound** for PAC. It states that if PAC wants to reduce WIP, it has only two levers:

- **Reduce Throughput Time ($T$):** This is the primary PAC lever. By reducing waiting, queueing, and transportation time, PAC can directly reduce WIP.
- **Reduce Throughput Rate ($TH$):** This is undesirable in most cases, as it means slower production.

Therefore, **the only practical way to achieve PAC's WIP-minimization objective is to reduce flow time**.

### Step 6: Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A manufacturing cell has an average throughput of 50 units per day. The average flow time for a unit is 4 days. Calculate the steady-state WIP.

**Solution:**

$$WIP = TH \times T$$

$$WIP = 50 \, \text{units/day} \times 4 \, \text{days}$$

$$WIP = 200 \, \text{units}$$

**Verification by Intuition:** Each day, 50 units enter and 50 units leave. At any instant, we see 4 days worth of units in the system. 4 days × 50 units/day = 200 units. ✓

## 3.2 Derivation: The Critical Ratio (CR) Priority Sequencing Rule

The Critical Ratio is one of the most tested PAC decision rules in KTU exams. It is a dynamic priority indicator used by PAC to determine which job should be processed next at a work center.

### Step 1: Definition

The Critical Ratio is defined as:

$$CR = \frac{D - T}{R}$$

Where:
- $D$ = Due Date (the date the job is promised to the customer)
- $T$ = Today's Date (the current date)
- $R$ = Remaining Process Time (the time still needed to complete the job)

### Step 2: Interpretation Logic

| CR Value | Interpretation | PAC Action |
|---|---|---|
| $CR > 1$ | Job is ahead of schedule | Low priority — can be deferred |
| $CR = 1$ | Job is exactly on schedule | Process as planned |
| $CR < 1$ | Job is behind schedule | High priority — expedite |
| $CR \leq 0$ | Job is overdue or impossible | Critical — take corrective action |

### Step 3: Step-by-Step Worked Example

**Problem:** Three jobs (J1, J2, J3) are waiting at a work center. The data is given below. Today is Day 50. Determine the priority sequence using the Critical Ratio rule.

| Job | Due Date (Day) | Remaining Process Time (Days) |
|---|---|---|
| J1 | 60 | 5 |
| J2 | 55 | 8 |
| J3 | 65 | 10 |

**Step 3a: Calculate CR for J1**

$$CR_{J1} = \frac{D_1 - T}{R_1} = \frac{60 - 50}{5} = \frac{10}{5} = 2.0$$

**Step 3b: Calculate CR for J2**

$$CR_{J2} = \frac{D_2 - T}{R_2} = \frac{55 - 50}{8} = \frac{5}{8} = 0.625$$

**Step 3c: Calculate CR for J3**

$$CR_{J3} = \frac{D_3 - T}{R_3} = \frac{65 - 50}{10} = \frac{15}{10} = 1.5$$

**Step 3d: Rank by CR (Lowest CR = Highest Priority)**

- J2: CR = 0.625 → **First**
- J3: CR = 1.5 → **Second**
- J1: CR = 2.0 → **Third**

**Conclusion:** The PAC system should process the jobs in the sequence **J2 → J3 → J1**, since J2 has the lowest CR and is therefore the most urgent.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why CR is Superior to Simple Due Date Sequencing:** The CR rule not only considers the due date but also accounts for the work *remaining*. A job due tomorrow but needing only 1 hour of work is less urgent than a job due in 3 days but needing 2 days of work. CR captures this nuance.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: How Each PAC Objective Maps to a PPC Function

The following matrix maps the six PAC objectives to their corresponding functional activities. This is a high-yield visualization for KTU 14-mark answers.

| PAC Objective | Primary PAC Function | Tool/Method | KPI Impacted |
|---|---|---|---|
| Effective Resource Utilization | Load Leveling & Capacity Planning | Finite Capacity Scheduling | Machine Utilization % |
| Meeting Delivery Schedules | Order Release & Dispatching | Priority Sequencing (CR, EDD) | On-Time Delivery % |
| Minimizing WIP Inventory | Synchronization of Operations | Kanban / Pull Systems | Little's Law $WIP$ |
| Reducing Lead Time | Setup Reduction | SMED, Group Technology | $T_{cycle}$ |
| Maintaining Quality | In-Process Inspection | Statistical Process Control | First-Pass Yield % |
| Cost Reduction & Flexibility | Real-Time Monitoring | Shop Floor Data Collection | Cost per Unit |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Position of PAC in the Production Planning Hierarchy

The following block-level architecture flow illustrates where PAC sits in the overall production planning structure of a manufacturing enterprise.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["LEVEL 1 STRATEGIC BUSINESS PLAN"] --> A2["LEVEL 2 AGGREGATE PRODUCTION PLAN"]
    A2 --> A3["LEVEL 3 MASTER PRODUCTION SCHEDULE MPS"]
    A3 --> A4["LEVEL 4 MATERIAL REQUIREMENTS PLANNING MRP"]
    A4 --> A5["LEVEL 5 PRODUCTION ACTIVITY CONTROL PAC"]
    A5 --> A6["LEVEL 6 SHOP FLOOR EXECUTION"]
    A6 --> A7["FINISHED GOODS INVENTORY"]
    A7 --> A8["CUSTOMER DELIVERY"]
    A8 -.-> A1
    A5 -.->|FEEDBACK LOOP| A3
    A5 -.->|FEEDBACK LOOP| A4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Read this diagram from top to bottom.** Notice the **two feedback loops** from the shop floor (via PAC) back to MPS and MRP. These loops are what make the system *self-correcting* — when PAC detects schedule slippage, the information is fed back to planning for re-prioritization.

## 4.2 The Six Objectives of PAC — Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram visualizes the six primary objectives of PAC and their interdependencies. Each objective is shown as a node, and the arrows indicate the causal relationships between them.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1["RESOURCE UTILIZATION"] 
    B2["DELIVERY SCHEDULES"]
    B3["WIP REDUCTION"]
    B4["LEAD TIME REDUCTION"]
    B5["QUALITY MAINTENANCE"]
    B6["COST REDUCTION"]
    
    B1 --> B3
    B1 --> B6
    B2 --> B6
    B3 --> B4
    B3 --> B6
    B4 --> B2
    B4 --> B6
    B5 --> B6
    B5 --> B3
    
    B1 -.->|ENABLES| B2
    B5 -.->|ENABLES| B2
```

**How to read this diagram:**
- **Solid arrows (→)** indicate **direct causal influence**. For example, WIP reduction directly leads to lead time reduction.
- **Dotted arrows (-.→)** indicate **enabling influence**. For example, high quality on the shop floor enables the PAC system to meet delivery schedules (since no time is wasted on rework).

## 4.3 The PAC Operational Cycle — Inputs, Processes, Outputs

The following schematic shows the input-process-output structure of a typical PAC system.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1["INPUT: PLANNED ORDERS FROM MRP"] 
    C2["INPUT: ROUTING SHEETS AND BILL OF MATERIALS"]
    C3["INPUT: WORK CENTER CAPACITY DATA"]
    
    C1 --> D1["ORDER RELEASE"]
    C2 --> D1
    C3 --> D2["LOAD PLANNING"]
    
    D1 --> D3["DISPATCHING"]
    D2 --> D3
    
    D3 --> D4["EXECUTING ON SHOP FLOOR"]
    D4 --> D5["MONITORING AND PROGRESS REPORTING"]
    D5 --> D6{"IS SCHEDULE ON TRACK?"}
    
    D6 -->|YES| E1["CONTINUE EXECUTION"]
    D6 -->|NO| E2["CORRECTIVE ACTION AND RESCHEDULING"]
    E2 --> D1
    
    D5 --> F1["OUTPUT: COMPLETED ORDERS"]
    D5 --> F2["OUTPUT: PERFORMANCE METRICS"]
    D5 --> F3["OUTPUT: FEEDBACK TO MRP AND MPS"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** This PAC cycle is essentially a **closed-loop control system**, very similar to a feedback control loop in engineering. The "Plan-Do-Check-Act" (PDCA) Deming cycle is embedded within it. Mentioning this analogy can earn bonus marks in KTU 14-mark descriptive answers.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q: Define Production Activity Control (PAC). List any four of its primary objectives.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Production Activity Control (PAC) is the execution-level function within a Manufacturing Resource Planning (MRP II) system that is responsible for releasing, scheduling, dispatching, and monitoring shop floor orders to ensure that the production plan generated by MRP is physically executed in the most efficient manner possible.

**Four Primary Objectives of PAC:**

1. **Effective Utilization of Resources** — Ensuring machines, labor, and materials are used optimally with minimal idle time.
2. **Meeting Delivery Schedules** — Ensuring customer orders are completed on or before the promised due date.
3. **Minimizing Work-In-Process (WIP) Inventory** — Reducing the number of partially completed units in the system to free up working capital and floor space.
4. **Reducing Manufacturing Lead Time** — Decreasing the total time required to convert raw materials into finished goods.

*(Any four from the six objectives are acceptable.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q: Explain the role of the Critical Ratio (CR) as a priority sequencing rule in Production Activity Control.**

**Model Answer:**

The Critical Ratio (CR) is a dynamic priority indicator used in PAC to determine the sequence in which jobs waiting at a work center should be processed. It is calculated as:

$$CR = \frac{D - T}{R}$$

Where $D$ is the due date, $T$ is today's date, and $R$ is the remaining process time.

**Interpretation in PAC:**

- A **low CR value** indicates that the job is **behind schedule** and should be given **high priority** (expedited).
- A **CR value greater than 1** indicates the job is **ahead of schedule** and can be **deferred** in favor of more urgent jobs.
- A **CR value less than or equal to 0** indicates the job is **overdue** and requires **immediate corrective action**.

The CR rule is superior to a simple earliest-due-date (EDD) rule because it accounts for both the urgency of the due date *and* the work remaining. This makes it a more accurate measure of true job urgency in PAC decision-making.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Descriptive Questions (with Internal Choice)

### Question Choice A (14 Marks) — **Module 4 Internal Choice Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**

**Q: (a)** Discuss in detail the **six primary objectives of Production Activity Control (PAC)**. Explain how each objective contributes to overall manufacturing competitiveness. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A work center has three jobs waiting to be processed. The due date is Day 60 for all jobs, and today is Day 50. The remaining process times are: Job A = 5 days, Job B = 12 days, Job C = 8 days. Using the **Critical Ratio (CR) rule**, determine the **sequence of processing** at this work center. Justify your answer. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

The six primary objectives of Production Activity Control (PAC) are as follows:

**1. Effective Utilization of Resources (≈1.5 marks):**
PAC aims to maximize the productive use of all shop floor resources — machines, labor, and materials. It uses techniques like **load leveling** and **finite capacity scheduling** to ensure that no work center is overloaded (which causes bottlenecks) or underloaded (which causes idle time). High resource utilization directly translates to lower per-unit production cost.

**2. Meeting Delivery Schedules (≈1.5 marks):**
PAC uses **priority sequencing rules** (such as Critical Ratio, Earliest Due Date, Slack per Operation) to ensure that jobs with the most urgent customer commitments are processed first. High on-time delivery performance is a key competitive differentiator and directly impacts customer satisfaction and retention.

**3. Minimizing Work-In-Process (WIP) Inventory (≈1 mark):**
By synchronizing the output of upstream work centers with the input requirements of downstream work centers, PAC reduces the accumulation of partially completed goods. Per **Little's Law** ($WIP = TH \times T$), WIP is directly proportional to flow time. PAC reduces WIP by attacking flow time.

**4. Reducing Manufacturing Lead Time (≈1 mark):**
PAC reduces the time from order release to order completion by eliminating non-value-added activities (waiting, transportation, excess queuing) and by optimizing the sequence of operations. Shorter lead times enable faster response to customer orders and lower safety stock requirements.

**5. Maintaining and Improving Product Quality (≈1 mark):**
PAC integrates quality checkpoints (often through **Statistical Process Control**) into the production schedule. By detecting deviations early, PAC prevents defective units from progressing downstream, where rework costs are exponentially higher.

**6. Cost Reduction and Operational Flexibility (≈1 mark):**
PAC provides real-time visibility into shop floor status, enabling early detection of disruptions (machine breakdowns, material shortages, rush orders). Early detection allows for cheaper corrective action. PAC also enables rapid re-prioritization, which is essential for operational flexibility.

**Conclusion:** Collectively, these six objectives align the shop floor execution with strategic business goals — delivering products *faster*, *cheaper*, *better*, and *more reliably* than competitors.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Given Data:**

| Parameter | Job A | Job B | Job C |
|---|---|---|---|
| Due Date ($D$) | Day 60 | Day 60 | Day 60 |
| Today ($T$) | Day 50 | Day 50 | Day 50 |
| Remaining Process Time ($R$) | 5 days | 12 days | 8 days |

**Step 1: Recall the CR Formula**

$$CR = \frac{D - T}{R}$$

**Step 2: Calculate CR for Job A**

$$CR_A = \frac{60 - 50}{5} = \frac{10}{5} = 2.0$$

**Step 3: Calculate CR for Job B**

$$CR_B = \frac{60 - 50}{12} = \frac{10}{12} \approx 0.833$$

**Step 4: Calculate CR for Job C**

$$CR_C = \frac{60 - 50}{8} = \frac{10}{8} = 1.25$$

**Step 5: Rank in Order of Increasing CR (Lowest CR = Highest Priority)**

- **Job B:** CR ≈ 0.833 → **Highest Priority (First)**
- **Job C:** CR = 1.25 → **Second Priority**
- **Job A:** CR = 2.0 → **Lowest Priority (Third)**

**Step 6: Recommended PAC Processing Sequence**

$$\text{Job B} \rightarrow \text{Job C} \rightarrow \text{Job A}$$

**Justification:** All three jobs have the same due date, but Job B has the largest remaining work (12 days). If Job B is not started immediately, it is most likely to miss its due date. The CR rule correctly identifies Job B as the most urgent, even though all due dates are identical. This demonstrates the superiority of CR over a simple FCFS (First-Come-First-Served) or EDD (Earliest Due Date) rule.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully!**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students often use the *earliest due date* rule when all due dates are the same, leading to no decision (FCFS by default). Examiners specifically test whether students understand that **CR accounts for remaining work** when due dates are tied. **Loss: 2–3 marks.**
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Students sometimes rank by *highest* CR instead of *lowest* CR. **Remember: Lower CR = Higher urgency = Process First.** **Loss: 2 marks.**
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Students forget to show the formula and simply write the final answer. Always show the formula, the substitution, and the calculation. **Loss: 1 mark for skipped working.**

---

### Question Choice B (14 Marks) — **Module 4 Internal Choice Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper]**

**Q: (a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **position of Production Activity Control (PAC) in the overall Production Planning and Control (PPC) hierarchy**. Describe the key functions performed by PAC. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturing cell produces 40 units per day. The average flow time of a unit through the cell is 6 days. The management wants to reduce WIP by 25% without changing the throughput rate. Calculate the **new flow time** required to achieve this target. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Block Diagram (≈2 marks):**

The PAC hierarchy flows from the strategic level down to the shop floor:

$$\text{Business Plan} \rightarrow \text{Aggregate Plan} \rightarrow \text{MPS} \rightarrow \text{MRP} \rightarrow \boxed{\text{PAC}} \rightarrow \text{Shop Floor} \rightarrow \text{Customer}$$

**Key Functions Performed by PAC (≈5 marks):**

1. **Order Release:** PAC takes the planned orders from MRP and releases them to the shop floor at the appropriate time, accounting for material availability and capacity readiness.
2. **Load Planning & Balancing:** PAC distributes the released work across work centers to balance the load, avoiding bottlenecks and idle time.
3. **Dispatching:** PAC issues dispatch lists (operation-by-operation instructions) to each work center, specifying which job to process next, on which machine, and in what priority.
4. **Expediting (Follow-Up):** PAC tracks the progress of each order and takes corrective action (overtime, re-routing, sub-contracting) when an order is falling behind schedule.
5. **Performance Reporting:** PAC generates shop floor performance metrics (utilization, OTD, WIP levels) and feeds this information back to MRP and MPS for replanning.
6. **Corrective Action:** When disruptions occur, PAC initiates recovery actions such as resequencing, lot splitting, or alternative routing.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Given Data:**

- Throughput Rate: $TH = 40$ units/day
- Initial Flow Time: $T_1 = 6$ days
- WIP Reduction Target: 25%

**Step 1: Apply Little's Law to Find Initial WIP**

$$WIP_1 = TH \times T_1 = 40 \times 6 = 240 \, \text{units}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Target WIP After 25% Reduction**

$$WIP_2 = WIP_1 \times (1 - 0.25) = 240 \times 0.75 = 180 \, \text{units}$$

**Step 3: Apply Little's Law to Find New Flow Time**

Since throughput rate must remain unchanged ($TH = 40$ units/day):

$$WIP_2 = TH \times T_2$$

$$180 = 40 \times T_2$$

$$T_2 = \frac{180}{40} = 4.5 \, \text{days}$$

**Step 4: Verification and Interpretation**

The flow time must be reduced from **6 days to 4.5 days** — a **25% reduction** in flow time. This is a direct demonstration of Little's Law: *to reduce WIP by 25% at constant throughput, the flow time must also be reduced by 25%.*

**Interpretation for PAC:** The PAC system must identify and eliminate non-value-added time totaling 1.5 days per unit. This can be achieved through **setup reduction (SMED)**, **queue reduction**, **process simplification**, or **layout optimization**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully!**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students confuse the percentage reduction. If WIP is reduced by 25%, flow time is also reduced by 25% (at constant throughput). Some students incorrectly multiply $T_1$ by 0.75 when the relationship is already linear. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Students forget to state Little's Law explicitly. Always **name the formula** before applying it. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Students omit the unit (days) in the final answer. Always include units. **Loss: 0.5 mark.**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

The following is a high-density, rapid-revision checklist for the topic **"Objectives of PAC"**.

- **PAC stands for Production Activity Control** — it is the *execution* layer of the PPC hierarchy, not the planning layer.
- **PAC sits between MRP and the Shop Floor** — it takes planned orders from MRP and converts them into physical production.
- **The Six Primary Objectives of PAC are:** (1) Effective Resource Utilization, (2) Meeting Delivery Schedules, (3) Minimizing WIP Inventory, (4) Reducing Lead Time, (5) Maintaining Quality, (6) Cost Reduction & Flexibility.
- **Little's Law** ($WIP = TH \times T$) is the foundational equation for the WIP-minimization objective.
- **Critical Ratio (CR)** is calculated as $CR = \frac{D - T}{R}$. **Lower CR = Higher Priority.**
- **PAC is a closed-loop control system** — it monitors execution and feeds information back to planning for replanning.
- **OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness)** is a key PAC metric: $OEE = \text{Availability} \times \text{Performance} \times \text{Quality}$.
- **On-Time Delivery (OTD)** is the most visible KPI of PAC effectiveness from the customer's perspective.
- **PAC objectives are not independent** — reducing WIP reduces lead time; better quality enables delivery; better utilization reduces cost. Always show these interconnections in 14-mark answers.
- **For KTU 14-mark answers:** Use **bullet structure**, **headings**, **formulas**, and **diagrams** (block diagrams or flowcharts) wherever possible. Examiners reward visual clarity and structured presentation.
- **Memorize the CR formula and its interpretation table** — it is the single most-tested quantitative concept in this topic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Scheduling Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET723 - PRODUCTION AND OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Scheduling Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 4 – Scheduling Techniques

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Scheduling?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> **Scheduling** is the process of allocating resources (machines, labour, facilities) to tasks over time to optimize one or more performance objectives such as **minimizing makespan, flow time, tardiness, or maximizing utilization and throughput**.

In simpler words, once we know *what* to make, *how much* to make, and *when* it is needed, scheduling answers the question:
**"Which job should be done on which machine, in what order, and at what time?"**

### 1.2 Scheduling vs Sequencing vs Planning

| Term | Meaning | Horizon |
|---|---|---|
| **Planning** | Deciding what, how much, and when to produce (aggregate) | Long term |
| **Sequencing** | Determining the *order* in which jobs will be processed | Medium term |
| **Scheduling** | Assigning specific start/end *times* to jobs on machines | Short term |

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy – The Airport Runway:**
> Imagine an airport with **two runways** and **five planes** waiting to land. Air traffic control must decide *which plane uses which runway, in what order, and for how long*. Their goal is to minimize total waiting time (flow time) so no plane circles forever burning fuel. Scheduling techniques like **Johnson's Rule** are the mathematical equivalent of this ATC decision — they are algorithms used every day in manufacturing, hospitals, airlines, and cloud computing.

### 1.4 Types of Production Scheduling Problems

| Problem Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **1-machine, n-jobs** | Single machine, multiple jobs | One doctor treating many patients |
| **2-machine, n-jobs** | Johnson's rule applies | Two-stage production line |
| **3-machine, n-jobs** | CDS heuristic | Three-stage process |
| **m-machine, n-jobs** | Flow shop, complex | Large assembly plant |
| **n-job, m-machine job shop** | Each job has unique route | Machine shop |
| **Project scheduling** | Network of activities | Construction |

### 1.5 Performance Measures (Objectives of Scheduling)

| Symbol | Measure | Definition | Goal |
|---|---|---|---|
| **F** | Flow time | Time job spends in system | Minimize |
| **F̄** | Average flow time | Sum F / n | Minimize |
| **T** | Tardiness | max(0, C − d) | Minimize |
| **L** | Lateness | C − d (can be negative) | Minimize |
| **Lmax** | Maximum lateness | max(Lᵢ) | Minimize |
| **Cmax** | Makespan | Completion time of last job | Minimize |
| **N̄** | Avg # jobs in system | (Σ F) / Cmax | Minimize |
| **U** | # tardy jobs | count(Tᵢ > 0) | Minimize |

> [!TIP]
> Where **Cᵢ** = completion time of job *i*, **dᵢ** = due date of job *i*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Basic Symbols Used in Scheduling

> [!NOTE]
> - **n** = number of jobs
> - **m** = number of machines
> - **pᵢ** (or **tᵢ**) = processing time of job *i* on a given machine
> - **rᵢ** = release date (ready time) of job *i*
> - **dᵢ** = due date (promised delivery) of job *i*
> - **wᵢ** = weight (priority) of job *i*
> - **Cᵢ** = completion time of job *i*
> - **Sᵢ** = start time of job *i*
> - **Fᵢ** = flow time = Cᵢ − rᵢ
> - **Lᵢ** = lateness = Cᵢ − dᵢ
> - **Tᵢ** = tardiness = max(0, Cᵢ − dᵢ)

### 2.2 Priority Dispatching Rules (for Single Machine)

These rules give the *priority* of jobs waiting in queue. They are simple, intuitive, and extremely common in KTU questions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Six Most Tested Priority Rules:**

1. **FCFS – First Come First Served**
   - Order jobs by their arrival/release time.
   - **Pros:** Fair. **Cons:** Often bad flow time.

2. **SPT – Shortest Processing Time**
   - Process the job with the smallest processing time next.
   - **Result (Rule):** SPT minimizes **Σ Fᵢ** (total flow time) and **F̄** (mean flow time). ✓ **Optimal!**

3. **LPT – Longest Processing Time**
   - Process longest job first.
   - **Result:** LPT minimizes **Σ wᵢCᵢ** when there are only **2 jobs**.

4. **EDD – Earliest Due Date**
   - Process job with smallest due date first.
   - **Result:** EDD minimizes **Lmax** (maximum lateness). ✓ **Optimal!**

5. **CR – Critical Ratio**
   - $CR_i = \dfrac{d_i - t}{p_i}$ (where *t* = current time)
   - Process job with **smallest** CR.
   - CR < 1 ⇒ job is "critical" (will be late).

6. **SLACK – Minimum Slack**
   - $Slack_i = d_i - t - p_i$
   - Process job with smallest slack first.

### 2.3 Sequencing Notation – Gantt Charts

A **Gantt chart** is a horizontal bar chart that visually maps jobs to machines along a time axis. It is the standard KTU tool for:
- Computing flow times
- Finding makespan
- Detecting idle time

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Purpose | Minimizes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_i = C_i - r_i$ | Flow time of job i | — |
| 2 | $\bar{F} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum F_i$ | Mean flow time | Minimize |
| 3 | $L_i = C_i - d_i$ | Lateness of job i | — |
| 4 | $T_i = \max(0,\, C_i - d_i)$ | Tardiness of job i | Minimize |
| 5 | $L_{max} = \max(L_i)$ | Max lateness | Minimize |
| 6 | $C_{max}$ | Makespan (last completion) | Minimize |
| 7 | $CR_i = \dfrac{d_i - t}{p_i}$ | Critical ratio | Schedule criterion |
| 8 | Johnson's Rule | 2-machine n-job sequencing | Cmax (makespan) |
| 9 | CDS Heuristic | 3-machine n-job sequencing | Cmax |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing cells:** Sequencing CNC operations to reduce queue time.
- **Hospitals:** OT scheduling using SPT rule.
- **Logistics:** Truck loading with EDD for dispatch.
- **Project management:** Job-shop scheduling in ERP (SAP, Oracle).
- **Cloud computing / OS:** CPU process scheduling (SPT = SJF algorithm).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Single Machine Scheduling – Worked Example (FCFS, SPT, EDD)

> [!NOTE]
> **Given:** 5 jobs with processing times and due dates.

| Job | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| p (hours) | 4 | 2 | 6 | 1 | 3 |
| d (hours) | 8 | 4 | 12 | 2 | 10 |

**Step A – Compute FCFS, SPT, EDD orderings:**

```
FCFS order : 1, 2, 3, 4, 5   (job numbers)
SPT  order : 4, 2, 5, 1, 3
EDD  order : 4, 2, 1, 5, 3
```

**Step B – Gantt Chart for SPT order:**

```
Time:    0 ── 1 ── 3 ── 6 ── 10 ── 16
         │ J4│ J2│ J5│ J1│ J3 │
```

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_4 &= 1, \quad C_2 = 3, \quad C_5 = 6, \quad C_1 = 10, \quad C_3 = 16 \\
F_4 &= 1, \quad F_2 = 3, \quad F_5 = 6, \quad F_1 = 10, \quad F_3 = 16 \\
L_4 &= 1-2 = -1, \; L_2 = 3-4 = -1, \; L_5 = 6-10 = -4 \\
L_1 &= 10-8 = +2, \; L_3 = 16-12 = +4 \\
L_{max} &= +4 \\
\bar F &= \dfrac{1+3+6+10+16}{5} = \dfrac{36}{5} = 7.2 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Observation:** SPT gives the *minimum ΣF* and *minimum F̄* but **not** the minimum Lmax. EDD gives minimum Lmax.

### 3.2 Johnson's Rule – 2 Machines, n Jobs (Detailed Derivation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Johnson's Algorithm (1954)** finds the sequence that minimizes total elapsed time (makespan, Cmax) for n jobs on 2 machines M1 and M2 where every job must be processed first on M1, then on M2.

#### The Algorithm

1. List processing times $(A_i, B_i)$ for each job *i* on M1 and M2.
2. Find the **smallest** processing time among all unreviewed entries.
3. **If** it is on M1 → place that job **as early as possible** (front of the sequence).
4. **If** it is on M2 → place that job **as late as possible** (back of the sequence).
5. Remove that job and repeat until all jobs are placed.

#### Numerical Example

**Given:** 4 jobs on 2 machines.

| Job | M1 | M2 |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 5 | 2 |
| 2 | 1 | 6 |
| 3 | 9 | 7 |
| 4 | 3 | 8 |
| 5 | 10 | 4 |

**Step-by-step application:**

```
Step 1: Minimum = 1  → Job 2 on M1 → place at FRONT.
        Sequence = [ 2, _, _, _, _ ]

Step 2: Remaining: (5,2),(9,7),(3,8),(10,4)
        Minimum = 2 → Job 1 on M2 → place at BACK.
        Sequence = [ 2, _, _, _, 1 ]

Step 3: Remaining: (9,7),(3,8),(10,4)
        Minimum = 3 → Job 4 on M1 → place at FRONT (first empty slot).
        Sequence = [ 2, 4, _, _, 1 ]

Step 4: Remaining: (9,7),(10,4)
        Minimum = 4 → Job 5 on M2 → place at BACK (last empty slot).
        Sequence = [ 2, 4, _, 5, 1 ]

Step 5: Only Job 3 left → place in remaining slot.
        Sequence = [ 2, 4, 3, 5, 1 ]
```

**Final Optimal Sequence:** **2 → 4 → 3 → 5 → 1**

**Gantt chart verification:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{M1 timeline:} &\quad 0\text{–}1 \; J2 \;|\; 1\text{–}4 \; J4 \;|\; 4\text{–}13 \; J3 \;|\; 13\text{–}23 \; J5 \;|\; 23\text{–}28 \; J1 \\
\text{M2 timeline:} &\quad 1\text{–}7 \; J2 \;|\; 7\text{–}15 \; J4 \;|\; 15\text{–}22 \; J3 \;|\; 22\text{–}26 \; J5 \;|\; 26\text{–}28 \; J1
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Idle time on M2:** between t = 0 and t = 1 → **1 unit of idle time**.
> **Makespan:** $C_{max} = 28$ hours.

### 3.3 CDS Heuristic – 3 Machines, n Jobs (Jackson's Extension)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CDS Algorithm (Campbell, Dudek, Smith, 1970)** generalises Johnson's rule to 3 machines by forming **m − 1 = 2** dummy 2-machine problems and applying Johnson on each, then choosing the sequence giving the minimum makespan.

**Steps:**

1. For each job *i*, compute pairs:
$$
G_1 = (A_i + B_i,\; B_i + C_i)
$$
$$
G_2 = (A_i + B_i,\; C_i)
\quad \text{(Jackson variant)}
$$
where A, B, C are processing times on M1, M2, M3.

2. Apply Johnson's Rule on each pair $G_k$.
3. Evaluate makespan of each candidate sequence using a Gantt chart.
4. Choose the sequence with **minimum Cmax**.

### 3.4 n Jobs on m Machines – Palmer's Heuristic (Slope Index)

For larger flow shops:

$$
\text{Slope Index}_i = \dfrac{m+1-2k}{m-1} \cdot \text{(job's position in m-machine order)}
$$

A simpler practical rule: order jobs by **decreasing slope index** = order by **decreasing $(A_i - C_i)$**.

> [!TIP]
> **Palmer's Heuristic** order key: arrange jobs so processing time **decreases** from M1 to Mn. Equivalent practical rule: sort by $(A_i - C_i)$ in descending order.

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix – Priority Rules

| Rule | Minimizes ΣF | Minimizes Lmax | Complexity | Use when |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| FCFS | No | No | O(n) | Fairness needed |
| **SPT** | **Yes ✓** | No | O(n log n) | Want fast throughput |
| **EDD** | No | **Yes ✓** | O(n log n) | Due dates critical |
| CR | No | Heuristic | O(n) | Dynamic scheduling |
| Slack | No | Heuristic | O(n) | Tight deadlines |

### 3.6 n Jobs, 2 Machines – Extension (n = 2)

For just **2 jobs on 2 machines**, the optimal makespan is independent of order if the two machines process jobs in identical order. Verify with:

$$
C_{max} = \min\left(A_1+A_2+B_1+B_2,\; A_1+A_2+B_2+B_1\right) - \text{depends on route}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Flow – Scheduling Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Job Arrival Queue"] --> B["Apply Priority Rule"]
    B --> C["Generate Sequence Order"]
    C --> D["Build Gantt Chart"]
    D --> E{"Performance Goal Met?"}
    E -- "Yes" --> F["Release Schedule to Shop Floor"]
    E -- "No" --> G["Re-prioritize and Re-sequence"]
    G --> B
```

### 4.2 Johnson's Rule – Algorithmic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Start: List A_i, B_i for all jobs"] --> scan["Scan for minimum p_i,j"]
    scan --> check{"Min on M1 or M2?"}
    check -- "M1" --> front["Place job at FRONT of sequence"]
    check -- "M2" --> back["Place job at BACK of sequence"]
    front --> remove["Remove job from list"]
    back --> remove
    remove --> more{"Any job left?"}
    more -- "Yes" --> scan
    more -- "No" --> output["Optimal Sequence + Gantt Chart"]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture – Production Scheduling System

```mermaid

    subgraph Input
        J1["Job Orders"]
        M1["Machine Specs"]
        D1["Due Dates"]
    end

    end
    subgraph Output
        O1["Sequenced Job Plan"]
        O2["Makespan Report"]
        O3["Tardiness Report"]
    end
    J1 --> P1
    M1 --> P1
    D1 --> P1
    P1 --> S1
    P1 --> C1
    S1 --> G1
    C1 --> G1
    G1 --> O1
    G1 --> O2
    G1 --> O3
```

### 4.4 Decision Tree – Which Priority Rule to Use?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1["What is your objective?"] --> Q2{"Minimize Flow Time?"}
    Q2 -- "Yes" --> R1["Use SPT Rule"]
    Q2 -- "No" --> Q3{"Minimize Max Lateness?"}
    Q3 -- "Yes" --> R2["Use EDD Rule"]
    Q3 -- "No" --> Q4{"Dynamic with changing due dates?"}
    Q4 -- "Yes" --> R3["Use CR or Slack Rule"]
    Q4 -- "No" --> R4["Use FCFS Rule"]
```

```mermaid
gantt

    dateFormat  HH

    Job 4 :a2, after a1, 3h
    Job 3 :a3, after a2, 9h
    Job 5 :a4, after a3, 10h
    Job 1 :a5, after a4, 5h
    section Machine 2
    Job 2 :b1, 01, 6h
    Job 4 :b2, after b1, 8h

```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between sequencing and scheduling. State any two performance measures used in scheduling.**

**Model Answer:**
**Sequencing** is the determination of the *order* in which jobs will be processed on machines, while **Scheduling** is the *detailed time-bound assignment* of jobs to specific machines specifying exact start and finish times.

**Two performance measures:**
1. **Makespan (Cmax)** – total time to complete all jobs; minimized.
2. **Average Flow Time (F̄)** – mean time jobs spend in the system; minimized.
*(Other valid: Lmax, Tardiness, # tardy jobs, Machine utilization.)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`
**Explain the SPT (Shortest Processing Time) dispatching rule. State its optimality property.**

**Model Answer:**
Under the **SPT rule**, when a machine becomes free, the next job processed is the one with the **shortest processing time** among all waiting jobs.

**Optimality property (Jackson's Theorem):** Among all possible sequences on a single machine, the SPT sequence **minimizes the sum of flow times** (ΣFᵢ) and consequently the mean flow time (F̄).

---

### Part B – Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme, each Part B question carries **14 marks** with **internal choice** between two questions (A and B). Each contains sub-parts (a) and (b) of **7 marks** each.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Five jobs are to be processed on a single machine. The processing times and due dates are:

| Job | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Processing time pᵢ (days) | 3 | 1 | 5 | 2 | 4 |
| Due date dᵢ (days) | 7 | 4 | 10 | 3 | 8 |

Determine the sequence using **(i) SPT** and **(ii) EDD** rules. Compute mean flow time and maximum lateness for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Solve the following **6-job, 2-machine** problem using **Johnson's Rule** to minimize makespan. Also draw the Gantt chart and compute total idle time. **(7 marks)**

| Job | M1 | M2 |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 8 | 6 |
| 2 | 3 | 9 |
| 3 | 7 | 5 |
| 4 | 2 | 10 |
| 5 | 5 | 4 |
| 6 | 6 | 8 |

---

#### Model Solution to Q(a)

**SPT order:** Sort by pᵢ ascending → **2, 4, 1, 5, 3**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_2 &= 1, \quad C_4 = 3, \quad C_1 = 6, \quad C_5 = 10, \quad C_3 = 15 \\
F &= [1, 3, 6, 10, 15] \\
\bar F &= \dfrac{1+3+6+10+15}{5} = \dfrac{35}{5} = 7.0 \text{ days} \\
L &= [1-7,\; 3-3,\; 6-4,\; 10-8,\; 15-10] = [-6,\; 0,\; +2,\; +2,\; +5] \\
L_{max} &= +5 \text{ days}
\end{aligned}
$$

**EDD order:** Sort by dᵢ ascending → **4, 2, 1, 5, 3**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_4 &= 2, \quad C_2 = 3, \quad C_1 = 6, \quad C_5 = 10, \quad C_3 = 15 \\
F &= [2, 3, 6, 10, 15] \\
\bar F &= \dfrac{2+3+6+10+15}{5} = \dfrac{36}{5} = 7.2 \text{ days} \\
L &= [2-3,\; 3-4,\; 6-7,\; 10-8,\; 15-10] = [-1,\; -1,\; -1,\; +2,\; +5] \\
L_{max} &= +5 \text{ days}
\end{aligned}
$$

| Rule | Sequence | F̄ | Lmax |
|---|---|---|---|
| SPT | 2-4-1-5-3 | **7.0 ✓** | 5 |
| EDD | 4-2-1-5-3 | 7.2 | **5** (tied) |

**Conclusion:** SPT gives the lower mean flow time; both give same Lmax in this case.

---

#### Model Solution to Q(b)

**Apply Johnson's Algorithm:**

```
Step 1: Min = 2 (Job 4 on M1) → FRONT  → [4, _, _, _, _, _]
Step 2: Min = 3 (Job 2 on M1) → FRONT  → [4, 2, _, _, _, _]
Step 3: Min = 4 (Job 5 on M2) → BACK   → [4, 2, _, _, _, 5]
Step 4: Min = 5 (Job 3 on M2) → BACK   → [4, 2, _, _, 3, 5]
Step 5: Min = 6 (Job 6 on M1) → FRONT  → [4, 2, 6, _, 3, 5]
Step 6: Min = 8 (Job 1 on M1) → FRONT  → [4, 2, 6, 1, 3, 5]
```

**Optimal Sequence: 4 → 2 → 6 → 1 → 3 → 5**

**Gantt Chart & Cmax:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{M1:} &\; 0\text{–}2\;J4 \;|\; 2\text{–}5\;J2 \;|\; 5\text{–}11\;J6 \;|\; 11\text{–}19\;J1 \;|\; 19\text{–}26\;J3 \;|\; 26\text{–}31\;J5 \\
\text{M2:} &\; 2\text{–}12\;J4 \;|\; 12\text{–}21\;J2 \;|\; 21\text{–}27\;J6 \;|\; 27\text{–}33\;J1 \;|\; 33\text{–}38\;J3 \;|\; 38\text{–}42\;J5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Makespan, $C_{max} = 42$ hours**

**Idle time on M2** = 0 (from t = 2 onwards, M2 is continuously busy).

> [!TIP]
> **Verification tip:** Total M1 time = 8+3+7+2+5+6 = 31. Total M2 time = 6+9+5+10+4+8 = 42. Idle M2 = 42 − 42 = 0. ✓

---

### Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice

`[KTU University Exam – May 2022]`

**(a)** Define **Critical Ratio (CR)**. For the data below, compute CR at t = 0 and find the sequence using CR rule. Compute mean tardiness. **(7 marks)**

| Job | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| pᵢ (days) | 4 | 6 | 2 | 8 | 5 |
| dᵢ (days) | 10 | 18 | 6 | 22 | 14 |

**(b)** A **5-job, 3-machine flow shop** has the following processing times. Use the **CDS heuristic** to find the optimal sequence and its makespan. **(7 marks)**

| Job | M1 | M2 | M3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 5 | 2 | 4 |
| 2 | 3 | 6 | 2 |
| 3 | 8 | 4 | 6 |
| 4 | 2 | 5 | 7 |
| 5 | 6 | 3 | 5 |

---

#### Model Solution to Q(a)

**Critical Ratio Formula:**
$$
CR_i = \dfrac{d_i - t}{p_i}, \quad \text{where } t = \text{current time}
$$

**At t = 0, compute CR for each job:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CR_1 &= (10-0)/4 = 2.50 \\
CR_2 &= (18-0)/6 = 3.00 \\
CR_3 &= (6-0)/2 = 3.00 \\
CR_4 &= (22-0)/8 = 2.75 \\
CR_5 &= (14-0)/5 = 2.80
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sort ascending (smallest CR first):**
Sequence: **J1 (2.50) → J4 (2.75) → J5 (2.80) → J2 (3.00) → J3 (3.00)**

**Compute completion, tardiness:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_1 &= 4, \; C_4 = 12, \; C_5 = 17, \; C_2 = 23, \; C_3 = 25 \\
T_i &= \max(0, C_i - d_i) \\
T_1 &= \max(0, 4-10) = 0 \\
T_4 &= \max(0, 12-22) = 0 \\
T_5 &= \max(0, 17-14) = 3 \\
T_2 &= \max(0, 23-18) = 5 \\
T_3 &= \max(0, 25-6) = 19 \\
\bar T &= \dfrac{0+0+3+5+19}{5} = 5.4 \text{ days}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### Model Solution to Q(b)

**Step 1 – Form 2 dummy 2-machine problems:**

**Pair G1 = (A+B, B+C):**
- J1: (5+2, 2+4) = (7, 6)
- J2: (3+6, 6+2) = (9, 8)
- J3: (8+4, 4+6) = (12, 10)
- J4: (2+5, 5+7) = (7, 12)
- J5: (6+3, 3+5) = (9, 8)

**Apply Johnson on G1:**

```
Min = 6 (J1, dummy M2) → BACK  → [_,_,_,_,1]
Min = 7 (J1=7 already used; J4=7 on M1) → FRONT → [4,_,_,_,1]
Min = 8 (J2=8 and J5=8 on dummy M2) → BACK → [4,_,_,2,1]  (place J2 first then J5)
Min = 8 (J5 on dummy M2) → BACK → [4,_,5,2,1]
Min = 9 → already placed both J2 and J5
Min = 12 (J3) → FRONT → [4,3,5,2,1]
```

**G1 Sequence: 4 → 3 → 5 → 2 → 1**

**Pair G2 = (A+B, C):**
- J1: (7, 4)
- J2: (9, 2)
- J3: (12, 6)
- J4: (7, 7)
- J5: (9, 5)

**Apply Johnson on G2:**

```
Min = 2 (J2 on M2') → BACK → [_,_,_,_,2]
Min = 4 (J1 on M2') → BACK → [_,_,_,1,2]
Min = 5 (J5 on M2') → BACK → [_,_,5,1,2]
Min = 7 (J1=7 used; J4=7 on M1) → FRONT → [4,_,5,1,2]
Min = 9 (J2=9 used; J5=9 used) — skip
Min = 12 (J3) → FRONT → [4,3,5,1,2]
```

**G2 Sequence: 4 → 3 → 5 → 1 → 2**

**Step 2 – Evaluate makespans via Gantt charts:**

For **G1: 4 → 3 → 5 → 2 → 1**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M1 &: 0\text{–}2 \; J4 \;|\; 2\text{–}10 \; J3 \;|\; 10\text{–}16 \; J5 \;|\; 16\text{–}19 \; J2 \;|\; 19\text{–}24 \; J1 \\
M2 &: 2\text{–}7 \; J4 \;|\; 7\text{–}11 \; J3 \;|\; 11\text{–}14 \; J5 \;|\; 14\text{–}20 \; J2 \;|\; 20\text{–}22 \; J1 \\
M3 &: 7\text{–}14 \; J4 \;|\; 14\text{–}20 \; J3 \;|\; 20\text{–}25 \; J5 \;|\; 25\text{–}27 \; J2 \;|\; 27\text{–}31 \; J1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Cmax for G1 = 31 hours**

For **G2: 4 → 3 → 5 → 1 → 2**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M1 &: 0\text{–}2 \; J4 \;|\; 2\text{–}10 \; J3 \;|\; 10\text{–}16 \; J5 \;|\; 16\text{–}21 \; J1 \;|\; 21\text{–}24 \; J2 \\
M2 &: 2\text{–}7 \; J4 \;|\; 7\text{–}11 \; J3 \;|\; 11\text{–}14 \; J5 \;|\; 14\text{–}16 \; J1 \;|\; 16\text{–}22 \; J2 \\
M3 &: 7\text{–}14 \; J4 \;|\; 14\text{–}20 \; J3 \;|\; 20\text{–}25 \; J5 \;|\; 25\text{–}29 \; J1 \;|\; 29\text{–}31 \; J2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Cmax for G2 = 31 hours**

> [!TIP]
> Both sequences yield the same makespan here. Either is acceptable. **Best sequence: 4 → 3 → 5 → 1 → 2** with **Cmax = 31 hours**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout – Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Johnson's Rule direction error** – students confuse "place at front" vs "place at back." Remember: **M1 → Front (early), M2 → Back (late).**
> 2. **Sign error in Tardiness** – Tardiness is **always ≥ 0**. Use `max(0, C − d)`, never allow negative tardiness.
> 3. **CDS shortcut** – students forget to test *both* G1 and G2 candidates via Gantt and pick the lower makespan.
> 4. **Idle time miscount** – the Gantt chart on M2 begins **only when M1 finishes the first job**, not at t = 0. Always account for the first job's M1 processing as initial idle on M2.
> 5. **Confusing ΣF minimization with Lmax minimization** – SPT minimizes flow time but **not** maximum lateness. State both clearly.
> 6. **No units in final answer** – KTU deducts half-mark if "hours/days" is missing.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist – Scheduling Techniques**

- ✅ **Scheduling ≠ Sequencing** – Sequencing is order; Scheduling includes specific times.
- ✅ **Performance metrics:** Cmax, F̄, Lmax, T̄, # tardy jobs.
- ✅ **SPT rule** → minimizes ΣF and F̄ on a single machine.
- ✅ **EDD rule** → minimizes Lmax on a single machine.
- ✅ **Critical Ratio** $CR = (d-t)/p$ → smaller CR = more urgent; CR < 1 means already late.
- ✅ **Slack rule** → process job with smallest `d − t − p` first.
- ✅ **Johnson's Rule (2 machines):** Find minimum; M1 → Front; M2 → Back.
- ✅ **CDS Heuristic (3 machines):** Build 2 dummy pairs (A+B, B+C) and (A+B, C); apply Johnson; pick lower Cmax.
- ✅ **Palmer's Heuristic (m machines):** Sort by $(A_i − Z_i)$ descending.
- ✅ **Gantt chart** is mandatory for makespan verification in every numerical.
- ✅ Always state **units** (hours/days) in the final answer.

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Last-Minute Formulas:**

$$
\bar F = \dfrac{\sum F_i}{n}, \quad L_i = C_i - d_i, \quad T_i = \max(0,\; C_i - d_i)
$$

$$
CR_i = \dfrac{d_i - t}{p_i}, \quad Slack_i = d_i - t - p_i, \quad \text{Cmax} = \text{total elapsed time}
$$

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Job Shop Production
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET723 - PRODUCTION AND OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Job Shop Production

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Job Shop Production — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> **Job Shop Production** is a type of manufacturing system characterized by the production of **low volumes of a wide variety of products**, where each product (or "job") requires a unique sequence of operations performed on **general-purpose machines** organized into **functional departments** (e.g., milling, grinding, assembly).

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a Job Shop is a **Flexibility-Focused, Low-Volume, High-Variety Production System** where products are manufactured to **customer specifications** rather than for inventory, and the path of each job through the shop is **unique and non-repetitive**.

### Key Identifiers of a Job Shop

| Parameter | Job Shop Value |
|---|---|
| Production Volume | **Low** (1 to ~100 units) |
| Product Variety | **Very High** (often one-off) |
| Machine Type | **General-purpose / versatile** |
| Layout | **Functional / Process Layout** |
| Workforce Skill Level | **Highly skilled** |
| Unit Cost | **High** |
| Lead Time | **Long and variable** |
| Routing | **Unique per job** |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Master Craftsman Workshop"

Imagine walking into a **traditional custom furniture workshop**. A customer walks in and asks for: *"Make me a teak dining table with 4 chairs, curved legs, and a glass top."* The carpenter:

1. **Designs** a unique blueprint for this specific customer.
2. Selects which machine (lathe, saw, sander) is needed **in what order**.
3. Routes the wood through **different workstations** based on the design.
4. Sometimes waits because another worker is using the needed machine.
5. Delivers a **unique, custom-made product**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is exactly how a **Job Shop** works. Every order is a new "custom project," routed uniquely through shared, general-purpose resources. Compare this to a **mass-production factory** (like a car assembly line) where identical products flow through a fixed sequence.

## 1.3 Classification Within Production Systems

Job Shop Production sits within the **Production System Continuum**:

$$
\text{Project Shop} \rightarrow \text{Job Shop} \rightarrow \text{Batch Production} \rightarrow \text{Mass Production} \rightarrow \text{Continuous Production}
$$

This continuum is **monotonically increasing in volume and decreasing in variety**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Logic of Job Shop Production

> [!NOTE]
> Job Shops are governed by the principle of **"Route Diversity under Resource Sharing."** Unlike flow shops where every product follows the same path, every job in a job shop follows a different path through the **same pool of machines**.

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

- **Step 1: Order Acceptance** — A customer order is received with unique specifications (drawing, tolerances, material).
- **Step 2: Process Planning** — A **Route Sheet** is prepared listing the exact sequence of operations and the machines required.
- **Step 3: Job Release** — The job is released to the shop floor with a **Job Order Number** and **Delivery Due Date**.
- **Step 4: Queuing at Work Centers** — Since multiple jobs compete for the same machine, jobs **queue up** at work centers.
- **Step 5: Dispatching & Sequencing** — A dispatcher decides **which job goes next** on each machine using priority rules (FCFS, SPT, EDD, etc.).
- **Step 6: Inspection & Quality Check** — Quality is verified at each operation since rework is expensive.
- **Step 7: Assembly (if required) & Delivery** — Sub-assemblies are brought together and dispatched.

### The "Why" Behind Each Step

| Step | Why It Matters |
|---|---|
| Process Planning | Without route sheets, jobs would **wander aimlessly** between machines, causing chaos. |
| Queuing | Since machines are shared, **waiting time** is unavoidable; queuing theory applies. |
| Sequencing | A bad sequencing rule can **inflate lead time by 30-50%**. |

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Key Equations for Job Shop Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These formulas are **frequently tested** in KTU ESE. Memorize the variables and the units.

| Formula | Meaning | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|
| $L_q = \dfrac{\lambda^2 \cdot \sigma^2 + \rho^2}{2 \cdot \mu \cdot (1 - \rho)}$ | Average jobs in queue (Kingman's formula) | $\lambda$ = arrival rate (jobs/hr), $\mu$ = service rate (jobs/hr), $\rho$ = utilization, $\sigma$ = std. dev. of service time (hr) |
| $\rho = \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu}$ | Server (machine) utilization | Dimensionless, $0 \le \rho < 1$ |
| $W_q = \dfrac{L_q}{\lambda}$ | Average waiting time in queue | Hours |
| $L = L_q + \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu}$ | Average number of jobs in system | Jobs |
| $W = W_q + \dfrac{1}{\mu}$ | Average time in system (Makespan per job) | Hours |
| $C_{max} = \max(C_i)$ | Makespan (completion time of last job) | Hours or days |
| $\text{Idle Time} = \sum_{j=1}^{m} \left( C_{max} - \sum_{i \in S_j} p_{ij} \right)$ | Total machine idle time | Hours |
| $\text{Utilization} (\%) = \dfrac{\sum_{i} p_{ij}}{m \cdot C_{max}} \times 100$ | Machine utilization | Percent |

## 2.3 Priority Dispatching Rules (Used in Job Shop Scheduling)

> [!TIP]
> KTU frequently asks: *"Which sequencing rule minimizes what?"* — Remember this table by heart.

| Rule | Full Form | Minimizes | Best Used When |
|---|---|---|---|
| FCFS | First-Come, First-Served | Fairness | No urgency |
| SPT | Shortest Processing Time | Average flow time | Many small jobs |
| LPT | Longest Processing Time | Difference in completion times | Few large jobs |
| EDD | Earliest Due Date | Maximum tardiness | Due-date critical |
| CR | Critical Ratio | Lateness | Mixed urgency |
| SLACK | Minimum Slack | Tardiness | Tight deadlines |

The **Critical Ratio (CR)** formula:

$$
CR = \dfrac{D_d - T_n}{T_r}
$$

Where $D_d$ is due date, $T_n$ is current time, and $T_r$ is remaining processing time.

> When $CR \le 1$, the job is **behind schedule** and should be expedited.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Job Shop principles underpin:

- **Tool & Die Making** industries
- **Aerospace component manufacturing** (custom turbine blades)
- **Defense equipment fabrication** (low-volume, high-precision)
- **Custom prototyping** firms
- **Heavy machinery repair workshops**
- **Shipbuilding and custom structural fabrication**

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Kingman's Approximation for Queue Waiting Time

The **VUT equation** (Variability-Utilization-Time) derived by Kingman (1972) is foundational in job shop analysis because every job shop inherently operates as a queueing network.

### Starting Assumption

We model each work center as an **M/G/1 queue** (Poisson arrivals, General service time, 1 server).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Let $s$ = service time per job at a machine (random variable with mean $\bar{s}$ and variance $\sigma_s^2$). The traffic intensity is:

$$
\rho = \lambda \cdot \bar{s}
$$

The **Pollaczek-Khinchine (P-K) Mean Value Formula** for M/G/1 gives the average waiting time in queue:

$$
W_q = \dfrac{\lambda \cdot \sigma_s^2 + \rho^2}{2 \cdot (1 - \rho)} \cdot \bar{s}
$$

Since utilization $\rho = \lambda \cdot \bar{s}$, we can write $\lambda = \rho / \bar{s}$. Substituting:

$$
W_q = \dfrac{(\rho / \bar{s}) \cdot \sigma_s^2 + \rho^2}{2 \cdot (1 - \rho)} \cdot \bar{s}
$$

Multiplying numerator terms:

$$
W_q = \dfrac{\rho \cdot \sigma_s^2 / \bar{s} + \rho^2}{2 \cdot (1 - \rho)} \cdot \bar{s}
$$

Distribute $\bar{s}$:

$$
W_q = \dfrac{\rho \cdot \sigma_s^2 + \rho^2 \cdot \bar{s}}{2 \cdot \bar{s} \cdot (1 - \rho)}
$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by $\bar{s}$:

$$
W_q = \dfrac{\rho \cdot (C_s^2 + \rho)}{2 \cdot (1 - \rho)} \cdot \bar{s}
$$

Where $C_s^2 = \sigma_s^2 / \bar{s}^2$ is the **coefficient of variation squared**.

This is the **exact VUT equation**.

### Kingman's Approximation

Kingman observed that in many practical settings, the $\rho^2$ term is small relative to the $\rho \cdot C_s^2$ term when $\rho$ is moderate to high. He proposed the simpler form:

$$
W_q \approx \dfrac{\rho}{1 - \rho} \cdot \dfrac{C_s^2 + 1}{2} \cdot \bar{s}
$$

This is the famous **VUT approximation**.

### Final Compact Form

$$
\boxed{W_q \approx \dfrac{\rho}{1 - \rho} \cdot \dfrac{(C_s^2 + 1)}{2} \cdot \bar{s}}
$$

This shows waiting time grows **hyperbolically** as utilization $\rho \to 1$. A small increase in utilization near capacity causes **waiting time to explode**.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> This is a classic KTU-style numerical.

**Problem:** A job shop work center receives jobs at $\lambda = 4$ jobs/hour. Mean service time $\bar{s} = 0.2$ hr/job. Standard deviation of service time $\sigma_s = 0.1$ hr. Find: (a) Utilization, (b) Average jobs in queue, (c) Average time in system.

### Solution

**Part (a): Utilization**

$$
\rho = \lambda \cdot \bar{s} = 4 \times 0.2 = 0.8 \text{ (or } 80\% \text{)}
$$

**Part (b): $L_q$ using P-K formula**

Compute $C_s^2$:

$$
C_s^2 = \dfrac{\sigma_s^2}{\bar{s}^2} = \dfrac{(0.1)^2}{(0.2)^2} = \dfrac{0.01}{0.04} = 0.25
$$

Apply P-K formula:

$$
L_q = \dfrac{\rho^2 + \lambda^2 \sigma_s^2}{2(1-\rho)} = \rho + \dfrac{\lambda^2 \sigma_s^2 + \rho^2}{2(1-\rho)} - \rho
$$

Simplified using $L_q = \rho + \dfrac{\lambda^2 \sigma_s^2 + \rho^2}{2(1-\rho)} - \rho$ — let us apply directly:

$$
L_q = \dfrac{(4)^2 (0.1)^2 + (0.8)^2}{2(1 - 0.8)} = \dfrac{1.6 + 0.64}{0.4} = \dfrac{2.24}{0.4} = 5.6 \text{ jobs}
$$

**Part (c): Time in System**

$$
W = W_q + \bar{s} = \dfrac{L_q}{\lambda} + \bar{s} = \dfrac{5.6}{4} + 0.2 = 1.4 + 0.2 = 1.6 \text{ hours}
$$

### Interpretation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> At 80% utilization, a job waits **5.6 machines-worth of jobs ahead of it** and spends **1.6 hours** in the system on average. Increasing utilization to 90% would **more than double** the queue length — this is why job shops cannot run machines at near-100% capacity without huge delays.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Dimension | Job Shop | Batch | Mass | Continuous |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Volume | **1–100 units** | 100–10,000 | 10,000+ | Millions |
| Variety | **Very High** | Medium | Low | Very Low |
| Lead Time | **Weeks–Months** | Days–Weeks | Hours–Days | Minutes |
| Cost/Unit | **High** | Medium | Low | Very Low |
| Equipment | General-purpose | Semi-specialized | Special | Highly Special |
| Workforce | **Highly Skilled** | Mixed | Semi-Skilled | Low-Skilled |
| Inventory | **Low raw, minimal WIP control** | Medium | High | Very High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Job Shop Functional Flow Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **architectural flow** of a Job Shop Production system, from order receipt to delivery.

```mermaid

    E --> F[Work Center Queue 2 - Milling]
    F --> G[Work Center Queue 3 - Grinding]
    G --> H[Work Center Queue 4 - Inspection]
    H --> I[Assembly Bay if Required]
    I --> J[Final Quality Inspection]
    J --> K[Finished Goods Dispatch]
    K --> L[Customer Delivery]

    M[Priority Dispatcher] -.-> E
    M -.-> F
    M -.-> G
    M -.-> H

    N[Shop Floor Supervisor] -.-> M
```
