# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

> *Define Job Shop Production. Mention any four of its salient characteristics.*

#### Model Answer

**Definition:** Job Shop Production is a manufacturing system that produces **small quantities of a wide variety of products**, where each product follows a **unique routing** through general-purpose machines arranged in functional departments.

**Four Salient Characteristics:**

1. **Low Volume, High Variety** — Typically produces 1 to 100 units of each product type.
2. **General-Purpose Machines** — Uses versatile equipment that can perform multiple operations.
3. **Highly Skilled Workforce** — Requires trained operators capable of handling diverse tasks.
4. **Functional Layout** — Machines are grouped by function (e.g., all lathes together, all mills together).
5. *Bonus:* Unique routing per job, long lead times, high unit cost.

**Mark Distribution:** 1 mark for definition + 2 marks (½ mark each) for four characteristics.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

> *Differentiate between Job Shop Production and Mass Production in any six dimensions.*

#### Model Answer

| Dimension | Job Shop | Mass Production |
|---|---|---|
| Volume | Low | Very High |
| Variety | High | Low |
| Equipment | General-purpose | Special-purpose |
| Skill Level | High | Low |
| Unit Cost | High | Low |
| Lead Time | Long | Short |
| Layout | Functional | Product/Line |
| Inventory | Low | High |

**Mark Distribution:** ½ mark per correct contrasting point × 6 dimensions = 3 marks.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4]**

> *(a) [7 Marks]* Explain the **operational sequence** of activities in a Job Shop Production system, from order receipt to final delivery. Use a suitable diagram.
>
> *(b) [7 Marks]* A job shop work center has jobs arriving at a rate of $\lambda = 5$ jobs/hour. The mean service time is $\bar{s} = 0.15$ hr/job with standard deviation $\sigma_s = 0.08$ hr. Compute: (i) Machine Utilization $\rho$, (ii) Average number of jobs in queue $L_q$, (iii) Average time a job spends in the system $W$.

#### Model Solution

### Part (a) — Operational Sequence

The operational sequence of Job Shop Production consists of **7 critical stages**:

1. **Order Acceptance & Documentation** — Customer orders are received with specifications (drawings, tolerances, delivery dates). Each order is assigned a unique **Job Order Number**.

2. **Process Planning** — A **Route Sheet** is prepared specifying the sequence of operations, machines, tools, and estimated times for that specific job.

3. **Material Issue & Job Release** — Raw materials are drawn from stores and the job is officially released to the shop floor.

4. **Queuing at Work Centers** — Jobs arrive at functional work centers (e.g., lathe, mill, grinder). Since multiple jobs compete for the same machine, they **queue up** following priority rules.

5. **Dispatching & Sequencing** — A shop floor dispatcher applies **priority rules** (FCFS, SPT, EDD, Critical Ratio) to decide which job processes next.

6. **Quality Inspection** — In-process inspection at each operation; final inspection before dispatch.

7. **Assembly & Delivery** — Sub-assemblies are joined (if applicable) and the finished job is dispatched to the customer.

```
[Draw a flowchart: Order → Planning → Release → Queue → Machine → QC → Delivery]
```

> Any labeled flowchart with 6–7 stages earns **full 7 marks**. If student misses dispatching/queuing logic, deduct **1 mark**.

### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given:**
- $\lambda = 5$ jobs/hr
- $\bar{s} = 0.15$ hr/job
- $\sigma_s = 0.08$ hr

**Step 1: Compute Utilization**

$$
\rho = \lambda \cdot \bar{s} = 5 \times 0.15 = 0.75
$$

**Step 2: Compute $C_s^2$**

$$
C_s^2 = \dfrac{\sigma_s^2}{\bar{s}^2} = \dfrac{(0.08)^2}{(0.15)^2} = \dfrac{0.0064}{0.0225} = 0.2844
$$

**Step 3: Compute $L_q$ using Pollaczek-Khinchine formula**

$$
L_q = \dfrac{\lambda^2 \sigma_s^2 + \rho^2}{2(1-\rho)}
$$

Substitute values:

$$
L_q = \dfrac{(5)^2 (0.08)^2 + (0.75)^2}{2(1 - 0.75)}
$$

$$
L_q = \dfrac{25 \times 0.0064 + 0.5625}{2 \times 0.25}
$$

$$
L_q = \dfrac{0.16 + 0.5625}{0.5} = \dfrac{0.7225}{0.5} = 1.445 \text{ jobs}
$$

**Step 4: Compute Average Time in System $W$**

$$
W_q = \dfrac{L_q}{\lambda} = \dfrac{1.445}{5} = 0.289 \text{ hours}
$$

$$
W = W_q + \bar{s} = 0.289 + 0.15 = 0.439 \text{ hours} \approx 26.34 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Final Answers:**

- (i) $\rho = 0.75$ **(1 mark)**
- (ii) $L_q = 1.445$ jobs **(3 marks)**
- (iii) $W = 0.439$ hours **(3 marks)**

---

### Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]**

> *(a) [7 Marks]* Discuss the **advantages and limitations** of Job Shop Production. Under what conditions would you recommend setting up a job shop?
>
> *(b) [7 Marks]* A job shop has 4 machines and processes 3 jobs. Processing times (in hours) are:

| Job | M1 | M2 | M3 | M4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| J1 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 5 |
| J2 | 5 | 2 | 6 | 1 |
| J3 | 3 | 6 | 4 | 2 |

> All jobs follow the same routing M1 → M2 → M3 → M4. Apply **FCFS and SPT rules** and determine which rule gives the lower average flow time.

#### Model Solution

### Part (a) — Advantages, Limitations & Suitability

**Advantages:**

1. **High Flexibility** — Can produce a wide variety of products by simply re-routing.
2. **Lower Initial Investment** — General-purpose machines cost less than special-purpose equipment.
3. **Customization Capability** — Ideal for bespoke customer requirements.
4. **Less Inventory Risk** — Products are made-to-order, not made-to-stock.
5. **Easy to Add New Products** — No major retooling required.

**Limitations:**

1. **High Unit Cost** — Setup and processing overheads are spread over few units.
2. **Long & Variable Lead Times** — Jobs queue unpredictably.
3. **Complex Scheduling** — Requires sophisticated dispatching.
4. **Higher WIP Inventory** — Jobs pile up between machines.
5. **Skill Dependency** — Productivity drops if skilled labor is unavailable.

**Recommended When:**

- Product variety is high and volume is low.
- Customer specifications are unique.
- Frequent design changes occur.
- Capital for special-purpose machinery is limited.
- Examples: Tool rooms, prototype workshops, defense shops.

### Part (b) — Flow Time Computation

**Routing:** M1 → M2 → M3 → M4 for all jobs. Order received: J1, J2, J3.

#### Case 1: FCFS Rule (Order: J1, J2, J3)

**At M1:**
- J1: starts at 0, ends at 4
- J2: starts at 4, ends at 9
- J3: starts at 9, ends at 12

**At M2:**
- J1: starts at 4, ends at 7
- J2: starts at 7, ends at 9
- J3: starts at 9, ends at 15

**At M3:**
- J1: starts at 7, ends at 9
- J2: starts at 9, ends at 15
- J3: starts at 15, ends at 19

**At M4:**
- J1: starts at 9, ends at 14
- J2: starts at 15, ends at 16
- J3: starts at 19, ends at 21

**Completion times:** J1 = 14, J2 = 16, J3 = 21

**Average Flow Time (FCFS):**

$$
\bar{F}_{FCFS} = \dfrac{14 + 16 + 21}{3} = \dfrac{51}{3} = 17 \text{ hours}
$$

#### Case 2: SPT Rule at Each Machine

**Total processing times per job:**
- J1: 4+3+2+5 = 14
- J2: 5+2+6+1 = 14
- J3: 3+6+4+2 = 15

**At M1 (SPT):** J3 (3), J1 (4), J2 (5)
- J3: 0–3, J1: 3–7, J2: 7–12

**At M2 (SPT order based on M2 times):** J2 (2), J1 (3), J3 (6)
- J2: 7–9, J1: 9–12, J3: 12–18

**At M3 (SPT):** J1 (2), J3 (4), J2 (6)
- J1: 12–14, J3: 18–22, J2: 14–20

**At M4 (SPT):** J2 (1), J3 (2), J1 (5)
- J2: 20–21, J3: 22–24, J1: 14–19

**Completion times:** J1 = 19, J2 = 21, J3 = 24

**Average Flow Time (SPT):**

$$
\bar{F}_{SPT} = \dfrac{19 + 21 + 24}{3} = \dfrac{64}{3} \approx 21.33 \text{ hours}
$$

**Conclusion:** **FCFS gives a lower average flow time (17 hrs) than SPT (21.33 hrs)** in this particular instance because the SPT rule's local optimum at each machine does not yield global optimum when routings are constrained.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. Students often **forget to wait** for machine availability, causing overlapping schedule errors. Always check: *"When does the previous operation finish?"*
> 2. Students frequently confuse **flow time** with **completion time**. Flow time = Completion Time − Release Time.
> 3. In numerical answers, **forgetting units** costs ½ mark. Always write "hours," "jobs/hr," etc.
> 4. Many students skip the **FCFS comparison step** and jump to SPT, losing 2 marks.
> 5. Avoid simply stating that "SPT always minimizes flow time" — this is only true **without sequencing constraints** across machines.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Last-Minute KTU Prep**

- **Definition:** Job Shop = Low Volume + High Variety + General-purpose machines + Functional layout + Unique routing.
- **Continuum:** Job Shop lies between **Project Shop** and **Batch Production**.
- **Key Features:** Skilled labor, high flexibility, long lead times, high unit cost, complex scheduling.
- **Process Layout:** Machines grouped by **function** (not by product).
- **Kingman's VUT Equation:**

$$
W_q \approx \dfrac{\rho}{1-\rho} \cdot \dfrac{(C_s^2 + 1)}{2} \cdot \bar{s}
$$

- **Utilization** $\rho = \lambda \cdot \bar{s}$. Avoid $\rho \geq 1$ (system becomes unstable).
- **Priority Rules:** FCFS (fairness), SPT (minimize avg flow time), EDD (minimize max tardiness), CR (handle urgency).
- **Critical Ratio** $CR = (D_d - T_n)/T_r$. If $CR < 1$, job is **behind schedule**.
- **WIP Inventory** is high in job shops due to queuing between operations.
- **Lead Time Reduction** requires better dispatching, not faster machines.
- **Suitable for:** Custom products, prototypes, defense equipment, aerospace parts, tool rooms.
- **Common numerical:** Expect Pollaczek-Khinchine / M/G/1 queue calculations (3 marks) + routing/sequencing table (7 marks).
- **Examiner trap:** Students forget that **SPT is locally optimal but not always globally optimal** in multi-machine job shops.
- **Drawing tip:** Always include **priority dispatching** as a control node in your flowcharts.
- **Difference trap:** Job Shop ≠ Batch. Batch repeats the same routing for a lot; Job Shop has **unique routing per job**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET723 - PRODUCTION AND OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Activity Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 4: Activity Planning — Core Foundations

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme for Production and Operations Management, **Activity Planning** is formally defined as the systematic process of determining the **what, when, where, who, and how** of production activities in an industrial or service organization. It is the operational heart of the Production Planning and Control (PPC) system, translating long-term strategic decisions into short-term executable shop-floor instructions.

According to standard operations management terminology aligned with KTU syllabus, Activity Planning encompasses four hierarchical tiers:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hierarchy of Production Planning (KTU Syllabus Standard)**
> 1. **Aggregate Production Planning (APP)** — Medium-range planning (3–18 months) that determines overall production quantities, workforce levels, and inventory levels for product families.
> 2. **Master Production Schedule (MPS)** — Disaggregates the aggregate plan into specific end-items, assigning quantities and timing per period.
> 3. **Material Requirements Planning (MRP)** — A computer-based system that explodes the MPS into time-phased requirements for raw materials, components, and sub-assemblies.
> 4. **Production Activity Control (PAC)** — Short-term, day-to-day execution mechanism that releases orders, monitors shop-floor progress, and expedites operations.

The objective of activity planning is to ensure that the **Right Item**, in the **Right Quantity**, at the **Right Time**, is produced through the **Right Process**, minimizing idle time, inventory, and total operating cost.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **Kerala-style wedding feast (Sadhya)** preparation. The head cook cannot simply start cooking randomly.

- **Aggregate Planning** is the master plan: deciding the total quantity of rice, sambar, and payasam for 500 guests over 3 days.
- **Master Production Schedule** is the daily menu: Monday — 5 kg rice, Tuesday — 5 kg rice, and exact items to be served on each banana leaf.
- **MRP** is the shopping list: based on the menu, what raw materials (coconut, vegetables, jaggery) are needed and when to buy them.
- **Production Activity Control** is the cook's actual execution: which helper chops vegetables first, what to start boiling at 6 AM, and which dish should be ready at 12:30 PM.

Without these levels, food would be undercooked, overcooked, or wasted. Similarly, an industrial firm without Activity Planning suffers from **stockouts, excess WIP (Work-In-Progress), missed delivery dates, and high operating costs**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric in Activity Planning:** The **Production Cycle Time** is the standard performance indicator measured in **days, hours, or units per shift (U/s)**. The **Efficiency** of an activity plan is often measured as:

$$
\text{Plan Efficiency} = \frac{\text{Actual Output}}{\text{Planned Output}} \times 100\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **Manufacturing Lead Time (LT)** is a standard metric expressed in **time units (hours, days, weeks)** and represents the total time between order release and finished goods delivery. A well-designed activity plan minimizes LT while maximizing **Machine Utilization** (typically targeted between **85%–90%**).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Module 4: Deep Theoretical Analysis

## 2.1 Levels of Activity Planning — Structured Logic

The hierarchical nature of activity planning follows a **top-down disaggregation** logic. Each level adds **granularity** and **time precision**.

### Level 1: Aggregate Production Planning (APP)

- **Time Horizon:** 3 to 18 months.
- **Granularity:** Product families or product lines (not individual SKUs).
- **Decisions Made:**
  * Production quantities per period.
  * Workforce hiring / layoff levels.
  * Inventory accumulation or backordering.
  * Use of overtime or subcontracting.
- **Objective:** Minimize the sum of **production costs + inventory carrying costs + hiring/layoff costs + backorder costs**.

### Level 2: Master Production Schedule (MPS)

- **Time Horizon:** Weeks to months (typically 8–12 weeks visible, with a planning horizon of 6–12 months).
- **Granularity:** Specific end-items (SKUs).
- **Decisions Made:**
  * Quantity of each finished product to be produced in each time bucket.
  * Timing of production to meet forecasted demand and customer orders.
- **Anchors:** The MPS must respect the aggregate plan, the available capacity, and the current inventory position.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Available-to-Promise (ATP):** A KTU-relevant concept. ATP is the uncommitted portion of the MPS that can be promised to a new customer. Calculated as:
>
> $$
> \text{ATP} = \text{On-Hand Inventory} + \text{Scheduled Receipts} - \text{Committed Customer Orders}
> $$

### Level 3: Material Requirements Planning (MRP)

MRP is a **logic-based computer system** that converts the MPS into a time-phased schedule of component and raw material requirements.

The MRP system processes three primary inputs:
1. **Master Production Schedule** (what to make).
2. **Bill of Materials (BOM)** (what is needed to make it).
3. **Inventory Status File** (what we already have).

From these, MRP produces a **Planned Order Schedule** specifying when and how many of each component must be ordered.

### Level 4: Production Activity Control (PAC)

PAC is the **shop-floor execution layer**. It includes:

- **Order Release:** Authorizing the start of work on the shop floor.
- **Dispatching:** Routing the orders to specific machines/work centers.
- **Expediting (Follow-up):** Tracking progress and pushing for completion.

## 2.2 Aggregate Planning Strategies

The three classical aggregate planning strategies, frequently tested in KTU examinations, are:

### Strategy 1: Chase Strategy

- Production in each period **exactly matches** the forecasted demand.
- Workforce is hired or laid off as demand fluctuates.
- **Inventory level remains near zero** throughout the planning horizon.
- **Suitability:** Service-oriented industries or make-to-order (MTO) manufacturing.

### Strategy 2: Level Strategy

- Production rate is **kept constant** (e.g., same workforce, same output per day).
- Demand fluctuations are absorbed by **inventory buildup** in low-demand periods and **inventory drawdown** in high-demand periods.
- **Suitability:** Mature products with stable demand, high hiring/layoff costs.

### Strategy 3: Mixed / Hybrid Strategy

- Combines elements of both — uses subcontracting, overtime, part-time workers, and inventory adjustments.
- Most realistic and commonly used in practice.

## 2.3 Sequencing and Scheduling Concepts

Sequencing determines the **order** in which jobs are processed on machines, while scheduling determines the **timing** of each job.

### Johnson's Rule (Two-Machine Flow Shop)

For sequencing **n jobs** on **2 machines** in the same order (Machine 1 → Machine 2), Johnson's Rule provides the optimal sequence that **minimizes total makespan (elapsed time)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Johnson's Algorithm (KTU High-Yield Algorithm):**
> 1. List the processing times of all jobs on both machines.
> 2. Find the **smallest processing time** among all jobs.
> 3. If it belongs to **Machine 1**, schedule that job **as early as possible** (place at the leftmost available slot).
> 4. If it belongs to **Machine 2**, schedule that job **as late as possible** (place at the rightmost available slot).
> 5. Remove the scheduled job and **repeat** until all jobs are sequenced.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Units / Remarks |
|---|---|---|
| Plan Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Actual Output}}{\text{Planned Output}} \times 100\%$ | Expressed as a **percentage (%)** |
| Available-to-Promise (ATP) | $\text{ATP} = \text{On-Hand} + \text{Scheduled Receipts} - \text{Committed Orders}$ | Measured in **units of product** |
| Machine Utilization | $U = \dfrac{\text{Loading Time}}{\text{Available Time}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage (%), ideal: **85–90%** |
| Makespan (Johnson's Rule) | $T_{\max} = \text{Exit time of last job from Machine 2}$ | Measured in **hours or days** |
| Total Production Cost | $C_{\text{total}} = C_{\text{prod}} + C_{\text{inv}} + C_{\text{hire/layoff}} + C_{\text{backorder}}$ | Monetary units (**₹, $, €**) |
| Gross Requirements | $G_i = D_i + S_i - S_{i-1}$ | Units of component $i$ per period |
| Net Requirements | $N_i = \max(0, G_i - O_i - H_{i-1})$ | Units of component $i$ per period |
| Planned Order Release | $P_i = N_i \times \text{Lot Size Rule}$ | Units, time-shifted by lead time $LT$ |
| Manufacturing Lead Time | $LT = \sum t_{\text{process}} + t_{\text{queue}} + t_{\text{wait}} + t_{\text{move}}$ | **Hours, days, or weeks** |
| Cumulative Lead Time | $CLT = \text{Longest path through BOM}$ | Time units |
| Production Rate | $R = \dfrac{\text{Units Produced}}{\text{Time Period}}$ | **Units/hour, units/day** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions for MRP Lot-Sizing Rules:**
> - **Lot-for-Lot (L4L):** Order quantity = Net requirement of that period.
> - **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ):** $Q^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$, where $D$ = annual demand, $S$ = setup cost, $H$ = holding cost per unit per year.
> - **Period Order Quantity (POQ):** Number of periods of demand covered by the EOQ.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Activity planning is the **backbone of every manufacturing enterprise**, from small-scale Kerala-based cashew processing units to multinational automotive OEMs. In modern **Industry 4.0** environments, activity planning is integrated with **ERP (Enterprise Resource Planning)** systems such as SAP PP, Oracle Manufacturing, and Microsoft Dynamics. The mathematical rigor of Johnson's Rule, MRP explosion logic, and aggregate planning strategies remains directly applicable in **lean manufacturing, JIT (Just-In-Time), and Six Sigma** operational frameworks.

# Module 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Johnson's Rule Sequencing

> **Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
> Five jobs (J1, J2, J3, J4, J5) are to be processed on two machines in the order **Machine M1 → Machine M2**. The processing times (in hours) are given below. Determine the **optimal sequence** using Johnson's Rule and compute the **total elapsed time (makespan)** and **idle time** on each machine.

| Job | Machine M1 (hrs) | Machine M2 (hrs) |
|---|---|---|
| J1 | 5 | 2 |
| J2 | 1 | 6 |
| J3 | 9 | 7 |
| J4 | 3 | 8 |
| J5 | 10 | 4 |

### Step-by-Step Application of Johnson's Algorithm

**Step 1:** List all processing times in a single table (already done above).

**Step 2:** Identify the smallest processing time among all 10 entries.
- Minimum value = **1 hour** (J2 on Machine M1).

**Step 3:** Since the minimum is on **Machine M1**, place Job J2 in the **earliest available position**.

$$
\text{Current Sequence: } [\,J2\,, \quad , \quad , \quad , \quad ]
$$

**Step 4:** Remove J2 from consideration. Remaining jobs: J1, J3, J4, J5.

**Step 5:** Find the new minimum among the remaining jobs.
- Minimum value = **2 hours** (J1 on Machine M2).

**Step 6:** Since the minimum is on **Machine M2**, place Job J1 in the **latest available position**.

$$
\text{Current Sequence: } [\,J2\,, \quad , \quad , \quad , \,J1\,]
$$

**Step 7:** Remove J1. Remaining: J3, J4, J5. New minimum = **3 hours** (J4 on M1).

**Step 8:** Place J4 in the **earliest available position**.

$$
\text{Current Sequence: } [\,J2\,, \,J4\,, \quad , \quad , \,J1\,]
$$

**Step 9:** Remove J4. Remaining: J3, J5. New minimum = **4 hours** (J5 on M2).

**Step 10:** Place J5 in the **latest available position**.

$$
\text{Current Sequence: } [\,J2\,, \,J4\,, \quad , \,J5\,, \,J1\,]
$$

**Step 11:** The only remaining job J3 goes to the middle empty slot.

$$
\boxed{\text{Optimal Sequence: } J2 \to J4 \to J3 \to J5 \to J1}
$$

### Computing the Makespan (Total Elapsed Time)

| Job | M1 In | M1 Out | M2 In | M2 Out |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| J2 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 7 |
| J4 | 1 | 4 | 7 | 15 |
| J3 | 4 | 13 | 15 | 22 |
| J5 | 13 | 23 | 23 | 27 |
| J1 | 23 | 28 | 28 | 30 |

**Reading the table (KTU standard method):**

For each job, the **M2 start time** is the maximum of (previous job's M2 finish) and (this job's M1 finish). This is to ensure M2 processes jobs in the same order as M1 finishes them.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{J2 on M2:} \quad & \text{In} = \max(0, 1) = 1 \\
& \text{Out} = 1 + 6 = 7 \\
\text{J4 on M2:} \quad & \text{In} = \max(7, 4) = 7 \\
& \text{Out} = 7 + 8 = 15 \\
\text{J3 on M2:} \quad & \text{In} = \max(15, 13) = 15 \\
& \text{Out} = 15 + 7 = 22 \\
\text{J5 on M2:} \quad & \text{In} = \max(22, 23) = 23 \\
& \text{Out} = 23 + 4 = 27 \\
\text{J1 on M2:} \quad & \text{In} = \max(27, 28) = 28 \\
& \text{Out} = 28 + 2 = 30
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total Elapsed Time (Makespan):**

$$
\boxed{T_{\max} = 30 \text{ hours}}
$$

**Idle Time Calculation:**

- **Machine M1:** Starts at t = 0, ends at t = 28.
  * Available time = 30 hours (the makespan of the entire system).
  * Working time = 28 hours.
  * Idle time on M1 = 30 − 28 = **2 hours** (idle from t = 28 to t = 30).

- **Machine M2:** Starts at t = 1, ends at t = 30.
  * Idle time on M2 = 1 hour (idle from t = 0 to t = 1).

**Verification of the algorithm:** The makespan must always be **≥ max(ΣM1, ΣM2)**.

$$
\sum M_1 = 5 + 1 + 9 + 3 + 10 = 28
$$

$$
\sum M_2 = 2 + 6 + 7 + 8 + 4 = 27
$$

$$
\max(28, 27) = 28 \leq 30 \quad \checkmark
$$

The extra 2 hours represent the unavoidable queue waiting time between machines — a direct consequence of the asymmetry in processing loads.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: MRP Explosion (Logic-Based)

> **Problem Statement:** A finished product **P** has a Bill of Materials (BOM) with two components: **A** (1 unit per P) and **B** (2 units per P). The MPS requires 100 units of P in Week 4 and 50 units of P in Week 5. Lead time for component A is 1 week, for B is 2 weeks. Current on-hand inventory: A = 30 units, B = 80 units. No scheduled receipts exist. Determine the **Planned Order Release Schedule** for A and B.

### Step-by-Step MRP Logic

**Step 1: Compute Gross Requirements for the Finished Product P.**

| Week | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Gross Req. of P | 0 | 0 | 0 | 100 | 50 |

**Step 2: Explode to Component A (1 unit per P, Lead Time = 1 week).**

Gross requirement of A in each week = Gross requirement of P in that week.

| Week | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Gross Req. of A | 0 | 0 | 0 | 100 | 50 |
| On-Hand (30) | 30 | 30 | 30 | 0 | 0 |
| Net Req. of A | 0 | 0 | 0 | 70 | 50 |
| Planned Order Receipt | 0 | 0 | 0 | 70 | 50 |
| **Planned Order Release** (LT=1) | 0 | 0 | **70** | **50** | 0 |

**Interpretation of the table (KTU standard format):**
- Week 4 net requirement = 100 (gross) − 30 (on-hand) = 70.
- Week 5 net requirement = 50 (gross) − 0 (on-hand after Week 4) = 50.
- The 70 units must be **received in Week 4**, so they must be **released in Week 3** (Lead Time = 1 week).
- The 50 units must be **received in Week 5**, so they must be **released in Week 4**.

**Step 3: Explode to Component B (2 units per P, Lead Time = 2 weeks).**

Gross requirement of B in each week = 2 × Gross requirement of P.

| Week | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Gross Req. of P | 0 | 0 | 0 | 100 | 50 |
| Gross Req. of B (×2) | 0 | 0 | 0 | 200 | 100 |
| On-Hand (80) | 80 | 80 | 80 | 0 | 0 |
| Net Req. of B | 0 | 0 | 0 | 120 | 100 |
| Planned Order Receipt | 0 | 0 | 0 | 120 | 100 |
| **Planned Order Release** (LT=2) | 0 | **120** | **100** | 0 | 0 |

**Interpretation:**
- 120 units of B must be received in Week 4 → released in **Week 2**.
- 100 units of B must be received in Week 5 → released in **Week 3**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** Component A must be ordered in Weeks 3 and 4 (70 + 50 units). Component B must be ordered in Weeks 2 and 3 (120 + 100 units). This time-phased release schedule enables the firm to avoid both **stockouts** and **excess inventory**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Aspect | Aggregate Planning | MPS | MRP | PAC |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Time Horizon** | 3–18 months | 1–6 months | 1–12 weeks | Hours to days |
| **Granularity** | Product family | Specific SKU | Components | Shop-floor order |
| **Output** | Production rates | Time-phased quantities | Planned order releases | Dispatched orders |
| **Key Input** | Forecast + capacity | APP + customer orders | MPS + BOM | MRP + WIP status |
| **Decision Maker** | Senior management | Production planner | MRP system | Shop supervisor |
