# Module 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]**
> **Define Aggregate Production Planning. List any two aggregate planning strategies.**

**Model Answer:**

Aggregate Production Planning (APP) is a medium-range (3 to 18 months) production planning activity that establishes general levels of output, inventory, workforce, and subcontracting in aggregate product families to meet forecasted demand at minimum total cost.

The two aggregate planning strategies are:
1. **Chase Strategy** — Production is varied each period to exactly match demand; workforce size fluctuates.
2. **Level Strategy** — Production rate and workforce are kept constant; demand variations are absorbed by inventory.

### Question A2
> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]**
> **What is the Available-to-Promise (ATP) quantity in a Master Production Schedule? Mention its significance.**

**Model Answer:**

Available-to-Promise (ATP) is the uncommitted balance of a finished product's scheduled production quantity in a given period, available to be promised to new customers.

$$
\text{ATP} = \text{On-Hand Inventory} + \text{Scheduled Receipts} - \text{Customer-Allocated Quantities}
$$

**Significance:** ATP allows sales teams to make **reliable delivery commitments** to customers without consulting the production planner, thereby improving customer service and order acceptance speed.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model Question]**
> **(a)** Explain the **four levels** of Production Planning and Control (PPC) hierarchy with their respective time horizons and decision types. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Five jobs are to be processed on two machines in the order **M1 → M2**. The processing times (in hours) are:

| Job | M1 (hrs) | M2 (hrs) |
|---|---|---|
| A | 11 | 9 |
| B | 5 | 18 |
| C | 7 | 14 |
| D | 2 | 6 |
| E | 9 | 5 |

> Determine the **optimal sequence** using Johnson's Rule. Also compute the **total elapsed time (makespan)** and **idle time** on both machines. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question A (a) — 7 Marks

The four levels of PPC hierarchy are:

1. **Aggregate Production Planning (APP)**
   - **Time Horizon:** 3 to 18 months.
   - **Decision Type:** Strategic — product family output, workforce levels, inventory build-up, subcontracting.
   - **Key Output:** A plan that minimizes the total of production, inventory, hiring/layoff, and backorder costs.

2. **Master Production Schedule (MPS)**
   - **Time Horizon:** 1 to 6 months (typically 8–12 weeks visible).
   - **Decision Type:** Tactical — specific end-items, exact quantities per time period.
   - **Key Output:** A time-phased schedule of finished products; drives MRP and forms the basis for Available-to-Promise calculations.

3. **Material Requirements Planning (MRP)**
   - **Time Horizon:** 1 to 12 weeks.
   - **Decision Type:** Operational — components, sub-assemblies, and raw material orders.
   - **Key Output:** Planned order releases, time-phased based on BOM and lead time.

4. **Production Activity Control (PAC)**
   - **Time Horizon:** Hours to days.
   - **Decision Type:** Execution — order release, dispatching, expediting, and shop-floor monitoring.
   - **Key Output:** Actual production completion, feedback on variances.

### Model Solution for Question A (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: List processing times.**

| Job | M1 | M2 |
|---|---|---|
| A | 11 | 9 |
| B | 5 | 18 |
| C | 7 | 14 |
| D | 2 | 6 |
| E | 9 | 5 |

**Step 2: Apply Johnson's Rule iteratively.**

- Min time = 2 hr (D on M1) → schedule D in the **earliest** slot. Sequence: `[D, _, _, _, _]`
- Next min = 5 hr (B on M1, E on M2; tie). Choose B on M1 (or E on M2; either is valid, but we follow strict smallest → M1). Sequence: `[D, B, _, _, _]`
- Next min = 5 hr (E on M2) → schedule E in the **latest** slot. Sequence: `[D, B, _, E, _]`
- Next min = 9 hr (E was taken; check again). Remaining: A (11, 9), C (7, 14).
- Min = 7 hr (C on M1) → schedule C in the **earliest** available slot. Sequence: `[D, B, C, E, _]`
- Last job A goes to the middle slot. Sequence: `[D, B, C, A, E]`

**Final Optimal Sequence: D → B → C → A → E**

**Step 3: Compute makespan.**

| Job | M1 In | M1 Out | M2 In (= max(prev M2 Out, this M1 Out)) | M2 Out (= M2 In + M2 time) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| D | 0 | 2 | 2 | 8 |
| B | 2 | 7 | 8 | 26 |
| C | 7 | 14 | 26 | 40 |
| A | 14 | 25 | 40 | 49 |
| E | 25 | 34 | 49 | 54 |

**Total Elapsed Time = 54 hours.**

**Idle Times:**
- **Machine M1:** Working time = 34 hours, available = 54 hours → Idle = **20 hours**.
- **Machine M2:** Working time = 54 hours, available = 54 hours → Idle = **0 hours** (M2 is the bottleneck).

**Verification:**

$$
\sum M_1 = 11+5+7+2+9 = 34
$$

$$
\sum M_2 = 9+18+14+6+5 = 52
$$

$$
\max(34, 52) = 52 \leq 54 \quad \checkmark
$$

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]**
> **(a)** Explain the **concept of MRP**. With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **inputs, processing logic, and outputs** of an MRP system. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A finished product **X** has a Bill of Materials as follows: **X = 2A + 1B**, and **A = 3C + 1D**. The MPS requires 50 units of X in Week 6. Lead time: X = 1 week, A = 2 weeks, B = 3 weeks, C = 1 week, D = 2 weeks. Initial on-hand inventory: A = 20, B = 30, C = 0, D = 50. Determine the **Planned Order Release Schedule** for all components. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question B (a) — 7 Marks

**Definition:** Material Requirements Planning (MRP) is a computer-based production planning and inventory control system used to manage dependent demand items — components and raw materials whose demand is derived from the production schedule of finished products.

**MRP Block Diagram (textual):**

```
INPUTS                              MRP LOGIC ENGINE              OUTPUTS
---------                           --------------------           ----------
Master Production Schedule   ---->                            ---> Gross Requirements
Bill of Materials (BOM)      ---->   Gross Requirements   ---> Net Requirements
Inventory Status File        ---->   Net  Requirements   ---> Planned Order Releases
Lead Time Information        ---->   Planned Order       ---> Exception Reports
                                       Release Schedule
                                   Order Rescheduling
```

**Processing Logic (Step-by-Step):**
1. Identify all components in the BOM of the finished product.
2. Compute **Gross Requirements** = (MPS quantity of parent) × (Quantity per assembly).
3. Subtract **On-Hand Inventory** and **Scheduled Receipts** to get **Net Requirements**.
4. **Lot-Size** the net requirements (e.g., Lot-for-Lot, EOQ, POQ).
5. **Offset** the planned order by the lead time to get the **Planned Order Release** date.

### Model Solution for Question B (b) — 7 Marks

**BOM Structure:**
- **X** requires 2A and 1B.
- **A** requires 3C and 1D.

**MPS:** 50 units of X needed in **Week 6**.

**Step 1: Explode X → A and B.**

| Week | 6 |
|---|---|
| Gross Req. of X | 50 |
| Gross Req. of A (50 × 2) | 100 |
| On-Hand A | 20 |
| Net Req. of A | 80 |
| Planned Order Receipt of A (Week 6, LT=2 weeks) | 80 |
| **Planned Order Release of A** | **Week 4** |

| Week | 6 |
|---|---|
| Gross Req. of B (50 × 1) | 50 |
| On-Hand B | 30 |
| Net Req. of B | 20 |
| Planned Order Receipt of B (Week 6, LT=3 weeks) | 20 |
| **Planned Order Release of B** | **Week 3** |

**Step 2: Explode A → C and D (assuming Lot-for-Lot, 80 units of A in Week 4).**

| Week | 4 |
|---|---|
| Gross Req. of A | 80 |
| Gross Req. of C (80 × 3) | 240 |
| On-Hand C | 0 |
| Net Req. of C | 240 |
| Planned Order Receipt of C (Week 4, LT=1 week) | 240 |
| **Planned Order Release of C** | **Week 3** |

| Week | 4 |
|---|---|
| Gross Req. of D (80 × 1) | 80 |
| On-Hand D | 50 |
| Net Req. of D | 30 |
| Planned Order Receipt of D (Week 4, LT=2 weeks) | 30 |
| **Planned Order Release of D** | **Week 2** |

**Final Planned Order Release Schedule:**

| Component | Quantity | Release Week |
|---|---|---|
| **B** | 20 | Week 3 |
| **A** | 80 | Week 4 |
| **C** | 240 | Week 3 |
| **D** | 30 | Week 2 |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Lead Time Offset:** Many students compute the **Planned Order Receipt** in the right week but forget to **back-schedule** the release by the lead time. This is the most common 2–3 mark deduction in KTU answer scripts.
> 2. **Net Requirement Calculation Error:** Students often fail to subtract the **on-hand inventory** properly, leading to inflated planned orders. Always check: $N_i = \max(0, G_i - O_i)$.
> 3. **Johnson's Rule Tie-Breaking:** When the minimum processing time appears on **both machines simultaneously**, students are confused. Convention: prioritize M1 (place earliest); but document the choice.
> 4. **Skipping Idle Time Computation:** In Johnson's Rule problems, KTU examiners frequently award a separate sub-mark for computing **idle time** on both machines. Do not omit it.
> 5. **Confusing APP and MPS:** Aggregate Planning deals with **product families** (e.g., "all ball bearings"), while MPS deals with **specific SKUs** (e.g., "6204-ZZ bearings"). Writing these interchangeably loses marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Activity Planning**

- **Activity Planning** translates strategic business plans into executable shop-floor instructions through a **4-tier hierarchy**: APP → MPS → MRP → PAC.
- **Aggregate Planning** uses three classical strategies: **Chase, Level, and Mixed**, optimizing a cost function comprising production, inventory, hiring, and backorder costs.
- **Master Production Schedule (MPS)** disaggregates the aggregate plan into time-phased quantities of specific end-items and is the foundation for **Available-to-Promise (ATP)** calculations.
- **MRP** is a **logic-based system** that processes MPS + BOM + Inventory Status + Lead Time to generate **Planned Order Releases** via gross-requirement explosion, on-hand subtraction, and lead-time offsetting.
- **Johnson's Rule** optimally sequences $n$ jobs on 2 machines; the resulting **makespan** is the total elapsed time, with idle time computed as $T_{\max} - \text{working time on each machine}$.
- **Production Activity Control (PAC)** performs the final execution step: **order release → dispatching → expediting**.
- Key performance metrics to memorize: **Efficiency (%), Utilization (%), Makespan (hours), Lead Time (days), ATP (units)**.
- The **capacity planning** check (CRP) must follow MRP to ensure the planned orders are within the **available machine and labor capacity**.
- **Lot-sizing rules** (L4L, EOQ, POQ) directly impact the magnitude and frequency of planned orders.
- For KTU examinations, always present: (i) Johnson's Rule sequence, (ii) complete makespan table, (iii) idle time on each machine, and (iv) MRP explosion table with proper lead-time offsets.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shop Loading
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET723 - PRODUCTION AND OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Shop Loading

# Shop Loading — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, **Shop Loading** is the operational function of **assigning specific jobs/work-orders to specific machines or work-centres** of a manufacturing facility over a defined planning horizon, subject to capacity, precedence, and due-date constraints. It is the *bridge* between the **Master Production Schedule (MPS)** and the actual execution on the shop floor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (NEP 2020 Aligned):**
> *Shop Loading is the process of determining which work-centres will process which jobs, in what quantities, and in what sequence during a given planning period, with the objective of balancing workload, minimizing tardiness, and maximizing machine utilisation.*

Shop Loading is a sub-module of the broader **Production Planning & Control (PPC)** hierarchy:

1. **Aggregate Planning** (macro)
2. **Master Production Schedule (MPS)**
3. **Material Requirements Planning (MRP)**
4. **Load Planning / Capacity Planning** ← *Shop Loading lives here*
5. **Shop Scheduling (Sequencing & Dispatching)**

## 1.1 Two Levels of Shop Loading

| Level | Question Answered | Time Horizon | Detail |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Order Loading** | *Which order goes to which machine?* | Short (days/weeks) | Machine-level, individual job |
| **Center Loading** | *Which work-centre processes which group of orders?* | Medium (weeks/months) | Department-level aggregate |

> [!NOTE]
> **Center Loading** aggregates orders to a department; **Order Loading** disaggregates them to individual machines. KTU examiners frequently test the distinction.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Restaurant Kitchen"

Imagine a busy restaurant kitchen on a Saturday night. The **head chef (production planner)** has:
- **Dishes (jobs)** that need to be cooked
- **Cooking stations — tandoor, griddle, fryer, wok, cold-station (machines/work-centres)**
- A **timeline from 7 PM to 11 PM (planning horizon)**

**Shop Loading** is the act of writing on the kitchen board:
> *"Order #204 → Tandoor, start at 7:15 PM, needs 20 min"*
> *"Order #205 → Griddle, start at 7:20 PM, needs 12 min"*

If the chef ignores station capacity → **Infinite Loading** (chefs get overbooked, tickets pile up).
If the chef respects each station's max throughput → **Finite Loading** (balanced ticket board).
Loading decisions determine whether the food comes out **hot, on-time, and unstressed** — or whether the kitchen collapses.

## 1.3 Loading Philosophy — The Four Canonical Models

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly requires students to differentiate the following loading strategies:

### A. Finite Loading vs Infinite Loading

| Aspect | **Infinite Loading** | **Finite Loading** |
|---|---|---|
| **Capacity Assumption** | Capacity is treated as **unlimited / stretchable** (use of overtime, subcontracting) | Capacity is treated as **rigid and fixed** |
| **Workload Behaviour** | Workload may exceed capacity → backlog forms | Workload is **truncated** at capacity; excess work is **deferred** |
| **Use Case** | Make-to-stock; demand-driven environment | Make-to-order; bottleneck-shop environment |
| **Cost Implication** | Lower inventory, higher capacity cost | Higher inventory, lower capacity cost |
| **Implementation** | Simple | Complex (needs capacity matrix + Gantt) |
| **KTU Tag** | "Push-based" loading | "Pull-based" loading |

### B. Forward Loading vs Backward Loading

| Aspect | **Forward Loading** | **Backward Loading** |
|---|---|---|
| **Starting Point** | Begins at **period 0 (today)** | Begins at the **due date** |
| **Direction** | Forward in time | Backward in time |
| **Use When** | Demand is firm, capacity is loose | Due dates are critical, capacity is tight |
| **Output** | Earliest completion | Latest start time |
| **Best For** | Job-shop planning, order promising | Assembly line, due-date-driven scheduling |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric (KTU board favourite):** *Machine Utilisation* = **Total Loaded Hours / Total Available Hours × 100%**

## 1.4 Loading Inputs and Outputs

**Inputs:**
- Open orders from MPS
- Routing file (operation sequence per job)
- Standard times per operation
- Work-centre capacity calendar
- Priority/due-date rules

**Outputs:**
- **Load Profile** (hours/work-centre/period)
- **Load Gantt Chart** (visual schedule)
- **Overload / Underload Report**
- **Dispatch List** (which job next, on which machine)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Shop Loading — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Stage Loading Decision Process

### Stage 1 — Demand Aggregation
Pull all open orders, group by part-number, multiply by routing time per operation to get **load-hours required** at each work-centre.

### Stage 2 — Capacity Comparison
Compare required load-hours with available capacity-hours at each work-centre. The **difference** decides the action:

$$
\Delta_{WC} = L_{WC}^{required} - C_{WC}^{available}
$$

- If $\Delta_{WC} > 0$ → **Overload** (need finite loading, OT, sub-contracting, or reschedule)
- If $\Delta_{WC} < 0$ → **Underload** (idle capacity, seek new orders)
- If $\Delta_{WC} = 0$ → **Perfectly balanced** (rare in practice)

### Stage 3 — Assignment
Distribute jobs to machines using:
- **Assignment Problem** (Hungarian Method) for 1-to-1 matching
- **Heuristics** (priority rules) for n-to-m matching with multiple jobs per machine

## 2.2 The Load Profile (KTU Board Favourite Table)

A **Load Profile** is a tabular report showing the workload (in hours) on each work-centre across the planning periods.

| Work-Centre | Capacity (hrs/period) | Period 1 | Period 2 | Period 3 | Period 4 | Total Load | Utilisation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| WC-1 (Lathe) | 160 | 180 | 160 | 140 | 150 | 630 | **98.4%** |
| WC-2 (Milling) | 160 | 120 | 130 | 110 | 100 | 460 | **71.9%** |
| WC-3 (Grinding) | 160 | 90 | 95 | 80 | 85 | 350 | **54.7%** |
| WC-4 (Assembly) | 160 | 200 | 180 | 170 | 160 | 710 | **110.9%** ⚠ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb (KTU):** A work-centre is **Overloaded** when utilisation > 100%; **Balanced** between 85–95%; **Under-loaded** below 70%. Anything > 100% in two consecutive periods triggers a **Capacity Adjustment Action**.

## 2.3 The KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

> [!NOTE]
> Memorise this table. KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Exam) and Module Internal tests routinely ask derivations/numericals based on these.

| # | Formula / Metric | LaTeX Expression | Unit | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Machine Utilisation | $U_i = \dfrac{\sum t_{ij}}{C_i^{avail}} \times 100$ | % | Load profile analysis |
| 2 | Load Imbalance Index | $LII = \dfrac{\max(L_i) - \min(L_i)}{C_{avg}}$ | ratio | Comparing work-centre loads |
| 3 | Number of Machines Required (rounded up) | $m = \left\lceil \dfrac{T}{\text{Avail hrs per machine}} \right\rceil$ | integer | Capacity planning |
| 4 | Cycle Time | $CT = \dfrac{\text{Available Time}}{\text{Demand}}$ | time/unit | Line balancing |
| 5 | Sum of Processing Times (1 machine) | $\sum p_i$ | hrs | Makespan calculation |
| 6 | Makespan (n jobs, 1 machine) | $F_{max} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i$ | hrs | Single-machine flow time |
| 7 | Flow Time of job *i* | $F_i = \sum_{k=1}^{i} p_k$ | hrs | Cumulative completion |
| 8 | Average Flow Time | $\bar{F} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum F_i$ | hrs | Performance metric |
| 9 | Tardiness | $T_i = \max(0, C_i - d_i)$ | hrs | Due-date performance |
| 10 | Lateness (signed) | $L_i = C_i - d_i$ | hrs | Early/late detection |
| 11 | Number of Tardy Jobs | $n_T = \sum \mathbb{1}(C_i > d_i)$ | count | Lateness minimisation |
| 12 | Machine Idle Time | $I_i = C_{max} - \sum p_{ij}$ | hrs | Work-centre efficiency |
| 13 | Hungarian Step-1: Row Reduction | $c'_{ij} = c_{ij} - \min_j c_{ij}$ | — | Assignment problem |
| 14 | Hungarian Step-2: Column Reduction | $c''_{ij} = c'_{ij} - \min_i c'_{ij}$ | — | Assignment problem |
| 15 | Min number of lines covering zeros | $N$ vs $n$ (order) | — | Optimality test |
| 16 | Unbalanced → Balanced (dummy row/col) | $c_{n+1, j} = 0$ or $c_{i, m+1} = 0$ | — | Rectangular matrix |

> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** For absolute value, the table uses inline notation to avoid breaking Markdown — i.e., $\vert c_{ij} - c'_{ij} \vert$ instead of the pipe character.

## 2.4 The Assignment Problem — Theory

The classic **Assignment Problem** in shop loading is:

> *Assign **n jobs to n machines** (1-to-1) such that the total processing cost is minimised, with each job assigned to exactly one machine and each machine receiving exactly one job.*

Mathematically:

$$
\text{Minimise } Z = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} c_{ij}\, x_{ij}
$$

Subject to:
- $\sum_{j=1}^{n} x_{ij} = 1 \quad \forall i$ (each job assigned to exactly one machine)
- $\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_{ij} = 1 \quad \forall j$ (each machine gets exactly one job)
- $x_{ij} \in \{0, 1\}$

Where $c_{ij}$ is the cost (time/cost) of assigning job *i* to machine *j*, and $x_{ij} = 1$ if assigned, else 0.

The **Hungarian Method** (Kuhn, 1955) solves this in $O(n^3)$ time.

## 2.5 Loading Priority Rules (Used When n > m)

When there are **more jobs than machines**, a **priority rule** sequences them. KTU 2024 syllabus lists:

| Rule | Acronym | Decision Criterion | Minimises |
|---|---|---|---|
| First-Come-First-Served | **FCFS** | Earliest arrival date | Average flow time (empirically) |
| Shortest Processing Time | **SPT** | $\min p_i$ | Mean flow time, WIP, lateness |
| Longest Processing Time | **LPT** | $\max p_i$ | Makespan on parallel machines |
| Earliest Due Date | **EDD** | $\min d_i$ | Maximum tardiness $T_{max}$ |
| Critical Ratio | **CR** | $\min CR = \dfrac{d_i - t}{p_i}$ | Due-date tightness |
| Slack Time | **SLK** | $\min(d_i - t - p_i)$ | Lateness |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** *SPT is provably optimal for minimising mean flow time on a single machine (Smith's Rule, 1956).* This is a 2-mark KTU favourite.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Hungarian Method for Shop Loading (3 Jobs × 3 Machines)

> **Problem (KTU-Style):** Three jobs (J1, J2, J3) are to be loaded onto three machines (M1, M2, M3). The processing time matrix (in hours) is given below. Find the **optimal assignment** that minimises the total processing time.

|  | M1 | M2 | M3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **J1** | 9 | 2 | 7 |
| **J2** | 6 | 4 | 2 |
| **J3** | 5 | 8 | 6 |

### Step 1 — Row Reduction
Subtract the **minimum element of each row** from every element in that row.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Row 1 min} &= \min(9, 2, 7) = 2 \\
\text{New Row 1: } & (9-2,\ 2-2,\ 7-2) = (7, 0, 5) \\
\text{Row 2 min} &= \min(6, 4, 2) = 2 \\
\text{New Row 2: } & (6-2,\ 4-2,\ 2-2) = (4, 2, 0) \\
\text{Row 3 min} &= \min(5, 8, 6) = 5 \\
\text{New Row 3: } & (5-5,\ 8-5,\ 6-5) = (0, 3, 1)
\end{aligned}
$$

Reduced matrix after Step 1:

|  | M1 | M2 | M3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **J1** | 7 | **0** | 5 |
| **J2** | 4 | 2 | **0** |
| **J3** | **0** | 3 | 1 |

### Step 2 — Column Reduction
Subtract the **minimum element of each column** from every element in that column.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Col 1 min} &= \min(7, 4, 0) = 0 \quad (\text{no change}) \\
\text{Col 2 min} &= \min(0, 2, 3) = 0 \quad (\text{no change}) \\
\text{Col 3 min} &= \min(5, 0, 1) = 0 \quad (\text{no change})
\end{aligned}
$$

Matrix unchanged. (Lucky case — both steps often achieve this.)

### Step 3 — Cover All Zeros with Minimum Number of Lines
Draw the **minimum number of horizontal and vertical lines** to cover all the zeros.

|  | M1 | M2 | M3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **J1** | 7 | **0** ✗ | 5 |
| **J2** | 4 | 2 | **0** ✗ |
| **J3** | **0** ✗ | 3 | 1 |

Lines drawn: **Row 1, Row 2, Column 1** → total **3 lines** = $n$ (number of rows/columns).

> [!NOTE]
> **Optimality Test:** If the minimum number of covering lines equals $n$, the matrix is **optimal** and an assignment can be made directly from the zero pattern.

### Step 4 — Make the Assignment
Match zeros such that one zero per row and one zero per column is selected:

- **J1 → M2** (only zero in row 1 at column 2)
- **J2 → M3** (only zero in row 2 at column 3)
- **J3 → M1** (only zero in row 3 at column 1)

### Step 5 — Compute Total Cost
Read original processing times for the assigned pairs:

$$
\begin{aligned}
J1 \to M2 &: c_{12} = 2 \text{ hrs} \\
J2 \to M3 &: c_{23} = 2 \text{ hrs} \\
J3 \to M1 &: c_{31} = 5 \text{ hrs} \\
Z_{min} &= 2 + 2 + 5 = 9 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **✅ OPTIMAL LOADING: Total time = 9 hours** (J1→M2, J2→M3, J3→M1)

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Unbalanced Assignment (3 Jobs × 4 Machines)

> **Problem:** Add M4 to the above. Processing times: M4 column = (4, 6, 5).

Since 3 jobs ≠ 4 machines, add a **dummy job J4** with all-zero times:

|  | M1 | M2 | M3 | M4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **J1** | 9 | 2 | 7 | 4 |
| **J2** | 6 | 4 | 2 | 6 |
| **J3** | 5 | 8 | 6 | 5 |
| **J4 (dummy)** | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |

Apply Hungarian method as before. The dummy row will be assigned to the **most expensive machine** (the one that minimises loss of productivity), and the real jobs will occupy the remaining three. This is how KTU tests **unbalanced loading**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Finite Loading with Capacity Constraint

> **Problem:** A work-centre has **160 hours/month**. Four jobs are waiting with processing times **8, 6, 10, 12 hours** and due dates in months 1, 1, 2, 3 respectively. Apply **SPT** under **finite loading** and report utilisation and tardiness.

### Step 1 — Order jobs by SPT

$$
p = \{6,\ 8,\ 10,\ 12\} \text{ hrs (sorted ascending)}
$$

### Step 2 — Sequence and Accumulate Load

| Order | Job (by SPT) | $p_i$ (hrs) | Cum. Load $F_i$ | Period (160 hr) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | J2 | 6 | 6 | Period 1 |
| 2 | J1 | 8 | 14 | Period 1 |
| 3 | J3 | 10 | 24 | Period 1 |
| 4 | J4 | 12 | 36 | Period 1 |

### Step 3 — Period Assignment (Finite Load)

Total load = **36 hours** in Period 1. Available = 160 hrs. **No overload** → all four jobs go in Period 1.

### Step 4 — Compute Performance Metrics

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mean Flow Time } \bar{F} &= \frac{6+14+24+36}{4} = \frac{80}{4} = 20 \text{ hrs} \\
\text{Makespan } C_{max} &= 36 \text{ hrs} \\
\text{Available Time} &= 160 \text{ hrs} \\
\text{Utilisation } U &= \frac{36}{160} \times 100 = 22.5\% \\
\text{Idle Time} &= 160 - 36 = 124 \text{ hrs (Under-loaded)} \\
\text{Tardiness } T_i &= \max(0, C_i - d_i) \\
\text{All complete in Period 1; due dates in P1, P1, P2, P3 } \\
T_{J3} &= \max(0, 24 - 160) = 0 \\
T_{J4} &= \max(0, 36 - 320) = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conclusion:** All jobs are early (negative lateness), zero tardiness. Work-centre is **under-loaded** at 22.5% — seek new orders or reduce capacity.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Load Profile Construction

> **Problem:** WC-A processes 4 jobs. Their operations and standard times are:

| Job | Op-10 (hrs) | Op-20 (hrs) | Op-30 (hrs) | Due Period |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| J-A | 5 | 8 | 4 | 3 |
| J-B | 6 | 4 | 6 | 2 |
| J-C | 3 | 5 | 7 | 4 |
| J-D | 4 | 6 | 5 | 2 |

Capacity = **30 hours/period** at each work-centre. Construct the **load profile** and identify overloads.

### Step 1 — Aggregate load by work-centre

| Work-Centre | Load (hrs) |
|---|---|
| WC-Op10 | 5+6+3+4 = **18** |
| WC-Op20 | 8+4+5+6 = **23** |
| WC-Op30 | 4+6+7+5 = **22** |

### Step 2 — Distribute load into periods (Forward Loading, SPT within period)

Total work = 18 + 23 + 22 = **63 hours** across 3 periods (capacity = 90 hrs total).
**No overload overall** — distribute to meet due dates.

For example, prioritising jobs with due-date in period 2 first:

| Period | WC-Op10 (cap 30) | WC-Op20 (cap 30) | WC-Op30 (cap 30) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | J-D (4) | J-B (4) | J-B (6) |
| 2 | J-B (6) | J-D (6) | J-D (5) |
| 3 | J-A (5), J-C (3) | J-A (8), J-C (5) | J-A (4), J-C (7) |
| 4 | — | — | — |

> **Load Profile Utilisation:**
> - Period 1: $\frac{4+4+6}{90} \times 100 = 15.6\%$
> - Period 2: $\frac{6+6+5}{90} \times 100 = 18.9\%$
> - Period 3: $\frac{8+13+11}{90} \times 100 = 35.6\%$

**Overall Utilisation** = $\frac{63}{90} \times 100 = 70.0\%$ (Under-loaded — typical for a job-shop).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Loading Method to Use?

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** This matrix is the most-tested concept — examiners ask "Which loading method is appropriate for the following scenario?" — use this as a cheat-sheet.

| Scenario | Infinite / Finite | Forward / Backward | Priority Rule |
|---|---|---|---|
| Make-to-stock, high volume | **Infinite** | **Forward** | FCFS |
| Make-to-order, tight due dates | **Finite** | **Backward** | EDD or CR |
| Bottleneck work-centre, scarce capacity | **Finite** | **Forward** | SPT (for flow) or LPT (for parallel machines) |
| Demand surge, willing to use OT/sub-contract | **Infinite** | **Forward** | CR |
| Job-shop, 1-to-1 assignment with costs | **Finite** | **Backward** | **Hungarian Method** |
| Assembly line, balanced flow | **Finite** | **Backward** | SPT |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Shop Loading Decision Flow (Master Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Open Orders from MPS] --> B[Aggregate Demand by Work-Centre]
    B --> C[Compute Required Load-Hours per WC per Period]
    C --> D{Capacity Check}
    D -- Load <= Capacity --> E[Infinite Loading - Accept All Orders]
    D -- Load > Capacity --> F[Finite Loading - Trigger Adjustment]
    F --> G{Adjustment Strategy}
    G -- Use Overtime --> H[Expand Capacity]
    G -- Subcontract --> I[Outsource Excess]
    G -- Reschedule --> J[Defer to Later Period]
    H --> K[Build Load Gantt Chart]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    E --> K
    K --> L[Generate Dispatch List]
    L --> M[Monitor and Feedback to MPS]
    M --> A
```

## 4.2 Center Loading vs Order Loading (Hierarchical Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph CP[Capacity Planning Level]
        M1[MPS - Master Production Schedule]
    end
    subgraph CL[Center Loading Level - Aggregated]
        W1[WC-1 Lathe Dept]
        W2[WC-2 Milling Dept]
        W3[WC-3 Grinding Dept]
        W4[WC-4 Assembly Dept]
    end
    subgraph OL[Order Loading Level - Disaggregated]
        X1[Lathe-1]
        X2[Lathe-2]
        Y1[Mill-A]
        Y2[Mill-B]
        Z1[Grinder-X]
        A1[Assembly Station]
    end
    M1 --> W1
    M1 --> W2
    M1 --> W3
    M1 --> W4
    W1 --> X1
    W1 --> X2
    W2 --> Y1
    W2 --> Y2
    W3 --> Z1
    W4 --> A1
```

## 4.3 Hungarian Method Algorithm Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Start: n x n Cost Matrix] --> S1[Step 1: Row Reduction - subtract row min]
    S1 --> S2[Step 2: Column Reduction - subtract col min]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Cover all zeros with min lines]
    S3 --> T{Number of lines = n?}
    T -- Yes --> A[Step 4: Make assignment from zeros]
    T -- No --> S4[Step 4: Find min uncovered element]
    S4 --> S5[Subtract from uncovered, add to doubly-covered]
    S5 --> S3
    A --> R[Read optimal assignment from original matrix]
    R --> Z[Compute minimum total cost Z]
```

## 4.4 Finite vs Infinite Loading — Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Q: Is the work-centre a bottleneck?] -->|Yes| FIN[Use Finite Loading]
    Q -->|No| INF{Is demand stable and forecastable?}
    INF -->|Yes| INF2[Use Infinite Loading]
    INF -->|No| FIN2[Use Finite Loading]
    FIN --> FB{Backward start? due-date tight?}
    FIN2 --> FB
    FB -->|Yes| BK[Backward Loading]
    FB -->|No| FW[Forward Loading]
    INF2 --> FWB{Subcontracting available?}
    FWB -->|Yes| INF3[Infinite + OT - Forward]
    FWB -->|No| FIN3[Finite + Reallocation]
```

## 4.5 Gantt Chart — Visual Block Architecture of a Loaded Shop Floor

```mermaid
gantt
    title Shop Floor Loading Gantt - Week 22
    dateFormat  HH:mm
    axisFormat  %H:%M
    section Lathe M1
    Job-A Op10    :a1, 08:00, 3h
    Job-B Op10    :a2, after a1, 2h
    Job-C Op10    :a3, after a2, 4h
    section Mill M2
    Job-A Op20    :b1, 08:00, 4h
    Job-B Op20    :b2, after b1, 2h
    Job-C Op20    :b3, after b2, 3h
    section Grinder M3
    Job-A Op30    :c1, 13:00, 2h
    Job-B Op30    :c2, after c1, 3h
    Job-C Op30    :c3, after c2, 4h
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A Gantt chart of shop loading visualises **which job occupies which machine, for how long, starting when**. Empty blocks indicate idle time (visible in the Gantt above between 08:00 and 13:00 on the Grinder — an **under-loaded** work-centre).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question Paper Set B]**
**Differentiate between Finite Loading and Infinite Loading. State one situation where each is most appropriate. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Finite Loading | Infinite Loading |
|---|---|---|
| **Capacity Assumption** | Treats work-centre capacity as fixed/rigid | Treats capacity as variable/expandable |
| **Workload Behaviour** | Truncates at capacity; backlog may be deferred | Accepts all orders; backlog grows |
| **Use Case** | Job-shops with bottleneck work-centres | Make-to-stock environments with stable demand |
| **Sub-contracting/OT** | Avoided | Readily used |
| **Cost Implication** | Higher inventory carrying cost | Higher capacity cost |

**Example — Finite:** A precision aerospace machine shop with a 5-axis CNC as bottleneck.
**Example — Infinite:** An FMCG bottling plant producing for inventory to meet seasonal demand.

*(Word count limit: ~100 words. Award full marks if all four parameters and one example each are given.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**What is the difference between Center Loading and Order Loading? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

- **Center Loading** is the *aggregated* assignment of orders to **departments or work-centres** over a medium-term horizon (weeks/months). It produces a **Load Profile** showing total hours per work-centre per period and identifies overloaded/under-loaded centres.

- **Order Loading** is the *disaggregated* assignment of individual orders to **specific machines** within a work-centre over a short-term horizon (days/weeks). It produces a **Dispatch List** and a **Gantt Chart** showing job-machine-time mapping.

- **Hierarchical relationship:** Center Loading comes *first* (top-down decomposition of MPS) and is followed by Order Loading (bottom-up detailed execution).

> **Examiner's key:** Award 1 mark for each of: (a) definition of center loading, (b) definition of order loading, (c) hierarchical relationship/time-horizon contrast.

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **QUESTION A — OPTION 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]**

> **A machine shop has 4 jobs (J1, J2, J3, J4) that need to be assigned to 4 machines (M1, M2, M3, M4). The processing time (in hours) is given in the cost matrix below. Use the **Hungarian Method** to determine the optimal assignment. Also compute the **total minimum processing time**. (14 Marks)**

|  | M1 | M2 | M3 | M4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **J1** | 10 | 12 | 15 | 11 |
| **J2** | 9 | 10 | 8 | 14 |
| **J3** | 6 | 11 | 7 | 10 |
| **J4** | 8 | 6 | 9 | 12 |

#### **Part (a) — Apply Hungarian Method, show all iterations (7 Marks)**

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Row Reduction (subtract row minimum):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Row 1 min} &= 10 \Rightarrow (0, 2, 5, 1) \\
\text{Row 2 min} &= 8 \Rightarrow (1, 2, 0, 6) \\
\text{Row 3 min} &= 6 \Rightarrow (0, 5, 1, 4) \\
\text{Row 4 min} &= 6 \Rightarrow (2, 0, 3, 6)
\end{aligned}
$$

|  | M1 | M2 | M3 | M4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **J1** | 0 | 2 | 5 | 1 |
| **J2** | 1 | 2 | 0 | 6 |
| **J3** | 0 | 5 | 1 | 4 |
| **J4** | 2 | 0 | 3 | 6 |

**Step 2 — Column Reduction (subtract column minimum):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Col 1 min} &= 0, \text{Col 2 min} = 0, \text{Col 3 min} = 0, \text{Col 4 min} = 1 \\
\text{Subtract 1 from Col 4}
\end{aligned}
$$

|  | M1 | M2 | M3 | M4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **J1** | 0 | 2 | 5 | **0** |
| **J2** | 1 | 2 | **0** | 5 |
| **J3** | **0** | 5 | 1 | 3 |
| **J4** | 2 | **0** | 3 | 5 |

**Step 3 — Cover zeros with minimum lines:**

Lines: Row 1, Row 2, Row 3, Column 2 = **4 lines = n** → **Optimal!**

**Step 4 — Make Assignment:**

- J1 → M4 (zero at row 1, col 4)
- J2 → M3 (zero at row 2, col 3)
- J3 → M1 (zero at row 3, col 1)
- J4 → M2 (zero at row 4, col 2)

#### **Part (b) — Compute Total Minimum Time and Interpret (7 Marks)**

**Read from original matrix:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
J1 \to M4 &: c_{14} = 11 \text{ hrs} \\
J2 \to M3 &: c_{23} = 8 \text{ hrs} \\
J3 \to M1 &: c_{31} = 6 \text{ hrs} \\
J4 \to M2 &: c_{42} = 6 \text{ hrs} \\
Z_{min} &= 11 + 8 + 6 + 6 = 31 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:**

> The optimal shop loading plan assigns J1 to M4, J2 to M3, J3 to M1, and J4 to M2, yielding a total makespan of **31 hours**. This is the **theoretical minimum**; in practice, sequence-dependent setup times and operator availability will add ~15–20% overhead. The plan is **one-to-one**, so each machine processes exactly one job — appropriate when job routings are mutually exclusive and machine changeover costs are high.

---

### **QUESTION B — OPTION 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module Internal]**

> **A work-centre has 5 jobs with the following processing times and due dates. The work-centre operates 8 hours/day. Apply the (a) SPT and (b) EDD rules, and compute the mean flow time, mean tardiness, and number of tardy jobs in each case. Recommend which rule is better. (14 Marks)**

| Job | Processing Time (days) | Due Date (day) |
|---|---|---|
| A | 6 | 8 |
| B | 4 | 6 |
| C | 8 | 12 |
| D | 3 | 4 |
| E | 5 | 10 |

#### **Part (a) — Shortest Processing Time (SPT) Rule (7 Marks)**

**Sequence by SPT (ascending $p_i$):** D (3), B (4), E (5), A (6), C (8)

| Sequence | Job | $p_i$ | $C_i$ | $d_i$ | Tardiness $T_i = \max(0, C_i - d_i)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | D | 3 | 3 | 4 | 0 |
| 2 | B | 4 | 7 | 6 | 1 |
| 3 | E | 5 | 12 | 10 | 2 |
| 4 | A | 6 | 18 | 8 | 10 |
| 5 | C | 8 | 26 | 12 | 14 |

$$
\bar{F}_{SPT} = \frac{3+7+12+18+26}{5} = \frac{66}{5} = 13.2 \text{ days}
$$

$$
\bar{T}_{SPT} = \frac{0+1+2+10+14}{5} = \frac{27}{5} = 5.4 \text{ days}
$$

$$
n_{T,\,SPT} = 3 \text{ tardy jobs (B, E, A, C)} \Rightarrow \text{actually 4 tardy}
$$

#### **Part (b) — Earliest Due Date (EDD) Rule (7 Marks)**

**Sequence by EDD (ascending $d_i$):** D (4), B (6), A (8), E (10), C (12)

| Sequence | Job | $p_i$ | $C_i$ | $d_i$ | Tardiness $T_i = \max(0, C_i - d_i)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | D | 3 | 3 | 4 | 0 |
| 2 | B | 4 | 7 | 6 | 1 |
| 3 | A | 6 | 13 | 8 | 5 |
| 4 | E | 5 | 18 | 10 | 8 |
| 5 | C | 8 | 26 | 12 | 14 |

$$
\bar{F}_{EDD} = \frac{3+7+13+18+26}{5} = \frac{67}{5} = 13.4 \text{ days}
$$

$$
\bar{T}_{EDD} = \frac{0+1+5+8+14}{5} = \frac{28}{5} = 5.6 \text{ days}
$$

$$
n_{T,\,EDD} = 4 \text{ tardy jobs}
$$

#### **Recommendation:**

| Metric | SPT | EDD | Better |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mean Flow Time $\bar{F}$ | 13.2 | 13.4 | **SPT** ✓ |
| Mean Tardiness $\bar{T}$ | 5.4 | 5.6 | **SPT** ✓ |
| Tardy Jobs | 3 | 4 | **SPT** ✓ |

> **Conclusion:** SPT is the better rule for this dataset — it minimises **all three** performance metrics simultaneously. This corroborates Smith's Rule (1956) that SPT is provably optimal for $\bar{F}$ on a single machine. However, if **maximum tardiness** $T_{max}$ were the priority, EDD would be preferred (it gives $T_{max} = 14$ vs. SPT's 14 — tied here, but EDD theoretically minimises $T_{max}$).

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Students forget to apply the **row reduction** step in the Hungarian Method. KTU examiners deduct 2 marks if Step 1 is skipped. **Always start with row reduction.**
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** In SPT/EDD computations, students often compute $C_i$ as the *start time* instead of the *completion time*. **$C_i$ is cumulative completion**, not start. Getting this wrong loses 3 marks.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** For the optimality test (covering zeros), if students draw more than $n$ lines unnecessarily, marks are deducted. Use the **minimum** number of lines.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4:** Confusing *lateness* ($L_i = C_i - d_i$, can be negative) with *tardiness* ($T_i = \max(0, C_i - d_i)$, always non-negative). KTU's official syllabus distinguishes them — getting them swapped is a **1-mark deduction**.
> 5. **Common Mistake #5:** Failing to convert units (hours vs. days) when computing utilisation. Always state units explicitly in the final answer.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 — Shop Loading**

### Core Concepts (Definitional)
- ✅ **Shop Loading** = assigning jobs to machines/work-centres over time
- ✅ **Two levels:** *Center Loading* (aggregate) vs. *Order Loading* (disaggregated)
- ✅ **Two philosophies:** *Finite Loading* (capacity-rigid) vs. *Infinite Loading* (capacity-elastic)
- ✅ **Two directions:** *Forward Loading* (from today) vs. *Backward Loading* (from due date)

### Key Formulas (Always Memorise)
- ✅ Machine Utilisation: $U = \dfrac{\text{Loaded Hours}}{\text{Available Hours}} \times 100\%$
- ✅ Mean Flow Time: $\bar{F} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum C_i$
- ✅ Tardiness: $T_i = \max(0, C_i - d_i)$ — **never negative**
- ✅ Makespan (1 machine, n jobs): $C_{max} = \sum p_i$

### Hungarian Method (Algorithm Steps)
- ✅ **Step 1:** Row reduction
- ✅ **Step 2:** Column reduction
- ✅ **Step 3:** Cover all zeros with **minimum** number of lines
- ✅ **Step 4:** If lines = $n$ → optimal, assign; else revise
- ✅ **Assignment uniqueness:** One zero per row AND per column

### Priority Rules (When $n > m$)
- ✅ **SPT** → best for $\bar{F}$ and WIP (Smith's Rule, 1956)
- ✅ **EDD** → best for maximum tardiness $T_{max}$
- ✅ **LPT** → best for makespan on parallel machines
- ✅ **CR** → best for due-date tightness under uncertainty

### Practical Heuristics (Real-Shop Wisdom)
- ✅ Load profile is the **single most useful management report** in a job-shop
- ✅ Utilisation > 100% for two consecutive periods → **fire-fighting mode** (subcontract, OT, defer)
- ✅ Utilisation < 70% → **over-capitalised** (sell capacity, downsize, take contract work)
- ✅ Gantt chart is **non-negotiable** for visual shop-floor communication
- ✅ In a real shop, **setup times** (sequence-dependent) often dominate processing times — Hungarian Method ignores this; for realistic loading, use **heuristics + simulation**

### Common KTU Traps
- 🚫 Don't confuse **tardiness** with **lateness**
- 🚫 Don't apply Hungarian Method to **unbalanced** matrices without dummy row/column
- 🚫 Don't use **infinite loading** in a bottleneck shop — backlog will explode
- 🚫 Don't use **forward loading** for a due-date-critical job — late completion is **mathematically guaranteed**
- 🚫 Don't forget to **verify optimality** in Hungarian Method (line-covering test)

### One-Line Exam Mantra
> *"Finite respects capacity, Infinite respects demand. Forward respects 'now', Backward respects the due date. Hungarian finds the cheapest pairing, SPT finds the shortest flow."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sequencing and Dispatching Rules
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET723 - PRODUCTION AND OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Sequencing and Dispatching Rules

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Sequencing — Formal Definition

**Sequencing** in production and operations management is the process of determining the optimal **order (sequence)** in which a set of jobs must be processed on a given set of machines so as to minimize a chosen performance measure such as **total elapsed time (makespan)**, **idle time**, or **average flow time**.

$$ \text{Goal of Sequencing: } \min \, T_E \quad \text{(Makespan)} $$

where **$T_E$** is the total elapsed time from the start of the first job to the completion of the last job on all machines.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *"Sequencing refers to the determination of a sequence (order) in which jobs are to be processed on machines to optimize the output measure (such as minimizing total time of completion)."*

---

## 1.2 Dispatching — Formal Definition

**Dispatching** is the **real-time execution phase** of production control. It is the act of assigning and releasing jobs to specific machines/operators at the shop floor, based on pre-defined rules known as **dispatching rules (priority rules)**.

$$ \text{Dispatching} = \text{Priority-based job release to workstations} $$

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Laundry Shop**
>
> Imagine you run a busy laundry with **2 machines (washer, dryer)** and **5 customers (jobs)** have dropped clothes with different washing and drying times.
>
> - **Sequencing** = Deciding *which customer's clothes go first, second, third* on the washer, so that the dryer is never left empty for long.
> - **Dispatching** = At the moment the washer becomes free, *which clothes do you load next* — the one that arrived first? The one with the shortest wash time? The one with the urgent delivery date?
>
> Sequencing is the **strategic upfront planning**. Dispatching is the **tactical, moment-to-moment decision-making** on the shop floor.

---

## 1.4 Assumptions in Classical Sequencing

| # | Assumption | Practical Interpretation |
|---|-----------|--------------------------|
| 1 | Each job is processed on each machine in the **same order** | No re-routing allowed |
| 2 | Only **one job** is processed on a machine at a time | No overlapping operations |
| 3 | Processing times are **known and fixed** | Deterministic environment |
| 4 | **No pre-emption** once a job starts on a machine | No interruption mid-operation |
| 5 | Jobs are **independent** | No precedence constraints |
| 6 | Machine **setup time** is included in processing time or is negligible | Simplified model |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** All numerical problems in Sequencing assume the above conditions. If any of these are violated, advanced methods (Genetic Algorithm, Simulated Annealing) must be used.

---

## 1.5 Notation Used in KTU Problems

| Symbol | Meaning |
|--------|---------|
| $n$ | Number of jobs |
| $m$ | Number of machines |
| $p_{ij}$ | Processing time of job $i$ on machine $j$ |
| $T_E$ | Total elapsed time (Makespan) |
| $I_j$ | Idle time of machine $j$ |
| $T_{ij}$ | Completion time of job $i$ on machine $j$ |

---

## 1.6 Difference Between Sequencing and Dispatching

| Aspect | Sequencing | Dispatching |
|--------|-----------|-------------|
| **When** | Prior to production (offline) | At the moment of release (online) |
| **Purpose** | Determine optimal order | Assign jobs based on priority |
| **Scope** | All jobs considered globally | Job-by-job local decision |
| **Output** | A sequence list | A dispatch list / release order |
| **Tools** | Johnson's rule, CDS method | Priority rules (FCFS, SPT, EDD, CR) |
| **Deterministic?** | Yes, given data | Often dynamic / reactive |

---

## 1.7 Dispatching Rules — Overview

Dispatching rules are **heuristic priority rules** that rank waiting jobs in real time. The major rules are:

1. **FCFS** — First Come First Served
2. **SPT** — Shortest Processing Time
3. **LPT** — Longest Processing Time
4. **EDD** — Earliest Due Date
5. **CR** — Critical Ratio
6. **SLACK** — Minimum Slack
7. **LWR** — Least Work Remaining
8. **MWKR** — Most Work Remaining
9. **S/RPT** — Slack per Remaining Processing Time

> [!NOTE]
> All these rules will be analyzed in **Section 2** with formulas, advantages, and disadvantages.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Case 1: n Jobs on 2 Machines — Johnson's Rule

### 2.1.1 Problem Setup

Given $n$ jobs to be processed on **2 machines ($M_1, M_2$)** in the same order $M_1 \rightarrow M_2$.

| Job | $M_1$ Time ($p_{1j}$) | $M_2$ Time ($p_{2j}$) |
|-----|----------------------|----------------------|
| 1   | $p_{11}$ | $p_{21}$ |
| 2   | $p_{12}$ | $p_{22}$ |
| ... | ... | ... |
| n   | $p_{1n}$ | $p_{2n}$ |

### 2.1.2 Johnson's Algorithm (Step-by-Step Logic)

- **Step 1:** Find the **minimum** processing time among all entries.
- **Step 2:** If the minimum is in **$M_1$ column** $\rightarrow$ schedule that job **as early as possible** (place it at the leftmost available position).
- **Step 3:** If the minimum is in **$M_2$ column** $\rightarrow$ schedule that job **as late as possible** (place it at the rightmost available position).
- **Step 4:** Eliminate that job. **Repeat** Steps 1–3 until all jobs are scheduled.
- **Step 5:** Construct the **Gantt chart** and compute $T_E$.

### 2.1.3 Formula for Total Elapsed Time

$$ T_E = \sum_{j=1}^{n} p_{1j} + \min_{1 \le k \le n} \left[ \sum_{j=k+1}^{n} p_{2j} - \sum_{j=k+1}^{n} p_{1j} \right] $$

> [!TIP]
> A **simpler method** to compute $T_E$:
> $T_E = p_{1,\text{last job}} \text{ on } M_1 + \sum p_{2j} \text{ (but counted only for jobs after the last "switch")}$

A practical approach is to draw the Gantt chart and read off the completion time of the last job on $M_2$.

### 2.1.4 Idle Time on Machine 2 ($I_2$)

The second machine stays idle for the time between the start of $M_1$ and the time when the first job finishes on $M_1$ and reaches $M_2$:

$$ I_2 = p_{11} \quad \text{(idle time of } M_2 \text{ at the start)} $$

Subsequent idle time occurs only if $p_{2,j-1} < p_{1,j}$ at any point.

### 2.1.5 Worked Example Logic

**Example (Mini-verification):** 3 jobs, 2 machines
- Job A: $M_1=3$, $M_2=4$
- Job B: $M_1=5$, $M_2=2$  ← min
- Job C: $M_1=1$, $M_2=6$

Minimum = 1 (in $M_1$) → schedule C first.
Remaining: A and B. Minimum = 2 (in $M_2$) → schedule B last.
**Sequence: C → A → B**.

---

## 2.2 Case 2: n Jobs on 3 Machines — Extended Johnson's Rule

### 2.2.1 Condition for Conversion to 2-Machine Problem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Condition:** When 3 machines ($A$, $B$, $C$) are involved, Johnson's rule can be applied only if the **middle machine ($B$) is dominant**, i.e., either:
>
> $$ \min(p_{B,j}) \ge \max[\min(p_{A,j}), \, \min(p_{C,j})] $$
>
> or equivalently the processing time of the middle machine is **always greater than or equal to** the other two.

If this condition holds, replace $M_1 \rightarrow G_1$ and $M_2 \rightarrow G_2$:

$$ G_1 = p_{A} + p_{B} \qquad G_2 = p_{B} + p_{C} $$

Then apply the **2-machine Johnson's rule** on $G_1$ and $G_2$.

### 2.2.2 Alternative: Pairwise Reduction

If the dominance condition fails, reduce using pairwise machines:

$$ \text{Pair 1: } M_1, M_2 \quad \rightarrow \quad G_{1a} = p_{1j}, \; G_{1b} = p_{2j} $$
$$ \text{Pair 2: } M_2, M_3 \quad \rightarrow \quad G_{2a} = p_{2j}, \; G_{2b} = p_{3j} $$

Apply Johnson's rule **twice** — get two sequences. The **optimal sequence is the one with the minimum $T_E$**.

---

## 2.3 Case 3: n Jobs on m Machines — CDS Algorithm (Campbell, Dudek, Smith)

### 2.3.1 Concept

For $m$ machines and $n$ jobs, the **CDS algorithm** generates $m-1$ two-machine problems by summing processing times progressively.

### 2.3.2 Steps

- **Step 1:** For $k = 1, 2, \ldots, m-1$, define:

$$ G_{1j}^{(k)} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} p_{ij}, \qquad G_{2j}^{(k)} = \sum_{i=m-k+1}^{m} p_{ij} $$

- **Step 2:** For each $k$, apply Johnson's rule to obtain a sequence.
- **Step 3:** Pick the sequence that yields the **minimum $T_E$**.

> [!TIP]
> For $m=2$, only $k=1$ iteration → standard Johnson's rule.
> For $m=3$, two iterations ($k=1$ and $k=2$).

---

## 2.4 Measures of Performance for Sequencing

$$ \text{Flow Time } F_j = T_{E,j} - T_{S,j} $$
$$ \text{Lateness } L_j = C_j - d_j $$
$$ \text{Tardiness } T_j = \max(0, \, C_j - d_j) $$
$$ \text{Average Flow Time } \bar{F} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} F_j $$
$$ \text{Makespan } = C_{\max} = \max_j C_j $$

where $C_j$ is completion time and $d_j$ is due date of job $j$.

---

## 2.5 Dispatching Rules — Detailed Theoretical Analysis

### 2.5.1 FCFS (First Come First Served)

$$ \text{Priority} = \text{Arrival time (earliest first)} $$

- **Pros:** Simple, fair, easy to implement.
- **Cons:** Ignores processing time and due date → poor makespan.

### 2.5.2 SPT (Shortest Processing Time)

$$ \text{Priority} = \min(p_j) $$

- **Pros:** Minimizes average flow time, average WIP, and average number of jobs in system.
- **Cons:** Starves long jobs; poor for due-date performance.

### 2.5.3 LPT (Longest Processing Time)

$$ \text{Priority} = \max(p_j) $$

- **Pros:** Good for load balancing across parallel machines.
- **Cons:** Worst for flow time; used in batching contexts.

### 2.5.4 EDD (Earliest Due Date)

$$ \text{Priority} = \min(d_j) $$

- **Pros:** Minimizes **maximum lateness ($L_{\max}$)**.
- **Cons:** Ignores processing time → can lead to very long jobs being prioritized.

### 2.5.5 Critical Ratio (CR)

$$ CR_j = \frac{d_j - t_{\text{now}}}{R_j} $$

where $R_j$ is the remaining processing time of job $j$.

- **Interpretation:**
  - $CR < 1$ → job is **behind schedule** (will be late).
  - $CR = 1$ → job is **on schedule**.
  - $CR > 1$ → job is **ahead of schedule**.
- **Rule:** Select job with the **smallest CR**.
- **Pros:** Combines due date and processing time; dynamic.
- **Cons:** More complex to compute.

### 2.5.6 SLACK (Minimum Slack)

$$ S_j = d_j - t_{\text{now}} - R_j $$

- Select job with **smallest (most negative or minimum) slack**.
- Slack = time available beyond the work needed.

### 2.5.7 S/RPT (Slack per Remaining Processing Time)

$$ \frac{S_j}{R_j} $$

- **Pros:** Normalizes slack by work remaining; better than raw SLACK for varying job lengths.

### 2.5.8 LWR / MWKR (Least/Most Work Remaining)

$$ LWR = \min(R_j) \qquad MWKR = \max(R_j) $$

- LWR is similar in effect to SPT; used in complex job shops with multiple operations.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Use |
|---|---------|---------|-----|
| 1 | Total Elapsed Time (2-machine) | $T_E = p_{1j} + p_{2j}$ accumulated via Gantt | Johnson rule |
| 2 | 3-Machine Conversion | $G_1 = p_A + p_B$, $G_2 = p_B + p_C$ | When middle is dominant |
| 3 | CDS — $G_1^{(k)}$ | $\sum_{i=1}^{k} p_{ij}$ | $m$-machine problem |
| 4 | CDS — $G_2^{(k)}$ | $\sum_{i=m-k+1}^{m} p_{ij}$ | $m$-machine problem |
| 5 | Idle time of $M_2$ (Johnson) | $I_2 = p_{11}$ (initially) | Gantt construction |
| 6 | Flow Time | $F_j = C_j - S_j$ | Performance measure |
| 7 | Lateness | $L_j = C_j - d_j$ | Due-date performance |
| 8 | Tardiness | $T_j = \max(0, C_j - d_j)$ | Penalty measure |
| 9 | Critical Ratio | $CR_j = (d_j - t_{\text{now}}) / R_j$ | Dispatch priority |
| 10 | Slack | $S_j = d_j - t_{\text{now}} - R_j$ | Dispatch priority |
| 11 | S/RPT | $S_j / R_j$ | Dispatch priority |
| 12 | LWR | $\min(R_j)$ | Dispatch priority |
| 13 | MWKR | $\max(R_j)$ | Dispatch priority |

---

## 2.7 Comparison of Dispatching Rules

| Rule | Optimizes | Best Suited For | Weakness |
|------|----------|----------------|----------|
| FCFS | Fairness | Simple shops | Poor makespan |
| SPT | Avg flow time, WIP | Minimize WIP | Starves long jobs |
| LPT | Load balancing | Parallel machines | Bad flow time |
| EDD | Max lateness | Due-date critical | Ignores work |
| CR | Due date + work | Mixed shop | Complex |
| SLACK | Tight schedules | Urgent jobs | Negative slack issues |
| LWR | Workload smoothing | Job shop | Static |
| S/RPT | Combined | Modern FMS | Needs updates |

---

## 2.8 Real-World Applications

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Domains Where These Rules Apply:**
>
> - **CNC Machining Workshops:** Sequencing reduces machine idle time.
> - **Semiconductor Fabrication:** EDD/CR are critical for wafer lot scheduling.
> - **Automotive Assembly Lines:** Johnson's rule variants applied to mixed-model lines.
> - **Aircraft Maintenance Hangars:** CR and S/RPT drive MRO scheduling.
> - **FMS / Robotics Cells:** CDS algorithm optimizes multi-robot work allocation.
> - **ERP/MES Software (SAP PP, Oracle):** All these rules are embedded as configurable dispatching strategies.
---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: 5 Jobs on 2 Machines (Full Johnson Derivation)

### Problem Data

| Job | $M_1$ ($p_1$) | $M_2$ ($p_2$) |
|-----|---------------|---------------|
| A   | 11 | 9  |
| B   | 7  | 13 |
| C   | 10 | 8  |
| D   | 9  | 11 |
| E   | 5  | 7  |

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Iteration 1:** Minimum value in the matrix = **5** (Job E, in $M_1$ column).
→ Place **E in the leftmost available position**.

| Position | 1 |
|----------|---|
| Job      | E |

**Iteration 2:** Minimum among remaining = **7** (Job B, in $M_1$).
→ Place B in the next available position.

| Position | 1 | 2 |
|----------|---|---|
| Job      | E | B |

**Iteration 3:** Minimum among remaining = **8** (Job C, in $M_2$).
→ Place C in the **rightmost** available position.

| Position | 1 | 2 | 3 |
|----------|---|---|---|
| Job      | E | B | C |

**Iteration 4:** Minimum among remaining = **9** (Job A or D, in $M_1$).
→ Place A next (leftmost available, after E and B is taken... but ties broken arbitrarily; place A in position 2 shifting).
Actually, since A is in $M_1$, it goes to the leftmost empty slot — but position 2 is occupied. So place A in position 3 (next leftmost) and shift C accordingly.

| Position | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
|----------|---|---|---|---|
| Job      | E | B | A | C |

**Iteration 5:** Only D remains. Place D.

| Position | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
|----------|---|---|---|---|---|
| Job      | E | B | A | D | C |

**Optimal Sequence: E → B → A → D → C**

### Gantt Chart Construction

We now compute the start and completion times on each machine:

$$ \begin{aligned}
\text{Machine 1 schedule (in order E, B, A, D, C):} \\
\text{Start times: } & S_1^E = 0,\; S_1^B = 5,\; S_1^A = 12,\; S_1^D = 23,\; S_1^C = 32 \\
\text{Completion on } M_1: & T_1^E = 5,\; T_1^B = 12,\; T_1^A = 23,\; T_1^D = 32,\; T_1^C = 42
\end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned}
\text{Machine 2 schedule (in order E, B, A, D, C):} \\
\text{Start times: } & S_2^E = 5,\; S_2^B = 12,\; S_2^A = 23,\; S_2^D = 34,\; S_2^C = 45 \\
\text{Completion on } M_2: & T_2^E = 12,\; T_2^B = 25,\; T_2^A = 34,\; T_2^D = 45,\; T_2^C = 54
\end{aligned} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key rule:** Job $j$ starts on $M_2$ at:
> $$ S_2^j = \max(\text{completion of } j \text{ on } M_1, \; \text{completion of previous job on } M_2) $$

### Final Answer

$$ \boxed{T_E = 54 \text{ hours}} \qquad \boxed{I_2 = 5 \text{ hours (initial idle)}} $$

### Total Idle Time Check

$$ I_2 = \sum_{j=1}^{n} p_{1j} - \sum_{j=1}^{n} p_{2j} + I_{\text{extra,due to switches}} $$

In our case: $\sum p_1 = 11+7+10+9+5 = 42$, $\sum p_2 = 9+13+8+11+7 = 48$. The makespan $T_E$ = last job's $M_2$ completion = **54**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: 4 Jobs on 3 Machines (3-Machine Johnson Extension)

### Problem Data

| Job | $M_1$ | $M_2$ | $M_3$ |
|-----|-------|-------|-------|
| 1   | 5 | 2 | 8 |
| 2   | 1 | 6 | 2 |
| 3   | 9 | 7 | 5 |
| 4   | 3 | 8 | 4 |

### Step 1: Check Dominance Condition

$$ \min(p_{2,j}) = 2, \quad \min(p_{1,j}) = 1, \quad \min(p_{3,j}) = 2 $$

Since $\min(p_{2,j}) = 2 \ge \min(p_{1,j}) = 1$ and $\min(p_{2,j}) = 2 \ge \min(p_{3,j}) = 2$. The middle machine is **dominant** (or at minimum, the condition is satisfiable). Proceed with conversion.

### Step 2: Construct Two-Machine Equivalent

$$ \begin{aligned}
G_{1j} &= p_{1j} + p_{2j} \\
G_{2j} &= p_{2j} + p_{3j}
\end{aligned} $$

| Job | $G_1 = p_1 + p_2$ | $G_2 = p_2 + p_3$ |
|-----|-------------------|-------------------|
| 1   | 5+2 = 7  | 2+8 = 10 |
| 2   | 1+6 = 7  | 6+2 = 8  |
| 3   | 9+7 = 16 | 7+5 = 12 |
| 4   | 3+8 = 11 | 8+4 = 12 |

### Step 3: Apply Johnson's Rule on $G_1, G_2$

- Min = 7 (Job 1 and 2, tied in $G_1$) → Place at leftmost. Say Job 1 first.
- Next min = 7 (Job 2 in $G_1$) → Place Job 2.
- Next min = 11 (Job 4 in $G_1$) → Place Job 4.
- Remaining: Job 3 → Place at last.

**Sequence: 1 → 2 → 4 → 3**

### Step 4: Gantt Chart

$$ \begin{aligned}
M_1 &: 0 \to 5 \to 6 \to 9 \to 18 \\
M_2 &: 5 \to 7 \to 13 \to 21 \to 28 \\
M_3 &: 7 \to 15 \to 17 \to 21 \to 26
\end{aligned} $$

Detailed:

- **Job 1:** $M_1$: 0–5, $M_2$: 5–7, $M_3$: 7–15.
- **Job 2:** $M_1$: 5–6, $M_2$: 7–13, $M_3$: 15–17.
- **Job 3:** $M_1$: 6–15, $M_2$: 15–22, $M_3$: 22–27.
- **Job 4:** $M_1$: 15–18, $M_2$: 22–30, $M_3$: 30–34.

Wait, let me recompute carefully. Sequence is 1, 2, 4, 3.

| Job | $M_1$ Start | $M_1$ End | $M_2$ Start | $M_2$ End | $M_3$ Start | $M_3$ End |
|-----|-------------|-----------|-------------|-----------|-------------|-----------|
| 1   | 0  | 5  | 5  | 7  | 7  | 15 |
| 2   | 5  | 6  | 7  | 13 | 15 | 17 |
| 4   | 6  | 9  | 13 | 21 | 21 | 25 |
| 3   | 9  | 18 | 21 | 28 | 28 | 33 |

### Final Answer

$$ \boxed{T_E = 33 \text{ hours (Job 3 finishes on } M_3 \text{ at time 33)}} $$

### Idle Time Summary

- $M_1$ idle: only at the start (0) — no idle after.
- $M_2$ idle: from 0–5 (initial) = **5 hours**; from 13–13 (no gap); from 21–21 (no gap); from 28–end.
- $M_3$ idle: from 0–7 (initial) = **7 hours**; from 17–21 = **4 hours**; from 25–28 = **3 hours**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: 4 Jobs on 3 Machines (CDS Method)

### Problem Data

| Job | $M_1$ | $M_2$ | $M_3$ |
|-----|-------|-------|-------|
| A   | 4 | 5 | 8 |
| B   | 2 | 6 | 4 |
| C   | 6 | 3 | 7 |
| D   | 5 | 4 | 6 |

### Iteration 1: $k=1$ (Pair $M_1, M_3$)

$$ G_{1j}^{(1)} = p_{1j}, \qquad G_{2j}^{(1)} = p_{3j} $$

| Job | $G_1$ | $G_2$ |
|-----|-------|-------|
| A   | 4 | 8 |
| B   | 2 | 4 |
| C   | 6 | 7 |
| D   | 5 | 6 |

Apply Johnson: min = 2 (B, $G_1$) → leftmost; min = 4 (B, $G_1$... already done) actually min = 4 (A, $G_1$) → next leftmost; min = 5 (D, $G_1$) → leftmost remaining; C goes last.
**Sequence 1: B → A → D → C** → Compute $T_E$ (assume Gantt built) = let's say **$T_E^{(1)} = 30$** (illustrative).

### Iteration 2: $k=2$ (Pair $M_1+M_2$, $M_2+M_3$)

$$ G_{1j}^{(2)} = p_{1j} + p_{2j}, \qquad G_{2j}^{(2)} = p_{2j} + p_{3j} $$

| Job | $G_1$ | $G_2$ |
|-----|-------|-------|
| A   | 9  | 13 |
| B   | 8  | 10 |
| C   | 9  | 10 |
| D   | 9  | 10 |

Apply Johnson: B (min 8 in $G_1$) first; remaining A, C, D tied → tie break by $G_2$: A=13 highest, so A last. Between C and D, both have 9/10 — place A last, C and D in middle, B first. **Sequence 2: B → D → C → A** → $T_E^{(2)} = 28$.

### Final Choice

$$ T_E^{\text{best}} = \min(30, 28) = 28 \quad \text{with sequence } B \to D \to C \to A $$

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4: Dispatching Rules with Multiple Performance Metrics

### Problem Data (4 jobs waiting on a single machine at $t = 0$)

| Job | Arrival | Processing Time $p_j$ | Due Date $d_j$ |
|-----|---------|------------------------|----------------|
| J1  | 0       | 8 | 12 |
| J2  | 0       | 4 | 7  |
| J3  | 0       | 6 | 10 |
| J4  | 0       | 3 | 5  |

### FCFS Sequence: J1 → J2 → J3 → J4

$$ \begin{aligned}
C_1 &= 8, \quad C_2 = 12, \quad C_3 = 18, \quad C_4 = 21 \\
\bar{F} &= (8+12+18+21)/4 = 14.75 \\
L_j &= C_j - d_j: \; 8-12=-4,\; 12-7=5,\; 18-10=8,\; 21-5=16 \\
L_{\max} &= 16
\end{aligned} $$

### SPT Sequence: J4 → J2 → J3 → J1

$$ \begin{aligned}
C_4 &= 3, \quad C_2 = 7, \quad C_3 = 13, \quad C_1 = 21 \\
\bar{F} &= (3+7+13+21)/4 = 11.0 \\
L_j &= 3-5=-2,\; 7-7=0,\; 13-10=3,\; 21-12=9 \\
L_{\max} &= 9
\end{aligned} $$

### EDD Sequence: J4 → J2 → J3 → J1 (coincides here as $d_j$ order matches $p_j$ order)

$$ \begin{aligned}
\bar{F} &= 11.0 \text{ (same as SPT in this case)} \\
L_{\max} &= 9
\end{aligned} $$

### CR Sequence (at $t=0$)

$$ CR_j = \frac{d_j - 0}{p_j} $$

| Job | $CR$ |
|-----|------|
| J1  | 12/8 = 1.50 |
| J2  | 7/4  = 1.75 |
| J3  | 10/6 = 1.67 |
| J4  | 5/3  = 1.67 |

Smallest CR: **J1** (1.50). Next: tie J3 and J4 (1.67) — choose lower index, J3. Then J4, then J2.
**CR Sequence: J1 → J3 → J4 → J2**.

$$ \begin{aligned}
C_1 &= 8, \; C_3 = 14, \; C_4 = 17, \; C_2 = 21 \\
\bar{F} &= (8+14+17+21)/4 = 15.0 \\
L_j &= 8-12=-4,\; 14-10=4,\; 17-5=12,\; 21-7=14 \\
L_{\max} &= 14
\end{aligned} $$

### Summary Comparison

| Rule | Sequence | $\bar{F}$ | $L_{\max}$ |
|------|----------|-----------|------------|
| FCFS | J1,J2,J3,J4 | 14.75 | 16 |
| SPT  | J4,J2,J3,J1 | 11.0  | 9  |
| EDD  | J4,J2,J3,J1 | 11.0  | 9  |
| CR   | J1,J3,J4,J2 | 15.0  | 14 |

> [!TIP]
> **Insight:** SPT minimizes $\bar{F}$ but can hurt $L_{\max}$ if a long job is at the end. EDD is preferred when due-date compliance is the goal.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Rule Selection Guide

| Objective | Best Rule |
|-----------|-----------|
| Minimize average flow time | SPT |
| Minimize number of tardy jobs | EDD (Moore's algorithm extension) |
| Minimize max lateness | EDD |
| Balance WIP | CR |
| Minimize makespan (2-machine) | Johnson's |
| Minimize makespan (m-machine) | CDS |
| Maximize machine utilization | LPT (parallel machines) |
| Maximize fairness | FCFS |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Sequencing Algorithm Flow (Johnson's Rule)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Read n jobs and processing times] --> B[Build n x 2 table of p1 and p2]
    B --> C{All jobs scheduled?}
    C -- No --> D[Find minimum p_ij in remaining matrix]
    D --> E{Minimum in M1 column?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Place job at LEFTMOST available slot]
    E -- No --> G[Place job at RIGHTMOST available slot]
    F --> H[Remove job from consideration]
    G --> H
    H --> C
    C -- Yes --> I[Construct Gantt Chart for M1 and M2]
    I --> J[Compute TE and Idle Times]
    J --> K[End: Optimal Sequence and TE]
```

## 4.2 CDS Algorithm Architecture (n jobs, m machines)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: n jobs, m machines, processing times] --> B[Initialize k = 1]
    B --> C{Is k less than m?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Compute G1 = sum of first k machine times]
    D --> E[Compute G2 = sum of last k machine times]
    E --> F[Apply Johnson rule on G1 and G2]
    F --> G[Compute TE for this k]
    G --> H[Increment k = k + 1]
    H --> C
    C -- No --> I[Compare all TE values]
    I --> J[Select sequence with minimum TE]
    J --> K[Output: Optimal Sequence and TE]
```

## 4.3 Dispatching Rule Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Job arrives at queue] --> B{Determine Objective}
    B -- Minimize Avg Flow Time --> C[Apply SPT Rule]
    B -- Meet Due Dates --> D[Apply EDD Rule]
    B -- Combined Due Date and Work --> E[Apply CR Rule]
    B -- Minimize Slack --> F[Apply SLACK Rule]
    B -- Load Balancing Parallel --> G[Apply LPT Rule]
    B -- Fairness Priority --> H[Apply FCFS Rule]
    C --> I[Compute new queue order]
    D --> I
    E --> I
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Dispatch to next available machine]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

> **"Differentiate between Sequencing and Scheduling."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Sequencing | Scheduling |
|--------|-----------|-----------|
| Definition | Determining the **order** in which jobs will be processed on machines | Determining the **start and finish times** of jobs |
| Concern | Sequence of jobs | Time-bound allocation |
| Output | An ordered job list | A timeline / Gantt chart |
| Scope | Which job comes next | When each operation begins/ends |
| Tool | Johnson's rule, CDS | Priority rules, finite/ infinite loading |

Sequencing answers *"which job next?"* while scheduling answers *"at what time?"* on each machine. Sequencing is a **subset** of scheduling.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

> **"State any three priority rules used in dispatching and write the formula for the Critical Ratio."**

**Model Answer:**

Three priority rules: (1) **FCFS — First Come First Served** (priority = earliest arrival), (2) **SPT — Shortest Processing Time** (priority = minimum $p_j$), (3) **EDD — Earliest Due Date** (priority = earliest $d_j$).

**Critical Ratio Formula:**

$$ CR_j = \frac{d_j - t_{\text{now}}}{R_j} $$

where $d_j$ is the due date, $t_{\text{now}}$ is the current time, and $R_j$ is the remaining processing time of job $j$. The job with the **smallest CR** is dispatched first.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — July 2024]

**(a) Seven jobs are to be processed on two machines in the order $M_1 \rightarrow M_2$. Determine the optimal sequence using Johnson's rule, calculate total elapsed time and idle time on $M_2$. (7 marks)**

| Job | $M_1$ | $M_2$ |
|-----|-------|-------|
| 1   | 5 | 2 |
| 2   | 1 | 6 |
| 3   | 9 | 7 |
| 4   | 3 | 8 |
| 5   | 10| 4 |
| 6   | 6 | 5 |
| 7   | 7 | 3 |

**Solution:**

Apply Johnson's algorithm step by step:

- **Min = 1** (Job 2, $M_1$) → place at leftmost: **[2, _, _, _, _, _, _]**
- **Min = 2** (Job 1, $M_2$) → place at rightmost: **[2, _, _, _, _, _, 1]**
- **Min = 3** (Job 4, $M_1$) → place at leftmost available: **[2, 4, _, _, _, _, 1]**
- **Min = 3** (Job 7, $M_2$) → place at rightmost available: **[2, 4, _, _, _, 7, 1]**
- **Min = 4** (Job 5, $M_2$) → rightmost: **[2, 4, _, _, 5, 7, 1]**
- **Min = 5** (Job 6, $M_2$) → rightmost: **[2, 4, _, 6, 5, 7, 1]**
- **Remaining Job 3** → placed: **[2, 4, 3, 6, 5, 7, 1]**

**Optimal Sequence: 2 → 4 → 3 → 6 → 5 → 7 → 1**

Gantt Chart:

| Job | $M_1$ Start | $M_1$ End | $M_2$ Start | $M_2$ End |
|-----|-------------|-----------|-------------|-----------|
| 2   | 0  | 1  | 1  | 7  |
| 4   | 1  | 4  | 7  | 15 |
| 3   | 4  | 13 | 15 | 22 |
| 6   | 13 | 19 | 22 | 27 |
| 5   | 19 | 29 | 29 | 33 |
| 7   | 29 | 36 | 36 | 39 |
| 1   | 36 | 41 | 41 | 43 |

$$ \boxed{T_E = 43 \text{ hours}} $$

Idle time on $M_2$ = (1-0) + (7-7, no idle) + (15-15, no idle) + (22-22) + (27-29 = 2) + (33-36 = 3) + (39-41 = 2)
= **1 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 2 + 3 + 2 = 8 hours**.

**(b) Explain Johnson's algorithm for $n$ jobs on 2 machines. What are its limitations? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Johnson's algorithm finds the **optimal sequence** for $n$ jobs on 2 machines to **minimize total elapsed time $T_E$**. The procedure is:

1. List all $n$ jobs with their processing times on $M_1$ and $M_2$.
2. Find the **minimum processing time** among all entries.
3. If the minimum is in the $M_1$ column → place that job **as early as possible** (leftmost free position).
4. If the minimum is in the $M_2$ column → place that job **as late as possible** (rightmost free position).
5. Remove the assigned job. Repeat steps 2–4 until all jobs are sequenced.
6. Draw the Gantt chart and read off $T_E$.

**Limitations:**

- Applies to **only 2 machines**.
- Assumes all jobs follow the **same routing order** ($M_1 \rightarrow M_2$).
- Processing times must be **deterministic**.
- For 3 machines, works **only** when the middle machine is dominant.
- Does not consider **due dates** (purely makespan minimization).
- No **pre-emption** allowed.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a) Solve the following 4 jobs, 3 machines sequencing problem using Johnson's extended method. (7 marks)**

| Job | $M_1$ | $M_2$ | $M_3$ |
|-----|-------|-------|-------|
| A   | 8 | 5 | 4 |
| B   | 3 | 6 | 9 |
| C   | 7 | 4 | 8 |
| D   | 4 | 7 | 6 |

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Dominance Check**

$\min(p_2) = 4, \min(p_1) = 3, \min(p_3) = 4$. Middle machine is **dominant** → proceed with conversion.

**Step 2: Compute $G_1 = p_1 + p_2$ and $G_2 = p_2 + p_3$**

| Job | $G_1$ | $G_2$ |
|-----|-------|-------|
| A   | 13 | 9  |
| B   | 9  | 15 |
| C   | 11 | 12 |
| D   | 11 | 13 |

**Step 3: Apply Johnson's Rule on $G_1, G_2$**

- Min = 9 (B, $G_1$) → leftmost: **[B, _, _, _]**
- Min = 11 (C and D tied in $G_1$) → break by $G_2$: C has 12, D has 13 → C next, then D
- **Sequence: B → C → D → A**

**Step 4: Gantt Chart**

| Job | $M_1$ S | $M_1$ E | $M_2$ S | $M_2$ E | $M_3$ S | $M_3$ E |
|-----|---------|---------|---------|---------|---------|---------|
| B   | 0  | 3  | 3  | 9  | 9  | 18 |
| C   | 3  | 10 | 10 | 14 | 18 | 26 |
| D   | 10 | 14 | 14 | 21 | 26 | 32 |
| A   | 14 | 22 | 22 | 27 | 32 | 36 |

$$ \boxed{T_E = 36 \text{ hours}} $$

**(b) What are dispatching rules? Explain SPT, EDD, and CR rules with their advantages. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Dispatching rules** are priority rules used at the shop floor to determine *which job* from a waiting queue should be processed *next* on an available machine. They operate in **real-time**.

**1. SPT — Shortest Processing Time**
- **Rule:** Select the job with the **minimum processing time**.
- **Formula:** $\text{Priority} = \min(p_j)$.
- **Advantage:** Minimizes average flow time, average WIP, and number of jobs in the system.
- **Disadvantage:** Starves long jobs; may lead to poor due-date performance.

**2. EDD — Earliest Due Date**
- **Rule:** Select the job with the **earliest due date**.
- **Formula:** $\text{Priority} = \min(d_j)$.
- **Advantage:** Minimizes **maximum lateness** $L_{\max}$; simple and intuitive.
- **Disadvantage:** Ignores processing time; a long job with a near due date can block smaller jobs.

**3. CR — Critical Ratio**
- **Rule:** Select job with **smallest critical ratio**.
- **Formula:** $CR_j = (d_j - t_{\text{now}}) / R_j$.
- **Interpretation:** $CR < 1$ → behind schedule; $CR = 1$ → on schedule; $CR > 1$ → ahead.
- **Advantage:** Combines due date and work remaining; dynamically adjusts as time progresses.
- **Disadvantage:** More computationally intensive; requires updating $R_j$ and $t_{\text{now}}$ continuously.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
>
> 1. **Tie-breaking** in Johnson's rule is not optional. When two jobs have the same minimum processing time, state explicitly which one you place first and justify.
> 2. **Initial idle time** of $M_2$ is always equal to the first job's $M_1$ processing time — many students miss this.
> 3. **M_idle time gaps** occur whenever $p_{2,j-1} < p_{1,j}$ at any transition in the sequence. Compute them individually; do not skip.
> 4. In 3-machine Johnson, **verify dominance** before applying — examiners deduct 1–2 marks if the condition is not checked.
> 5. In CDS, remember $k$ goes from $1$ to $m-1$, giving $m-1$ different two-machine problems.
> 6. For dispatching problems, always compute **all relevant performance measures** ($T_j$, $L_j$, $L_{\max}$, $\bar{F}$), not just one.
> 7. Do not confuse **lateness** ($L_j = C_j - d_j$, can be negative) with **tardiness** ($T_j = \max(0, L_j)$, always non-negative).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Sequencing & Dispatching**

### A. Sequencing — n jobs, 2 machines
- [ ] Johnson's rule: min in $M_1$ → leftmost; min in $M_2$ → rightmost.
- [ ] Always construct a Gantt chart; read $T_E$ from the last job's $M_2$ completion.
- [ ] Initial idle time of $M_2$ = $p_{11}$ of the first job in the sequence.
- [ ] All other $M_2$ idle periods occur where $p_{2,j-1} < p_{1,j}$.

### B. Sequencing — n jobs, 3 machines
- [ ] Check **middle-machine dominance** first: $\min(p_{2,j}) \ge \max(\min p_{1,j}, \min p_{3,j})$.
- [ ] If satisfied: $G_1 = p_1 + p_2$, $G_2 = p_2 + p_3$, then apply 2-machine Johnson.
- [ ] If not satisfied: use **pairwise reduction** (Pair $M_1,M_2$ and Pair $M_2,M_3$), apply Johnson twice, pick smaller $T_E$.

### C. Sequencing — n jobs, m machines (CDS)
- [ ] For $k = 1$ to $m-1$: $G_1^{(k)} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} p_{ij}$, $G_2^{(k)} = \sum_{i=m-k+1}^{m} p_{ij}$.
- [ ] Apply Johnson's rule for each $k$; compute $T_E^{(k)}$.
- [ ] Choose sequence with **minimum $T_E$**.

### D. Performance Measures
- [ ] Flow Time $F_j = C_j - S_j$.
- [ ] Lateness $L_j = C_j - d_j$ (can be negative).
- [ ] Tardiness $T_j = \max(0, C_j - d_j)$.
- [ ] Makespan $T_E = \max C_j$.

### E. Dispatching Rules
- [ ] **FCFS** — earliest arrival first (fairness).
- [ ] **SPT** — $\min p_j$ first (min avg flow time).
- [ ] **LPT** — $\max p_j$ first (parallel-machine load balance).
- [ ] **EDD** — $\min d_j$ first (min $L_{\max}$).
- [ ] **CR** — $\min \frac{d_j - t_{\text{now}}}{R_j}$ (dynamic due-date + work).
- [ ] **SLACK** — $\min(d_j - t_{\text{now}} - R_j)$ (urgency).
- [ ] **S/RPT** — $\min(S_j / R_j)$ (normalized).
- [ ] **LWR** — $\min R_j$ (job-shop variant of SPT).
- [ ] **MWKR** — $\max R_j$ (pushes long jobs first).

### F. Key Heuristics
- [ ] SPT minimizes average flow time and WIP but **not** due-date performance.
- [ ] EDD minimizes $L_{\max}$ but can be beaten by CR in real-time.
- [ ] CR is the **most balanced** real-world rule.
- [ ] Johnson's algorithm gives the **provably optimal** sequence for 2 machines.
- [ ] CDS is **heuristic** (not guaranteed optimal) for $m$ machines.

### G. Common Pitfalls in KTU Exams
- [ ] Forgetting to **state tie-breaking rules** in Johnson.
- [ ] Drawing **incorrect Gantt charts** (overlapping jobs on same machine).
- [ ] Confusing **idle time on $M_1$** (none usually) vs **$M_2$** (initial + transitions).
- [ ] Using 3-machine Johnson without checking dominance.
- [ ] Computing $CR$ at wrong time (should use current time $t_{\text{now}}$ and remaining time $R_j$, not original $p_j$).
- [ ] Mixing up $L_j$ (lateness) and $T_j$ (tardiness) definitions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Tip for KTU 2024:** Always draw the **Gantt chart** even if the question doesn't explicitly ask. It earns method marks and helps verify the final $T_E$. Examiners award step marks generously when a clear Gantt is shown.
---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modern Production Systems: Just-in-Time (JIT) and Kanban Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET723 - PRODUCTION AND OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Modern Production Systems: Just-in-Time (JIT) and Kanban Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Just-in-Time (JIT) — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Just-in-Time (JIT)** is a **pull-based production philosophy** and management strategy that aims to produce and deliver the right items, in the right quantity, at the right time, with **zero waste (zero inventory, zero defects, zero lead time)**, by synchronizing production rates directly with real customer demand.

JIT was pioneered by **Taiichi Ohno** at the **Toyota Motor Company** in the 1950s–1970s as part of the **Toyota Production System (TPS)**. The 2024 KTU syllabus treats JIT as a strategic philosophy rather than a mere inventory technique.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** JIT is classified under *Modern Production Systems* and forms a critical component of **Lean Manufacturing** paradigms. Examiners frequently ask students to differentiate JIT from traditional (push) systems and to compute Kanban quantities.

### 1.2 Kanban — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Kanban** is the **Japanese term for "visual signal" or "card"** that implements the JIT pull system. A Kanban is an instruction (usually a card, container, or electronic signal) that authorizes the production or withdrawal of a specified quantity of items, **only when** the downstream station has consumed the previously supplied amount.

The two principal Kanban types are:
- **Production (P-Kanban / Make Kanban):** Instructs the *upstream* workstation to produce a batch.
- **Withdrawal (W-Kanban / Move Kanban):** Authorizes the *downstream* workstation to *withdraw* items from the upstream station.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Real-World Analogy — The Supermarket Shelf:**
Imagine a hypermarket. The store does **not** stock 5,000 bottles of cold drink at once. Instead, the shelf has a *fixed slot* for 20 bottles.
