When a customer picks up the 15th bottle, the store employee **notices the empty slot**, walks to the back warehouse, and brings **exactly 5 more bottles** to refill. The customer *pulls* the product; the warehouse *reacts* to that pull.

- The **shelf slot** = the **Kanban card / container** (defining the lot size).
- The **customer taking a bottle** = the **demand signal** that triggers production.
- The **store employee refilling** = the **upstream production station** starting work.
- The **back warehouse** = the **supplier/previous process**.

> [!TIP]
> If you understand the supermarket shelf, you understand JIT/Kanban. Production is triggered *only* by actual consumption, eliminating overproduction, excess WIP, and storage costs.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Constants

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Unit | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| Demand Rate | $D$ | units / day | Average consumption |
| Lead Time | $L$ | days | Time to replenish |
| Container Size | $C$ | units / container | Items per Kanban card |
| Safety Factor | $S$ | dimensionless (0–1) | Buffer for variability |
| Number of Kanbans | $N$ | whole number (integer) | Always rounded **up** |
| Takt Time | $T_t$ | seconds / unit | Pace of production |

**Takt Time** (German for "rhythm") is a foundational JIT metric:

$$T_t = \frac{\text{Available Production Time per Day}}{\text{Customer Demand per Day}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Takt time dictates the **heartbeat** of the production line. It is **not** cycle time — it is the *customer's required rate* expressed in time-per-unit.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Seven Wastes (Muda) Eliminated by JIT

JIT's primary objective is the **elimination of waste (Muda)**. The seven classical wastes are:

1. **Overproduction** — making more than demanded.
2. **Waiting** — idle time of workers, machines, or materials.
3. **Transportation** — unnecessary movement of goods.
4. **Over-processing** — doing more work than the customer values.
5. **Inventory** — excess raw material, WIP, or finished goods.
6. **Motion** — unnecessary human movement.
7. **Defects** — rework, scrap, and warranty costs.

> A **8th waste** — *unused human talent/creativity* — has been added in modern lean literature.

### 2.2 The Seven Principles of JIT (KTU High-Yield)

The 2024 syllabus emphasizes the following **operational pillars** of JIT:

- **Zero Inventory:** Produce only what is needed, when needed.
- **Zero Defects:** Build quality into the process (Jidoka / Autonomation).
- **Zero Setup Time (SMED):** Single-Minute Exchange of Dies for quick changeovers.
- **Zero Lead Time:** Compress order-to-delivery cycles.
- **Zero Handling:** Minimize material movement.
- **Zero Breakdown (TPM):** Total Productive Maintenance ensures machine reliability.
- **Lot Size of One:** Produce one unit at a time, flow continuously.

### 2.3 JIT vs. Traditional (Push) Production — Analytical Comparison

| Dimension | Traditional (Push) | JIT (Pull) |
|---|---|---|
| Trigger | Forecast / Schedule | Actual downstream demand |
| Inventory | High (buffer stock) | Minimal (zero target) |
| Lead Time | Long, unpredictable | Short, controlled |
| Quality | Inspect at end | Build-in (Poka-Yoke, Jidoka) |
| Suppliers | Many, adversarial | Few, long-term partnership |
| Batch Size | Large (economies of scale) | Small (often = 1) |
| WIP | High | Low |
| Communication | Forecasts, MRP | Kanban signals |
| Flexibility | Low | High |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Kanban formula is the single most-tested quantitative question in this module.** Memorize the formula, the meaning of every variable, and the rule of rounding.

**Master Formula — Number of Kanbans:**

$$N = \left\lceil \frac{D \times L \times (1+S)}{C} \right\rceil$$

| Variable | Meaning | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| $N$ | Number of Kanban cards / containers required | dimensionless (integer) | Always **round up** ($\lceil \cdot \rceil$) |
| $D$ | Demand rate (consumption) | units / time period | Average per period |
| $L$ | Lead time to replenish one container | time period | e.g., days, hours |
| $S$ | Safety stock factor | dimensionless | $0 \leq S \leq 1$ |
| $C$ | Container capacity | units / container | Items per Kanban |

**Auxiliary Formulas:**

| Concept | Formula |
|---|---|
| Takt Time | $T_t = \dfrac{T_{\text{available}}}{D_{\text{day}}}$ |
| Lead Time Demand | $\text{LTD} = D \times L$ |
| Safety Stock (units) | $\text{SS} = D \times L \times S$ |
| Cycle Time (Little's Law) | $\text{CT} = \dfrac{\text{WIP}}{T_t}$ |
| Total Cost (Economic order link) | $\text{TC} = D \cdot C_p + \dfrac{Q}{2} \cdot C_h + \dfrac{D}{Q} \cdot C_o$ |

> [!TIP]
> The vertical-bar ceiling operator $\lceil x \rceil$ rounds $x$ **up** to the next integer. If you compute $N = 4.2$, the answer is $5$ Kanbans — never $4$.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

JIT/Kanban is the operational backbone of:
- **Automotive industry** (Toyota, Honda, Tata Motors).
- **Electronics assembly** (Apple's Foxconn supply chain).
- **Healthcare** — kanban-based replenishment of surgical supplies.
- **Service operations** — restaurants applying pull-based cooking ("order-driven kitchen").

Its strategic value lies in **cash-flow liberation**: by converting inventory from a liability to a flow, working capital is released, responsiveness to demand surges improves, and defect detection happens *in-process* rather than at the end of a long production run.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Kanban Sizing Formula

The Kanban formula is derived from the requirement that the system must be able to **satisfy demand during the lead time** that elapses between issuing a withdrawal signal and receiving replenishment. Below is the complete, exam-ready derivation.

**Step 1 — Define the replenishment requirement.**
During the lead time $L$ at demand rate $D$, the total number of units consumed (or expected to be consumed) is:

$$\text{Lead Time Demand (LTD)} = D \times L$$

**Step 2 — Introduce the safety factor.**
To guard against demand variability, supply delays, and quality rejections, we inflate the lead-time demand by a multiplicative safety factor $(1+S)$:

$$\text{Buffered Demand} = D \times L \times (1+S)$$

**Step 3 — Convert demand into the number of containers.**
Since each Kanban card represents exactly one container of size $C$ units, the number of containers required is the ratio of buffered demand to container capacity:

$$N_{\text{exact}} = \frac{D \times L \times (1+S)}{C}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the integer (ceiling) operator.**
Kanbans are physical entities; a fraction of a card is meaningless. Hence the smallest integer greater than or equal to the exact value is chosen:

$$N = \left\lceil \frac{D \times L \times (1+S)}{C} \right\rceil$$

> [!NOTE]
> The ceiling function is the *only* rounding direction permitted in KTU solutions. Rounding down would create perpetual stockouts and lose 3–4 marks in numerical questions.

**Step 5 — Compact presentation (for board exams).**
Many textbooks compress the derivation by writing:

$$
N = \frac{d \times (1 + \alpha) \times (L + S_l)}{C}
$$

where $d$ is the daily demand, $\alpha$ is the allowance for rejects/defects, $L$ is the average lead time, $S_l$ is the safety lead-time allowance, and $C$ is the container size. Both forms are equivalent for KTU-level questions.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Step-by-Step)

> **Problem [KTU University Exam – May 2024 style]:**
> A workstation consumes 200 units of part **X** per day. The supplier requires **0.5 days** to deliver a container of **50 units**. Management wishes to maintain a safety factor of **20%**. Determine the number of Kanban cards required.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify variables from the problem statement.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= 200 \text{ units/day} \\
L &= 0.5 \text{ days} \\
C &= 50 \text{ units/container} \\
S &= 0.20
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the lead-time demand.**

$$
\text{LTD} = D \times L = 200 \times 0.5 = 100 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the safety factor.**

$$
\text{Buffered Demand} = 100 \times (1 + 0.20) = 100 \times 1.20 = 120 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 4 — Divide by container size.**

$$
N_{\text{exact}} = \frac{120}{50} = 2.40 \text{ containers}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the ceiling operator.**

$$
N = \lceil 2.40 \rceil = 3 \text{ Kanbans}
$$

**Verification:** With 3 Kanbans circulating, the system holds a maximum of $3 \times 50 = 150$ units, which exceeds the buffered demand of 120 units. ✅

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Insight:** If the question states *"maintain a safety stock of $S$ units"* (an absolute number) instead of a percentage, modify Step 3 to:
> $$\text{Buffered Demand} = D \times L + S$$
> and then proceed. This is a common KTU twist worth practicing.

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — When JIT Works vs. Fails

| Condition | JIT is Suitable ✅ | JIT is Risky ❌ |
|---|---|---|
| Demand pattern | Stable, high volume, repetitive | Highly volatile / lumpy |
| Lead times | Short & reliable | Long & variable |
| Supplier base | Few, local, reliable | Many, distant, unreliable |
| Setup times | Quick (SMED applied) | Long changeovers |
| Quality system | Mature (TQM, Poka-Yoke) | Inspection-based |
| Product mix | Low | Very high (mass customization) |
| Workforce | Multi-skilled, empowered | Narrow, rigid |

> [!WARNING]
> The **1997 Aisin Fire** (Toyota supplier plant) demonstrated JIT's vulnerability: a single supplier's fire halted production across Toyota for days. KTU examiners frequently quote this case to test students' understanding of JIT's *limitation*.

### 3.4 Linkage to Lean, Six Sigma, and TQM

| System | Core Idea | Overlap with JIT |
|---|---|---|
| **TQM** | Customer satisfaction via quality | JIT's "zero defects" principle |
| **Six Sigma** | Reduce variation to 3.4 DPMO | JIT's pull system requires low variation |
| **Lean** | Eliminate waste, create value | JIT *is* a pillar of Lean |
| **Agile** | Rapid response to change | JIT's short lead times enable agility |
| **Theory of Constraints** | Optimize the bottleneck | JIT exposes bottlenecks via low WIP |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow — JIT Pull System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    customer[Customer Demand] --> order[Customer Order Received]
    order --> ship[Shipping / Dispatch]
    ship --> assembly[Final Assembly Line]
    assembly -->|W-Kanban| subassy1[Sub-Assembly Station A]
    subassy1 -->|W-Kanban| subassy2[Sub-Assembly Station B]
    subassy2 -->|W-Kanban| machine[Machine Shop]
    machine -->|P-Kanban Trigger| rawmat[Raw Material Store]
    rawmat -->|Supplier Delivery| machine

    classDef pullClass fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef signalClass fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:1px
    class customer,order,ship,assembly,subassy1,subassy2,machine,rawmat pullClass
```

**Reading the diagram:** Demand flows from right (customer) to left (raw material). The arrows marked `W-Kanban` and `P-Kanban` represent the *reverse* information flow that authorizes movement and production.

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Two-Card Kanban Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph upstream[UPSTREAM PROCESS]
        proc[Production Station]
    end
    subgraph downstream[DOWNSTREAM PROCESS]
        cons[Consumption Station]
    end

    pcard[Production Kanban P] -->|1. Triggers production| proc
    proc -->|2. Produces C units| store[Output Store]
    store -->|3. Container moved| cons
    cons -->|4. Empty container + P-Kanban returned| proc
    wcard[Withdrawal Kanban W] -->|Authorizes withdrawal| store
    wcard -->|Empty W-Kanban returned upstream| proc

    classDef card fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef station fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    class pcard,wcard card
    class proc,cons,store station
```

### 4.3 JIT vs. Push — Comparative Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PUSH[TRADITIONAL PUSH SYSTEM]
        fc1[Forecast / MRP] --> r1[Release Order to Production]
        r1 --> w1[Build Inventory in WIP]
        w1 --> s1[Push to Next Station]
        s1 --> fg1[Finished Goods Warehouse]
        fg1 --> dm1[Customer]
    end

    subgraph PULL[JIT PULL SYSTEM]
        dm2[Customer Demand Signal] --> s2[Withdraw from Previous Station]
        s2 --> pr2[Produce ONLY Replenishment Quantity]
        pr2 --> s2
    end

    classDef pushStyle fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef pullStyle fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    class fc1,r1,w1,s1,fg1,dm1 pushStyle
    class dm2,s2,pr2 pullStyle
```

### 4.4 Kanban Signal Sequence — Mermaid Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Cust as Customer
    participant Ship as Shipping
    participant Asm as Assembly Line
    participant Mach as Machine Shop
    participant Sup as Supplier

    Cust->>Ship: Demand placed (100 units)
    Ship->>Asm: Withdraw 100 units (W-Kanban)
    Asm->>Mach: W-Kanban triggers withdrawal
    Mach->>Sup: P-Kanban triggers production
    Sup->>Mach: Delivers 1 container (C units)
    Mach->>Asm: Container moved
    Asm->>Ship: Assembled units moved
    Ship->>Cust: 100 units delivered
    Note over Mach,Sup: Empty container + Kanban return upstream
```

### 4.5 JIT Implementation Roadmap — Block View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    phase1[Phase 1: Foundation - 5S, TPM] --> phase2[Phase 2: Stabilize - SMED, Setup Reduction]
    phase2 --> phase3[Phase 3: Pull System - Kanban Implementation]
    phase3 --> phase4[Phase 4: Quality - Jidoka, Poka-Yoke]
    phase4 --> phase5[Phase 5: Continuous Improvement - Kaizen]

    classDef phase fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px
    class phase1,phase2,phase3,phase4,phase5 phase
```

### 4.6 KPI Dashboard for JIT Operations

| KPI Category | Metric | JIT Target |
|---|---|---|
| Inventory | Days of Inventory On-Hand (DOH) | **< 5 days** |
| Quality | First-Pass Yield (FPY) | **> 99%** |
| Delivery | On-Time-In-Full (OTIF) | **> 98%** |
| Lead Time | Order-to-Delivery (OTD) | **< 1 week** |
| Setup | SMED changeover time | **< 10 minutes** |
| Equipment | Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE) | **> 85%** |
| Workforce | Suggestions per employee per year | **> 20** |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Q1. Define Just-in-Time (JIT) production system. List any three of its key elements.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
JIT is a pull-based production philosophy that aims to produce the right items, in the right quantity, at the right time, with minimum waste, by synchronizing production with actual customer demand. It was developed by Taiichi Ohno at Toyota. (1 mark)

**Three key elements:** (any three, 1 mark each / total capped at 2 marks)
1. **Pull system** — production is triggered by downstream consumption (Kanban).
2. **Takt time / Continuous flow** — production pace matches customer demand rate.
3. **Zero defects / Jidoka** — quality is built into the process; defects stop the line.
4. **Small lot sizes (lot size of one)** — minimizes WIP and inventory.
5. **Supplier partnerships** — few, reliable, co-located suppliers.
6. **Quick setups (SMED)** — enables small-batch economics.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]**
> **Q2. What is a Kanban? Differentiate between Production Kanban (P-Kanban) and Withdrawal Kanban (W-Kanban).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Kanban** is a visual signal (card, container, or electronic instruction) used within a pull system to authorize the production or movement of items, ensuring that nothing is produced or moved until the downstream station signals a need. (1 mark)

**Difference:** (2 marks — table form recommended)

| Aspect | P-Kanban (Production) | W-Kanban (Withdrawal) |
|---|---|---|
| Issued to | Upstream production station | Downstream consumption station |
| Function | Authorizes *production* of a batch | Authorizes *withdrawal* of a batch |
| Direction of flow | From upstream → triggered by signal | From downstream → moves the item |
| When used | When stock at upstream falls to trigger level | When downstream consumes items and needs replenishment |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU End Semester Exam – May 2024, Module 4 – Choice 1]**
> **Question A (14 Marks):**
> **(a)** Explain the philosophy and core principles of Just-in-Time (JIT) production. Discuss its strategic benefits and limitations in modern manufacturing. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A manufacturing cell uses a Kanban system to control the flow of a critical component. The demand for the component is **450 units per day**, the lead time to produce a container is **0.25 days**, the container size is **30 units**, and the management requires a **25% safety stock** factor. Calculate the number of Kanbans required. Also, state what would happen if the number of Kanbans is reduced by one. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

### Part (a) — JIT Philosophy, Principles, Benefits & Limitations (7 marks)

> **Philosophy (2 marks):**
> JIT is a pull-based, demand-driven production philosophy that seeks to eliminate all forms of waste (Muda) and produce only what is needed, when it is needed, and in the amount needed. Originating from Toyota Production System (TPS) under Taiichi Ohno, JIT views inventory as a *liability* rather than an asset — every unit of inventory hides problems such as defects, overproduction, and waiting.

> **Core Principles (2 marks):**
> 1. **Pull System** — Work is authorized only by downstream demand (Kanban).
> 2. **Takt Time & Continuous Flow** — Production pace = customer demand rate.
> 3. **Zero Defects (Jidoka)** — Stop the line on defect detection; build quality in.
> 4. **Small Lot Sizes / Lot Size of One** — Reduce WIP, expose bottlenecks.
> 5. **Quick Setups (SMED)** — Enable economic small batches.
> 6. **Supplier Partnership** — Few, co-located, JIT-certified vendors.

> **Strategic Benefits (1.5 marks):**
> - Reduced inventory carrying cost and working-capital lock-up.
> - Shorter lead times and faster response to demand changes.
> - Improved product quality through in-process control.
> - Lower floor-space requirement; reduced material handling.
> - Higher workforce involvement (Kaizen culture).

> **Limitations (1.5 marks):**
> - Highly vulnerable to supply-chain disruptions (e.g., Aisin Fire 1997).
> - Requires stable, predictable demand — unsuitable for highly seasonal products.
> - Demands a mature quality system and multi-skilled workforce.
> - High initial implementation cost (training, supplier development).
> - Reduced buffer stock can lead to stockouts in case of demand surges.

---

### Part (b) — Kanban Numerical (7 marks)

**Given data:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= 450 \text{ units/day} \\
L &= 0.25 \text{ days} \\
C &= 30 \text{ units/container} \\
S &= 0.25 \text{ (i.e., 25\% safety factor)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Apply the Kanban formula.**

$$
N = \left\lceil \frac{D \times L \times (1+S)}{C} \right\rceil
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$
N = \left\lceil \frac{450 \times 0.25 \times (1 + 0.25)}{30} \right\rceil
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the numerator.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} &= 450 \times 0.25 \times 1.25 \\
&= 112.5 \times 1.25 \\
&= 140.625 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Divide by container size.**

$$
N_{\text{exact}} = \frac{140.625}{30} = 4.6875
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the ceiling operator.**

$$
\boxed{N = \lceil 4.6875 \rceil = 5 \text{ Kanbans}}
$$

**Step 6 — Effect of reducing by one Kanban (1 mark):**
If $N = 4$, the maximum stock in the system becomes $4 \times 30 = 120$ units. However, the buffered lead-time demand is $140.625$ units. Since $120 < 140.625$, the system will experience **frequent stockouts and production starvation**, the line will be starved during the lead time, and the JIT principle of "zero stockout" will be violated. Hence, **reducing by one Kanban is not feasible** for the given parameters.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice):**
> **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the working of a **two-card Kanban system** (Production Kanban and Withdrawal Kanban) in a multi-stage production line. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A workshop produces a sub-assembly at a demand rate of **800 units per day**. The replenishment lead time is **0.4 days**, the container size is **40 units**, and a safety stock of **50 units** is to be maintained. Determine the number of Kanban cards. Briefly explain why a Kanban system cannot function with **zero Kanbans**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

### Part (a) — Two-Card Kanban Working (7 marks)

> **Diagram (3 marks) — Reproduce in exam:**

```
[Downstream Station]                                 [Upstream Station]
       |                                                   |
       |<----- W-Kanban issued (withdrawal)               |
       |                                                  (1) Authorize withdrawal
       |                                                   |
       |  Empty container returned to upstream -----------|
       |                                                   |
       |  P-Kanban issued to upstream -------------------->|
       |                                                  (2) Authorize production
       |                                                   |
       |<----- New full container returned ---------------|
```

> **Working Explanation (4 marks):**
> 1. The downstream station consumes items from its input store. When stock drops to a trigger level, a **Withdrawal Kanban (W-Kanban)** is sent upstream to authorize the movement of a full container.
> 2. The upstream station receives the W-Kanban, detaches the **Production Kanban (P-Kanban)** from the empty container, and attaches it to its production schedule — authorizing the production of **one container of $C$ units**.
> 3. The empty container is sent back upstream for refilling.
> 4. Once the upstream station completes production, the full container is moved downstream along with its W-Kanban; the P-Kanban stays with the upstream production as a "production authority card."
> 5. This cycle repeats, ensuring that **no production occurs without a corresponding withdrawal authorization** — enforcing the pull discipline.
> 6. The number of P-Kanbans and W-Kanbans circulating in the system is determined by the Kanban sizing formula.

> **Key Insight (extra 1 mark):** The W-Kanban and P-Kanban are physically attached to each container; detaching the W-Kanban releases the P-Kanban — a beautifully elegant decoupling mechanism.

---

### Part (b) — Kanban Numerical (7 marks)

**Given data:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= 800 \text{ units/day} \\
L &= 0.4 \text{ days} \\
C &= 40 \text{ units/container} \\
S &= 50 \text{ units (absolute safety stock)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Use the absolute-safety-stock variant of the Kanban formula:**

$$
N = \left\lceil \frac{D \times L + S}{C} \right\rceil
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$
N = \left\lceil \frac{800 \times 0.4 + 50}{40} \right\rceil
$$

**Step 3 — Compute lead-time demand.**

$$
D \times L = 800 \times 0.4 = 320 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 4 — Add safety stock.**

$$
320 + 50 = 370 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 5 — Divide by container size.**

$$
N_{\text{exact}} = \frac{370}{40} = 9.25
$$

**Step 6 — Apply the ceiling operator.**

$$
\boxed{N = \lceil 9.25 \rceil = 10 \text{ Kanbans}}
$$

**Step 7 — Why zero Kanbans cannot work (2 marks):**
If $N = 0$, no containers are ever produced or moved, meaning **no material flows** in the system. The pull mechanism requires at least one Kanban in circulation to act as the demand signal that authorizes replenishment. With zero Kanbans, the system has **no trigger, no authorization, and no inventory buffer** — production halts immediately, and customer demand cannot be met. Hence, **the minimum number of Kanbans is always 1** (or more, depending on demand and lead time).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Deduction Zones:**
> 1. **Rounding down instead of ceiling** — losing 1–2 marks in numerical questions. Always use $\lceil \cdot \rceil$.
> 2. **Confusing *lead time* with *cycle time*** — lead time is the replenishment duration; cycle time is the workstation's processing time per unit.
> 3. **Mixing up absolute safety stock ($S$ units) with safety factor ($S$ %)** — read the problem wording carefully.
> 4. **Forgetting to mention Taiichi Ohno / Toyota** in JIT definition — 0.5 mark deduction if origin is omitted.
> 5. **Drawing Kanban diagram without arrows showing signal flow** — full marks require a clear *direction* of W-Kanban and P-Kanban movement.
> 6. **Stating JIT has *no limitations*** — examiners *expect* students to mention supply-chain vulnerability (Aisin case study is a high-yield addition).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 4 — JIT & Kanban):**

- ✅ **JIT** = *Pull* system, demand-triggered, zero-waste philosophy, pioneered by **Taiichi Ohno at Toyota (1950s–70s)**.
- ✅ **Seven Wastes (Muda):** Overproduction, Waiting, Transport, Over-processing, Inventory, Motion, Defects (+ 8th: Unused talent).
- ✅ **Takt Time** $T_t = \dfrac{\text{Available time}}{\text{Daily demand}}$ — sets the production pace.
- ✅ **Kanban** = visual signal/card authorizing production or withdrawal; only physical *pull* trigger.
- ✅ **Two Kanban Types:** **W-Kanban** (withdrawal, downstream → upstream) and **P-Kanban** (production, upstream authorizes itself).
- ✅ **Master Formula:** $N = \left\lceil \dfrac{D \times L \times (1+S)}{C} \right\rceil$ — *always* round **up** with ceiling.
- ✅ **Variants:** Absolute safety stock → $N = \lceil (D \times L + S) / C \rceil$.
- ✅ **JIT Pillars:** Jidoka, SMED, TPM, Kaizen, 5S, Poka-Yoke, Heijunka (level scheduling).
- ✅ **Benefits:** ↓ Inventory, ↓ Lead time, ↑ Quality, ↑ Flexibility, ↓ Floor space, ↓ WIP.
- ✅ **Limitations:** Requires stable demand, reliable suppliers, mature quality system; vulnerable to disruptions (1997 Aisin fire case).
- ✅ **Push vs Pull:** Push = forecast-driven, inventory-heavy; Pull = demand-driven, inventory-light.
- ✅ **JIT Linkages:** Core of Lean; supports TQM, Six Sigma, Theory of Constraints, Agile manufacturing.
- ✅ **Most-tested KTU question type:** Kanban numerical (3–4 marks inside a 14-mark question).
- ✅ **Examiner loves:** Toyota/Taiichi Ohno mention, diagram of two-card flow, calculation with ceiling, Aisin case in limitations.
- ✅ **Examiner penalizes:** Rounding down, mixing P/W Kanban roles, omitting the pull system trigger explanation.

> **Final Exam Mantra:** *"JIT pulls demand through the line; Kanban is the card that makes the pull visible. Count the cards with $N = \lceil (D \times L \times (1+S)) / C \rceil$ — and always round up."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lean Manufacturing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET723 - PRODUCTION AND OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Lean Manufacturing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Lean Manufacturing** is a systematic, philosophy-driven methodology of production and operations management that aims to create maximum value for the end customer while minimizing all forms of waste (*muda*), unevenness (*mura*), and overburden (*muri*). It is a socio-technical framework derived from the **Toyota Production System (TPS)** that focuses on the relentless, continuous improvement of flow, quality, and cycle time by rigorously distinguishing value-adding activities from non-value-adding activities.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:** Lean is NOT a cost-cutting program. It is a **value-creation strategy** that happens to expose and eliminate waste as a natural by-product. The "lean" refers to the lean use of resources, inventory, space, time, and human effort.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen

Imagine a busy restaurant kitchen on a Saturday night.

- A **traditional batch kitchen** pre-cooks large trays of rice, gravy, and curries hours in advance, stacks them on the counter, and hopes customers order them before they get cold. Most trays are reheated, some are thrown away, and the chef has no clue what the customer actually wanted.
- A **lean kitchen** takes the order first (pull system), then pulls just enough ingredients from cold storage (Just-In-Time), cooks each dish fresh in 7 minutes (Takt Time), serves it hot, and immediately starts the next order. The chef only stocks what the menu demands and continuously tweaks the recipe based on customer feedback (Kaizen).

> [!NOTE]
> The "freshly cooked meal on demand" is the **Value**. The "pre-cooked, cold, reheated food" is the **Waste**. Lean Manufacturing brings this same philosophy to factories producing cars, electronics, software, and services.

### 1.3 The Three Pillars of the Toyota Production System (TPS)

Lean rests on three foundational pillars that every KTU answer on this topic must mention:

1. **Just-In-Time (JIT)** — Producing and delivering the right items, in the right quantity, at the exact time they are needed.
2. **Jidoka (Autonomation)** — "Automation with a human touch." Building quality into the process so that any abnormality halts production immediately (the *Andon cord*).
3. **Kaizen (Continuous Improvement)** — Engaging every employee, from the shop floor to the CEO, in incremental, perpetual improvement.

> [!NOTE]
> **Muda, Mura, Muri** — The three enemies of Lean:
> - **Muda** = Waste (activities that consume resources but create no value)
> - **Mura** = Unevenness (irregular production flow, demand fluctuation)
> - **Muri** = Overburden (excessive strain on workers or equipment)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Guiding Principles of Lean

The five principles, originally articulated by Womack and Jones, form the structural backbone of every Lean transformation:

1. **Value** — Define value strictly from the customer's perspective. The customer is willing to pay for features that solve their problem; everything else is waste.
2. **Value Stream** — Map the end-to-end sequence of steps (from raw material to delivered product) required to deliver that value. Identify which steps are value-adding, which are necessary non-value-adding, and which are pure waste.
3. **Flow** — Make the value-adding steps occur in a tight, continuous, uninterrupted sequence without waiting, batching, or queuing.
4. **Pull** — Let the customer *pull* the product from you. Do not push inventory onto the next station. Produce only when a downstream signal is received.
5. **Perfection** — Repeat the process indefinitely. Pursue a state of zero waste, zero defects, and zero inventory through relentless Kaizen.

### 2.2 The Seven (Eight) Wastes of Lean — *TIMWOOD + 1*

Every KTU question on Lean wastes requires precise recall. Memorize the acronym **TIMWOOD**:

| # | Waste | Japanese Term | Manufacturing Example | Office / Service Example |
|---|-------|---------------|----------------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **T**ransportation | *Idō* | Moving parts between distant warehouses | Sending emails through unnecessary approval chains |
| 2 | **I**nventory | *Zaiko* | Pile-up of raw materials or finished goods | Unused stationery stacked in a cupboard |
| 3 | **M**otion | *Ugoki* | Worker bending/walking to fetch tools | Searching for files in disorganized cabinets |
| 4 | **W**aiting | *Taiki* | Machine idle while waiting for the next part | Employees idle waiting for a manager's approval |
| 5 | **O**verproduction | *Seisan* | Producing more than the next station can use | Printing 500 copies of a report when 50 are needed |
| 6 | **O**ver-processing | *Fukasareta shori* | Polishing a surface that will never be seen | Writing a 20-page report when 2 pages will do |
| 7 | **D**efects | *Hinshitsu* | Reworking a wrongly machined part | Re-typing a document because of typos |
| 8 | **U**nused Talent (8th) | — | Not using operator suggestions | Ignoring trainee ideas in process design |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Overproduction is the worst waste** because it hides all the other wastes. Producing extra inventory hides waiting time, motion, transport issues, and defects. This is why JIT calls it the *root waste* of mass production.

### 2.3 Core Lean Tools & Techniques (KTU-Required Vocabulary)

| Tool | Purpose | KTU Buzzword |
|------|---------|--------------|
| **5S** | Workplace organization: Sort, Set-in-order, Shine, Standardize, Sustain | Foundation of all Lean tools |
| **Kanban** | Visual signal-based pull system controlling WIP | The "card" or "signal" mechanism of JIT |
| **Poka-Yoke** | Mistake-proofing devices that prevent defects at the source | "Fool-proofing" |
| **Value Stream Mapping (VSM)** | Pencil-and-paper diagram of material and information flow | The #1 diagnostic tool of Lean |
| **Single-Minute Exchange of Die (SMED)** | Reducing setup/changeover time to under 10 minutes | Enables small lot production |
| **Heijunka** | Leveling production volume and mix over time | Eliminates *mura* |
| **Andon** | Visual signal/light board that alerts abnormalities | The "stop the line" signal |
| **Gemba Walks** | Managers going to the actual workplace to observe | "Go see for yourself" |
| **A3 Problem Solving** | Structured 1-page report format for root-cause analysis | Toyota's thinking process |
| **Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)** | Proactive and preventive maintenance by all employees | Eliminates *muri* on machines |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **frequently asked numerical problems** in KTU OEIET723 Module 4. Practice each with the worked examples in Section 3.

| Metric | Formula | Variables & Units |
|--------|---------|-------------------|
| **Takt Time** | $$T_{takt} = \frac{T_{available}}{D}$$ | $T_{available}$ = Net available production time (seconds/minutes/day); $D$ = Customer demand (units/day) |
| **Cycle Time** | $T_{cycle} = \frac{T_{net\;prod}}{N}$ | $T_{net\;prod}$ = Net available production time; $N$ = Number of units produced |
| **Lead Time** | $T_{lead} = T_{pre} + T_{proc} + T_{wait} + T_{queue} + T_{move}$ | Sum of all time a unit spends in the system |
| **Value-Added Ratio (VAR)** | $\text{VAR} = \frac{T_{VA}}{T_{lead}} \times 100\%$ | $T_{VA}$ = Value-Added time; $T_{lead}$ = Total Lead Time |
| **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)** | $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$ | $A$ = Availability, $P$ = Performance, $Q$ = Quality (all decimals) |
| **Availability** | $A = \frac{T_{run}}{T_{planned}}$ | $T_{run}$ = Actual running time; $T_{planned}$ = Planned production time |
| **Performance** | $P = \frac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times N_{total}}{T_{run}}$ | $N_{total}$ = Total pieces produced |
| **Quality (First Pass Yield)** | $Q = \frac{N_{good}}{N_{total}}$ | $N_{good}$ = Good pieces; $N_{total}$ = Total pieces produced |
| **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** | $\text{RTY} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_{i}$ | Product of yields of all individual process steps |
| **Little's Law (Lean Queuing)** | $I = R \times T$ | $I$ = WIP; $R$ = Throughput rate; $T$ = Lead Time |
| **Inventory Turns** | $\text{IT} = \frac{\text{COGS}}{\text{Average Inventory}}$ | Turnover of stock per year |
| **Setup Reduction Ratio** | $\text{SR} = \frac{T_{old} - T_{new}}{T_{old}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage reduction in changeover time |

> [!WARNING]
> Do NOT confuse **Takt Time** with **Cycle Time** in exams.
> - **Takt Time** = the *pace* the customer demands (i.e., how often a unit must be finished).
> - **Cycle Time** = how long the workstation *actually* takes to finish one unit.
> If $T_{cycle} < T_{takt}$, the line is healthy. If $T_{cycle} > T_{takt}$, the line cannot keep up with demand.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Lean Manufacturing is used globally in automotive (Toyota, Tesla), aerospace (Boeing), electronics (Foxconn for Apple, Samsung), healthcare (Mayo Clinic, Virginia Mason), construction (Lean Construction Institute), and software development (the Agile/Scrum methodology is a direct descendant of Lean). In KTU context, students of Mechanical, Production, and Industrial Engineering often implement 5S in their college workshops and use Kanban boards in final-year project management.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Numerical Problem 1 — Takt Time & Cycle Time Matching

**Problem Statement:**
A TV manufacturing assembly line operates for **2 shifts of 8 hours each** per day. Each shift includes a **45-minute lunch break** and **20 minutes of planned morning and afternoon tea breaks**. The customer demand is **480 TVs per day**. The station has a theoretical **Cycle Time of 2 minutes per unit**. Determine:
(a) The Takt Time
(b) Whether the line can meet the demand
(c) The number of operators required (assuming 1 operator can attend 1 station at a time)

**Step 1 — Calculate the Net Available Production Time per Day**

$$
T_{available} = (2 \times 8 \times 60) - (2 \times 45) - (2 \times 20)
$$

$$
T_{available} = 960 - 90 - 40 = 830 \text{ minutes/day}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the Takt Time**

$$
T_{takt} = \frac{T_{available}}{D} = \frac{830 \text{ min/day}}{480 \text{ units/day}}
$$

$$
T_{takt} = 1.729 \text{ min/unit} \approx 1 \text{ min } 44 \text{ sec/unit}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with the Actual Cycle Time**

Given $T_{cycle} = 2$ min/unit and $T_{takt} = 1.729$ min/unit.

Since $T_{cycle} > T_{takt}$, the workstation is **slower than the customer demand pace**. The line **cannot meet the demand** with the current setup.

**Step 4 — Calculate the Number of Operators Required**

To match demand, the **combined effective cycle time** of the operators must equal or be less than Takt Time.

$$
N_{operators} = \left\lceil \frac{T_{cycle}}{T_{takt}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{2}{1.729} \right\rceil = \lceil 1.157 \rceil = 2 \text{ operators}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** With 2 operators working in parallel on similar workstations, the effective cycle time becomes $T_{cycle}/2 = 1$ min/unit, which is well below the Takt Time of 1.729 min/unit, providing a healthy buffer. This is the *line balancing* aspect of Lean.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem 2 — OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness)

**Problem Statement:**
A CNC machine has the following data over a single shift (8 hours = 480 minutes):
- **Planned production time** = 480 min
- **Planned downtime (setup, breaks)** = 60 min
- **Unplanned stoppages (breakdowns)** = 30 min
- **Ideal cycle time** = 1.5 min/unit
- **Total pieces produced** = 240 units
- **Defective pieces** = 24 units

Calculate the **OEE** and identify the **World-Class Benchmark** comparison.

**Step 1 — Calculate Availability**

$$
A = \frac{T_{run}}{T_{planned}} = \frac{480 - 60 - 30}{480 - 60} = \frac{390}{420}
$$

$$
A = 0.9286 \;(92.86\%)
$$

> *Note:* Availability is typically calculated against the *Planned Production Time* (which excludes planned downtime). We subtract the breakdown time from the planned production time to get actual run time.

**Step 2 — Calculate Performance**

$$
P = \frac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times N_{total}}{T_{run}} = \frac{1.5 \times 240}{390}
$$

$$
P = \frac{360}{390} = 0.9231 \;(92.31\%)
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Quality (First Pass Yield)**

$$
Q = \frac{N_{good}}{N_{total}} = \frac{240 - 24}{240} = \frac{216}{240}
$$

$$
Q = 0.9000 \;(90.00\%)
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate OEE**

$$
\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q = 0.9286 \times 0.9231 \times 0.9000
$$

$$
\text{OEE} = 0.7713 \approx 77.13\%
$$

**Step 5 — World-Class Comparison**

| OEE Score | Benchmark |
|-----------|-----------|
| **85%** | **World-Class (Lean Standard)** |
| 60% – 70% | Typical average factory |
| 40% – 60% | Significant improvement opportunity |
| Below 40% | Urgent intervention required |

> [!WARNING]
> The machine in this example is at **77.13%**, which is above the global average but below the 85% world-class standard. The largest loss is **Quality (10%)**, suggesting Poka-Yoke mistake-proofing and root-cause analysis of the 24 defects would yield the highest ROI.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem 3 — Value-Added Ratio & Lead Time Reduction

**Problem Statement:**
A product has a total lead time of **28 days** from customer order to delivery. Value Stream Mapping reveals the following breakdown:

| Stage | Time Spent | Classification |
|-------|------------|----------------|
| Order processing | 2 days | Non-Value-Added (Necessary) |
| Wait for material | 6 days | Pure Waste |
| Machining | 4 days | Value-Added |
| Inspection | 1 day | Non-Value-Added (Necessary) |
| Wait for next operation | 5 days | Pure Waste |
| Assembly | 3 days | Value-Added |
| Rework of defects | 2 days | Pure Waste |
| Storage in finished goods | 4 days | Pure Waste |
| Shipping | 1 day | Non-Value-Added (Necessary) |

Compute: (a) Value-Added Time, (b) Non-Value-Added (Waste) Time, (c) VAR, (d) % of Pure Waste Eliminated after Kaizen (assume pure waste is halved).

**Step 1 — Sum the Value-Added Time**

$$
T_{VA} = T_{machining} + T_{assembly} = 4 + 3 = 7 \text{ days}
$$

**Step 2 — Sum the Pure Waste Time (and verify)**

$$
T_{waste} = 6 + 5 + 2 + 4 = 17 \text{ days (pure waste)}
$$

$$
T_{necessary\;NVA} = 2 + 1 + 1 = 4 \text{ days}
$$

Verification:

$$
T_{VA} + T_{NVA} + T_{waste} = 7 + 4 + 17 = 28 \text{ days} \;\checkmark
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Value-Added Ratio**

$$
\text{VAR} = \frac{T_{VA}}{T_{lead}} \times 100\% = \frac{7}{28} \times 100\% = 25\%
$$

**Step 4 — Post-Kaizen State (Pure Waste Halved)**

$$
T_{waste,\;new} = \frac{17}{2} = 8.5 \text{ days}
$$

$$
T_{lead,\;new} = 7 + 4 + 8.5 = 19.5 \text{ days}
$$

$$
\%\text{ Lead Time Reduction} = \frac{28 - 19.5}{28} \times 100\% = 30.36\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice that the **Value-Added Time (7 days) did not change**. Lean does not typically shorten value-added steps (because they are essential). It eliminates or reduces the **17 days of pure waste**. This is a classic KTU-style answer that demonstrates deep understanding.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Lean Tools vs. The Three Wastes They Address

| Lean Tool | Muda (Waste) | Mura (Unevenness) | Muri (Overburden) |
|-----------|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 5S | ✔ (Motion, Inventory) | — | — |
| Kanban | ✔ (Inventory, Overproduction) | ✔ (Levels flow) | — |
| Heijunka | ✔ (Inventory) | ✔ (Level scheduling) | ✔ (Level load) |
| Poka-Yoke | ✔ (Defects) | — | — |
| SMED | ✔ (Waiting) | ✔ (Small batches) | ✔ (Frequent changeovers reduce batch size stress) |
| Jidoka | ✔ (Defects, Overproduction) | — | ✔ (Stops overload) |
| TPM | ✔ (Breakdowns) | — | ✔ (Machine overburden) |
| Kaizen | ✔ (All wastes) | ✔ | ✔ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Five Principles of Lean — Visual Flow

This diagram shows the sequence of the five Lean principles and how they form a continuous feedback loop back to *Value* through *Perfection*.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["PRINCIPLE 1 - Define VALUE from customer view"] --> P2["PRINCIPLE 2 - Map the VALUE STREAM end to end"]
    P2 --> P3["PRINCIPLE 3 - Create continuous FLOW"]
    P3 --> P4["PRINCIPLE 4 - Establish PULL from the customer"]
    P4 --> P5["PRINCIPLE 5 - Pursue PERFECTION continuously"]
    P5 -.Kaizen feedback loop.-> P1
    P2 --> VSM["Tool used: Value Stream Mapping VSM"]
    P3 --> CELL["Tool used: Cellular Layout and Line Balancing"]
    P4 --> KBN["Tool used: Kanban Signal System"]
    P5 --> KZ["Tool used: PDCA and Kaizen Events"]
```

### 4.2 The Seven Wastes of Lean (TIMWOOD)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    WASTE["LEAN WASTES TIMWOOD"] --> T1["T - TRANSPORTATION moving parts unnecessarily"]
    WASTE --> I1["I - INVENTORY excess raw WIP or finished goods"]
    WASTE --> M1["M - MOTION worker movement to fetch tools"]
    WASTE --> W1["W - WAITING idle time of people or machines"]
    WASTE --> O1["O - OVERPRODUCTION making too much too early"]
    WASTE --> O2["O - OVER PROCESSING more work than customer values"]
    WASTE --> D1["D - DEFECTS rework and scrap"]
    T1 --- NOTE["Overproduction is the worst waste because it hides all the other wastes"]
    O1 --- NOTE
```

### 4.3 The House of the Toyota Production System (TPS)

This is the most iconic Lean diagram and is a KTU-favorite.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOF["GOAL - Highest Quality Lowest Cost Shortest Lead Time via Highly Motivated Employees"]
    PILLAR1["PILLAR 1 - JUST IN TIME - Right item Right quantity Right time"]
    PILLAR2["PILLAR 2 - JIDOKA Autonomation - Stop the line on abnormality"]
    FOUNDATION["FOUNDATION - HEIJUNKA Level Production - Standardization - Kaizen"]
    PILLAR1 --> ROOF
    PILLAR2 --> ROOF
    FOUNDATION --> PILLAR1
    FOUNDATION --> PILLAR2
    TOOL_K["Tool Kanban and Heijunka Box"] -.supports.-> PILLAR1
    TOOL_A["Tool Andon Cord and Poka Yoke"] -.supports.-> PILLAR2
```

### 4.4 The Kanban Pull System Mechanism (4-Card Example)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CUST["Customer Demand Signal"] --> WH["Finished Goods Warehouse"]
    WH --> ASSY["Assembly Station"]
    ASSY --> MACH1["Machining Station A"]
    ASSY --> MACH2["Machining Station B"]
    MACH1 --> STORE["Raw Store"]
    MACH2 --> STORE
    K1["Kanban 1 Withdrawal"] -.signals.-> WH
    K2["Kanban 2 Production Order to Assembly"] -.signals.-> ASSY
    K3["Kanban 3 Withdrawal of parts from Machining"] -.signals.-> MACH1
    K4["Kanban 4 Production Order to Machining"] -.signals.-> MACH1
    style CUST fill:#fff4cc
    style WH fill:#cce5ff
    style ASSY fill:#d4edda
    style MACH1 fill:#f8d7da
    style MACH2 fill:#f8d7da
    style STORE fill:#e2d6f3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How the Kanban Loop Works:**
> 1. Customer demand triggers a *Withdrawal Kanban* at the warehouse.
> 2. The warehouse withdraws parts from the assembly station using a *Withdrawal Kanban* and returns a *Production Order Kanban* to assembly.
> 3. The assembly station then withdraws raw parts from the machining station using a *Withdrawal Kanban* and gives a *Production Order Kanban* to machining.
> 4. Machining produces only the exact quantity needed to replace what was withdrawn. **No overproduction, no inventory buildup.**

### 4.5 The PDCA (Deming) Cycle of Continuous Improvement

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PLAN["PLAN - Identify the problem Analyze root cause Plan a countermeasure"]
    DO["DO - Implement the countermeasure on a small scale"]
    CHECK["CHECK - Study the results Did it work Compare to standard"]
    ACT["ACT - Standardize if successful OR revise and loop back to PLAN"]
    PLAN --> DO
    DO --> CHECK
    CHECK --> ACT
    ACT -.if not standardized.-> PLAN
    ACT -.if standardized.-> NEXT["Move to next improvement opportunity"]
    NEXT --> PLAN
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2023]**
**Define Lean Manufacturing. List any four tools used in Lean Manufacturing.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):**
Lean Manufacturing is a systematic production philosophy that aims to create maximum value for the customer while eliminating all forms of waste (*muda*), unevenness (*mura*), and overburden (*muri*). It originates from the Toyota Production System and focuses on continuous improvement (Kaizen) and respect for people.

**Four Tools (1.5 Marks — 0.375 each):**
1. **5S** — Workplace organization (Sort, Set-in-order, Shine, Standardize, Sustain)
2. **Kanban** — Visual pull signal for Just-In-Time production
3. **Poka-Yoke** — Mistake-proofing devices
4. **Value Stream Mapping (VSM)** — End-to-end flow analysis

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often confuse "Lean" with "JIT" or "Six Sigma". Lean is the *overarching philosophy*; JIT and Jidoka are its *pillars*; 5S, Kanban, and VSM are its *tools*. Writing "JIT is Lean" will cost marks — JIT is only one component.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**
**What is meant by "Value-Added" and "Non-Value-Added" activities? Give one example of each from a manufacturing context.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Value-Added (VA) Activity (1.5 Marks):** An activity that transforms the product or service in a way the customer is willing to pay for. It physically changes the product, is done right the first time, and is essential to delivering the customer's requirement.
  *Example:* Welding two metal sheets together to form a chassis component.

- **Non-Value-Added (NVA) Activity (1.5 Marks):** An activity that consumes time, resources, or space but does not transform the product in a way the customer values.
  *Example:* Storing raw materials in a warehouse for 10 days before they are used.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** A common error is to classify **all inspection** as non-value-added. The KTU accepted answer is: *Inspection is a "necessary non-value-added" activity* — necessary because quality must be verified, but it does not transform the product. Use the exact phrase "Necessary Non-Value-Added" to score full marks.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

**As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, attempt any ONE full question from the choice provided.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **seven wastes (TIMWOOD)** of Lean Manufacturing in detail. For each waste, give one specific manufacturing example and one specific office/service example. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A production line operates on a single 8-hour shift with a **60-minute lunch break** and two **15-minute tea breaks**. The customer demand is **300 units per day**.
   (i) Calculate the **Takt Time** in minutes.
   (ii) The workstation has a **cycle time of 1.4 minutes**. Can the line meet the demand? Justify.
   (iii) If the cycle time is **1.8 minutes**, how many workstations are needed in parallel to meet the demand? **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question A**

#### Part (a) — Seven Wastes Explanation (7 Marks — 1 Mark per waste)

1. **T — Transportation** (1 Mark)
   *Manufacturing:* Moving finished goods from one end of a sprawling factory to the dispatch bay using forklifts.
   *Office:* Emailing an attachment to 5 people in a chain, when it could be sent to all 5 in one go.

2. **I — Inventory** (1 Mark)
   *Manufacturing:* Stacking 6 months of unsold components in a corner of the shop floor.
   *Office:* Printing 1000 copies of a flyer when only 200 are required.

3. **M — Motion** (1 Mark)
   *Manufacturing:* A worker walking 20 meters to fetch a wrench during every operation cycle.
   *Office:* A clerk standing up to access a printer located 10 meters away repeatedly.

4. **W — Waiting** (1 Mark)
   *Manufacturing:* A CNC machine idle for 45 minutes while the operator finishes another job.
   *Office:* A junior engineer waiting 3 days for a manager's signature on a routine purchase order.

5. **O — Overproduction** (1 Mark)
   *Manufacturing:* Producing 1000 units in a week when the next station can use only 600.
   *Office:* Generating a 200-slide deck for a meeting that will end in 10 minutes.

6. **O — Over-processing** (1 Mark)
   *Manufacturing:* Hand-polishing a surface that will be hidden under a cover and never seen.
   *Office:* Running a statistical analysis to 5 decimal places when a report needs only 1 decimal.

7. **D — Defects** (1 Mark)
   *Manufacturing:* Producing 50 units out of 500 with a misaligned hole requiring rework.
   *Office:* Typing a customer letter with the wrong date, requiring reprinting and re-signing.

> [!NOTE]
> For full 7 marks, students must clearly state the **definition + manufacturing example + office example** for each waste. Skipping the office example typically loses 0.25 mark per waste.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

**(i) Calculate Takt Time (2 Marks)**

$$
T_{available} = (8 \times 60) - 60 - (2 \times 15) = 480 - 60 - 30 = 390 \text{ minutes/day}
$$

$$
T_{takt} = \frac{T_{available}}{D} = \frac{390}{300} = 1.3 \text{ min/unit}
$$

**(ii) Can the line meet demand if $T_{cycle} = 1.4$ min? (2.5 Marks)**

Since $T_{cycle} = 1.4$ min/unit $> T_{takt} = 1.3$ min/unit, the workstation is **slower** than the rate at which the customer demands units. The line **cannot meet the demand** with the current single workstation. There will be a backlog of 0.1 minute per unit, or roughly 30 minutes of accumulated delay over 300 units.

**(iii) Number of Workstations if $T_{cycle} = 1.8$ min (2.5 Marks)**

$$
N = \left\lceil \frac{T_{cycle}}{T_{takt}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{1.8}{1.3} \right\rceil = \lceil 1.3846 \rceil = 2 \text{ workstations}
$$

Effective combined cycle time = $1.8 / 2 = 0.9$ min/unit, which is well below the Takt Time of 1.3 min/unit. Two workstations are sufficient and provide a healthy **buffer of 0.4 min/unit** for variation and breakdowns.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **House of the Toyota Production System (TPS)** with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of **Jidoka** and **Heijunka** in the Lean framework. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A CNC machine operates for **480 minutes** per day. The data for the day is as follows:
   - Planned downtime (lunch + breaks) = **60 min**
   - Unplanned stoppages = **45 min**
   - Ideal cycle time = **2 min/unit**
   - Total units produced = **180 units**
   - Defective units = **18 units**

   Calculate the **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)** and compare it with the **world-class benchmark of 85%**. Identify the **biggest loss factor** and suggest **one Lean tool** to improve it. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question B**

#### Part (a) — House of TPS (7 Marks)

**Diagram (3 Marks):**
The House of TPS has a **roof, two pillars, and a foundation**.
*(Draw a triangle/house showing the Goal as the roof, Just-In-Time and Jidoka as the two pillars, and Heijunka + Standardization + Kaizen as the foundation.)*

**The Three Components (4 Marks — distribute as below):**

1. **The Roof — Goal (1 Mark):** The highest quality, lowest cost, and shortest lead time, achieved through the most highly motivated workforce.

2. **The Pillars (2 Marks — 1 Mark each):**
   - **Just-In-Time (JIT):** Producing only what is needed, only when it is needed, and only in the amount needed. It is operationalized through the **Kanban** system, which acts as a pull signal to control WIP and trigger production at the previous station.
   - **Jidoka (Autonomation):** "Automation with a human touch." It gives machines and operators the authority to **stop the production line** the moment a defect or abnormality is detected (the *Andon* system). This prevents defective units from flowing downstream and forces an immediate root-cause fix.

3. **The Foundation (1 Mark — 0.5 each):**
   - **Heijunka:** Leveling of production volume and product mix over a period of time to smooth demand on people, machines, and suppliers. It eliminates *mura* and prevents *muri*.
   - **Kaizen & Standardization:** Continuous, incremental improvement by all employees, built on standardized work that serves as the baseline for future improvements.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** A common error is forgetting the **foundation**. The two pillars rest on Heijunka and Kaizen — these are *not* optional. The TPS House is not just a roof and two pillars.

#### Part (b) — OEE Calculation (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Availability (1.5 Marks)**

$$
A = \frac{T_{run}}{T_{planned\;prod\;time}} = \frac{480 - 60 - 45}{480 - 60} = \frac{375}{420} = 0.8929 \;(89.29\%)
$$

**Step 2 — Performance (1.5 Marks)**

$$
P = \frac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times N_{total}}{T_{run}} = \frac{2 \times 180}{375} = \frac{360}{375} = 0.9600 \;(96.00\%)
$$

**Step 3 — Quality (1.5 Marks)**

$$
Q = \frac{N_{good}}{N_{total}} = \frac{180 - 18}{180} = \frac{162}{180} = 0.9000 \;(90.00\%)
$$

**Step 4 — OEE (1.5 Marks)**

$$
\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q = 0.8929 \times 0.9600 \times 0.9000
$$

$$
\text{OEE} = 0.7715 \approx 77.15\%
$$

**Step 5 — Benchmark Comparison and Recommendation (1 Mark)**

The achieved OEE of **77.15%** is **below the world-class benchmark of 85%** by approximately 7.85 percentage points.

**Biggest Loss Factor:** Comparing the three components, **Quality (90%)** and **Availability (89.29%)** are the lowest contributors. Quality has the largest individual gap from 100%, indicating that defects are the dominant loss.

**Recommended Lean Tool:** **Poka-Yoke (Mistake-Proofing)** — to prevent the root cause of the 18 defects from occurring in the first place, thereby raising Quality toward 99%+ and pushing the OEE above the 85% world-class standard.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist — Lean Manufacturing (KTU Module 4)**

- **Lean = Value Maximization + Waste Elimination.** It is *not* a cost-cutting program; it is a value-creation strategy.
- **Origin:** Toyota Production System (TPS), Japan. Coined as "Lean" by Womack & Jones in the book *The Machine That Changed the World*.
- **Three Enemies:** **Muda** (waste), **Mura** (unevenness), **Muri** (overburden). Lean fights all three.
- **Two Pillars of TPS:** **JIT** (right thing, right time, right quantity) and **Jidoka** (stop the line on abnormality).
- **Foundation of TPS:** **Heijunka** (level production), **Standardization**, and **Kaizen** (continuous improvement).
- **Five Principles of Lean:** **Value → Value Stream → Flow → Pull → Perfection** (continuous loop).
- **Seven Wastes = TIMWOOD:** **T**ransport, **I**nventory, **M**otion, **W**aiting, **O**verproduction, **O**ver-processing, **D**efects. *(Add **U**nused Talent as the 8th waste.)*
- **Worst Waste = Overproduction**, because it hides all other wastes.
- **Takt Time** = Pace dictated by the customer ($T_{available} / D$). **Cycle Time** = Pace of the workstation. If Cycle > Takt, the line cannot meet demand.
- **OEE World-Class Benchmark = 85%.** OEE = Availability × Performance × Quality.
- **VAR** (Value-Added Ratio) = $T_{VA} / T_{lead}$. Most factories have VAR < 25%. Lean aims to push it up by eliminating pure waste.
- **Little's Law:** $I = R \times T$ (Inventory = Throughput × Lead Time). Reducing lead time directly reduces WIP.
- **5S** = Sort, Set-in-order, Shine, Standardize, Sustain. It is the foundation of every other Lean tool.
- **Kanban** = Visual pull signal. It is the *tool*; JIT is the *philosophy*.
- **Poka-Yoke** = Mistake-proofing. Prevents defects at the source, rather than detecting them later.
- **SMED** = Single-Minute Exchange of Die. Reduces changeover time to < 10 minutes to enable small lot production.
- **VSM (Value Stream Mapping)** = The single most important diagnostic tool. It differentiates *Value-Added*, *Necessary NVA*, and *Pure Waste*.
- **Kaizen** = Continuous, small, incremental improvements by everyone. Contrasts with *Kaikaku* (radical breakthrough change).
- **Gemba** = "The actual place." Managers must go to the shop floor to see the reality, not the reports.
- **Formula Memorization Order for KTU:** Takt Time → Cycle Time → OEE → VAR → Little's Law → RTY.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final KTU Exam Tips:**
> 1. Always **diagram the House of TPS** in any 14-mark answer about Lean framework questions.
> 2. In numerical problems, **show all units** (min/unit, units/day, %) explicitly to score full method marks.
> 3. Mention *value-added vs non-value-added* classification when describing any Lean tool to demonstrate conceptual depth.
> 4. Never write "Lean means less people." Lean is about **respect for people** and engaging workers in problem-solving.
> 5. Connect every tool back to one of the **three enemies (Muda, Mura, Muri)** to score the higher Bloom's *Analyze* level marks in the 2024 OBE pattern.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Kaizen and Continuous Improvement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEIET723 - PRODUCTION AND OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Kaizen and Continuous Improvement # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of Kaizen **Kaizen** (改善) is a Japanese term derived from two Kanji characters: - **Kai (改)** meaning "change" or "to modify" - **Zen (善)** meaning "good" or "for the better" In KTU 2024 Scheme Operations Management parlance, **Kaizen** is formally defined as a **continuous, incremental, philosophy-driven improvement methodology** that engages every employee — from the Chief Executive Officer (CEO) to the shop-floor worker — in identifying, analyzing, and implementing small, low-cost improvements to existing processes, products, and systems. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Standard):** > *Kaizen is a Japanese management philosophy centred on continuous, incremental improvement of processes through the elimination of waste (Muda), inconsistency (Mura), and overburden (Muri), actively involving all levels of an organization.* **Continuous Improvement (CI)**, often used interchangeably with Kaizen, refers to the ongoing, perpetual enhancement of products, services, and processes through systematic, structured methodologies such as the **Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA)** cycle, **Kaizen Events**, and **5S workplace organization**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are driving a car from Kerala to Delhi. You do not just build a new car every time — instead, you make **small improvements to the existing route**: better navigation, faster toll booths, smoother roads, lighter luggage. The destination (Delhi) does not change, but the **journey keeps getting faster and cheaper**. That is **Kaizen** — you do not reinvent the wheel; you make the wheel slightly rounder every day. A second analogy: consider your **mobile phone**. Apple/Samsung release small software updates every few weeks, not a brand-new phone. Each update removes bugs, improves battery life, and refines the camera. This **relentless, incremental upgrading** of an existing system is the very essence of Kaizen. ## 1.3 Key Terminology (Standard KTU Glossary) | Term | Japanese | English Meaning | |------|----------|-----------------| | Muda | 無駄 | Waste (any activity consuming resources without adding value) | | Mura | 斑 | Unevenness / Inconsistency | | Muri | 無理 | Overburden / Unreasonable strain | | Gemba | 現場 | The "actual place" where work happens (shop floor) | | Poka-Yoke | ポカヨケ | Mistake-proofing | | Kanban | 看板 | Visual signal / Just-in-Time card | | 5S | — | Sort, Set in order, Shine, Standardize, Sustain | | PDCA | — | Plan-Do-Check-Act (Deming Cycle) | > [!NOTE] > **Origin & Relevance:** Kaizen was popularized in the West by **Masaaki Imai** through his seminal 1986 book *Kaizen: The Key to Japan's Competitive Success*. It became the philosophical backbone of the **Toyota Production System (TPS)** and is now a mandatory topic in KTU's POM syllabus. ## 1.4 The Three Pillars of Kaizen 1. **Housekeeping (5S)** — Foundation of all improvement 2. **Waste Elimination (Muda Removal)** — Lean focus 3. **Standardization** — Documenting best practices to lock in gains > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Module 4):** Students must be able to differentiate between **Kaizen (philosophy)** and **Kaikaku (radical/innovative change)** and explain the role of the **PDCA cycle** in operationalizing continuous improvement. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Philosophy of Kaizen — Operational Breakdown Kaizen is built upon five foundational operating principles: ### 2.1.1 Principle 1: **Process-Oriented Thinking (改善はプロセスから)** - Improvement starts by understanding the **current process**, not blaming people. - Focus is on the **system**, not the individual. - *Why?* Because 85% of operational problems are systemic (based on Deming's 85/15 rule). ### 2.1.2 Principle 2: **PDCA-Driven Improvement Cycle** The Deming Cycle (also called the Shewhart Cycle) is the **engine** of Kaizen. $$\text{Plan} \rightarrow \text{Do} \rightarrow \text{Check} \rightarrow \text{Act} \rightarrow \text{Plan} \rightarrow \cdots$$ | Stage | Action | Tool Used | |-------|--------|-----------| | **Plan** | Identify problem, analyse root cause, propose countermeasure | Fishbone (Ishikawa), 5-Why analysis, Pareto chart | | **Do** | Implement the countermeasure on a small scale | Pilot run, Gemba walk | | **Check** | Verify effectiveness using data | Control charts, before-after comparison | | **Act** | Standardize if successful; loop back if not | SOP creation, training rollout | ### 2.1.3 Principle 3: **Gemba-Based Problem Solving (現場主義)** - All improvement must begin at the **Gemba** — the actual place where value is created. - Managers must go to the shop floor, observe, ask questions, and respect front-line workers' knowledge. ### 2.1.4 Principle 4: **Quality at the Source (自工程完結)** - Do not pass defective products to the next process. - Use **Poka-Yoke** (mistake-proofing) devices. - Build **Jidoka** (autonomation) — machines that detect defects and stop automatically. ### 2.1.5 Principle 5: **Standardization Before Improvement (標準化)** - No improvement is complete until the new method is **written, trained, and audited**. - The most important output of any Kaizen event is an updated **Standard Operating Procedure (SOP)**. ## 2.2 The 5S Methodology — Foundation of Kaizen **5S** is a systematic workplace organization technique that forms the **literal and figurative floor on which Kaizen stands**. | Step | Japanese | English | Description | |------|----------|---------|-------------| | 1S | Seiri | **Sort** | Remove all unnecessary items from the workplace | | 2S | Seiton | **Set in Order** | Arrange remaining items for ease of use (a place for everything) | | 3S | Seiso | **Shine** | Clean the workplace — cleaning is inspection | | 4S | Seiketsu | **Standardize** | Create visual standards, schedules, checklists | | 5S | Shitsuke | **Sustain** | Discipline to maintain the first four S's through training and audits | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Exam Tip:** Memorize the Japanese terms (Seiri, Seiton, Seiso, Seiketsu, Shitsuke) — KTU examiners frequently award marks for correct Japanese terminology. ## 2.3 The Seven Wastes (Muda) — The TIMWOOD Framework In a Kaizen/Lean context, **waste (Muda)** is any human activity that absorbs resources but creates no value for the customer. | # | Japanese Term | English | Example in Manufacturing | |---|---------------|---------|--------------------------| | 1 | Transport | Unnecessary movement of materials | Moving parts between distant workstations | | 2 | Inventory | Excess stock | Storing 6 months of raw material | | 3 | Motion | Unnecessary human movement | Bending, walking, reaching | | 4 | Waiting | Idle time | Machine breakdown, delayed approvals | | 5 | Overproduction | Making more than needed | Producing before an order is received | | 6 | Over-processing | Doing more work than required | Excessive polishing, redundant inspections | | 7 | Defects | Rework and scrap | Manufacturing defective products | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mnemonic: TIMWOOD** — T-I-M-W-O-O-D — Standard KTU-recommended acronym for remembering the seven wastes. ## 2.4 Kaizen vs. Kaikaku — Comparison Matrix | Parameter | **Kaizen** | **Kaikaku** | |-----------|------------|-------------| | Nature | Incremental | Radical / Breakthrough | | Risk | Low | High | | Cost | Low | High | | Speed of Implementation | Slow & steady | Rapid | | Employee Involvement | All employees | Cross-functional project teams | | Investment | Minimal | Significant (new machines, IT) | | Examples | Reducing setup time by 5% | Installing an automated robotic line | ## 2.5 PDCA Cycle — Mathematical Representation While PDCA is qualitative, its iterative nature can be represented as a converging sequence where process variance reduces with each cycle: $$\sigma_{n+1}^{2} = \sigma_{n}^{2} - \Delta_{n}$$ Where: - $\sigma_{n}^{2}$ = process variance after $n$-th PDCA cycle - $\Delta_{n}$ = variance reduction achieved in cycle $n$ - As $n \to \infty$, $\sigma_{n}^{2} \to 0$ (perfect process, theoretically) ## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | Formula / Concept | Expression | Application | |-------------------|------------|-------------| | **Waste Index (WI)** | $WI = \dfrac{\text{NVA Time}}{\text{Total Cycle Time}}$ | Measures non-value-added proportion of work | | **First Time Right (FTR)** | $FTR = \dfrac{\text{Defect-Free Units}}{\text{Total Units}} \times 100\%$ | Quality metric for Kaizen projects | | **Process Capability Index** | $C_{pk} = \min\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ | Statistical validation of Kaizen improvement | | **OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness)** | $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ | Where A=Availability, P=Performance, Q=Quality | | **Kaizen Savings** | $KS = \sum (Cost_{before} - Cost_{after})_{i}$ | Annualized savings from implemented Kaizens | | **PDCA Convergence** | $\sigma_{n+1}^{2} = \sigma_{n}^{2} - \Delta_{n}$ | Variance reduction per cycle | > [!CAUTION] > **Critical KTU Rule:** Always present efficiency metrics as percentages and round to **2 decimal places** in board examinations. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Calculating Waste Index and OEE Improvement **Problem Statement (KTU Pattern):** An automotive component shop operates a CNC machine for **480 minutes per shift**. Records for one shift show: - Ideal cycle time per part = **4 minutes** - Total parts produced = **90 units** - Defective parts = **8 units** - Machine breakdown time = **45 minutes** The shop-floor team implemented a Kaizen project and in the next shift: - Defective parts reduced to **3 units** - Breakdown time reduced to **20 minutes** - Total production remained 90 units Compute the **OEE before and after Kaizen** and the percentage improvement. ### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Compute Loading Time The **Loading Time** is the total scheduled operating time minus planned stoppages. Assuming no planned stoppages: $$\text{Loading Time} = 480 \text{ minutes}$$ ### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Compute Operating Time (Before Kaizen) $$\text{Operating Time} = \text{Loading Time} - \text{Downtime}$$ $$\text{Operating Time}_{before} = 480 - 45 = 435 \text{ minutes}$$ ### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Compute Availability (A) $$A = \dfrac{\text{Operating Time}}{\text{Loading Time}}$$ $$A_{before} = \dfrac{435}{480} = 0.9063 = 90.63\%$$ $$A_{after} = \dfrac{460}{480} = 0.9583 = 95.83\%$$ ### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Compute Performance (P) $$P = \dfrac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Pieces Produced}}{\text{Operating Time}}$$ $$P_{before} = \dfrac{4 \times 90}{435} = \dfrac{360}{435} = 0.8276 = 82.76\%$$ $$P_{after} = \dfrac{4 \times 90}{460} = \dfrac{360}{460} = 0.7826 = 78.26\%$$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** Performance dropped slightly because machine ran faster but idle time remained. Kaizen should next target motion waste. ### 3.1.5 Step 5 — Compute Quality (Q) $$Q = \dfrac{\text{Good Units}}{\text{Total Units}}$$ $$Q_{before} = \dfrac{90 - 8}{90} = \dfrac{82}{90} = 0.9111 = 91.11\%$$ $$Q_{after} = \dfrac{90 - 3}{90} = \dfrac{87}{90} = 0.9667 = 96.67\%$$ ### 3.1.6 Step 6 — Compute OEE $$OEE = A \times P \times Q$$ $$OEE_{before} = 0.9063 \times 0.8276 \times 0.9111 = 0.6834 = 68.34\%$$ $$OEE_{after} = 0.9583 \times 0.7826 \times 0.9667 = 0.7249 = 72.49\%$$ ### 3.1.7 Step 7 — Compute Percentage Improvement $$\% \text{Improvement} = \dfrac{OEE_{after} - OEE_{before}}{OEE_{before}} \times 100\%$$ $$\% \text{Improvement} = \dfrac{72.49 - 68.34}{68.34} \times 100\% = 6.07\%$$ **Result:** The Kaizen event delivered a **6.07% improvement in OEE**, primarily driven by reduced downtime and defects. ## 3.2 Worked Example 2: PDCA Cycle Application to a Real Problem **Problem Statement:** A KTU workshop reports that **15% of casting jobs are rejected** due to surface defects. Apply the **PDCA cycle** to solve this problem. ### 3.2.1 Step 1 — PLAN (Identify and Analyse) $$\text{Rejection Rate} = \dfrac{\text{Rejected Castings}}{\text{Total Castings}} \times 100\% = 15\%$$ - Root cause analysis using **5-Why**: - *Why 1:* Surface defects appear → *Why 2:* Mould sand contamination → *Why 3:* Sand not sieved regularly → *Why 4:* No cleaning schedule → *Why 5:* No SOP enforced. - **Countermeasure:** Introduce a daily sand-sieving SOP and a visual checklist (Poka-Yoke). ### 3.2.2 Step 2 — DO (Pilot Implementation) - Implement new SOP on **one shift (10 castings)**. - Measure rejection rate: $$R_{pilot} = \dfrac{1}{10} \times 100\% = 10\%$$ ### 3.2.3 Step 3 — CHECK (Validation) - Compare against baseline: $$\Delta R = 15\% - 10\% = 5\% \text{ reduction}$$ - Validate using statistical control chart (X̄-R chart) — no special-cause variation. ### 3.2.4 Step 4 — ACT (Standardize) - Roll out SOP across all shifts. - Train all moulders. - **Variance reduction** ($\Delta_{n}$) calculation: $$\Delta_{1} = \sigma_{before}^{2} - \sigma_{after}^{2}$$ $$\Delta_{1} = (0.15)(0.85) - (0.10)(0.90) = 0.1275 - 0.0900 = 0.0375$$ $$\Delta_{1} = 3.75\% \text{ (variance reduction)}$$ **Result:** One PDCA cycle reduced surface-defect variance by **3.75%**. Repeat cycle to converge toward zero defects. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Kaizen Tools to Waste Types | Waste Type (TIMWOOD) | Primary Kaizen Tool | Secondary Tool | KTU Exam Weightage | |---------------------|--------------------|--------------------|--------------------| | **T**ransport | Layout redesign | Spaghetti diagram | Medium | | **I**nventory | Just-in-Time (JIT) | Kanban | High | | **M**otion | 5S + Ergonomics | Work standardization | Medium | | **W**aiting | SMED (Single Minute Exchange of Die) | TPM | High | | **O**verproduction | Takt-time alignment | Heijunka (level scheduling) | High | | **O**ver-processing | Value stream mapping | VA/NVA analysis | Medium | | **D**efects | Poka-Yoke | Jidoka, FMEA | Very High | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Mapping:** Memorize the link between each waste type and its countermeasure tool — this is a guaranteed **Part A (3-mark)** question. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The PDCA Cycle — Operational Flow Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeP["PLAN
Identify Problem
Root Cause Analysis
Set Countermeasure"] nodeD["DO
Pilot Implementation
Small-Scale Trial
Collect Data"] nodeC["CHECK
Compare Actual vs Target
Statistical Validation
Control Charts"] nodeA["ACT
Standardize Success
Update SOP
Train Workforce
Loop Back to PLAN"] nodeP --> nodeD nodeD --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeA nodeA --> nodeP ``` ## 4.2 The 5S Methodology — Sequential Implementation Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR node1["1S SEIRI
SORT
Remove Unnecessary Items"] node2["2S SEITON
SET IN ORDER
Arrange for Easy Access"] node3["3S SEISO
SHINE
Clean and Inspect"] node4["4S SEIKETSU
STANDARDIZE
Create Visual Standards"] node5["5S SHITSUKE
SUSTAIN
Discipline and Audit"] node1 --> node2 node2 --> node3 node3 --> node4 node4 --> node5 node5 -.Audit.-> node1 ``` ## 4.3 The Seven Wastes (TIMWOOD) — Block-Level Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeM["Muda Waste Elimination
KAIZEN Foundation"] nodeT["T Transport"] nodeI["I Inventory"] nodeM1["M Motion"] nodeW["W Waiting"] nodeO1["O Overproduction"] nodeO2["O Over-Processing"] nodeD["D Defects"] nodeM --> nodeT nodeM --> nodeI nodeM --> nodeM1 nodeM --> nodeW nodeM --> nodeO1 nodeM --> nodeO2 nodeM --> nodeD ``` ## 4.4 Gemba Kaizen — Information Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeG["Gemba The Actual Place
Shop Floor Engagement"] nodeO["Observe
Go and See"] nodeId["Identify Waste
Muda Mura Muri"] nodeAn["Analyse Root Cause
5 Why Fishbone"] nodeIm["Implement Countermeasure
Pilot via PDCA"] nodeSt["Standardize and Sustain
5S + SOP Update"] nodeG --> nodeO nodeO --> nodeId nodeId --> nodeAn nodeAn --> nodeIm nodeIm --> nodeSt nodeSt --> nodeG ``` ## 4.5 Kaizen vs. Innovation (Kaikaku) — Strategic Positioning ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeK["KAIZEN Continuous Improvement"] nodeKi["KAIKAKU Radical Innovation"] nodeK --> nodeLow["Low Cost"] nodeK --> nodeInc["Incremental"] nodeK --> nodeEmp["All Employees"] nodeKi --> nodeHigh["High Cost"] nodeKi --> nodeRad["Radical Breakthrough"] nodeKi --> nodePM["Project Teams"] ``` ## 4.6 Kaizen Event Lifecycle — Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeSt["Stage 1 Problem Identification"] nodeFo["Stage 2 Form Kaizen Team"] nodeTr["Stage 3 Training on Tools"] nodePi["Stage 4 Pilot Implementation"] nodeRe["Stage 5 Results Review"] nodeSD["Stage 6 Standardization and Documentation"] nodeSt --> nodeFo nodeFo --> nodeTr nodeTr --> nodePi nodePi --> nodeRe nodeRe --> nodeSD nodeSD --> nodeSt ``` > [!TIP] > **Visual Aid Usage:** KTU examiners often award additional marks for clear, labelled diagrams. Practice drawing the PDCA cycle, 5S pyramid, and TIMWOOD wheel in every descriptive answer. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define Kaizen. Explain its relevance in modern manufacturing.** **Model Answer:** Kaizen (改善) is a Japanese management philosophy that emphasizes **continuous, incremental improvement** of processes, products, and services through the active involvement of all employees — from top management to shop-floor workers. **Relevance in Modern Manufacturing:** - Reduces waste (Muda) and operational cost. - Improves product quality and customer satisfaction. - Engages workforce, boosting morale and ownership. - Builds a culture of **data-driven, scientific problem solving** (PDCA). - Complements **Industry 4.0** by providing the human-centric improvement layer. *(Marks: Definition 1 + Relevance 2 = 3)* ### Question A2: **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **List the 5S of workplace organization and state the Japanese term for "Sustain".** **Model Answer:** The 5S are: | S | Japanese | English | |---|----------|---------| | 1S | Seiri | Sort | | 2S | Seiton | Set in Order | | 3S | Seiso | Shine | | 4S | Seiketsu | Standardize | | 5S | **Shitsuke** | **Sustain** | *(Marks: List 2 + Japanese term 1 = 3)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice ### Question B-A: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]** **(a)** Explain the **PDCA (Deming) Cycle** in detail. Discuss how it serves as the operational engine of continuous improvement. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Identify and briefly explain the **seven wastes (TIMWOOD)** of Lean Manufacturing with one manufacturing example for each. **(7 Marks)** ### Model Solution — (a) PDCA Cycle The **Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA) Cycle**, also known as the **Deming Cycle** or **Shewhart Cycle**, is a four-stage iterative problem-solving framework that operationalizes Kaizen. **Step 1 — PLAN:** -
