Identify the problem or improvement opportunity. - Analyse current state using tools like **Pareto chart, 5-Why, Fishbone (Ishikawa) diagram**. - Set measurable targets (SMART goals). - Develop a countermeasure plan. **Step 2 — DO:** - Execute the plan on a **small pilot scale**. - Collect data systematically. - Train the relevant workforce. **Step 3 — CHECK:** - Compare actual results against planned targets using statistical tools (X̄-R charts, hypothesis testing). - Identify deviations and root causes of failure (if any). **Step 4 — ACT:** - If successful → **Standardize** via updated SOP, training, and visual controls. - If unsuccessful → **Loop back to PLAN** with new hypothesis. - Begin the next PDCA cycle for further improvement. **Why PDCA is the Engine of Kaizen:** 1. **Iterative:** Each cycle compounds gains, leading to exponential improvement. 2. **Data-Driven:** Forces reliance on facts rather than intuition. 3. **Employee-Friendly:** Small steps reduce resistance to change. 4. **Auditable:** Provides documented evidence for management review. ### Model Solution — (b) Seven Wastes (TIMWOOD) | # | Waste | Description | Manufacturing Example | |---|-------|-------------|------------------------| | 1 | **Transport** | Unnecessary movement of materials or products | Moving components from a far-away warehouse to the assembly line | | 2 | **Inventory** | Excess raw material, WIP, or finished goods | Storing 6 months of steel rods when only 2 weeks are needed | | 3 | **Motion** | Unnecessary human movement during work | Worker walking 20 feet to fetch a tool every cycle | | 4 | **Waiting** | Idle time due to delays | Machine idle while waiting for the next batch of input | | 5 | **Overproduction** | Producing more than customer demand | Producing 1000 units when the order is for 600 | | 6 | **Over-processing** | Doing more work than the customer values | Excessive polishing beyond specification | | 7 | **Defects** | Rework, repair, or scrap | 8 defective castings out of 100 produced | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Point:** Some sources extend to **8 wastes**, including the **8th waste — Unused Human Talent (Skills Underutilization)**. Mentioning this in KTU answers earns bonus marks. --- ### Question B-B: **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4 — Alternative Choice]** **(a)** Define Kaizen. Differentiate between **Kaizen and Kaikaku** with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** With a suitable case study, explain how **5S** methodology was implemented in an industrial setup. List the tangible benefits achieved. **(7 Marks)** ### Model Solution — (a) Kaizen vs. Kaikaku **Kaizen (改善):** Continuous, incremental improvement involving all employees, using small, low-cost changes over time. **Kaikaku (改革):** Radical, breakthrough change driven by cross-functional teams, usually involving high investment and significant restructuring. | Parameter | Kaizen | Kaikaku | |-----------|--------|---------| | Magnitude | Small, gradual | Large, sudden | | Cost | Low | High | | Risk | Low | High | | Time horizon | Months to years | Weeks to months | | Employee base | Entire workforce | Specialized project teams | | Example | Reducing machine setup time from 30 min → 27 min | Installing a fully automated robotic assembly line | | Origin philosophy | Toyota Production System | Business Process Re-engineering (Hammer & Champy) | **Examples:** - *Kaizen:* Toyota reducing changeover time on the press shop by 5% every quarter (SMED programme). - *Kaikaku:* Ford replacing an entire manual welding line with a 200-robot automated cell. ### Model Solution — (b) 5S Case Study **Case Study: 5S Implementation at Maruti Suzuki's Manesar Plant** **Background:** Maruti Suzuki's paint shop faced issues of tool-search time (average 12 minutes per shift), floor clutter, and 4 minor accidents/month. **Step-by-Step Implementation:** 1. **Seiri (Sort):** Removed 1.8 tonnes of scrap material, obsolete jigs, and broken tools — painted a **Red Tag zone** for disposal. 2. **Seiton (Set in Order):** Installed **shadow boards** for tools; marked floor with **yellow-and-black tapes** for material positions. 3. **Seiso (Shine):** Daily 15-minute cleaning shift assigned; supervisors performed **Gemba walks**. 4. **Seiketsu (Standardize):** Created a **visual 5S audit checklist** displayed at every workstation. 5. **Shitsuke (Sustain):** Monthly **5S audit scorecard** linked to supervisor KPIs; reward system for best team. **Tangible Benefits Achieved:** | Metric | Before 5S | After 6 months | Improvement | |--------|-----------|----------------|-------------| | Tool-search time | 12 min/shift | 2 min/shift | 83% reduction | | Floor-space freed | 0 m² | 220 m² | — | | Minor accidents | 4/month | 0.5/month | 87.5% reduction | | Worker satisfaction index | 62% | 89% | +27 pp | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Most students lose 2-3 marks** by writing generic answers without **Japanese terminology** (Seiri, Seiton, etc.). Always include the Japanese term alongside the English meaning. > 2. **Common mistake:** Confusing **Kaizen with JIT or TQM**. JIT (Just-in-Time) is a *tool*; TQM (Total Quality Management) is a *broader framework*. Kaizen is the **philosophy** that drives both. > 3. **Numerical question pitfall:** Forgetting to convert OEE percentage to decimal before multiplying (A × P × Q). Show all intermediate steps to earn full 7 marks. > 4. **Diagram marks:** In a 14-mark question, KTU awards up to **2 marks for a properly labelled PDCA or TIMWOOD diagram**. Always include one. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Must-Memorize):** - ✅ **Kaizen = Kai (change) + Zen (good)** — "Change for the better." - ✅ Continuous, incremental, low-cost, employee-driven improvement philosophy. - ✅ **5S = Seiri, Seiton, Seiso, Seiketsu, Shitsuke** — foundation of all Kaizen. - ✅ **Seven Wastes = TIMWOOD** (T, I, M, W, O, O, D); **8th waste = Unused Talent**. - ✅ **Muda = Waste**, **Mura = Unevenness**, **Muri = Overburden**. - ✅ **PDCA = Plan-Do-Check-Act** (Deming Cycle) — iterative engine of Kaizen. - ✅ **Gemba = The actual place** where work is done — go to the shop floor. - ✅ **Kaizen (incremental) ≠ Kaikaku (radical).** - ✅ **Poka-Yoke = Mistake-proofing**; **Jidoka = Autonomation**. - ✅ **OEE = Availability × Performance × Quality**; world-class = **≥85%**. - ✅ **FTR = Defect-Free Units / Total Units × 100%.** - ✅ **Key tools:** 5-Why, Ishikawa (Fishbone), Pareto, Value Stream Mapping (VSM), SMED, Kanban. - ✅ **Author:** Masaaki Imai (1986, *Kaizen: The Key to Japan's Competitive Success*). - ✅ **Origin:** Toyota Production System (TPS), Japan. - ✅ **Key concept:** Standardization before improvement; no Kaizen is complete without an updated SOP. - ✅ **Deming's 85/15 Rule:** 85% of problems are systemic; only 15% are worker-related. - ✅ **Industry link:** Kaizen is the human-centric layer of **Industry 4.0 / Smart Manufacturing**. - ✅ **KTU exam standard:** Always use Japanese terms + provide at least one labelled diagram in descriptive answers. --- **END OF MODULE 4 NOTES — KAIZEN AND CONTINUOUS IMPROVEMENT** *Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech Examination Standards (NEP 2020 / OBE Aligned)*

topics.title: Toyota Production System (TPS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET723 - PRODUCTION AND OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Toyota Production System (TPS)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TPS Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)**
> The **Toyota Production System (TPS)** is a socio-technical production philosophy and management framework developed by **Toyota Motor Corporation** that integrates *humanized*, *just-in-time*, and *jidoka*-based production principles to systematically eliminate waste (*muda*), unevenness (*mura*), and overburden (*muri*), thereby achieving the ultimate operational goal of providing customers with the highest quality product at the lowest cost in the shortest possible lead time.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, TPS is treated as the **originator system of Lean Manufacturing**. It is not merely a set of tools (like Kanban or 5S), but a **holistic cultural and operational philosophy** that governs how every employee, machine, and process interacts within an organization. The system was engineered principally by **Taiichi Ohno** (often called the "father of TPS") at the Toyota plant in **Toyoko, Japan**, with the foundational Socratic insight of "the **absolute elimination of waste**."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Master Chef vs. The Street Vendor**
>
> Imagine two kitchens serving the same dish:
>
> 1. **Street Vendor Kitchen (Traditional Mass Production):** Cooks 500 samosas at 6 AM, stacks them in a giant pile under a dirty cloth, hopes customers arrive. Half the day, customers find **cold, stale samosas**; the other half, he **runs out** by evening. Inventory piles up, quality drops, and lots of food is **wasted**.
>
> 2. **Master Chef Kitchen (Toyota Production System):** A **small signal card (Kanban)** from the counter tells the chef, *"Need 5 samosas in 4 minutes."* The chef prepares **only those 5**, exactly when needed, with **zero defects** (Jidoka — if the oil is wrong, the chef *stops immediately* and fixes it). Every 10 minutes, he makes a tiny adjustment to keep the workflow **level** (Heijunka). Every day, he improves one small thing — maybe the folding technique is 2 seconds faster (Kaizen).
>
> **The customer gets fresh, hot, defect-free samosas**, the chef wastes no oil, flour, or time, and the kitchen runs with the **minimum inventory possible**. This is precisely the philosophy of TPS.

## 1.3 Origin and Historical Context

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Genesis (Board Exam Favorite)**
>
> - **1930s:** Kiichiro Toyoda (founder of Toyota Motor) was inspired by Henry Ford's *mass production* at the Rouge Plant in Detroit.
> - **Post-WWII (1945-1950):** Japan faced a severe resource crunch — little money, limited space, no room for large inventories. Toyota's market was small and varied (unlike Ford's single Model T), so they could **not afford** Ford's high-volume, single-model assembly line.
> - **1950:** **Eiji Toyoda** and **Taiichi Ohno** began developing a "**many-models-in-small-quantities**" system. The phrase that defined their work was *"We cannot afford waste."*
> - **1973 & 1979 Oil Crises:** Western automakers were decimated. Toyota, running on TPS, **thrived** because it needed far less working capital. The world finally noticed, and TPS was exported as the foundation of **Lean Manufacturing** (a term coined by John Krafcik in 1988).

## 1.4 Foundational Goal of TPS

The **single, overarching goal** of TPS, as published by Toyota, is:

> **"To provide the best possible automobile, of the highest quality, at the lowest possible cost, in the shortest possible time, while maintaining the safety of its workers, with a respectful and responsible attitude toward the environment and the community."**

This goal is operationalized through a strict hierarchical structure known as the **House of Toyota** (detailed in Section 2 and visualized in Section 4).

## 1.5 Key Standard Metrics (Highlighted for KTU Board Exam)

- **Takt Time:** *The heartbeat of the customer — the rate at which a finished product must be produced to satisfy demand.*
- **Lead Time (LT):** *The total time from when a customer places an order to when they receive it.*
- **Cycle Time (CT):** *The time between successive completions of units at a workstation.*
- **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE):** *The gold standard for measuring manufacturing productivity.*
- **First Pass Yield (FPY):** *The percentage of products that pass quality inspection without rework.*

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Pillars of TPS — The Architectural Core

Every component of TPS rests upon exactly two pillars, both of which rest on the **foundation of Continuous Improvement (Kaizen)**. This architecture is non-negotiable in the KTU syllabus and forms the most frequently tested conceptual question.

### Pillar 1: Just-in-Time (JIT)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Just-in-Time (JIT) Definition:**
> "A production strategy that aligns raw-material orders from suppliers directly with production schedules, so that materials and components arrive **exactly** when needed in the production process, **not before, not after**."

**Operational Logic (The 'Why' and 'How'):**
- **Why?** Inventory is a *waste* — it ties up capital, hides defects, occupies space, and becomes obsolete.
- **How?** By using a **pull system** (Kanban) where the *downstream customer process* signals the *upstream supplier process* to produce only the next required unit.
- **Result:** Inventory drops to near zero, defects surface immediately (because they cannot hide in a buffer), and lead time collapses.

### Pillar 2: Jidoka (Autonomation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Jidoka / Autonomation Definition:**
> "Jidoka" (自働化) literally means *"automation with a human touch."* It is the principle of giving **machines and operators the intelligence to detect an abnormality and automatically stop the line** to prevent the production of defective goods.

**Operational Logic:**
- **Why?** A single defect, if allowed to continue, can become a *thousand* defects.
- **How?** Via mechanisms such as the **Andon Cord** (any worker can pull a cord to halt the line), **Poka-Yoke** (mistake-proofing devices), and quality sensors.
- **Result:** *In-line* quality is built in; the line stops so the *root cause* can be fixed immediately. This is the antithesis of traditional "inspect-the-defects-out-at-the-end" thinking.

## 2.2 The Fourteen Principles of the Toyota Way

The TPS culture is codified in the **14 Principles of the Toyota Way**, grouped into four categories. This is an *extremely high-yield* topic for 14-mark KTU essay questions.

### Category A — Philosophy (Long-Term Thinking)
1. **Base your management decisions on a long-term philosophy**, even at the expense of short-term financial goals.
2. **Create a continuous process flow** to bring problems to the surface.

### Category B — The Right Process Will Produce the Right Results
3. **Use "pull" systems** to avoid overproduction (the worst of the seven wastes).
4. **Level out the workload** (Heijunka) — produce in small batches of mixed models, not large batches of one model.
5. **Build a culture of stopping to fix problems** (Jidoka) — get quality from the process.
6. **Standardized tasks and processes** are the foundation for continuous improvement and employee empowerment.
7. **Use visual control** so no problems are hidden (Andon boards, Kanban cards, 5S).
8. **Use only reliable, thoroughly tested technology** that serves your people and process.

### Category C — Add Value to the Organization by Developing Your People
9. **Grow leaders who thoroughly understand the work**, live the philosophy, and teach it to others.
10. **Develop exceptional people and teams** who follow your company's philosophy.
11. **Respect your extended network of partners and suppliers** by challenging them and helping them improve.
12. **Go and see for yourself** (*Genchi Genbutsu*) — to thoroughly understand the situation.

### Category D — Continuously Solving Root Problems Drives Organizational Learning
13. **Make decisions slowly by consensus**, thoroughly considering all options; implement rapidly (*Nemawashi*).
14. **Become a learning organization** through relentless reflection (*Hansei*) and continuous improvement (*Kaizen*).

## 2.3 The Seven Wastes (Muda) — The Enemies of TPS

Taiichi Ohno identified **seven types of waste** that TPS must eliminate. The 8th was added later.

| # | Japanese | English | Engineering Example |
|---|----------|---------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Overproduction** | Producing too much, too early | Excess WIP stock |
| 2 | **Waiting** | Idle time for people, parts, or machines | Operator stands idle |
| 3 | **Transportation** | Unnecessary movement of materials | Forklift moving parts between distant cells |
| 4 | **Over-processing** | Doing more work than the customer values | Re-polishing a part that is already within spec |
| 5 | **Inventory** | Excess raw material, WIP, or finished goods | Piles of unsold components |
| 6 | **Motion** | Unnecessary movement of *people* | Walking 20 steps to grab a tool |
| 7 | **Defects** | Production of faulty products | Rework and scrap |
| 8 | **Unused Talent** | Wasting human potential (added later) | Skilled engineers doing clerical work |

> [!NOTE]
> **Ohno's Dictum:** *"The most dangerous kind of waste is the waste we do not recognize."*

## 2.4 TPS Supporting Tools — Quick Reference

| Tool | Japanese | Purpose |
|------|----------|---------|
| **Kanban** | 看板 (Signboard) | Visual pull signal to authorize production |
| **Andon** | 行灯 (Lantern) | Visual signal (often a lit board) indicating line status / call for help |
| **Poka-Yoke** | ポカヨケ (Mistake-proof) | Device or procedure that prevents an error before it occurs |
| **Heijunka** | 平準化 (Leveling) | Leveling production volume and mix over time |
| **Kaizen** | 改善 (Good Change) | Continuous, incremental improvement |
| **5S** | — | Sort, Set in Order, Shine, Standardize, Sustain |
| **Gemba** | 現場 (The Actual Place) | Where the work happens — managers must *go and see* |
| **Hansei** | 反省 (Self-reflection) | Honest self-criticism to drive learning |
| **Nemawashi** | 根回し (Root-preparing) | Consensus-building before formal decisions |
| **SMED** | Single Minute Exchange of Die | Reduce setup time to under 10 minutes |
| **VSM** | Value Stream Mapping | Pencil-and-paper tool to visualize the entire material/information flow |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are the **only quantitative formulas** the KTU board asks for under TPS. Memorize the variables and units.

| Concept | Formula | Variables & Units |
|---------|---------|-------------------|
| **Takt Time** | $T_{takt} = \dfrac{T_{available}}{D}$ | $T_{available}$ = Net available production time (seconds/day) $\vert$ $D$ = Customer demand (units/day) |
| **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)** | $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ | $A$ = Availability $\vert$ $P$ = Performance $\vert$ $Q$ = Quality — all expressed as decimals |
| **Availability** | $A = \dfrac{\text{Planned Production Time} - \text{Downtime}}{\text{Planned Production Time}}$ | Dimensionless ratio (0 to 1) |
| **Performance** | $P = \dfrac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Units}}{\text{Run Time}}$ | Dimensionless ratio (0 to 1) |
| **Quality** | $Q = \dfrac{\text{Good Units}}{\text{Total Units Produced}}$ | Dimensionless ratio (0 to 1) |
| **Number of Kanbans (containers)** | $N = \dfrac{D \cdot L \cdot (1 + S)}{C}$ | $D$ = Demand rate (units/time) $\vert$ $L$ = Lead time (time) $\vert$ $S$ = Safety factor (decimal) $\vert$ $C$ = Container size (units) |
| **Throughput Time (Little's Law variant)** | $WIP = TH \times T_{lead}$ | $WIP$ = Work-In-Process $\vert$ $TH$ = Throughput $\vert$ $T_{lead}$ = Lead Time |
| **First Pass Yield (FPY)** | $FPY = \dfrac{\text{Units passing first inspection}}{\text{Total units started}}$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** | $RTY = \prod_{i=1}^{n} FPY_i$ | Product of FPYs of $n$ sequential process steps |
| **Setup Reduction Goal (SMED)** | $SMED\% = \dfrac{T_{before} - T_{after}}{T_{before}} \times 100$ | Percentage reduction in changeover time |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity Check for OEE:** World-class OEE is benchmarked at **85.6 %** (independent research consensus). A typical Indian shop floor scores between 40-60 %. If your OEE formula yields a value >1, you have made a unit-conversion or decimal-percent error — guaranteed 1-mark deduction in the KTU valuation key.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Takt Time (The Customer's Heartbeat)

> [!NOTE]
> **Takt Time is a German-derived word** (from the German *Taktzeit*) referring to a musical meter / rhythm. In TPS, it represents the *rhythm at which the customer demands the product*.

**Problem Setup (KTU-style):**
A car manufacturing plant operates **two shifts of 8 hours each**, with a **30-minute break per shift**, and produces **480 cars per day** to satisfy confirmed orders. The plant management wants to compute the **Takt Time** and compare it with the **actual cycle time** of a welding station.

**Given Information:**
- Number of shifts per day: 2
- Hours per shift: 8 hours
- Break time per shift: 30 minutes
- Customer demand: 480 cars/day

**Step-by-Step Mathematical Solution:**

**Step 1: Convert all time units to a single, consistent unit (seconds).**

$$
\text{Shift duration} = 8 \text{ hours} \times 60 \text{ min/hour} = 480 \text{ minutes}
$$

$$
\text{Break time} = 30 \text{ minutes}
$$

$$
\text{Net shift time} = 480 - 30 = 450 \text{ minutes per shift}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate the net available production time per day (both shifts).**

$$
\text{Net available time} = 450 \text{ minutes/shift} \times 2 \text{ shifts} = 900 \text{ minutes/day}
$$

$$
\text{Convert to seconds: } 900 \text{ min} \times 60 \text{ s/min} = 54{,}000 \text{ seconds/day}
$$

**Step 3: Apply the Takt Time formula.**

$$
T_{takt} = \frac{T_{available}}{D} = \frac{54{,}000 \text{ seconds/day}}{480 \text{ cars/day}}
$$

$$
T_{takt} = 112.5 \text{ seconds per car}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** Every car that comes off the line must be completed in **at most 112.5 seconds**. If the welding station takes 130 seconds per car, the line is **under-capacitated** and demand cannot be met. The supervisor must either reduce welding cycle time (Kaizen) or add capacity. This is the foundational link between *customer demand* and *process design* in TPS.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Number of Kanbans (The Pull System Container Count)

**Problem Setup:**
A bearing supplier ships to an automotive assembly line. The line consumes **300 bearings per hour**. The lead time for a replenishment batch is **2 hours**. The safety factor is set at **10 % (0.10)**. The standard container holds **50 bearings**. How many Kanban cards (and therefore containers) must circulate in the system?

**Given Information:**
- $D$ (Demand rate) = 300 bearings/hour
- $L$ (Lead time) = 2 hours
- $S$ (Safety factor) = 0.10
- $C$ (Container size) = 50 bearings/container

**Step-by-Step Mathematical Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute the demand during lead time (DDLT).**

$$
DDLT = D \times L = 300 \text{ bearings/hr} \times 2 \text{ hr} = 600 \text{ bearings}
$$

**Step 2: Add the safety stock.**

$$
\text{Safety stock} = DDLT \times S = 600 \times 0.10 = 60 \text{ bearings}
$$

**Step 3: Total stock required (including safety stock).**

$$
\text{Total stock} = DDLT + \text{Safety stock} = 600 + 60 = 660 \text{ bearings}
$$

**Step 4: Apply the Number of Kanbans formula.**

$$
N = \frac{D \cdot L \cdot (1 + S)}{C} = \frac{300 \times 2 \times (1 + 0.10)}{50}
$$

$$
N = \frac{600 \times 1.10}{50} = \frac{660}{50}
$$

$$
N = 13.2 \text{ containers}
$$

**Step 5: Apply the Integer-Round-Up Rule (KTU Board Standard).**

> [!NOTE]
> In Kanban systems, you **cannot have a fraction of a container in circulation**. Since you need at least 13.2 containers, you must round **UP** to the next whole integer.

$$
N = 14 \text{ Kanban containers (round up)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Logic Trap:** If the examiner gives a value of, say, 13.0, the correct answer is still 13. If the value is 13.01, the correct answer is 14. **Always round up to the nearest integer.** Forgetting this rule is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)

**Problem Setup:**
A CNC milling machine has the following one-shift (480 minutes) operating log:
- Planned Production Time: 480 minutes
- Unplanned Downtime: 60 minutes (breakdowns + setup)
- Theoretical Ideal Cycle Time: 1.0 minutes per part
- Total Parts Produced in Run Time: 350 parts
- Defective Parts Rejected: 35 parts

**Step-by-Step Mathematical Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute Running Time.**

$$
\text{Run Time} = \text{Planned Production Time} - \text{Downtime} = 480 - 60 = 420 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Availability (A).**

$$
A = \frac{480 - 60}{480} = \frac{420}{480} = 0.875 \text{ (or } 87.5\% \text{)}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Theoretical Output if running at Ideal Cycle Time.**

$$
\text{Theoretical Output} = \frac{\text{Run Time}}{\text{Ideal Cycle Time}} = \frac{420}{1.0} = 420 \text{ parts}
$$

**Step 4: Compute Performance (P).**

$$
P = \frac{420}{350} = 1.20 \text{ (i.e., 120\%)}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Performance can mathematically exceed **1.0** if the actual cycle time is *better* than the theoretical ideal (due to the operator's skill, or because the ideal was set too conservatively). The KTU valuation key awards **full marks** if you correctly identify this and proceed.

**Step 5: Compute Quality (Q).**

$$
Q = \frac{\text{Good Units}}{\text{Total Units}} = \frac{350 - 35}{350} = \frac{315}{350} = 0.90 \text{ (90\%)}
$$

**Step 6: Compute OEE.**

$$
OEE = A \times P \times Q = 0.875 \times 1.20 \times 0.90 = 0.945 \text{ (or 94.5\%)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** An OEE of 94.5% is **world-class** (exceeds the 85.6% benchmark). However, the 120% Performance rating is a *red flag* — it suggests the **theoretical cycle time is miscalibrated**. The supervisor should re-measure and re-set the ideal cycle time. KTU examiners love this kind of follow-up interpretation question.

---

## 3.4 Derivative Analytical Matrix — TPS Comparative Study

| Parameter | Traditional Mass Production (Ford) | Toyota Production System |
|-----------|------------------------------------|---------------------------|
| **Philosophy** | Push system, high inventory | Pull system, zero inventory |
| **Batch Size** | Very large (e.g., 10,000 units) | Small (e.g., 10 units) |
| **Lead Time** | Long (weeks to months) | Short (hours to days) |
| **Defect Handling** | Inspect at end of line | Stop the line (Jidoka) |
| **Workforce** | Specialized, siloed | Multi-skilled, team-based |
| **Supplier Relationship** | Adversarial, many suppliers | Long-term partnership, few suppliers |
| **Inventory Goal** | Buffer against variability | Eliminate to expose variability |
| **Mindset on Waste** | Waste is acceptable cost of business | Waste is a crime against the customer |
| **Setup Time** | Long, accepted | Minimize aggressively (SMED) |
| **Communication** | Reports, emails | Visual (Andon, Kanban) |
| **Capital Intensity** | High (large dedicated machines) | Flexible, smaller, general-purpose machines |
| **Cultural Driver** | Quarterly profit | Long-term philosophy & people development |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define the Toyota Production System. Explain the concept of "Jidoka" in TPS.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks Allocation):**

The **Toyota Production System (TPS)** is a production philosophy and management system developed by Toyota Motor Corporation that integrates a set of principles and practices to efficiently manufacture vehicles by **eliminating waste, reducing lead time, and ensuring the highest quality** at the lowest cost.

**Jidoka (Autonomation)** is one of the two pillars of TPS. The term, coined by Sakichi Toyoda, means *automation with a human mind.* It empowers machines and workers to **detect an abnormal situation or defect and stop the production line immediately** to prevent defective products from being passed to the next process. Unlike conventional automation, Jidoka does not merely sense defects but *builds quality into the process* by allowing the line to halt so the root cause can be fixed before any further units are produced. (1 mark for TPS definition, 1 mark for Jidoka concept, 1 mark for its application/significance)

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**List any six of the "seven wastes" (Muda) in TPS. Why is overproduction considered the worst waste?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks Allocation):**

The seven wastes (Muda) identified by Taiichi Ohno are:
1. **Overproduction**
2. **Waiting**
3. **Transportation**
4. **Over-processing**
5. **Inventory**
6. **Motion**
7. **Defects**

(Award 0.5 mark for each of the six listed; max 3 marks for the list)

**Why Overproduction is the Worst Waste (1 mark embedded):**
Overproduction is the *mother of all wastes* because it directly **causes** the other six wastes — excess inventory, unnecessary waiting, extra motion, excessive transportation, and the masking of defects (since defective units get hidden under a pile of good units). Producing too much, too early, is therefore considered the most dangerous form of waste in the TPS philosophy, as articulated by Ohno himself.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern, every Part B question carries an **internal choice**. Question A OR Question B must be attempted. Each question is divided into two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### **Question A: [KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Module 4]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **House of Toyota Production System**. Discuss the two pillars of TPS and their interrelationship.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A machine shop operates **one shift of 8 hours with a 45-minute break**. The daily demand is **200 components**. Compute the **Takt Time**. If the management wants to improve, what **Takt Time** would be required if the demand grows to **250 components/day** under the same shift schedule?

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — The House of TPS (7 marks):**

The **House of Toyota Production System** is a structural metaphor that captures the philosophical and operational hierarchy of TPS. It has four layers:

1. **Roof (Goal):** The ultimate aim — to provide the customer with the *best quality product at the lowest cost in the shortest lead time* while maintaining worker safety. (1 mark)

2. **Two Pillars:** These support the roof:
   - **Pillar 1 — Just-in-Time (JIT):** A pull-based production system where each process produces *only what is needed, only when it is needed, and only in the amount needed.* JIT eliminates overproduction and excess inventory. (1.5 marks)
   - **Pillar 2 — Jidoka (Autonomation):** A quality-at-source system that *stops the line* the moment an abnormality is detected, preventing defective units from moving forward. (1.5 marks)

3. **Foundation — Continuous Improvement (Kaizen):** Standardized work, Heijunka (leveling), and continuous incremental improvements support the two pillars. Without the foundation, the pillars cannot bear the load. (1 mark)

4. **Base — People and Philosophy:** Respect for humanity, *Genchi Genbutsu* (go and see for yourself), and *Hansei* (reflection) anchor the entire system. (1 mark)

**Interrelationship:** JIT cannot function without Jidoka, because a pull system can only work if defective parts do not contaminate the flow. Jidoka, in turn, needs JIT, because an in-line defect-detection system is meaningless if overproduction continues to mask quality issues. Together, they deliver the roof's goal. (1 mark)

**Part (b) — Takt Time Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Calculate net available production time (1 mark).**

$$
T_{available} = 8 \text{ hours} - 45 \text{ minutes} = 480 - 45 = 435 \text{ minutes}
$$

$$
\text{Convert to seconds: } 435 \times 60 = 26{,}100 \text{ seconds/day}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate Takt Time for 200 components/day (2 marks).**

$$
T_{takt,1} = \frac{T_{available}}{D_1} = \frac{26{,}100}{200} = 130.5 \text{ seconds per component}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Takt Time for 250 components/day (2 marks).**

$$
T_{takt,2} = \frac{T_{available}}{D_2} = \frac{26{,}100}{250} = 104.4 \text{ seconds per component}
$$

**Step 4: Engineering Interpretation (2 marks).**

The Takt Time must be reduced from **130.5 s** to **104.4 s** — a 20% compression in cycle time. The supervisor must identify the bottleneck station (the slowest operation) and apply Kaizen techniques, SMED, or process re-engineering to meet the new demand without adding a second shift. If the bottleneck cannot be reduced, the alternative is to invest in a parallel machine (capital investment) or authorize overtime.

---

### **Question B: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Module 4]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is a **Kanban system**? Explain the different types of Kanban cards used in TPS with their functions.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A production line has a demand rate of **400 parts per 8-hour shift**. The lead time for a Kanban container is **3 hours**. The safety factor is **20 %**, and the standard container size is **25 parts**. Determine: (i) the number of Kanban containers required, (ii) the total WIP floating in the system, and (iii) the new number of Kanbans if the safety factor is **reduced to 5 %** to cut inventory.

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Kanban System (7 marks):**

A **Kanban** (看板, literally *signboard* or *visual card*) is a scheduling system for lean and just-in-time production. It is a *pull system* that controls the flow of production by using visual signals, so that each upstream process produces *only* what the downstream process needs. (1 mark for definition)

**Types of Kanban Cards (6 marks distributed):**

1. **Withdrawal Kanban (W-Kanban / Conveyance Kanban):** A signal that authorizes the *downstream process* to withdraw parts from the *upstream process's* output buffer. It travels on the part's container itself. (2 marks)
2. **Production Kanban (P-Kanban / Order Kanban):** A signal that authorizes the *upstream process* to start producing a specific quantity (the standard container) of a specific part. It stays at the upstream cell. (2 marks)
3. **Signal Kanban (Sub-category):** Used in production leveling (Heijunka) to authorize production of a *full batch* when the supermarket of a specific part is depleted. (1 mark)
4. **Emergency Kanban (Sub-category):** A red card used to trigger extraordinary replenishment when abnormal demand spikes. (1 mark)

**Operating Rule:** No Kanban = No production. Any container moved without a card is an immediate *abnormal* (a clear violation of TPS rules, escalated to the Team Leader).

**Part (b) — Kanban Calculations (7 marks):**

**Given Data:**
- $D$ (Demand rate) = 400 parts / 8-hour shift
- $L$ (Lead time) = 3 hours
- $S_1$ (Initial safety factor) = 20 % = 0.20
- $C$ (Container size) = 25 parts

**Step 1: Compute the hourly demand rate (1 mark).**

$$
D = \frac{400 \text{ parts}}{8 \text{ hours}} = 50 \text{ parts/hour}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Number of Kanbans with $S = 20\%$ (2 marks).**

$$
N_1 = \frac{D \times L \times (1 + S)}{C} = \frac{50 \times 3 \times (1 + 0.20)}{25}
$$

$$
N_1 = \frac{150 \times 1.20}{25} = \frac{180}{25} = 7.2
$$

$$
N_1 = 8 \text{ containers (round up to next integer)}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Total WIP in the system (1 mark).**

$$
WIP = N_1 \times C = 8 \times 25 = 200 \text{ parts}
$$

**Step 4: Recompute Number of Kanbans with $S = 5\%$ (2 marks).**

$$
N_2 = \frac{50 \times 3 \times (1 + 0.05)}{25} = \frac{150 \times 1.05}{25} = \frac{157.5}{25} = 6.3
$$

$$
N_2 = 7 \text{ containers (round up to next integer)}
$$

**Step 5: Engineering Interpretation (1 mark).**

Reducing the safety factor from 20% to 5% allows the line to **drop from 8 to 7 containers**, eliminating 25 parts of WIP. However, the safety-stock reduction also means the line has *less buffer* against demand variability. This trade-off must be accepted by top management, as it sacrifices some robustness for lower inventory and faster defect detection — a hallmark TPS decision.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks on TPS Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing JIT with Jidoka:** Many students treat them as synonyms. They are *complementary pillars*, not the same thing. JIT is about *timing*; Jidoka is about *quality at the source*.
> 2. **Forgetting the Integer Round-Up Rule for Kanban:** The number of Kanban containers is **always an integer** and is **always rounded up** (ceiling function). Writing 7.2 as a final answer guarantees a 1-mark cut.
> 3. **Mishandling Takt Time Units:** Takt Time must always be in **seconds per unit** (the most KTU-asked unit). Forgetting to convert hours-to-seconds is the most common arithmetic trap.
> 4. **Listing Six Wastes Instead of Seven:** If the question asks for "any six," you must list **exactly six** — listing seven means you have not read the question carefully.
> 5. **Ignoring the "Why" Behind Principles:** A 14-mark question on the Toyota Way 14 Principles that lists all 14 without explaining *why* each one matters (and how it differs from a Western manufacturer) typically scores only 7-8 marks. Always add a sentence of contextual interpretation.
> 6. **OEE Components Misnaming:** OEE = Availability × Performance × Quality. Students often write "Uptime × Speed × Yield" which is conceptually correct but **not** the KTU-preferred terminology. Use A × P × Q.
> 7. **Mis-defining Heijunka:** Students often write "Heijunka = small batches." This is **incomplete**. Heijunka is the *leveling of production volume AND product mix over time* — both dimensions are required for full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for TPS (Module 4):**
>
> - **TPS Goal:** Highest quality, lowest cost, shortest lead time — the *roof* of the House of TPS.
> - **Two Pillars:** **JIT** (timing/pull) and **Jidoka** (quality/stop-on-defect). Both rest on the *foundation* of **Kaizen**.
> - **Originator:** **Taiichi Ohno** at Toyota in the 1950s-70s; inspired by Ford's mass production but constrained by Japan's lack of capital.
> - **Seven Wastes (Muda):** Overproduction, Waiting, Transportation, Over-processing, Inventory, Motion, Defects (+ Unused Talent as 8th).
> - **Three Enemies:** **Muda** (waste), **Mura** (unevenness), **Muri** (overburden). All three must be eliminated.
> - **Kanban:** Visual pull signal. **No Kanban = No production.** Always round up to the next integer container count.
> - **Kanban Formula:** $N = \dfrac{D \cdot L \cdot (1 + S)}{C}$.
> - **Takt Time Formula:** $T_{takt} = \dfrac{T_{available}}{D}$ — must be in **seconds per unit**.
> - **OEE Formula:** $A \times P \times Q$. World-class benchmark = **85.6 %**.
> - **14 Principles:** 1-2 Philosophy, 3-8 Process, 9-12 People, 13-14 Problem Solving.
> - **Heijunka:** Level the *volume and mix* of production, not just the volume.
> - **Poka-Yoke:** Mistake-proofing device — prevents the error before it happens, not after.
> - **Jidoka:** Stop the line at the *first* defect, fix the *root cause*, then resume.
> - **5S:** Sort, Set in Order, Shine, Standardize, Sustain — the visual housekeeping backbone of TPS.
> - **SMED:** Single Minute Exchange of Die — push changeover time below 10 minutes.
> - **Genchi Genbutsu:** "Go and see for yourself" — managers must visit the *Gemba* (actual workplace).
> - **Lean vs. TPS:** Lean is the *Western codification* of TPS, popularized by Womack & Jones in *The Machine That Changed the World* (1990). The terms are functionally equivalent, but TPS is the *original* system.
> - **Andon:** Visual signaling system (light or board) that displays line status and calls for help.
> - **Standardized Work:** The documented, agreed-upon best-known method for performing a task — the prerequisite for Kaizen.
> - **Hansei:** Self-reflection after a failure — a core cultural mechanism for organizational learning.
> - **Nemawashi:** Informal consensus-building *before* a formal decision is announced — the slow-decide, fast-execute Toyota principle.
> - **Pull vs. Push:** TPS is **PULL** (downstream signals upstream). Ford-era mass production is **PUSH** (forecast schedules production).
> - **Single-Piece Flow:** TPS ideal — produce one unit at a time, all the way through the line, no batching.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Business Process Re-engineering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET723 - PRODUCTION AND OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Business Process Re-engineering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Business Process Re-engineering (BPR)** is defined as the **fundamental rethinking and radical redesign of business processes** to achieve **dramatic improvements** in critical, contemporary measures of performance such as **cost, quality, service, and speed**.

The term was popularized by **Michael Hammer and James Champy** in their landmark 1993 book *"Reengineering the Corporation: A Manifesto for Business Revolution"*. According to Hammer and Champy, BPR is not about incremental improvement or tweaking existing systems; it demands a **clean-slate approach** where existing assumptions about how work is performed are discarded entirely.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** BPR sits at the intersection of Operations Management, Strategic Management, and Information Technology. The KTU board expects students to understand *why* re-engineering differs from continuous improvement, *how* it is implemented through structured phases, and *what* role IT plays in enabling process transformation.

### Key Terminology Decoded

| Term | KTU Board Definition |
|------|----------------------|
| **Business Process** | A set of logically related tasks performed to achieve a defined business outcome (e.g., order fulfillment, loan approval). |
| **Re-engineering** | Starting from a blank sheet; questioning every rule, assumption, and procedure that exists today. |
| **Radical Redesign** | Discarding existing structures; not just automating old processes but inventing entirely new ones. |
| **Dramatic Improvement** | Breakthrough gains of the order of **10x to 100x** (Hammer), not marginal 5–10% gains. |
| **Process Owner** | The single individual held accountable for the end-to-end performance of a re-engineered process. |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Think of BPR like renovating an old house.**

Imagine you bought a **50-year-old house** that was built when:
- The family owned one car
- Nobody had personal computers
- Entertainment meant a small TV in one room
- All cooking was done on a single gas stove

**Option A – Renovation (Continuous Improvement / TQM):** You repaint the walls, replace broken tiles, fix the leaking roof, and add a new coat of polish. The house *looks* better, but the **fundamental layout** (a small kitchen at the back, isolated living room, no dedicated workspace) is still the same. You've spent money, but the *flow* of daily life is unchanged.

**Option B – Demolition & Rebuild (BPR):** You knock down the entire structure and ask: *"If we were designing a house for how a modern family actually lives in 2026 — with work-from-home, open kitchens, smart appliances, and entertainment zones — what should it look like?"* You then build a **completely new house** from scratch that serves modern needs **10 times better** in terms of energy efficiency, comfort, and utility.

**BPR is Option B.** It is **NOT** a software upgrade, **NOT** an employee training program, and **NOT** a re-organisation chart shuffle. It is a *fundamental, top-down re-thinking* of how work gets done.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Misconception:** BPR is often confused with **Business Process Improvement (BPI)** or **Total Quality Management (TQM)**. BPR aims for **order-of-magnitude** breakthroughs; TQM/BPI aim for **incremental** gains (typically 5–30%) through continuous, bottom-up refinement. The KTU board regularly tests this distinction.

## 1.3 The KTU-Approved Distinction: BPR vs BPI vs TQM

| Dimension | BPR | BPI (Kaizen) | TQM |
|-----------|-----|--------------|-----|
| **Change Level** | Radical / Revolutionary | Incremental | Incremental |
| **Starting Point** | Zero — clean slate | Existing process | Existing process |
| **Frequency** | One-time, project-based | Continuous | Continuous |
| **Typical Gain** | 10x – 100x | 5% – 30% | 5% – 30% |
| **Leadership** | Top-down (CEO/Senior Mgmt) | Bottom-up (Workers) | Middle-out |
| **Risk** | High | Low | Low |
| **IT Role** | Enabler (often critical) | Supportive | Supportive |
| **Time Horizon** | Months to a few years | Ongoing | Ongoing |
| **Scope** | Cross-functional processes | Departmental | Departmental |

This comparison is a **high-frequency KTU exam topic**. Memorize the table — questions on "differentiate BPR from TQM" appear almost every semester.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of BPR (Hammer & Champy's Principles)

Hammer and Champy identified **four key enablers** that make BPR succeed. The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically requires students to articulate these principles in their own words.

### Pillar 1 — Organize Around Outcomes, Not Tasks
- **Logic:** Traditional organizations split work into narrow *tasks* performed by specialists (e.g., one clerk checks credit, another verifies address, a third approves the loan). Information passes hand-to-hand, creating delays and errors.
- **BPR Approach:** Collapse the task chain into a single **case manager** or **self-service digital system** that owns the outcome end-to-end.
- **Why it works:** Eliminates handoffs, reduces cycle time, gives the customer one point of contact.

### Pillar 2 — Have Those Who Use the Output Perform the Process
- **Logic:** Compiling reports just to feed other departments is waste. If the downstream user needs the data, give them the *tools* to generate it themselves.
- **BPR Approach:** Decentralize data capture and decision rights.
- **Example:** A salesperson uses a CRM dashboard to generate their own customer reports instead of requesting them from a central MIS team.

### Pillar 3 — Subsume Information-Processing Work Into the Real Work That Produces the Information
- **Logic:** In traditional firms, the person *doing* the work is separate from the person *recording* the work. The doer works, the recorder fills forms later.
- **BPR Approach:** Capture data *at the source* and *at the time of activity* (e.g., barcodes at checkout, sensors on machines).
- **Benefit:** One source of truth; eliminates reconciliation work.

### Pillar 4 — Treat Geographically Dispersed Resources as If They Were Centralized
- **Logic:** Modern technology (cloud, VPN, video conferencing) makes distance irrelevant.
- **BPR Approach:** Build *virtual* teams and shared service centres that pool expertise across regions.
- **Example:** A multinational's 24/7 customer support hub that follows the sun — calls are routed to whichever office is currently open.

## 2.2 BPR vs. Reorganizing vs. Restructuring

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU board sometimes frames this as: *"Re-engineering is not the same as downsizing, restructuring, or reorganisation."* Below is the precise distinction.

| Concept | Focus | Outcome |
|---------|-------|---------|
| **Downsizing** | Reducing headcount | Smaller workforce; same process |
| **Restructuring** | Changing reporting lines, org chart | New hierarchy; same process |
| **Reorganizing** | Merging/splitting departments | New boxes on paper; same process |
| **Re-engineering** | Redesigning the **process** itself | New process; org chart follows |

> BPR is **process-first**, not **structure-first**. The famous Hammer quote: *"Don't automate, obliterate."*

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield BPR Framework Cheat Sheet

Since BPR is qualitative, the "formula sheet" takes the form of a structured framework. Memorize this table verbatim.

| Stage / Element | KTU-Standard Description | Typical Duration (Industry Practice) |
|-----------------|--------------------------|--------------------------------------|
| **Envision** | Identify need for re-engineering; define strategic goals and scope | 1 – 2 months |
| **Initiate** | Secure top management sponsorship; appoint process owner; form BPR team | 1 – 2 months |
| **Diagnose** | Map existing ("As-Is") processes; identify pain points, bottlenecks, non-value-adding steps | 2 – 3 months |
| **Redesign** | Design new ("To-Be") process; prototype; benchmark; pilot | 3 – 6 months |
| **Implement** | Roll out new process; change management, training, IT deployment | 6 – 12 months |
| **Improve / Sustain** | Continuous monitoring, KPI tracking, refinement | Ongoing |

## 2.4 Drivers (Forces) That Trigger BPR

The KTU 2024 module identifies **three categories of drivers**. Examiners expect at least two to be discussed with industry examples.

### Category A — External Drivers
1. **Hypercompetition:** Global markets force firms to rethink cost structures.
2. **Customer Empowerment:** Empowered customers demand 24/7 service, personalization.
3. **Technological Disruption:** Internet, mobile, AI, cloud, IoT enable new process models.

### Category B — Internal Drivers
1. **Cost Pressure:** Profit margins eroding; need order-of-magnitude savings.
2. **Quality Gaps:** Defect rates or complaint volumes that incremental fixes cannot address.
3. **Cycle Time:** Lead times measured in weeks when industry standard is hours.
4. **Strategic Pivot:** New business model (e.g., Tata Motors moving from truck-only to passenger cars).

### Category C — Catalytic Drivers
1. **Mergers & Acquisitions:** Combined entities must harmonize incompatible processes.
2. **Regulatory Change:** New compliance requirements (e.g., GST in India forced ERP re-engineering across SMEs).
3. **Crisis Events:** A near-failure that exposes structural weaknesses.

## 2.5 Roles in a BPR Project

| Role | Responsibility |
|------|----------------|
| **Process Owner** | Single accountable leader for the end-to-end process |
| **BPR Steering Committee** | Senior executives sponsoring and resourcing the project |
| **Re-engineering Team** | Cross-functional task force (4–8 members) doing the actual redesign |
| **Re-engineering Czar / Leader** | Facilitates the team; reports to steering committee |
| **Process Workers** | Subject matter experts providing ground-level inputs |

## 2.6 Why BPR Projects Fail (KTU-High-Yield)

> [!WARNING]
> KTU questions on BPR often end with: *"Discuss the reasons for failure of BPR projects."* Memorize at least 5 reasons.

1. **Lack of Top Management Commitment** — Most common cause; BPR dies without CEO-level sponsorship.
2. **Narrow Focus on Technology** — Treating BPR as an IT project instead of a business transformation.
3. **Ignoring Process People Issues** — Resistance to change, fear of job loss, skill obsolescence.
4. **Underestimating Cultural Barriers** — Attempting radical change in a deeply hierarchical culture.
5. **Inadequate Vision** — Re-engineering for the wrong reasons (e.g., to justify a pre-decided software purchase).
6. **Attempting Too Much Too Fast** — Trying to re-engineer the entire company in one go.
7. **Lack of Process Ownership** — No one is held accountable for end-to-end performance.
8. **Poor Project Management** — BPR treated as a vague initiative rather than a structured program.

## 2.7 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Operations

BPR is a **core operations management tool** because it directly reshapes the *flow* of work that produces goods and services. Practical applications include:

- **Manufacturing:** Replacing batch-and-queue assembly with cellular manufacturing or one-piece flow.
- **Supply Chain:** Re-engineering procurement from purchase-order-based to vendor-managed inventory.
- **Healthcare:** Redesigning patient admission to eliminate redundant form-filling and waiting.
- **Banking:** Loan approval compressed from 7 days to 30 minutes via straight-through processing.
- **IT Services:** DevOps re-engineering software delivery from monthly releases to hourly deployments.

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix: BPR Methodology (BPR Phases in Detail)

Since BPR is a process rather than a formula, the "derivation" here is an **exhaustive, step-by-step exposition of the BPR lifecycle** that the KTU board expects students to be able to reproduce in 14-mark answers. Each phase is broken into *sub-steps* with explicit *reasoning*, which is exactly what examiners award marks for.

---

## 3.1 The Six-Phase BPR Lifecycle (Detailed Walkthrough)

### **Phase 1 — Envision (Setting the Strategic Compass)**

**Step 1.1:** Identify the business problem or opportunity that incremental improvement cannot solve.
*Reasoning:* If a 10% improvement will do, BPR is overkill. BPR is reserved for situations where the *survival or strategic leap* of the firm is at stake.

**Step 1.2:** Articulate the strategic objectives in measurable terms.
*Reasoning:* Vague goals ("be world-class") fail. Strong goals are SMART — Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound.
Example: *"Reduce order-to-delivery cycle time from 14 days to 24 hours within 18 months."*

**Step 1.3:** Define scope — which processes will be re-engineered and which will not.
*Reasoning:* Attempting the whole company is the #1 cause of BPR failure. Typical scope: 2–4 critical processes.

**Step 1.4:** Identify key stakeholders and secure initial buy-in.
*Reasoning:* BPR without executive sponsorship is a non-starter.

---

### **Phase 2 — Initiate (Mobilising the Project)**

**Step 2.1:** Appoint a senior **Process Owner** with authority over the end-to-end process.
*Reasoning:* Without ownership, the project becomes an academic exercise. The process owner is the single throat to choke.

**Step 2.2:** Charter a **Re-engineering Team** (cross-functional, 4–8 members).
*Reasoning:* Process knowledge is siloed; a cross-functional team is the only way to see the *real* end-to-end process.

**Step 2.3:** Appoint a **Re-engineering Czar / Facilitator**.
*Reasoning:* An external or internal facilitator brings methodological rigour and shields the team from political interference.

**Step 2.4:** Develop a project plan, budget, and risk register.
*Reasoning:* BPR is a project, not a slogan.

---

### **Phase 3 — Diagnose (Understanding the As-Is Process)**

**Step 3.1:** Map the **"As-Is"** process using standard notation.
*Tools Used:* Flowcharts, SIPOC diagrams, swim-lane diagrams, value-stream mapping, Gemba walks.

**Step 3.2:** Collect data on cycle time, defect rate, cost per transaction, customer satisfaction.
*Reasoning:* Diagnosis without data is opinion.

**Step 3.3:** Identify **non-value-adding activities** (waste — transportation, inventory, motion, waiting, over-processing, over-production, defects, unused talent — the **DOWNTIME** wastes from Lean).

**Step 3.4:** Identify root causes using techniques like the **5 Whys**, fishbone (Ishikawa) diagrams, and Pareto analysis.

**Step 3.5:** Quantify the gap between current performance and strategic objective.
*Reasoning:* This gap justifies the radical intervention.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked to "diagnose," always present an *As-Is* diagram and a list of bottlenecks. A diagnosis without a visual map is considered incomplete.

---

### **Phase 4 — Redesign (Inventing the To-Be Process)**

**Step 4.1:** Question every assumption of the existing process.
*Key Question Pattern:* "Why do we do it this way? What if we did the opposite? What if we eliminated the step entirely?"

**Step 4.2:** Apply the four BPR enablers (Section 2.1) to generate redesign alternatives.

**Step 4.3:** Benchmark against world-class performers (within or outside the industry).
*Reasoning:* Best-in-class performance defines the new design target.

**Step 4.4:** Prototype the **"To-Be"** process.
*Methods:* Role-playing, simulation, rapid prototyping of supporting IT systems.

**Step 4.5:** Evaluate alternatives using cost-benefit analysis, risk assessment, and stakeholder impact.
*Reasoning:* BPR is not a religious exercise; it must be financially justified.

**Step 4.6:** Select the final To-Be design and document it in detail.
*Outputs:* New process map, role definitions, IT requirements, change impact assessment.

---

### **Phase 5 — Implement (Making the Redesign Real)**

**Step 5.1:** Develop a **change management plan** — communication, training, incentives.
*Reasoning:* BPR fails on the "soft side" more often than on the technical side.

**Step 5.2:** Pilot the new process in a controlled environment (one region, one product line, one customer segment).
*Reasoning:* Pilot exposes unforeseen issues at limited cost.

**Step 5.3:** Refine the design based on pilot learning.

**Step 5.4:** Full rollout — phased or big-bang depending on risk profile.

**Step 5.5:** Deploy enabling technology (ERP, workflow automation, CRM, analytics).

**Step 5.6:** Communicate continuously — celebrate quick wins to build momentum.

---

### **Phase 6 — Improve / Sustain (Locking in Gains)**

**Step 6.1:** Establish **process performance metrics (KPIs)** aligned with the strategic goals.

**Step 6.2:** Implement process monitoring and exception reporting.

**Step 6.3:** Institutionalise continuous improvement *within* the new process (so BPR gains don't erode).

**Step 6.4:** Reward the new behaviour; sunset the old process formally.

**Step 6.5:** Capture lessons learned for future BPR cycles.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: BPR Techniques & Their Purpose

The KTU board frequently tests *"List the techniques used in BPR and explain any three."* The matrix below is your single source of truth.

| Technique | Purpose | Stage Where Used |
|-----------|---------|------------------|
| **Process Mapping (As-Is / To-Be)** | Visualise the workflow | Diagnose, Redesign |
| **SIPOC Diagram** | Define Suppliers, Inputs, Process, Outputs, Customers | Initiate, Diagnose |
| **Value Stream Mapping (VSM)** | Identify value-added vs non-value-added steps | Diagnose |
| **Benchmarking** | Compare against best-in-class | Redesign |
| **5 Whys Analysis** | Drill to root cause | Diagnose |
| **Fishbone (Ishikawa) Diagram** | Categorise root causes | Diagnose |
| **Pareto Analysis** | Prioritise the vital few issues | Diagnose |
| **Simulation / Modelling** | Test To-Be design virtually | Redesign |
| **Cost-Benefit Analysis** | Justify redesign financially | Redesign |
| **SWOT Analysis** | Assess internal/external enablers and barriers | Envision |
| **Force Field Analysis (Lewin)** | Identify driving and restraining forces | Implement |
| **Kotter's 8-Step Change Model** | Structure the change journey | Implement |
| **Balanced Scorecard** | Measure BPR outcomes | Improve/Sustain |
| **Business Process Model & Notation (BPMN)** | Standardised process documentation | All phases |
| **IT Enablers (ERP, CRM, Workflow, RPA, AI)** | Automate the new process | Implement |

## 3.3 Quantitative Framing: BPR ROI Calculation (Board-Approved Method)

Although BPR is qualitative, the KTU board expects students to be able to compute a **simple BPR business case** when numerical data is given. The structure is:

$$\text{BPR Net Benefit} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{(\Delta \text{Revenue}_t + \Delta \text{Cost Saving}_t - \text{Implementation Cost}_t)}{(1 + r)^t}$$

Where:
- $\Delta \text{Revenue}_t$ = Incremental revenue in year $t$ from faster / better process
- $\Delta \text{Cost Saving}_t$ = Reduction in operating cost in year $t$
- $\text{Implementation Cost}_t$ = BPR project cost (consulting, IT, training, transition)
- $r$ = Discount rate (firm's cost of capital, typically 10% – 15%)
- $n$ = Evaluation horizon (typically 3 – 5 years)

The BPR project is **financially justified** if:

$$\text{NPV}_{\text{BPR}} = \text{PV(Benefits)} - \text{PV(Costs)} > 0$$

And the **Payback Period** is less than the firm's threshold (typically 3 years).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** If a numerical question on BPR appears (e.g., "Compute the NPV of a BPR initiative given cost savings of ₹50 lakh/year for 4 years and implementation cost of ₹80 lakh at year 0, r = 12%"), apply the formula above step by step. The board awards full marks for *showing the discount factor table and summation explicitly*.

### Worked Example (Self-Study Reference)

Suppose a manufacturing firm invests **₹80 lakh** in a BPR project. Expected annual cost savings: **₹30 lakh/year** for 5 years. Discount rate: **10%**.

**Step 1:** Compute the discount factor for each year.

$$\text{DF}_t = \frac{1}{(1 + 0.10)^t}$$

**Step 2:** Multiply savings by discount factor.

| Year (t) | Savings (₹ lakh) | DF @ 10% | PV (₹ lakh) |
|----------|------------------|----------|-------------|
| 1 | 30 | 0.9091 | 27.27 |
| 2 | 30 | 0.8264 | 24.79 |
| 3 | 30 | 0.7513 | 22.54 |
| 4 | 30 | 0.6830 | 20.49 |
| 5 | 30 | 0.6209 | 18.63 |
| **Total** | | | **113.72** |

**Step 3:** Compute NPV.

$$\text{NPV} = 113.72 - 80.00 = 33.72 \text{ lakh}$$

**Decision:** NPV > 0, so the BPR initiative is **financially justified**. The firm earns ₹33.72 lakh in present-value terms above the hurdle rate.

> Students should be able to reproduce this table format in an exam if a numerical BPR problem is given.

> **Reading the Diagram:** The As-Is process had **6 sequential steps with 5 hand-offs** and typically took **5–7 working days**.
