# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Paper]**
**Define Business Process Re-engineering (BPR). Mention any two characteristics.**

**Model Answer:**

Business Process Re-engineering, as defined by Michael Hammer and James Champy (1993), is the **fundamental rethinking and radical redesign of business processes** to achieve **dramatic improvements** in critical measures of performance such as cost, quality, service, and speed.

**Two Key Characteristics:**

1. **Radical Redesign:** BPR does not focus on incremental improvement. It questions every existing assumption and starts from a clean slate, often achieving order-of-magnitude (10x–100x) gains.
2. **Process-Centric Focus:** BPR organises work around end-to-end *processes* rather than around narrow functional tasks or departmental silos, ensuring customer value is delivered holistically.

*(Additional acceptable points: top-down leadership, cross-functional teams, enabler role of IT, dramatic gains in cycle time.)* **[3 marks]**

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Business Process Re-engineering (BPR) and Total Quality Management (TQM).**

**Model Answer:**

| Dimension | BPR | TQM |
|-----------|-----|-----|
| **Nature of Change** | Radical and revolutionary | Incremental and continuous |
| **Typical Performance Gain** | Order-of-magnitude (10x–100x) | Small, gradual (5%–30%) |
| **Approach Direction** | Top-down, led by senior management | Bottom-up, driven by employees |
| **Process Focus** | Re-designs the *process itself* | Refines the *existing* process |
| **Risk Level** | High | Low |
| **Time Horizon** | Project-based, one-time | Continuous, ongoing |
| **Technology Role** | Often a critical enabler | Supportive, not central |

Hence, TQM optimises what already exists through continuous improvement, while BPR discards the old and invents an entirely new process. **[3 marks]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and is split into sub-parts, typically **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. The examiner provides an internal choice; you answer *either* Question A *or* Question B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper, Module 4]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **drivers that trigger BPR initiatives** in a modern organisation. Categorise them and give suitable industry examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **six phases of the BPR lifecycle** with a clear focus on what happens during the *Diagnose* and *Redesign* phases. Illustrate with the As-Is and To-Be process of a loan-approval system. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer — (a) Drivers of BPR

BPR is not a routine exercise; it is triggered by forces that make the *current* way of working untenable. These drivers are categorised as **external, internal, and catalytic**.

**1. External Drivers**
- **Hypercompetition:** Globalisation has exposed firms to competitors from low-cost geographies. Example: Indian banks re-engineering retail loan processes in response to fintechs offering instant approvals.
- **Customer Empowerment:** Digital customers expect Amazon-like speed. Example: HDFC Bank's re-engineered net-banking that mirrors mobile-app UX.
- **Technology Disruption:** Cloud, AI, blockchain, and IoT have made old processes obsolete. Example: Maruti Suzuki re-engineering dealer ordering via a cloud-based portal replacing fax-based systems.

**2. Internal Drivers**
- **Cost Pressure:** When margins shrink due to inflation or competition. Example: A FMCG firm re-engineering distribution to reduce warehouse touchpoints.
- **Quality Gaps:** High defect rates or service failures that piecemeal fixes cannot solve. Example: Re-engineering of hospital admission process to reduce medication errors.
- **Cycle-Time Bottlenecks:** When lead times are 10x industry benchmark. Example: Passport office re-engineering in India under the Passport Seva Project.
- **Strategic Pivot:** Entering a new market. Example: Tata Motors re-engineering procurement and supplier networks when launching the Tata Nano.

**3. Catalytic Drivers**
- **Mergers and Acquisitions:** Combined firms must harmonise conflicting processes.
- **Regulatory Change:** GST implementation forced SMEs to re-engineer their entire accounting and invoicing flow.
- **Crisis Events:** A near-bankruptcy that exposes the inadequacy of piecemeal fixes.

**Conclusion:** BPR is *triggered* when incremental, continuous improvement cannot deliver the magnitude of change the organisation needs to survive or leap ahead. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer — (b) Six Phases of BPR Lifecycle

**Phase 1 — Envision:** Identify the strategic need; define scope; set measurable objectives (e.g., reduce loan approval cycle from 7 days to 30 minutes).

**Phase 2 — Initiate:** Secure top management sponsorship; appoint a process owner; form a cross-functional BPR team of 4–8 members; appoint a facilitator.

**Phase 3 — Diagnose (Critical Phase):** Map the *As-Is* process end-to-end. For a loan approval system, this means tracing the application from branch intake to disbursement, identifying every handoff (intake clerk → credit bureau clerk → collateral assessor → manager → disbursement). Use **SIPOC diagrams, value-stream mapping, and 5-Whys** to identify non-value-adding activities such as redundant manual data entry, multiple status printouts, and sequential handoffs. Quantify the pain: average cycle time = 5–7 days, error rate = 8%, customer complaints = high.

**Phase 4 — Redesign (Critical Phase):** Question every rule. Apply Hammer's four enablers. Design a *To-Be* process where the customer fills an online form, an automated rule engine performs credit scoring using bureau APIs, an AI-based decision engine approves/rejects, and digital disbursement credits the customer's account — all within 30 minutes. Benchmark against the best-in-class fintech.

**Phase 5 — Implement:** Pilot in one branch; refine; roll out bank-wide; deploy IT systems; train staff in new roles (case managers rather than clerks); communicate continuously.

**Phase 6 — Improve/Sustain:** Track KPIs (cycle time, error rate, NPS); institutionalise; harvest gains; share lessons with other BPR initiatives.

**Visualisation:**

| Dimension | As-Is (Before BPR) | To-Be (After BPR) |
|-----------|--------------------|--------------------|
| Steps | 6 sequential | 4 streamlined |
| Handoffs | 5 | 2 |
| Cycle Time | 5–7 days | < 30 minutes |
| Error Rate | 8% | < 0.5% |
| Customer Touch-points | Multiple (branch visits) | Single (digital) |
| Decision-making | Manual, hierarchical | Automated, rule-based |

This table is exactly the kind of structured comparison the KTU board awards full marks for. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper, Module 4]**

**(a)** What are the **four key enablers (principles) of BPR** proposed by Hammer and Champy? Explain each with an industrial example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **major reasons for failure of BPR projects**. As a project manager, what steps would you take to **ensure success** of a BPR initiative in an Indian manufacturing firm? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer — (a) Four Enablers of BPR

**Enabler 1 — Organise Around Outcomes, Not Tasks**
Collapse task-based silos into a single process owner or case manager.
*Industrial Example:* In a re-engineered insurance claim process, a single claims case manager handles the entire claim from intimation to settlement, eliminating the historical handoffs between surveyors, assessors, and approvers. Claim settlement time dropped from 30 days to 7 days.

**Enabler 2 — Have Those Who Use the Output Perform the Process**
Give downstream users the tools to access and process data themselves, eliminating the intermediate reporting layer.
*Industrial Example:* Sales teams at a leading FMCG firm now generate their own territory-wise sales dashboards via a self-service BI tool (e.g., Power BI), removing the dependence on a central MIS team and reducing report turnaround from 5 days to 5 minutes.

**Enabler 3 — Subsume Information-Processing Work Into the Real Work That Produces the Information**
Capture data at the source, at the time of activity.
*Industrial Example:* In a modern car assembly line, RFID tags on components automatically register their position and status as they pass through stations, eliminating manual barcode scanning and reconciliation.

**Enabler 4 — Treat Geographically Dispersed Resources as If They Were Centralized**
Leverage IT to pool expertise across geographies.
*Industrial Example:* A multinational engineering firm's 24×7 technical support follows the sun — calls are routed to whichever global support centre is currently open, eliminating the need for three independent regional teams and improving service quality. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer — (b) Reasons for Failure and Success Strategies

**Major Reasons for BPR Failure:**

1. **Lack of Top Management Commitment** — BPR cannot survive as a middle-management experiment. Without CEO sponsorship, resource allocation and political will evaporate.
2. **Treating BPR as an IT Project** — Installing ERP does not constitute re-engineering. Technology is an *enabler*, not the *purpose*.
3. **Resistance to Change** — Employees fear job loss, skill obsolescence, and status erosion. Without addressing the human side, BPR triggers sabotage or passive non-compliance.
4. **Cultural Incompatibility** — Radical change in a deeply hierarchical, risk-averse culture (common in many Indian manufacturing firms) is met with passive resistance.
5. **Unrealistic Scope** — Attempting to re-engineer the entire firm at once dilutes focus and overwhelms the organisation.
6. **No Process Owner** — Diffuse accountability means no one drives end-to-end performance.
7. **Poor Communication** — A re-engineered process surprises employees on launch day, leading to chaos.

**Success Strategy for an Indian Manufacturing Firm (Project Manager's Plan):**

1. **Secure CEO Sponsorship First:** Present a clear business case (cost, quality, speed gains) to the promoter/CEO; obtain board-level endorsement before any operational step.
2. **Select a High-Impact, Bounded Process:** Start with one critical process (e.g., order-to-delivery) rather than the entire value chain. Pilot success builds momentum.
3. **Appoint a Strong Process Owner:** A senior plant head with authority to override functional silos.
4. **Invest in Change Management:** Conduct town-halls, communicate the *why* of BPR (survival, growth — not just layoffs), and provide reskilling guarantees.
5. **Leverage IT Thoughtfully:** Choose ERP modules (e.g., SAP PP, MM) that *fit* the new process — not the other way round.
6. **Pilot → Learn → Scale:** Run the redesigned process on one product line, capture learnings, then roll out plant-wide.
7. **Measure and Celebrate:** Define 5–6 KPIs (cycle time, defect rate, OEE, cost per unit), track them visibly, and celebrate early wins to sustain buy-in.
8. **Plan for Sustained Improvement:** BPR gains erode quickly without a continuous-improvement layer (e.g., Lean daily management) on top of the redesigned process.

**Conclusion:** BPR succeeds when it is treated as a *business transformation* led by the top, owned by a single accountable leader, enabled by technology, and embraced by the workforce. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — BPR Questions**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse BPR with downsizing.** If the question asks "is BPR the same as cost-cutting?" — answer a clear **NO**. BPR may *lead* to lower cost, but its focus is on *process redesign* for *multiple* outcomes.
> 2. **Always mention Hammer and Champy by name** when defining BPR — examiners expect this attribution for full credit.
> 3. **Never present BPR as a one-time event without a sustain phase.** The Improve/Sustain phase is mandatory in any 14-mark answer on BPR lifecycle.
> 4. **In numerical BPR questions**, students often forget to *show the discount factor calculation*. Always write $\text{DF}_t = \frac{1}{(1+r)^t}$ explicitly, then tabulate, then sum. Marks are awarded for the table, not just the final answer.
> 5. **Avoid vague phrases like "BPR improves efficiency."** Use *specific* metrics: cycle time, defect rate, cost per transaction, customer satisfaction score.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Mile Revision — Read this 30 minutes before the exam.**

- **Definition (Hammer & Champy 1993):** Fundamental rethinking and radical redesign of business processes to achieve **dramatic improvements** in cost, quality, service, and speed.
- **BPR ≠ TQM ≠ BPI:** BPR is *radical* and *top-down*; TQM/BPI are *incremental* and *bottom-up*. BPR aims for **10x–100x**; TQM/BPI aim for **5%–30%**.
- **The Four Enablers (Hammer):**
  1. Organise around *outcomes*, not tasks.
  2. Have users *perform* the process.
  3. *Subsume* information processing into the real work.
  4. Treat *distributed* resources as centralised.
- **Six Phases of BPR:** Envision → Initiate → Diagnose → Redesign → Implement → Improve/Sustain.
- **Roles:** Process Owner, Steering Committee, Re-engineering Team, Re-engineering Czar (Facilitator), Process Workers.
- **Drivers:** External (hypercompetition, customer empowerment, technology); Internal (cost, quality, cycle time, strategic pivot); Catalytic (M&A, regulation, crisis).
- **Failure Causes:** No top management commitment, narrow IT focus, people issues, cultural barriers, no process owner, attempting too much too fast.
- **Success Factors:** CEO sponsorship, bounded scope, strong process owner, change management, IT as enabler, pilot before scale, KPI tracking, continuous improvement on top.
- **Techniques to memorise:** Process mapping, SIPOC, VSM, 5 Whys, Fishbone, Pareto, Benchmarking, Simulation, BPMN, Force-Field Analysis, Kotter's 8 Steps, Balanced Scorecard.
- **Numerical Skill:** BPR business case using NPV — show discount factors explicitly, then sum PV, then subtract cost.
- **Real-World Anchors (use as examples in answers):**
  - Ford's accounts payable re-engineering (1990s — reduced headcount by 75%).
  - Tata Steel's "Total Quality" and BPR initiatives in Jamshedpur.
  - Maruti Suzuki's dealer-ordering portal.
  - HDFC Bank's loan-approval straight-through processing.
  - Indian Passport Seva Project.
  - GST-mandated re-engineering of SME accounting.

> **Closing Note for KTU 2024 Students:** BPR questions are *high-mark, high-discrimination* items. They reward students who can write structured, example-rich, framework-anchored answers. Memorise the *definitions, the four enablers, the six phases, and the BPR-vs-TQM contrast* — these four elements alone can fetch 8–10 marks in a typical 14-mark question. Augment with one well-remembered Indian industry example to push your answer to full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: World Class Manufacturing Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET723 - PRODUCTION AND OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** World Class Manufacturing Concepts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **World Class Manufacturing (WCM)** is defined as the set of **modern production management practices, principles, and philosophies** that, when collectively adopted by a manufacturing organization, enable it to achieve **superior performance** across the four cardinal competitive dimensions: **Quality, Cost, Delivery, and Flexibility** — surpassing the recognized industry leaders (best-in-class global competitors) on a sustained, long-term basis.

The term was popularized by **Richard J. Schonberger** in his landmark work *"World Class Manufacturing: The Lessons of Simplicity Applied"* (1986) and further developed by **Robert Hall** in *"Attaining Manufacturing Excellence"* (1987). In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, WCM is treated as an **integrative umbrella concept** that subsumes TQM, JIT, TPM, Lean, and Six Sigma into a single unified competitive strategy.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Olympic Athlete Factory"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of a manufacturing firm as an **Olympic athlete**.
> - A sprinter is *fast* → but a World-Class athlete is **fast, strong, agile, consistent, and injury-resistant simultaneously**.
> - A factory that only achieves *low cost* is like a sprinter who can only run downhill — fast, but brittle.
> - **WCM** means the factory is excellent in **all dimensions, all the time, at world-leading benchmarks**, and it keeps getting better (continuous improvement).

The **"World Class"** label is not self-declared. It is a **relative standard** measured against the **best global competitor** in that product/process category.

---

### 1.3 The Three Foundational Building Blocks of WCM

| S.No. | Building Block | KTU Definition |
|---|---|---|
| **1** | **Technology** | Modern, often computer-integrated manufacturing (CIM) hardware & software (CNC, Robotics, ERP, MES) |
| **2** | **Systems** | Management and information systems that integrate all functions (MRP-II, ERP, SPC) |
| **3** | **People** | Skilled, empowered, multi-functional workforce committed to continuous improvement (Kaizen) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight (Hall's Doctrine):** Out of these three blocks, **PEOPLE is the most critical**. Even with the best technology and systems, without an involved, trained, and empowered workforce, WCM cannot be attained. The human element is the **"glue"** that activates the other two blocks.

---

### 1.4 Schematic Position of WCM in Manufacturing Strategy

WCM is the **apex of the manufacturing evolution hierarchy**:

$$
\text{Craft Production} \rightarrow \text{Mass Production} \rightarrow \text{Lean Production} \rightarrow \textbf{World Class Manufacturing (WCM)}
$$

It is **NOT a tool or a software package** — it is a **holistic business philosophy and operating model**.

---

### 1.5 Why KTU Stresses WCM in Module 4

- Module 4 transitions from *internal quality* (TQM) → *external competitiveness* (WCM).
- WCM is the **bridge** that connects TQM, JIT, TPM, Lean to **strategic business outcomes**.
- KTU frequently tests the student's ability to **compare and integrate** the sub-philosophies (TQM/JIT/TPM/Lean) under the WCM umbrella.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework

### 2.1 Schonberger's 10 Strategic Principles of WCM

These are the **most frequently tested** principles in KTU ESE. Richard Schonberger distilled observations from Japanese and Western best-practice plants into **10 universal principles**:

| # | Principle | Core Idea (Why & How) |
|---|---|---|
| **P1** | **Go to the Gemba (Workplace)** | Managers must go to the actual place of work (shop floor) to observe, ask, and listen — *data over opinion*. |
| **P2** | **POSMOC — Possession of Single Minute Exchange of Dies** | Reduce setup/changeover time to **< 10 minutes (Single Digit)** for flexibility. |
| **P3** | **Group Technology (Cellular Layout)** | Organize machines into **cells** by part-family, not by function. |
| **P4** | **Just-In-Time (JIT) Production** | Produce **only what is needed, only when needed, only in the amount needed** → zero inventory. |
| **P5** | **Short Cycle Manufacturing (Quick Flow)** | Reduce **throughput / lead time** drastically (orders of magnitude, not percentages). |
| **P6** | **Continuous Improvement (Kaizen)** | Small, incremental, daily improvements by *every employee* — 1% better every day. |
| **P7** | **Customer-Driven Quality (QFD/Customer Focus)** | Quality defined by **customer requirements**, not by internal specs. |
| **P8** | **Total Preventive Maintenance (TPM)** | Zero breakdowns, zero defects from machines → operator-owned maintenance. |
| **P9** | **Supplier Integration (Partnership)** | Treat suppliers as **partners**, reduce supplier base, share data. |
| **P10**| **Modern Manufacturing Technology (Hard Automation)** | Use IT/CIM/FMS to eliminate waste, paperwork, and non-value-adding activities. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Trick: "G-P-J-S-Q-C-Q-T-S-M"** → Arrange the first letters: Gemba, POSMOC, Group-tech, JIT, Short-cycle, Continuous, Customer-quality, TPM, Supplier, Modern-tech.

---

### 2.2 Robert Hall's Three-Block Model (Detailed)

Hall's model (1987) is the **second most-tested WCM framework** in KTU exams.

$$
\boxed{\text{WCM Achievement} = f(\text{People}, \text{Systems}, \text{Technology})}
$$

**Detailed weightage assigned by Hall:**

| Block | Hall's Estimated Contribution | KTU Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| **People** | **~ 50%** | Workforce competence, empowerment, involvement, training, leadership |
| **Systems** | **~ 30%** | Quality systems (ISO), MRP, JIT, TQM systems, SPC |
| **Technology** | **~ 20%** | CAD/CAM, FMS, CIM, Robotics, ERP |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often rank Technology highest. **People is the single largest contributor** to WCM success according to Hall. A question may offer 1 mark for this prioritization.

---

### 2.3 The Constituent Philosophies of WCM (Sub-Umbrellas)

WCM is achieved **only by orchestrating multiple sub-philosophies**. KTU expects a comparative treatment:

| Philosophy | Primary Focus | Key Tool/Method | Origin |
|---|---|---|---|
| **TQM** | Customer satisfaction, quality of output | SPC, QCC, Pareto, Fishbone | Deming, Juran, Crosby |
| **JIT** | Eliminate inventory waste | Kanban, Pull system, POSMOC | Toyota (Ohno) |
| **TPM** | Eliminate machine breakdown loss | OEE, Autonomous Maintenance | Nakajima (JIPM) |
| **Lean Manufacturing** | Eliminate ALL waste (Muda) | Value Stream Mapping, 5S | Womack & Jones |
| **Six Sigma** | Reduce variation to 3.4 DPMO | DMAIC, DMADV, Black/Green Belts | Motorola (Harry, Schroeder) |
| **5S** | Workplace organization | Sort, Set-in-order, Shine, Standardize, Sustain | Toyota |
| **Kaizen** | Continuous small improvements | PDCA, Gemba walks | Imai |
| **Benchmarking** | Learn from best-in-class | Gap analysis, Best Practice Transfer | Xerox (Camp) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Standard:** WCM = **TQM ∪ JIT ∪ TPM ∪ Lean ∪ Six Sigma ∪ Kaizen ∪ Benchmarking** — *all of them, integrated*. A plant using only TQM is *not* WCM; it must integrate all.

---

### 2.4 WCM Key Performance Metrics (Formula Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU Formula Sheet below uses `\mid` instead of `|` to preserve markdown table integrity. All metrics are industry-standard.

| Metric | Formula | Target (World Class) | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness)** | $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$ | $\geq 85\%$ | % |
| **First Pass Yield (FPY)** | $\text{FPY} = \dfrac{\text{Good Units}}{\text{Total Units Started}}$ | $\geq 99\%$ | % |
| **On-Time Delivery (OTD)** | $\text{OTD} = \dfrac{\text{Orders Delivered On-Time}}{\text{Total Orders}}$ | $\geq 98\%$ | % |
| **Inventory Turns (IT)** | $\text{IT} = \dfrac{\text{COGS}}{\text{Average Inventory}}$ | $\geq 12$ (Best: $\geq 30$) | turns/year |
| **Manufacturing Cycle Time (MCT)** | $\text{MCT} = \dfrac{\text{Value-Added Time}}{\text{Lead Time}}$ | Approaches 1 | ratio |
| **DPMO (Defects Per Million Opportunities)** | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{D \times 1{,}000{,}000}{N \times O}$ | $\leq 3.4$ (Six Sigma) | defects/M opportunities |
| **Sigma Level** | $\sigma = \text{NORMSINV}\!\left(1.5 - \dfrac{Y}{100}\right) + 1.5$ | $\geq 4.5$ (WCM), $\geq 6$ (Best) | $\sigma$ |
| **Set-up Time (POSMOC)** | $\text{ST}_{\text{new}} \leq 10$ minutes | Single digit | min |

Where for OEE:
- $A$ = **Availability** = $\dfrac{\text{Operating Time}}{\text{Planned Production Time}}$
- $P$ = **Performance** = $\dfrac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Pieces}}{\text{Operating Time}}$
- $Q$ = **Quality** = $\dfrac{\text{Good Pieces}}{\text{Total Pieces Produced}}$

---

### 2.5 The WCM Implementation Roadmap (KTU 5-Phase Model)

A plant progresses through **5 maturity levels** toward WCM:

$$
\text{Level 1 (Initial)} \rightarrow \text{Level 2 (Repeatable)} \rightarrow \text{Level 3 (Defined)} \rightarrow \text{Level 4 (Managed)} \rightarrow \textbf{Level 5 (Optimizing / WCM)}
$$

| Phase | Action | KTU Buzzword |
|---|---|---|
| **I** | Top management commitment & Vision | *Leadership* |
| **II** | Baseline benchmarking & gap analysis | *Measure* |
| **III** | Process re-engineering (TQM/JIT/TPM rollout) | *Implement* |
| **IV** | Workforce training, empowerment, Kaizen culture | *People* |
| **V** | Continuous improvement, re-benchmark, sustain | *Sustain* |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** Companies like **Toyota, Intel, Bosch, GE** use this phased approach. KTU questions on "steps to become a WCM company" expect these 5 phases in order.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Numerical Examples

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — OEE Computation (14-Mark Style Problem)

> **Problem (KTU-style):** A CNC cell operates for **400 minutes** in an 8-hour shift. Planned downtime (breaks, changeovers) = **60 min**. The ideal cycle time per part = **1.2 min**. Total parts produced = **250**, of which **240** are good. Compute OEE.

**Given Data (Tabulated):**

$$
\text{Planned Production Time} = 480 - 60 = 420 \text{ min}
$$
$$
\text{Operating Time} = 400 \text{ min}
$$
$$
\text{Ideal Cycle Time} = 1.2 \text{ min/part}
$$
$$
\text{Total Pieces} = 250, \quad \text{Good Pieces} = 240
$$

**Step 1 — Availability ($A$):**

$$
A = \dfrac{\text{Operating Time}}{\text{Planned Production Time}} = \dfrac{400}{420}
$$

$$
A = 0.9524 = 95.24\%
$$

**Step 2 — Performance ($P$):**

$$
P = \dfrac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Pieces}}{\text{Operating Time}} = \dfrac{1.2 \times 250}{400}
$$

$$
P = \dfrac{300}{400} = 0.7500 = 75.00\%
$$

**Step 3 — Quality ($Q$):**

$$
Q = \dfrac{\text{Good Pieces}}{\text{Total Pieces}} = \dfrac{240}{250}
$$

$$
Q = 0.9600 = 96.00\%
$$

**Step 4 — OEE:**

$$
\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q = 0.9524 \times 0.7500 \times 0.9600
$$

$$
\text{OEE} = 0.6857 = \mathbf{68.57\%}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Since OEE = 68.57% < 85%, the cell is **NOT World Class**. Performance (75%) is the weakest dimension. The KTU model answer will award 1 mark for identifying the **biggest loss category** and 1 mark for suggesting **POSMOC / SMED** to improve setup, or **cycle-time optimization** to improve performance.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — DPMO and Sigma Level

> **Problem:** In a manufacturing process, **40 defects** are observed in **5 production lots**, each lot containing **1,000 units**, with each unit having **8 defect opportunities**. Calculate DPMO and Sigma Level.

**Step 1 — Total Units Produced ($N$):**

$$
N = 5 \times 1{,}000 = 5{,}000 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 2 — Total Defect Opportunities per Unit ($O$):**

$$
O = 8 \text{ opportunities/unit}
$$

**Step 3 — Total Opportunities ($N \times O$):**

$$
N \times O = 5{,}000 \times 8 = 40{,}000
$$

**Step 4 — DPMO Calculation:**

$$
\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{D \times 1{,}000{,}000}{N \times O} = \dfrac{40 \times 1{,}000{,}000}{40{,}000}
$$

$$
\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{40{,}000{,}000}{40{,}000} = \mathbf{1{,}000 \text{ DPMO}}
$$

**Step 5 — Yield ($Y$):**

$$
Y = 1 - \dfrac{\text{DPMO}}{1{,}000{,}000} = 1 - 0.001 = 0.999 = 99.9\%
$$

**Step 6 — Sigma Level (using standard Z-tables with 1.5-sigma shift):**

$$
\sigma = \text{NORMSINV}(0.999) + 1.5
$$

$$
\sigma \approx 3.09 + 1.5 \approx \mathbf{4.59\ \sigma}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Verdict:** A 4.59σ process is **NOT World Class** (threshold ≥ 4.5σ for most industries, ≥ 6σ for excellence). Recommendations: **DMAIC project**, **Pareto analysis**, and **SPC implementation**.

---

### 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix — WCM vs. Lean vs. TQM

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is **directly usable as a 7-mark comparison answer** in Part B.

| Parameter | **TQM** | **JIT** | **TPM** | **Lean** | **Six Sigma** | **WCM (Integrated)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary Goal** | Customer satisfaction | Eliminate inventory | Zero breakdown | Eliminate waste | Reduce variation | **Integrate ALL** |
| **Focus** | Quality of output | Inventory & flow | Equipment | All 8 wastes | Defect rate | **All competitive dimensions** |
| **Origin** | USA (1950s) | Japan (Toyota, 1970s) | Japan (Nakajima) | USA (1990s) | USA (Motorola, 1986) | **USA (Schonberger, 1986)** |
| **Key Tool** | SPC, QFD | Kanban, POSMOC | OEE, AM | VSM | DMAIC | **All of them** |
| **Customer Focus** | Very High | Medium | Low | High | Medium | **Very High** |
| **People Focus** | High | High | High | High | Medium | **Highest (Hall: 50%)** |
| **Inventory** | Reduce | **Eliminate** | N/A | Reduce | N/A | **Eliminate** |
| **Quality Target** | Zero defects | Reduce rework | Zero defects | Reduce rework | **3.4 DPMO** | **Zero defects + 6σ** |
| **Implementation** | Top-down + Bottom-up | Bottom-up | Bottom-up | Bottom-up | Black Belts (project) | **Top-down + Bottom-up** |
| **Scope** | All departments | Production | Maintenance | All value stream | Process-specific | **Entire enterprise** |

---

### 3.4 Hall's WCM Score (Conceptual Derivation)

Many KTU questions ask to "**evaluate WCM readiness**." A simplified scoring model:

$$
\text{WCM Score} = 0.50 \times P + 0.30 \times S + 0.20 \times T
$$

Where:
- $P$ = People maturity (0–100) — based on training hours, empowerment index, Kaizen suggestions/employee
- $S$ = Systems maturity (0–100) — based on ISO certification, ERP coverage, SPC usage
- $T$ = Technology maturity (0–100) — based on CIM/FMS/Robotics penetration

> **Example:** A plant scores $P = 80$, $S = 70$, $T = 60$.

$$
\text{WCM Score} = 0.50 \times 80 + 0.30 \times 70 + 0.20 \times 60
$$

$$
\text{WCM Score} = 40 + 21 + 12 = \mathbf{73/100}
$$

> **Verdict:** A score of 73 is *good* but **not World Class** (≥ 85 required). Improvement priority: **People** (it carries 50% weight).

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 WCM Conceptual Architecture (Hall's Three-Block Foundation)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeWCM["WCM WORLD CLASS MANUFACTURING"]
    nodeP["PEOPLE BLOCK Weight 50%"]
    nodeS["SYSTEMS BLOCK Weight 30%"]
    nodeT["TECHNOLOGY BLOCK Weight 20%"]
    nodeT1["Workforce Training"]
    nodeT2["Empowerment"]
    nodeT3["Kaizen Culture"]
    nodeS1["TQM Systems"]
    nodeS2["JIT Systems"]
    nodeS3["ISO and SPC"]
    nodeTech1["CAD CAM FMS"]
    nodeTech2["Robotics CIM"]
    nodeTech3["ERP MES"]
    nodeOut["WORLD CLASS PERFORMANCE Quality Cost Delivery Flexibility"]
    nodeP --> nodeT1
    nodeP --> nodeT2
    nodeP --> nodeT3
    nodeS --> nodeS1
    nodeS --> nodeS2
    nodeS --> nodeS3
    nodeT --> nodeTech1
    nodeT --> nodeTech2
    nodeT --> nodeTech3
    nodeT1 --> nodeWCM
    nodeT2 --> nodeWCM
    nodeT3 --> nodeWCM
    nodeS1 --> nodeWCM
    nodeS2 --> nodeWCM
    nodeS3 --> nodeWCM
    nodeTech1 --> nodeWCM
    nodeTech2 --> nodeWCM
    nodeTech3 --> nodeWCM
    nodeWCM --> nodeOut
```

---

### 4.2 WCM Umbrella — Integration of Sub-Philosophies

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeWCM2["WCM UMBRELLA"]
    nodeTQM["TQM Customer Quality"]
    nodeJIT["JIT Zero Inventory"]
    nodeTPM["TPM Zero Breakdown"]
    nodeLean["Lean Zero Waste"]
    nodeSS["Six Sigma 3.4 DPMO"]
    node5S["5S Workplace"]
    nodeK["Kaizen Continuous"]
    nodeB["Benchmarking Best in Class"]
    nodeTQM --> nodeWCM2
    nodeJIT --> nodeWCM2
    nodeTPM --> nodeWCM2
    nodeLean --> nodeWCM2
    nodeSS --> nodeWCM2
    node5S --> nodeWCM2
    nodeK --> nodeWCM2
    nodeB --> nodeWCM2
```

---

### 4.3 Schonberger's 10 Principles Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeP01["Principle 1 Gemba Go to Workplace"]
    nodeP02["Principle 2 POSMOC Setup under 10 min"]
    nodeP03["Principle 3 Group Technology Cells"]
    nodeP04["Principle 4 JIT Produce only what is needed"]
    nodeP05["Principle 5 Short Cycle Manufacturing"]
    nodeP06["Principle 6 Kaizen Continuous Improvement"]
    nodeP07["Principle 7 Customer Driven Quality"]
    nodeP08["Principle 8 TPM Total Preventive Maintenance"]
    nodeP09["Principle 9 Supplier Partnership"]
    nodeP10["Principle 10 Modern Manufacturing Technology"]
    nodeP01 --> nodeP02
    nodeP02 --> nodeP03
    nodeP03 --> nodeP04
    nodeP04 --> nodeP05
    nodeP05 --> nodeP06
    nodeP06 --> nodeP07
    nodeP07 --> nodeP08
    nodeP08 --> nodeP09
    nodeP09 --> nodeP10
    nodeP10 --> nodeR["WORLD CLASS MANUFACTURING"]
```

---

### 4.4 WCM Implementation Roadmap (5-Phase Maturity Model)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodePh1["Phase 1 Leadership Commitment"]
    nodePh2["Phase 2 Benchmarking and Gap Analysis"]
    nodePh3["Phase 3 Reengineering TQM JIT TPM"]
    nodePh4["Phase 4 Training Empowerment Kaizen"]
    nodePh5["Phase 5 Sustain and Re benchmark"]
    nodePh1 --> nodePh2
    nodePh2 --> nodePh3
    nodePh3 --> nodePh4
    nodePh4 --> nodePh5
    nodePh5 --> nodeGoal["WCM LEVEL 5 OPTIMIZING"]
```

---

### 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture: WCM Performance Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeIN["INPUTS Raw Materials Information Customer Orders"]
    nodePE["PROCESS ENGINE Three Blocks Hall Model"]
    nodePB1["People 50 percent"]
    nodePB2["Systems 30 percent"]
    nodePB3["Technology 20 percent"]
    nodeMP["MEASUREMENT OEE FPY DPMO OTD"]
    nodeFB["FEEDBACK Continuous Improvement Loop"]
    nodeOUT["OUTPUTS World Class Quality Cost Delivery Flexibility"]
    nodeIN --> nodePE
    nodePB1 --> nodePE
    nodePB2 --> nodePE
    nodePB3 --> nodePE
    nodePE --> nodeMP
    nodeMP --> nodeFB
    nodeFB --> nodePE
    nodeMP --> nodeOUT
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**

**Q1. Define World Class Manufacturing. List any FOUR characteristics of a World Class Manufacturing organization.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
World Class Manufacturing (WCM) is a set of modern production and management practices that enables a manufacturing firm to achieve the highest levels of **Quality, Cost, Delivery, and Flexibility** in its industry, on a par with the best global competitors, by integrating people, systems, and technology.

**Any Four Characteristics (½ Mark each = 2 Marks):**
1. **Customer Focus** — Quality is defined and measured by customer satisfaction.
2. **Zero Defects** — Aim for 6σ quality (≤ 3.4 DPMO).
3. **Zero Inventory** — JIT-driven pull system with minimum WIP.
4. **Zero Breakdown** — TPM-driven OEE ≥ 85%.
5. **Continuous Improvement (Kaizen)** — 1% improvement every day in every process.
6. **People Empowerment** — Multi-skilled workforce owning quality and maintenance.
7. **Short Cycle Time** — Lead time drastically reduced (orders of magnitude).
8. **Supplier Partnership** — Long-term, win-win relationships with few suppliers.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper 2]**

**Q2. State the three-block model of WCM proposed by Robert Hall. Why are people given the highest weightage?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Three Blocks (1.5 Marks):**
Robert Hall (1987) proposed that WCM is built on three interdependent blocks:
- **People** (Workforce skills, training, empowerment, Kaizen culture)
- **Systems** (TQM, JIT, ISO, SPC, ERP)
- **Technology** (CAD/CAM, FMS, CIM, Robotics, Automation)

**Weightage of People (1 Mark):**
Hall assigned approximately **50% weightage to People**, 30% to Systems, and 20% to Technology. People dominate because **technology and systems are inert without skilled, motivated human beings to operate and improve them**. Empowered employees drive continuous improvement (Kaizen), solve problems at the source (Gemba), and sustain the gains.

**Why People are Highest (0.5 Mark):**
A machine cannot think creatively or identify a new waste category. Only trained, empowered people can sustain long-term competitive advantage through innovation and improvement.

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**(a) Explain in detail Schonberger's 10 Strategic Principles of World Class Manufacturing. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
Richard J. Schonberger (1986), in his seminal work *"World Class Manufacturing: The Lessons of Simplicity Applied"*, derived 10 universal principles by observing Japanese (and later Western) best-practice plants. These principles are the **operational backbone** of WCM.

**The 10 Principles (6 Marks — 0.6 each):**

1. **Go to the Gemba (Workplace)** — Managers must physically go to the shop floor to observe processes first-hand. Decisions are made on **observed data**, not on reports. This enables quick identification of non-value-adding activities.

2. **POSMOC — Possession of Single Minute Exchange of Dies** — Reduce all machine and tool setup/changeover times to **less than 10 minutes (single digit)**. This enables small lot production, which in turn reduces inventory and lead time.

3. **Group Technology / Cellular Manufacturing** — Reorganize the plant from **functional layout** (all lathes together) to **cellular layout** (machines grouped by part family). This drastically reduces movement, lead time, and WIP.

4. **Just-In-Time (JIT) Production** — Produce only the right quantity, at the right time, in the right place. Achieve **near-zero inventory** by using a *pull* system (Kanban) triggered by actual customer demand.

5. **Short Cycle Manufacturing (Quick Flow)** — Reduce total manufacturing lead time **by orders of magnitude** (e.g., from 4 weeks to 4 days). This is achieved by eliminating non-value-added activities (transport, waiting, over-processing).

6. **Continuous Improvement (Kaizen)** — Establish a culture of **small, daily, employee-driven improvements**. Cumulative 1% daily improvement ≈ 38× improvement per year (compound effect).

7. **Customer-Driven Quality (QFD)** — Quality specifications are derived from **customer requirements** using tools like Quality Function Deployment (QFD). Internal specs not traceable to customers are eliminated.

8. **Total Preventive Maintenance (TPM)** — Shift maintenance responsibility to **operators** (Autonomous Maintenance) to achieve zero breakdowns, zero defects, and zero accidents. OEE target ≥ 85%.

9. **Supplier Integration (Partnership)** — Reduce the supplier base to a few **strategic partners**. Share forecasts, costs, and improvement goals. Long-term contracts over short-term price wars.

10. **Modern Manufacturing Technology** — Use CIM, FMS, Robotics, and IT to **eliminate paperwork, manual handling, and information delays**. Technology supports, but does not replace, the human system.

**Conclusion (0 Marks reserved, but recommended for full marks):**
These 10 principles form a **coherent, mutually reinforcing system** — implementing only one or two (e.g., only JIT) does not yield WCM benefits; all 10 must be pursued concurrently.

**(b) Compare TQM, JIT, TPM, Lean, and Six Sigma as sub-philosophies of WCM. Discuss the role of benchmarking in achieving WCM. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Comparative Table (3.5 Marks — 0.5 per attribute):**

| Attribute | TQM | JIT | TPM | Lean | Six Sigma |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Focus** | Quality output | Inventory | Equipment | All waste | Variation |
| **Origin** | USA 1950s | Japan 1970s | Japan 1971 | USA 1990 | USA 1986 |
| **Key Tool** | SPC, QFD | Kanban, POSMOC | OEE, AM | VSM, 5S | DMAIC |
| **People Role** | High | High | High | High | Medium |
| **Inventory** | Reduce | Eliminate | N/A | Reduce | N/A |
| **Target** | Zero defects | Zero WIP | Zero breakdown | Zero waste | 3.4 DPMO |

**Sub-Philosophies under WCM (1 Mark):**
WCM is the **integrative umbrella** that requires **all five** philosophies working in harmony. A factory practicing only TQM still has inventory (JIT gap) and breakdowns (TPM gap), so it is not world class. **WCM = TQM ∪ JIT ∪ TPM ∪ Lean ∪ Six Sigma.**

**Benchmarking — Definition (1 Mark):**
Benchmarking is the **systematic process of measuring one's performance against the best-in-class competitors (world leaders)**, identifying the **performance gap**, and implementing **best practices** to close it.

**Types of Benchmarking (1 Mark):**
- **Internal** (best practices within own company)
- **Competitive** (against direct competitors)
- **Functional** (against best in same function, any industry)
- **Generic** (against world-class processes, any industry — *this drives WCM*)

**Role in WCM (0.5 Mark):**
Benchmarking provides the **external reference** for "world class" status. Without benchmarking, "world class" is an unverified claim. It identifies the gap, drives target setting, and prevents complacency.

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) "WCM is a journey, not a destination." Discuss the implementation phases of WCM with a suitable model. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**The Statement (1 Mark):**
*"WCM is a journey, not a destination"* captures the essence of WCM — it is an **ongoing pursuit of excellence** requiring continuous improvement (Kaizen). Even world leaders like Toyota continue to benchmark and improve. There is **no final "we have arrived"** state.

**Why a Phased Approach? (1 Mark):**
WCM involves **deep cultural, technical, and systemic change**. Sudden, wholesale change causes resistance and failure. A **phased, step-by-step approach** allows learning, quick wins, and stakeholder buy-in.

**The 5-Phase Implementation Model (4 Marks — 0.8 each):**

**Phase 1 — Leadership Commitment & Vision (Top-Down Start):**
Top management publicly commits to WCM, defines a clear vision, allocates budget, and appoints a WCM steering committee. Without leadership commitment, all efforts die.

**Phase 2 — Baseline Assessment & Benchmarking:**
Measure current performance (OEE, DPMO, OTD, Inventory Turns) and compare against world-class benchmarks. Identify gaps and prioritize the top 3–5 gaps based on impact.

**Phase 3 — Process Re-engineering & Tool Deployment:**
Roll out the constituent tools: **TQM (SPC, QFD), JIT (Kanban, POSMOC), TPM (Autonomous Maintenance), Lean (5S, VSM), Six Sigma (DMAIC)**. Pilot in one area, demonstrate success, then expand.

**Phase 4 — Workforce Empowerment & Kaizen Culture:**
Conduct mass training, deploy suggestion systems (e.g., 100 suggestions/employee/year), form Quality Circles, and empower operators to stop the line (Jidoka). The **People** block (50% weight) is built here.

**Phase 5 — Sustain, Audit & Re-benchmark:**
Use audits, KPIs, and recognition systems to **sustain gains**. Re-benchmark periodically — the world leader today may not be the world leader tomorrow. Loop back to Phase 2.

**Conclusion (1 Mark):**
The phased model ensures that WCM becomes a **self-renewing capability** of the organization, not a one-time project.

**(b) A CNC machining cell has the following data: Planned Production Time = 450 min, Operating Time = 380 min, Ideal Cycle Time = 0.8 min/part, Total Parts Produced = 400, Good Parts = 380. Compute OEE, interpret the result, and suggest improvements to reach world-class level. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Availability (1 Mark):**

$$
A = \dfrac{\text{Operating Time}}{\text{Planned Production Time}} = \dfrac{380}{450} = 0.8444 = 84.44\%
$$

**Step 2 — Performance (1 Mark):**

$$
P = \dfrac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Pieces}}{\text{Operating Time}} = \dfrac{0.8 \times 400}{380} = \dfrac{320}{380} = 0.8421 = 84.21\%
$$

**Step 3 — Quality (1 Mark):**

$$
Q = \dfrac{\text{Good Pieces}}{\text{Total Pieces}} = \dfrac{380}{400} = 0.9500 = 95.00\%
$$

**Step 4 — OEE (1 Mark):**

$$
\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q = 0.8444 \times 0.8421 \times 0.9500
$$

$$
\text{OEE} = 0.6755 = \mathbf{67.55\%}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation (1 Mark):**
The OEE of **67.55% is far below the world-class benchmark of 85%**. All three dimensions need improvement, with **Quality (95%)** being slightly better, and **Availability (84.44%)** and **Performance (84.21%)** needing the most attention.

**Step 6 — Recommendations to Reach WCM (2 Marks — 0.5 each):**
1. **TPM (Autonomous Maintenance)** to reduce unplanned downtime → improve Availability.
2. **POSMOC / SMED** to reduce changeover/setup time, and **cycle-time reduction** to improve Performance.
3. **Six Sigma DMAIC** + **Poka-Yoke (mistake-proofing)** to reduce defects → improve Quality.
4. **Kaizen events** for small, continuous improvements across all three dimensions.
5. **Operator training and empowerment** (People block) to sustain improvements.

**Conclusion:**
With a structured WCM implementation plan, the cell can realistically achieve OEE ≥ 85% within **12–18 months**, reaching world-class status.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing WCM with TQM alone** — TQM is a *subset* of WCM, not equivalent. Defining WCM as "advanced TQM" loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Reversing Hall's weightage** — Saying "Technology is most important" loses marks. **People = 50%** is the textbook answer.
> 3. **Skipping People block in implementation phases** — Many students list only TQM/JIT/TPM tools and forget the **cultural / training phase**. KTU deducts 1–2 marks for this.
> 4. **In OEE problems, forgetting to multiply A × P × Q** — Some students compute each separately and forget the final multiplication. Always show the **multiplication line explicitly**.
> 5. **Mixing up POSMOC (Schonberger) with SMED (Shingo)** — Both refer to the *same concept* of single-digit setup. Use the term your textbook uses consistently. Schonberger called it POSMOC.
> 6. **In comparison tables, omitting one philosophy** — KTU typically awards marks *proportionally* to the number of philosophies correctly compared. Missing one costs ~0.5–1 mark.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets — Read Before Every Exam:**

- **Definition:** WCM = World-leading performance in **Quality, Cost, Delivery, Flexibility**, sustained over time.
- **Originators:** **Schonberger** (10 principles, 1986) and **Hall** (3-block model, 1987).
- **Hall's 3 Blocks (with weightage):** **People (50%) > Systems (30%) > Technology (20%)** — *People always first*.
- **Schonberger's 10 Principles:** Gemba, POSMOC, Group Tech, JIT, Short-Cycle, Kaizen, Customer-Quality, TPM, Supplier Partnership, Modern Tech.
- **WCM Umbrella includes:** TQM + JIT + TPM + Lean + Six Sigma + 5S + Kaizen + Benchmarking — *all integrated*.
- **OEE Formula:** $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$ — World-class target = **≥ 85%**.
- **Six Sigma:** **3.4 DPMO** = 6σ (with 1.5σ shift convention).
- **Inventory Turns target:** **≥ 12/year** (world-class WCM often ≥ 30).
- **POSMOC:** Setup time **< 10 minutes** (single digit).
- **JIT:** Pull system, **Kanban**, zero inventory.
- **TPM:** Autonomous maintenance by **operators**, OEE focus.
- **5S:** Sort, Set-in-order, Shine, Standardize, Sustain.
- **Benchmarking:** Measuring against **best-in-class globally**, not just competitors.
- **5-Phase Implementation:** Leadership → Benchmark → Re-engineer → Empower People → Sustain/Re-benchmark.
- **KTU Mantra for WCM:** *Integration + People + Continuous Improvement + Benchmarking + All dimensions together.*
- **Common exam traps:** WCM ≠ TQM, People > Technology, Schonberger ≠ Hall (different frameworks, do not mix principles with blocks).
- **Real-world examples to mention in answers:** Toyota, Intel, Bosch, GE, Xerox (the original benchmarker).
